topics.title: Android Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Android Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Android Architecture** refers to the layered software stack that governs how an Android-based device — including smartphones, tablets, wearables, and IoT edge nodes — organizes its operating system components, system services, runtime environments, and user-facing applications. As defined in the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Mobile App Development for IoT (PECGT415)*, the architecture follows a **stack-of-layers model** (often called the *Android Software Stack*) comprising five primary tiers: the **Linux Kernel**, the **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)**, the **Android Runtime (ART)** with native **C/C++ Libraries**, the **Java API Framework**, and the **System & User Applications**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The Android architecture is the foundational framework for understanding how IoT mobile applications communicate with sensors, actuators, cloud services, and edge devices. Every IoT app developer must understand these layers because the choice of API directly determines the hardware reach and real-time performance of the IoT solution.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

To make this immediately intuitive, imagine an Android device as a **large, high-end restaurant kitchen** that serves millions of customer orders (user interactions and IoT data events) every day.

| Android Layer | Restaurant Analogy | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Linux Kernel** | The building foundation, gas pipes, water lines, and electrical wiring | Manages raw hardware resources (CPU, memory, power, drivers) |
| **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** | The kitchen equipment interfaces (oven knobs, burner dials) | Provides standardized interfaces to talk to hardware |
| **Android Runtime (ART)** | The head chef and cooking station | Executes app code efficiently, manages memory (garbage collection) |
| **Native C/C++ Libraries** | The recipe books and pre-prepared ingredients | Ready-made powerful functions (graphics, media, SQLite, WebKit) |
| **Java API Framework** | The waiters, order system, and menu | Provides high-level building blocks (UI components, Content Providers) |
| **Applications** | The dishes actually served to customers | End-user apps and IoT control panels |

In this analogy, when you order a dish through an **IoT app** (say, "turn on my smart living room light"), the request goes down through the layers — from the app, through the API framework, down to the kernel — and the actual command goes to the physical light bulb. The status of the bulb then travels back up the stack to display in your app.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Each layer is a *peer* of the layer above and below it. A layer only talks to its immediate neighbors, which is called the **principle of layered isolation**. This isolation is what makes Android secure, modular, and portable across thousands of different IoT hardware devices.

## 1.3 Why Android Architecture Matters for IoT

In the context of **Mobile App Development for IoT**, the architecture is critical because:

- **Heterogeneous Hardware Support:** IoT devices come in countless form factors (ESP32 boards, Raspberry Pi gateways, smart watches). Android's HAL allows the same app to work on all of them.
- **Real-time Sensor Access:** Layers like the **Sensor Framework** (in Java API) and **HAL sensors** provide structured access to accelerometers, gyroscopes, temperature, and heart-rate sensors vital for IoT.
- **Security Boundaries:** The **sandboxed application model** (each app runs in its own UID/process) ensures an IoT app cannot maliciously tamper with another app or system service.
- **Network & Connectivity Stacks:** Bluetooth, NFC, Wi-Fi Direct, and cellular protocols are implemented as **native libraries** accessed via high-level Java APIs, enabling IoT communication.

---

**Physical/Standard Metrics to Remember:**

- **API Levels:** Android has evolved through API levels 1 → **35 (Android 15)** as of 2024.
- **Process Isolation:** Each Android app runs in its own Linux process with a unique **UID (User Identifier)**.
- **ART Compilation:** Uses **Ahead-Of-Time (AOT)**, **Just-In-Time (JIT)**, and **profile-guided compilation**.
- **Dalvik vs ART:** Dalvik (Android < 5.0) used JIT; ART (Android ≥ 5.0) primarily uses AOT, with hybrid mode in Android 7+.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Layers — Operational Breakdown

### Layer 1: Linux Kernel (Bottom-most Layer)

- **Function:** Provides the foundational abstraction between physical hardware and the upper software layers. It is based on the **Linux kernel (currently 5.x/6.x LTS for Android 14/15)**.
- **Key Sub-components Active in IoT:**
  - **Binder IPC Driver:** Enables inter-process communication between apps and system services. For IoT, this is what allows an app to send data to a system service like `BluetoothManager`.
  - **Low Memory Killer (LMK):** Aggressively reclaims memory — crucial for IoT edge devices with limited RAM.
  - **Power Management (Wake Locks, wakelocks):** Allows IoT apps to keep the CPU awake to receive sensor data or maintain BLE connections even when the screen is off.
  - **Network Stack (TCP/IP, Wi-Fi, Cellular drivers):** Provides the base for IoT cloud communication.
  - **Camera, Audio, Bluetooth, USB drivers:** Direct hardware access.
- **Why it matters:** Without this layer, an IoT app couldn't talk to the physical Wi-Fi chip or the BLE radio.

### Layer 2: Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)

- **Function:** The HAL defines a **standard interface** for hardware vendors to expose their device-specific drivers to the higher-level Java API Framework. It is a **vendor-implemented shim** in C/C++.
- **Module Structure:** Each HAL interface is broken into *modules* (e.g., `camera.hal`, `sensors.hal`, `nfc.hal`, `bluetooth.hal`).
- **IoT Relevance:** A manufacturer of a new IoT edge gateway only needs to implement the HAL modules for their custom sensors. The rest of Android (and the IoT app) remains unchanged.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Point:** *"What is the role of HAL in Android?"* — HAL decouples the upper layers (Java API) from specific hardware implementations, allowing Android to run on diverse IoT hardware without recompiling the framework.

### Layer 3: Android Runtime (ART) + Native C/C++ Libraries

#### 3a. Native C/C++ Libraries
- These are pre-compiled, high-performance libraries that the Java framework calls via **JNI (Java Native Interface)**.
- Critical libraries for IoT:
  - **OpenGL ES / Vulkan:** Graphics rendering for IoT dashboards.
  - **Media Framework:** For streaming audio/video from IoT cameras.
  - **SQLite:** Local persistence of sensor data when offline.
  - **WebKit/Chromium:** Embedded web rendering for IoT web-based config UIs.
  - **FreeType:** Font rendering.
  - **SSL/crypto libraries:** Secure communication with IoT cloud (MQTT over TLS).

#### 3b. Android Runtime (ART)
- **Function:** The execution engine that runs Android applications.
- **Execution Model:**
  - Java/Kotlin source → compiled to **.class** files (bytecode) → translated to **.dex** (Dalvik Executable) by `dx`/`d8` tool → packaged in **.apk**.
  - At install/run time, ART converts `.dex` into **native machine code**.
- **Compilation Strategies:**
  - **AOT (Ahead-Of-Time):** Compiles the entire app at install time → faster runtime, larger storage.
  - **JIT (Just-In-Time):** Compiles on demand during runtime → smaller storage, slower first runs.
  - **Hybrid (since Android 7):** AOT for hot code paths, JIT for the rest.
- **Memory Management:** ART uses **Generational Garbage Collection** with concurrent mark-sweep, reducing pause times — important for real-time IoT feedback.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differences from Dalvik (Older Engine):**
> | Feature | Dalvik | ART |
> |---|---|---|
> | Compilation | JIT only | AOT + JIT hybrid |
> | GC pauses | Longer | Shorter (concurrent) |
> | App install time | Faster | Slower (due to AOT) |
> | Runtime speed | Slower | Faster |
> | Introduced | Android 1.0 | Android 5.0 (Lollipop) |

### Layer 4: Java API Framework (Application Framework)

- **Function:** Provides high-level building blocks that app developers (including IoT app developers) use. Written primarily in **Java/Kotlin**.
- **Major Service Components:**
  - **Activity Manager:** Manages the *activity lifecycle* (onCreate, onStart, onResume, onPause, onStop, onDestroy). Critical for IoT apps that lose/regain focus.
  - **Window Manager:** Manages the UI surface and on-screen windows.
  - **Content Providers:** Allow data sharing between apps (e.g., an IoT weather app sharing data with a smart home controller).
  - **View System:** The UI toolkit (Buttons, TextViews, RecyclerViews, etc.).
  - **Package Manager:** Tracks installed apps.
  - **Resource Manager:** Manages localized strings, layouts, drawables.
  - **Notification Manager:** Push alerts from IoT events (e.g., "Motion detected at front door").
  - **Location Manager, Sensor Manager, Telephony Manager:** Direct IoT-relevant APIs.

### Layer 5: System & User Applications (Top Layer)

- **System Apps:** Pre-installed (Phone, Contacts, Settings, Camera, Messaging).
- **User Apps:** Installed via APK from Play Store or sideloaded.
- **IoT-Specific App Examples:** Smart home controllers (Google Home, Alexa), industrial IoT dashboards, BLE scanner apps, MQTT client apps.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Table

| # | Layer | Language/Tech | Key Components | IoT Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linux Kernel | C | Binder IPC, LMK, Wi-Fi/BT drivers, Power Mgmt | Hardware control, process isolation |
| 2 | HAL | C/C++ (interface definitions) | `sensors.hal`, `bluetooth.hal`, `nfc.hal` | Vendor hardware abstraction |
| 3 | Native Libraries + ART | C/C++ + Java bytecode | OpenGL, SQLite, SSL, Media, ART runtime | High-perf, secure, persistent IoT ops |
| 4 | Java API Framework | Java/Kotlin | Activity Mgr, Sensor Mgr, Notification Mgr | Building IoT app UIs and services |
| 5 | Applications | Java/Kotlin + XML | APK packages | End-user IoT interfaces |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Home (Consumer IoT):** Apps use the **Sensor Framework** + **Bluetooth Manager** + **Wi-Fi APIs** to control smart bulbs, thermostats.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Apps use **Native C++ libraries** for processing large sensor streams, with **ART** ensuring low-latency execution.
- **Wearables (Health IoT):** Uses **HAL sensors** to read heart rate, and **Notification Manager** to deliver alerts.
- **Automotive IoT (Android Auto):** Uses **Car API** in the Java API Framework layer for in-vehicle infotainment.
- **Edge Gateways:** Android-based gateways (e.g., running on a Raspberry Pi 4 with Android Things / AOSP) use the full stack to aggregate sensors and forward to the cloud.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The "Android Things" project (now succeeded by AOSP for IoT) used exactly this architecture but stripped the UI layer to focus on edge IoT. Understanding each layer helps in deciding *where* in the stack to optimize an IoT solution.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Walkthrough: "How an IoT App Triggers a Smart Light"

This analytical walkthrough traces a single IoT command ("Turn ON living room light") as it propagates **down** and the response **up** through every architectural layer. This is the most exam-relevant conceptual flow for KTU.

### Step 1: User Taps the UI Button (Layer 5 — Application)

- The user opens an IoT smart-home app and taps a button in the UI.
- This UI is defined in an XML layout file, and the click is handled in the **Activity** (Java/Kotlin code).
- A click listener invokes a method like `lightService.turnOn()`.

### Step 2: Java API Framework is Invoked (Layer 4)

- The Activity calls a high-level API, e.g., `BluetoothManager`, `MqttClient`, or a custom `Retrofit` HTTP client.
- The framework handles threading, lifecycle, and IPC routing.
- **Example code path:**
  `App → BluetoothAdapter → BluetoothGattCallback (callback) → writeCharacteristic()`

### Step 3: IPC via Binder to System Service (Layer 4 → Layer 1)

- The app process cannot directly access hardware. It sends an **IPC (Inter-Process Communication)** call via the **Binder driver** (part of the Linux Kernel).
- The Binder forwards the request to a **system_server** process hosting the relevant system service (e.g., `bluetoothd`).
- The system service then prepares to talk to the HAL.

### Step 4: HAL Bridges to Vendor Driver (Layer 2)

- The system service calls the appropriate **HAL module** (e.g., `bluetooth.hal`).
- The HAL module is implemented by the **device vendor (OEM)** — say, Qualcomm or MediaTek.
- The HAL translates the generic Android command into vendor-specific calls.

### Step 5: Linux Kernel Driver Executes (Layer 1)

- The kernel's **Bluetooth driver** (e.g., `btusb.ko` for USB-based BT, or `hci_uart.ko` for UART) physically drives the Bluetooth radio.
- The radio transmits a BLE/Classic-Bluetooth packet to the **smart bulb** over the 2.4 GHz ISM band.

### Step 6: Smart Bulb Responds (External to Android)

- The bulb's microcontroller switches the LED driver on.
- It sends back an **acknowledgment (ACK)** packet over BLE.

### Step 7: Response Travels Back Up the Stack

- The kernel receives the BLE ACK.
- It propagates up via the HAL to the system service.
- The system service sends an **IPC reply** back to the app via Binder.
- The app updates the UI (button changes color to "ON") via the **View System**.

### Step 8: ART Manages Memory Throughout

- Throughout this flow, **ART** is responsible for executing the app's Java/Kotlin code and managing memory.
- It performs **garbage collection** of any temporary objects created during the operation.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: IoT Operation vs. Android Layer Responsible

| Step in IoT Operation | Android Layer(s) Used | Specific Component(s) | Failure Mode if Layer Faulty |
|---|---|---|---|
| User interface display | Layer 5 + Layer 4 | Activity, View System | UI doesn't render |
| Tap event handling | Layer 4 | Activity Manager, Event Listeners | Button unresponsive |
| Communication initiation | Layer 4 | BluetoothManager / MqttClient | App cannot reach device |
| Inter-process messaging | Layer 1 | Binder IPC Driver | App hangs, no service response |
| Vendor hardware control | Layer 2 | HAL modules | Device-specific bugs, crashes |
| Physical radio transmission | Layer 1 | Kernel driver (e.g., `btusb`) | No wireless transmission |
| Memory and code execution | Layer 3 | ART + Native Libraries | App crash, OOM (Out of Memory) |
| Notification of result | Layer 4 | NotificationManager | User not alerted to IoT event |
| Data persistence | Layer 3 | SQLite native library | Sensor data lost on reboot |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Android Build & Installation Flow (APK Creation)

This is another high-yield KTU topic — the lifecycle of an Android application from source to installation.

### Step 1: Source Code Compilation
- The developer writes Java/Kotlin source code (`.java`/`.kt`) and XML resources (`res/layout`, `res/values`).
- The Android **Asset Packaging Tool (AAPT2)** processes resources and generates `R.java` (resource references).

### Step 2: Bytecode Generation
- The Java compiler (`javac`) compiles `.java` files into `.class` files containing Java bytecode.
- Kotlin compiler (`kotlinc`) does the same for `.kt` files.

### Step 3: DEX Conversion
- The `d8` tool (modern) converts all `.class` files into a single `classes.dex` file (Dalvik Executable format).
- The DEX format is optimized for low-memory devices — relevant to IoT edge nodes.

### Step 4: APK Assembly
- All components — `classes.dex`, resources, native libraries (`lib/`) — are zipped into an **APK (Android Package)** file.
- The APK is signed using the developer's keystore.

### Step 5: Installation on Device
- The user installs the APK on a device.
- ART performs **AOT compilation** of the DEX into native machine code, storing the result in `/data/app/`.

### Step 6: Runtime Execution
- The app launches via the **Activity Manager**, and ART executes the compiled native code.
- Lifecycle methods (onCreate → onStart → onResume) are called.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Be able to name and order the tools: `javac` → `d8` → `AAPT2` → `apksigner` → APK. Also remember that on modern Android, **R8** replaces the older ProGuard for code shrinking and obfuscation.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis: Dalvik vs. ART

| Metric | Dalvik (Android ≤ 4.4) | ART (Android ≥ 5.0) |
|---|---|---|
| Compilation | JIT (runtime) | AOT (install time) + JIT (runtime) hybrid |
| Battery | Heavier (constant JIT) | Lighter (mostly AOT) |
| Storage | Less | More (AOT cache) |
| App install time | Quick | Slower |
| GC mechanism | Stop-the-world mark-sweep | Concurrent generational GC |
| 64-bit support | Limited | Native 64-bit |
| Used in modern IoT? | No | Yes (essential for Android 14/15 IoT) |

---

## 3.5 Process and Memory Model for IoT Apps

Each Android app runs in its own **Linux process** with:

- **Unique UID (User ID):** Provides file-system-level isolation.
- **Unique GID (Group ID):** Used for shared permissions.
- **SElinux context:** Enforces mandatory access control.
- **Dalvik VM instance:** Each app gets its own ART runtime instance.

**For IoT apps, this means:**
- An IoT app cannot read another app's private data unless explicitly granted.
- A misbehaving IoT app cannot directly crash another app — it can only crash its own process.
- The OS can kill background IoT apps to free memory using the **Low Memory Killer (LMK)** daemon.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Summary:** The Android architecture is fundamentally a *layered, isolated, modular* system. For IoT, this means: **hardware diversity is handled by HAL**, **performance is handled by ART + native libs**, **developer productivity is handled by the Java API Framework**, and **user interaction is handled by the Application Layer**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Android Software Stack — Mermaid Block Diagram

This is the **canonical KTU diagram** for Android Architecture. Memorize this for the 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5["LAYER 5: SYSTEM AND USER APPLICATIONS\nHome, Contacts, Settings, Camera, IoT Control Apps"]
    L4["LAYER 4: JAVA API FRAMEWORK\nActivity Manager, Window Manager, Content Providers,\nView System, Package Manager, Notification Manager,\nResource Manager, Sensor Manager, Location Manager"]
    L3A["LAYER 3a: NATIVE C/C++ LIBRARIES\nOpenGL ES, Media Framework, SQLite,\nWebKit, FreeType, SSL, libc"]
    L3B["LAYER 3b: ANDROID RUNTIME ART\nCore Libraries, ART Virtual Machine,\nAOT and JIT Compilation, Garbage Collector"]
    L2["LAYER 2: HARDWARE ABSTRACTION LAYER HAL\nStandard Interface Modules:\ncamera.hal, sensors.hal, bluetooth.hal, nfc.hal"]
    L1["LAYER 1: LINUX KERNEL\nBinder IPC, Power Management, Low Memory Killer,\nNetwork Stack, Drivers for Wi-Fi, BT, Camera, Audio"]

    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3A
    L4 --> L3B
    L3A --> L2
    L3B --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

## 4.2 IoT Command Flow — From App Tap to Hardware Action

This diagram traces the **same light-on command** you analyzed in Section 3, visualizing how the data crosses architectural boundaries.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["USER TAPS BUTTON\nIn IoT App\nLayer 5"] --> B["JAVA API FRAMEWORK\nBluetoothManager or MqttClient\nLayer 4"]
    B --> C["BINDER IPC DRIVER\nIn Linux Kernel\nLayer 1"]
    C --> D["SYSTEM SERVICE\nbluetoothd running in\nsystem_server process\nLayer 4 cross"]
    D --> E["HAL MODULE\nbluetooth.hal\nLayer 2"]
    E --> F["KERNEL DRIVER\nbtusb.ko or hci_uart.ko\nLayer 1"]
    F --> G["PHYSICAL BLE RADIO\n2.4 GHz Transmission\nExternal Hardware"]
    G --> H["SMART BULB MICROCONTROLLER\nSwitches LED Driver"]
    H --> I["ACK PACKET\nReturns to Device\nLayer 1"]
    I --> J["UI UPDATED\nButton shows ON State\nLayer 5"]
    J --> A
```

## 4.3 APK Build Pipeline — Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC["JAVA AND KOTLIN SOURCE\n.java and .kt files\nXML Resources res"]
    AAPT["AAPT2\nResource Compiler\nGenerates R.java"]
    JAVAC["JAVAC AND KOTLINC\nBytecode Compiler\nGenerates .class files"]
    D8["D8 TOOL\nConverts .class to classes.dex\nDalvik Executable Format"]
    R8["R8 OPTIMIZER\nShrinks, Obfuscates, Optimizes"]
    APK["APK ASSEMBLY\nZip of classes.dex, resources, libs"]
    SIGN["APKSIGNER\nSigns APK with Developer Keystore"]
    INSTALL["INSTALL ON DEVICE\nART performs AOT Compilation"]
    RUN["RUNTIME EXECUTION\nART runs Native Code\nLayer 3b"]

    SRC --> AAPT
    SRC --> JAVAC
    AAPT --> APK
    JAVAC --> D8
    D8 --> R8
    R8 --> APK
    APK --> SIGN
    SIGN --> INSTALL
    INSTALL --> RUN
```

## 4.4 Layer-to-IoT-Component Mapping Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER L5"]
        APP1["Smart Home App"]
        APP2["Industrial IoT Dashboard"]
        APP3["Wearable Health Monitor"]
    end

    subgraph API["API FRAMEWORK L4"]
        API1["Activity Manager"]
        API2["Sensor Manager"]
        API3["Bluetooth Manager"]
        API4["Notification Manager"]
    end

    subgraph RT["RUNTIME AND NATIVE L3"]
        RT1["ART VM"]
        RT2["SQLite Library"]
        RT3["SSL TLS Library"]
    end

    subgraph HAL["HARDWARE ABSTRACTION L2"]
        HAL1["sensors.hal"]
        HAL2["bluetooth.hal"]
        HAL3["wifi.hal"]
    end

    subgraph KERN["LINUX KERNEL L1"]
        KERN1["BT Driver"]
        KERN2["Wi-Fi Driver"]
        KERN3["Sensor Driver"]
        KERN4["Binder IPC"]
    end

    APP1 --> API1
    APP1 --> API3
    APP2 --> API2
    APP3 --> API2
    API1 --> RT1
    API2 --> HAL1
    API3 --> HAL2
    API4 --> RT1
    RT1 --> HAL2
    RT2 --> APP1
    RT3 --> APP2
    HAL1 --> KERN3
    HAL2 --> KERN1
    HAL3 --> KERN2
    API3 --> KERN4
```

## 4.5 ART vs. Dalvik Compilation Flow (Comparative)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DALVIK["DALVIK OLD ANDROID LESS THAN 5.0"]
        D1[".dex bytecode"] --> D2["JIT Compilation at Runtime"]
        D2 --> D3["Execute Native Code"]
        D2 --> D4["Higher Battery Drain"]
    end

    subgraph ARTMOD["ART MODERN ANDROID 5.0 PLUS"]
        A1[".dex bytecode"] --> A2["AOT Compilation at Install Time"]
        A2 --> A3["Store Native Cache"]
        A3 --> A4["Fast Runtime Execution"]
        A1 -.hybrid.-> A5["JIT for New Hot Code"]
        A5 --> A4
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) in Android. Why is it significant for IoT? [3 Marks]

> **[Model Answer]**
>
> The **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** is the second layer from the bottom in the Android software stack. It consists of **standardized interface modules** (e.g., `sensors.hal`, `bluetooth.hal`, `camera.hal`) implemented in C/C++ by the device manufacturer (OEM). The HAL sits between the Linux kernel and the Android Runtime/Java API Framework, providing a **uniform, vendor-neutral interface** that the higher-level system services can call.
>
> **Significance for IoT:**
> 1. **Hardware diversity:** IoT devices have varied sensors and radios. HAL lets the same Android framework (and same app) work across all of them.
> 2. **Vendor flexibility:** OEMs only need to implement the HAL module; they don't have to modify the framework.
> 3. **Faster IoT product development:** New IoT hardware can be supported simply by writing a new HAL module.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Dalvik Virtual Machine and Android Runtime (ART). [3 Marks]

> **[Model Answer]**
>
> | Feature | Dalvik | ART |
> |---|---|---|
> | Compilation strategy | Just-In-Time (JIT) — compiles at runtime | Ahead-Of-Time (AOT) + JIT hybrid — compiles at install/runtime |
> | Garbage collection | Stop-the-world, less efficient | Concurrent generational, less pause |
> | Battery consumption | Higher | Lower |
> | Storage footprint | Smaller | Larger (due to AOT cache) |
> | First introduced | Android 1.0 | Android 5.0 (Lollipop) |
> | Modern relevance | Deprecated | Standard in all modern Android (incl. IoT) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Explain the Android Architecture in detail with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each layer with focus on IoT applications. [14 Marks]

> **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Describe the five layers of Android architecture with a block diagram.**
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> The Android architecture is organized as a **stack of five layers**, where each layer serves the layer above it and uses the services of the layer below. From top to bottom, the layers are:
>
> **1. System and User Applications (Layer 5):** This is the topmost layer containing both **pre-installed system apps** (Phone, Contacts, Settings, Camera) and **user-installed apps** downloaded from the Play Store or sideloaded as APKs. For IoT, this includes smart-home controllers, industrial monitoring apps, BLE scanner apps, and MQTT client apps. These apps are what the user directly interacts with.
>
> **2. Java/Kotlin API Framework (Layer 4):** This layer provides the **high-level building blocks** for app development. Major components include:
> - **Activity Manager** — manages app lifecycle and activity stack.
> - **Window Manager** — manages on-screen windows.
> - **Content Providers** — enable inter-app data sharing.
> - **View System** — UI components (Buttons, TextViews, RecyclerViews).
> - **Package Manager** — tracks installed apps.
> - **Resource Manager** — handles localized resources.
> - **Notification Manager** — push notifications.
> - **Sensor Manager, Location Manager, Telephony Manager** — IoT-relevant APIs.
>
> **3. Android Runtime (ART) + Native C/C++ Libraries (Layer 3):** This layer is split into two sub-parts:
> - **Native Libraries** (C/C++): OpenGL ES, Media Framework, SQLite, WebKit, SSL — invoked via JNI.
> - **ART** — the execution engine for app code. It performs AOT + JIT compilation, manages memory via concurrent garbage collection, and supports 64-bit execution.
>
> **4. Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) (Layer 2):** A set of **standardized interface modules** implemented by device vendors. The HAL decouples the framework from specific hardware, allowing Android to run on diverse IoT hardware.
>
> **5. Linux Kernel (Layer 1):** The base of the stack. Includes:
> - **Binder IPC driver** for inter-process communication.
> - **Power management** (wakelocks) for IoT background tasks.
> - **Low Memory Killer** for memory-constrained IoT devices.
> - **Network stack** (TCP/IP, Wi-Fi, cellular).
> - **Hardware drivers** (BT, NFC, Camera, Audio, Sensors).
>
> **[Block Diagram — see Section 4.1]**

> **Part (b) [7 Marks]: Explain the role of each layer in IoT application development with examples.**
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> Each layer plays a distinct role in IoT:
>
> | Layer | IoT Role | Example |
> |---|---|---|
> | **L5: Applications** | Provide user-facing IoT interfaces | Google Home app, MQTT dashboard |
> | **L4: API Framework** | Provide high-level IoT APIs (Bluetooth, Sensors, Notifications) | `BluetoothManager.connect()` |
> | **L3a: Native Libraries** | High-performance operations on IoT data | SQLite for sensor logs, SSL for MQTT-TLS |
> | **L3b: ART** | Efficient execution of IoT app code with low GC pauses | Compiled `.dex` → native code |
> | **L2: HAL** | Vendor-neutral access to IoT hardware | `sensors.hal` for accelerometer |
> | **L1: Linux Kernel** | Direct hardware control, process isolation, power mgmt | `btusb.ko` driver for BLE radio |
>
> **Concrete IoT Example:** A *wearable health monitor* (e.g., a smart band) uses:
> - **L1** — the BLE driver to communicate with the paired smartphone.
> - **L2** — the `sensors.hal` module to expose heart-rate and step-count sensors.
> - **L3a** — SQLite to buffer readings when the phone is offline.
> - **L3b** — ART to run the data aggregation algorithm with low battery drain.
> - **L4** — `NotificationManager` to alert the user of abnormal readings.
> - **L5** — the user interface showing heart rate trends.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative): Discuss the Android Runtime (ART). Compare it with Dalvik. Explain the role of the Linux Kernel and HAL in supporting IoT hardware diversity. [14 Marks]

> **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Android Runtime (ART) — features, working, and comparison with Dalvik.**
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> The **Android Runtime (ART)** is the application runtime environment introduced in **Android 5.0 (Lollipop)** to replace the older Dalvik Virtual Machine. ART is responsible for **executing Android application code** (`.dex` format) on the device.
>
> **Working of ART:**
> 1. The developer's Java/Kotlin source is compiled to **bytecode** (`.class` files) using `javac`/`kotlinc`.
> 2. The `d8` tool converts `.class` files to a single `classes.dex` file.
> 3. The DEX file is packaged into the APK.
> 4. On installation, ART performs **AOT (Ahead-Of-Time) compilation** of the DEX into native machine code, storing the result on the device.
> 5. At runtime, additional **JIT (Just-In-Time) compilation** is performed for newly encountered code paths.
> 6. **Generational Garbage Collection** runs concurrently to reclaim memory with minimal pause times.
>
> **Comparison with Dalvik:**
> | Aspect | Dalvik | ART |
> |---|---|---|
> | Compilation | JIT only | AOT + JIT hybrid |
> | Compilation timing | Runtime | Install time + runtime |
> | Battery usage | High (constant JIT) | Low (mostly AOT) |
> | Storage | Less | More (AOT cache) |
> | GC | Stop-the-world, mark-sweep | Concurrent, generational |
> | 64-bit support | Limited | Native |
> | Status | Deprecated since Android 5.0 | Standard |
>
> **Significance for IoT:** ART's lower battery consumption and reduced GC pauses are critical for **always-on IoT apps** (e.g., continuous heart-rate monitoring, environmental sensing).

> **Part (b) [7 Marks]: Role of the Linux Kernel and HAL in supporting IoT hardware diversity.**
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Linux Kernel — Role in IoT:**
> - The Linux kernel is the **base of the Android stack**, derived from the upstream Linux kernel (currently 6.x LTS for Android 15).
> - It provides **device drivers** for hardware: Wi-Fi (`wlan.ko`), Bluetooth (`btusb.ko`, `hci_uart.ko`), NFC, GPS, camera, audio, and various sensors.
> - The **Binder IPC driver** enables secure inter-process communication between the IoT app (in user space) and system services (in system_server process).
> - The **Power Management subsystem** (with wakelocks) allows IoT apps to keep the CPU active in the background to maintain BLE connections or sample sensors.
> - The **Low Memory Killer (LMK)** daemon aggressively reclaims memory — vital for resource-constrained IoT edge devices.
> - The **Networking stack** (TCP/IP, UDP, mDNS, etc.) enables communication with cloud IoT platforms.
>
> **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) — Role in IoT:**
> - The HAL is a **set of standardized interface definitions** in C/C++. Each interface (e.g., `sensors.hal`, `bluetooth.hal`) defines a contract that vendors must implement.
> - Vendors write **vendor-specific implementations** of these HAL modules, which call their proprietary kernel drivers.
> - The Android framework and apps interact with HAL modules **without knowing the underlying hardware** — they only know the standard interface.
> - This allows a single Android build (and a single IoT app) to work on a wide range of IoT hardware: smartwatches, smart-home hubs, industrial gateways, automotive head-units, etc.
>
> **Joint Role in IoT Diversity:**
> The combination of Linux Kernel (driver layer) + HAL (abstraction layer) provides a **two-tier abstraction**:
> - **Kernel** abstracts raw hardware (e.g., a specific BLE chipset).
> - **HAL** abstracts the kernel-level details from the Android framework.
>
> This means a Qualcomm-based IoT gateway and a MediaTek-based one can run the **same IoT app** without modification, because the HAL hides the chipset differences.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing HAL with the Linux Kernel.** HAL is *not* part of the kernel — it sits **above** the kernel and provides vendor implementations of standard interfaces. The kernel provides drivers; the HAL provides abstraction.
> 2. **Mixing up Dalvik and ART.** Many students write "Dalvik uses AOT and ART uses JIT" — this is the **reverse** of reality. Dalvik used **JIT**; ART uses **AOT + JIT hybrid**.
> 3. **Forgetting the API Framework components.** When asked to "list components of the API Framework," students often mention only the Activity Manager. Always include: Window Manager, Content Providers, View System, Package Manager, Resource Manager, Notification Manager, and the Telephony/Location/Sensor Managers.
> 4. **Not drawing the block diagram in correct order.** The layers must be drawn from **top to bottom (or bottom to top) in the correct sequence**. Drawing them out of order is a guaranteed deduction.
> 5. **Ignoring IoT context.** This is a course on **Mobile App Development for IoT**. Any question about Android architecture must include at least one sentence tying the layer back to an IoT use case.
> 6. **Writing only the names of layers** without explaining their **role**. Listing the 5 layers without elaboration scores only 2-3 marks out of 7.
> 7. **Confusing APK components.** Students sometimes write "APK contains `.java` files" — no, APKs contain `.dex`, `.so` (native libs), resources, and manifest — **never** `.java` source.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Recall Checklist

- ✅ Android Architecture is a **5-layer stack**: Apps → API Framework → ART + Native Libs → HAL → Linux Kernel.
- ✅ **Linux Kernel** is the base; provides drivers, Binder IPC, power management, and the network stack.
- ✅ **HAL** provides vendor-neutral hardware interfaces; allows Android to run on diverse IoT hardware.
- ✅ **ART** replaced Dalvik in **Android 5.0**; uses **AOT + JIT hybrid** and **concurrent GC**.
- ✅ **Dalvik** used **JIT only** and is now deprecated.
- ✅ The **API Framework** contains the building blocks developers use: Activity Manager, Window Manager, Content Providers, View System, Package Manager, Resource Manager, Notification Manager, and the Sensor/Location/Telephony Managers.
- ✅ **Each Android app runs in its own Linux process** with a unique **UID** — this is the foundation of Android security.
- ✅ **APK = classes.dex + resources + native libs (.so) + manifest**, signed and zipped.
- ✅ Build pipeline: `javac/kotlinc` → `.class` → `d8` → `.dex` → `R8` (shrink/obfuscate) → `apksigner` → APK.
- ✅ In IoT, the architecture supports: BLE communication, sensor access, local SQLite persistence, MQTT/TLS security, and low-power background processing.
- ✅ Binder IPC is what allows the app to talk to system services — **a key enabler of IoT communication**.
- ✅ "Android Things" was a stripped-down AOSP variant for IoT — based on the same architecture, without the UI layer.
- ✅ API levels matter: target the **latest stable API** (API 35, Android 15) for modern IoT apps.
- ✅ The Java API Framework layer is where most IoT app development happens — choosing the right manager (Sensor, Bluetooth, Location) is critical.
- ✅ KTU expects you to draw a **neat block diagram** with the layers in the correct order and **explain each layer's role with an IoT example**.

### Key Acronyms to Memorize

| Acronym | Expansion | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **AOT** | Ahead-Of-Time | ART compilation at install |
| **JIT** | Just-In-Time | ART runtime compilation |
| **ART** | Android Runtime | Modern execution engine |
| **HAL** | Hardware Abstraction Layer | Vendor hardware interface |
| **APK** | Android Package Kit | App distribution format |
| **DEX** | Dalvik Executable | Bytecode format in APK |
| **IPC** | Inter-Process Communication | Binder-based app-to-service calls |
| **LMK** | Low Memory Killer | Reclaims memory in IoT devices |
| **JNI** | Java Native Interface | Java-to-C/C++ bridge |
| **UID** | User Identifier | Per-app Linux-level identity |

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Android Architecture**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech syllabus | PECGT415 — Mobile App Development for IoT*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Setting up Development Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Setting up Development Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Development Environment (for IoT Mobile Applications)** is an integrated collection of hardware, software tools, libraries, SDKs, emulators/simulators, and configuration services that collectively enable a developer to design, code, compile, debug, test, deploy, and maintain mobile applications capable of communicating with IoT (Internet of Things) devices, sensors, and cloud platforms.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECGT415)**, a properly configured development environment is the **foundational prerequisite** for all subsequent modules — including sensor interfacing, MQTT/HTTP communication, BLE pairing, and cloud telemetry — since none of these can be exercised without a working toolchain.

The environment typically consists of **five logical tiers**:

| Tier | Component Examples |
|------|--------------------|
| T1 — Host OS | Windows 10/11, macOS 12+, Ubuntu 22.04+ |
| T2 — IDE | Android Studio, VS Code, Xcode |
| T3 — SDKs | Android SDK, JDK 17, Flutter SDK, NDK |
| T4 — Build & Test Tools | Gradle, ADB, Genymotion/Emulator, Firebase Test Lab |
| T5 — IoT-Specific Add-ons | MQTT brokers, MQTTBox, Wireshark, Bluetooth HCI snoop, Postman |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** A properly configured Android Studio installation for IoT consumes approximately **8–12 GB** of disk space and requires a minimum of **8 GB RAM** (16 GB recommended) for smooth emulator operation.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Master Workshop for a Carpenter":**
>
> Imagine you are a carpenter who has been hired to build a smart wooden cabinet that can talk to a smart speaker (an IoT device). Before you can build the cabinet, you need to:
> 1. **Set up a workshop** (the IDE — your drawing desk + power tools).
> 2. **Stock the right materials** (SDKs — the wood, screws, polish).
> 3. **Install measuring tools** (emulators and debuggers — the measuring tape and level).
> 4. **Connect to the smart speaker's manual library** (cloud SDKs and IoT libraries).
> 5. **Test your prototype on a dummy cabinet** (emulator) before sending the real one.
>
> The development environment is that **workshop + toolset + manual library**. Without it, even the best carpenter cannot build a smart cabinet.

---

## 1.3 Why Setting Up the Environment is a Critical First Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1 lays the **engineering foundation**. Marks are often awarded in viva/practical exams specifically for the **correct configuration** of SDK paths, environment variables, ADB connectivity, and emulator sensors. A misconfigured `ANDROID_HOME` variable or missing `platform-tools` directory is the **single most common reason for failed lab evaluations**.

The setup process is non-trivial because IoT mobile apps straddle **three worlds simultaneously**:

1. The **mobile world** (Android/iOS UI, sensors, lifecycle).
2. The **network world** (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, WebSockets, BLE).
3. The **embedded world** (microcontrollers, sensors, actuators).

A well-prepared environment must therefore include tooling that can **bridge all three**.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Must-Know for KTU 2024)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **IDE** | Integrated Development Environment — a software suite combining editor, compiler, debugger. |
| **SDK** | Software Development Kit — a collection of libraries, APIs, and tools for a specific platform. |
| **JDK** | Java Development Kit — required to compile Java/Kotlin Android code. |
| **NDK** | Native Development Kit — allows C/C++ integration for performance-critical IoT code. |
| **ADB** | Android Debug Bridge — CLI tool to communicate with devices/emulators. |
| **AVD** | Android Virtual Device — a configurable emulator instance. |
| **Gradle** | Build automation tool used by Android for dependency management. |
| **MQTT** | Message Queuing Telemetry Transport — a lightweight IoT messaging protocol. |
| **BLE** | Bluetooth Low Energy — short-range wireless protocol for IoT peripherals. |
| **Repository** | Version-controlled storage of project source code (Git). |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Complete IoT Mobile App Development Environment

A production-grade environment is layered. Understanding each layer helps in debugging and in answering KTU's "explain-the-architecture" questions.

### Layer 1 — Host Operating System Layer
The OS hosts all other tools. KTU students most commonly use:
- **Windows 10/11** (most popular in Kerala engineering colleges).
- **Ubuntu 22.04 LTS** (preferred for IoT native code).
- **macOS Ventura/Sonoma** (required for iOS development).

### Layer 2 — JDK (Java Development Kit)
- Android development in 2024+ requires **JDK 17** (for AGP 8.x).
- The `JAVA_HOME` environment variable must point to the JDK install directory.
- Verification: `java -version` should return `17.x.x`.

### Layer 3 — IDE (Android Studio / VS Code)
**Android Studio** is the **official, recommended** IDE for Android IoT apps because it bundles:
- Code editor (Kotlin/Java/XML).
- Layout designer.
- Gradle integration.
- Emulator manager (AVD Manager).
- Device file explorer.
- Logcat.
- Profiler (CPU, memory, network, energy).

**VS Code** with extensions (e.g., "Android", "Flutter", "PlatformIO") is preferred for **cross-platform IoT** or when targeting **microcontrollers alongside mobile**.

### Layer 4 — Android SDK
Located in `$ANDROID_HOME`, containing:
- `platform-tools/` (adb, fastboot).
- `platforms/android-XX/` (API levels).
- `build-tools/` (aapt2, d8, zipalign).
- `cmdline-tools/` (sdkmanager).
- `system-images/` (for emulator).

> [!NOTE]
> **Recommended API Levels for IoT (2024):**
> - `compileSdk 34` (Android 14).
> - `minSdk 24` (Android 7.0) — covers **97%** of active devices.
> - `targetSdk 34`.

### Layer 5 — Emulator / Virtual Device
- AVDs simulate Android devices on a PC.
- For **IoT specifically**, emulators must be configured with **virtual sensors** (accelerometer, GPS, BLE).
- The **Extended Controls panel** in the emulator allows simulation of sensor values, incoming calls, battery states, etc.
- Recommended system image: **x86_64** with **Google Play Services** for Firebase/MQTT testing.

### Layer 6 — IoT-Specific Libraries & SDKs
These are added via `build.gradle` dependencies:

| Library | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `org.eclipse.paho.client.mqttv3` | MQTT client for Java/Kotlin |
| `paho.mqtt.android` | MQTT client optimized for Android |
| `com.google.android.gms:play-services-nearby` | BLE/Nearby device discovery |
| `io.reactivex.rxjava3` | Reactive streams for async sensor data |
| `com.squareup.retrofit2` | REST API calls to IoT cloud |
| `com.google.firebase` | FCM push, real-time DB, Auth |
| `com.amazonaws:aws-iot-device-sdk` | AWS IoT Core integration |

### Layer 7 — Hardware Bridge Tools
- **ADB** (Android Debug Bridge): `adb devices`, `adb install`, `adb logcat`.
- **Fastboot**: for flashing firmware.
- **Wireshark**: for packet inspection.
- **MQTTBox / MQTTX**: for testing MQTT publish/subscribe.
- **nRF Connect**: BLE scanner and debugger.

### Layer 8 — Version Control
- **Git** + **GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket** for source control.
- **`.gitignore`** must exclude `build/`, `.gradle/`, `local.properties`.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> This cheat sheet condenses the **environment-sizing logic** you may need for numerical questions.

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes |
|-----------|-----------------|------|-------|
| Min disk for Android Studio + SDK | $D_{min} = 8 + 4 + 2$ | **GB** | IDE (8) + SDK (4) + system images (2) |
| Min RAM for emulator | $R_{min} = 4 \cdot N_{AVD}$ | **GB** | $N_{AVD}$ = number of running AVDs |
| Recommended RAM | $R_{rec} = 16$ | **GB** | Single AVD with sensors |
| JDK minimum version | $V_{JDK} = 17$ | — | For AGP 8.x |
| Android compileSdk | $S_{compile} = 34$ | — | Android 14 |
| MQTT QoS levels | $\{0, 1, 2\}$ | — | At-most-once, At-least-once, Exactly-once |
| BLE MTU | $M_{MTU} \le 517$ | **bytes** | Default 23 bytes |
| Emulator boot time | $T_{boot} \approx 30\text{–}90$ | **seconds** | x86_64 with HAXM/Hypervisor |
| Gradle daemon heap | $H_{heap} \le 4$ | **GB** | Set in `gradle.properties` |

> **Important — LaTeX Safety:** All vertical bars in formulas are written using `\vert` or `\mid` (never raw `|`) to prevent markdown table breakage.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in the Real World

- **Start-up velocity:** A team with a pre-baked, containerized (Docker) Android development environment can onboard new IoT developers in **under 30 minutes**.
- **CI/CD pipelines:** GitHub Actions + Gradle + Firebase Test Lab automate **headless testing** of IoT mobile apps on 50+ device profiles.
- **Reproducibility:** A `Dockerfile` + `docker-compose.yml` for the entire toolchain eliminates the "works on my machine" syndrome — critical for KTU final-year IoT projects.
- **Industry adoption:** Companies like **Bosch, Siemens, Honeywell** ship IoT mobile apps whose CI/CD pipelines are built on these very toolchains.
- **Debugging reach:** A correctly configured ADB + Logcat + Wireshark triad lets you trace a sensor reading from the physical device → BLE packet → mobile app → MQTT broker → cloud dashboard.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Setup Procedure (Standard KTU Lab Workflow)

The following is the **canonical setup sequence** that aligns with the KTU 2024 Scheme lab manual and the **PECGT415** Module 1 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every step below is a **mandatory checkpoint** in the KTU practical record. Skipping any step typically results in **2–3 marks deduction** during the external lab evaluation.

### Step 1 — Verify Host System Requirements

$$
\text{Check}_{\text{disk}} = D_{free} \ge D_{min} = 12 \text{ GB}
$$

$$
\text{Check}_{\text{RAM}} = R_{total} \ge R_{min} = 8 \text{ GB}
$$

**Logic:** Both the IDE and SDK consume significant disk and memory. Insufficient resources cause Gradle build failures and AVD crashes.

---

### Step 2 — Install JDK 17

```bash
# Windows (chocolatey)
choco install temurin17

# Ubuntu
sudo apt install openjdk-17-jdk
```

**Verification:**

```bash
java -version
```

**Expected Output Logic:**

$$
\text{java.version} = "17.x.x" \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{JAVA\_HOME} \ne \text{NULL}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Set Environment Variables

For Windows PowerShell:

```powershell
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("JAVA_HOME", "C:\Program Files\Eclipse Adoptium\jdk-17", "User")
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("ANDROID_HOME", "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk", "User")
```

For Linux (`.bashrc`):

```bash
export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-17-openjdk-amd64
export ANDROID_HOME=$HOME/Android/Sdk
export PATH=$PATH:$ANDROID_HOME/platform-tools:$ANDROID_HOME/cmdline-tools/latest/bin
```

**Logic:** These variables act as **global pointers** that Gradle, ADB, and Android Studio query at runtime. Without them, builds fail with `JAVA_HOME not set` or `SDK location not found` errors.

---

### Step 4 — Install Android Studio

Download from the official site and run the installer. The first launch triggers the **Setup Wizard** which:
- Downloads Android SDK platform 34.
- Downloads build-tools 34.x.
- Downloads platform-tools.
- Downloads emulator + system image (x86_64).
- Creates an AVD.

**Total time:** 20–45 minutes depending on bandwidth.

---

### Step 5 — Configure SDK Manager

Open **Tools → SDK Manager** and ensure the following are installed:

$$
\text{SDK}_{\text{required}} = \{\text{platforms;android-34},\ \text{build-tools;34.0.0},\ \text{platform-tools},\ \text{system-images;android-34;google\_apis;x86\_64}\}
$$

---

### Step 6 — Create an AVD Configured for IoT

Open **Device Manager → Create Device**:
- Hardware: **Pixel 6** (or Pixel 7).
- System image: **API 34, x86_64, Google APIs**.
- AVD name: `IoT_Pixel6_API34`.

**IoT-specific enhancements** (in AVD's `config.ini`):

```ini
hw.sensors.accelerometer=yes
hw.sensors.gyroscope=yes
hw.sensors.gps=yes
hw.sensors.proximity=yes
hw.bluetooth=yes
hw.lcd.density=420
```

**Logic:** These flags enable **virtual sensors** that the emulator's Extended Controls panel can inject, allowing you to test location-based IoT, motion-based IoT, and BLE-based IoT without physical hardware.

---

### Step 7 — Install IoT-Specific Dependencies via Gradle

In `app/build.gradle.kts`:

$$
D_{IoT} = \{d_{MQTT},\ d_{Retrofit},\ d_{RxJava},\ d_{Firebase},\ d_{AWS\_IoT}\}
$$

**Model snippet:**

```kotlin
dependencies {
    implementation("org.eclipse.paho:org.eclipse.paho.client.mqttv3:1.2.5")
    implementation("com.squareup.retrofit2:retrofit:2.9.0")
    implementation("io.reactivex.rxjava3:rxjava:3.1.8")
    implementation("platform("com.google.firebase:firebase-bom:32.7.0")")
}
```

**Logic:** Each dependency is fetched from Maven Central / Google Maven, compiled, and linked. Gradle resolves transitive dependencies automatically.

---

### Step 8 — Verify ADB Connectivity

$$
\text{ADB}_{\text{status}} =
\begin{cases}
\text{device} & \text{if } \text{adb devices returns a serial} \\
\text{offline} & \text{if driver missing} \\
\text{unauthorized} & \text{if USB debugging not accepted}
\end{cases}
$$

**Test commands:**

```bash
adb devices
adb shell getprop ro.build.version.release
```

---

### Step 9 — Test MQTT Connectivity (IoT Smoke Test)

Install **MQTTBox** or use **MQTTX**, then publish to `test/topic` and subscribe from your Android app:

$$
\text{Topic} = \text{iot/ktu/\{deviceId\}/\{sensor\}}
$$

**Payload model:**

$$
P = \{ "v": V_{sensor},\ "ts": T_{epoch},\ "id": ID_{device} \}
$$

where $V_{sensor}$ is the sensor value, $T_{epoch}$ is the Unix timestamp, and $ID_{device}$ is the unique device identifier.

---

### Step 10 — Initialize Version Control

```bash
git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial IoT app scaffold"
git remote add origin https://github.com/username/iot-app.git
git push -u origin main
```

**Logic:** Version control is **mandatory** for KTU final-year IoT project submissions. The `.gitignore` must exclude:

$$
\text{Excluded} = \{ \text{build/},\ \text{.gradle/},\ \text{local.properties},\ \text{*.iml} \}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Setup Issues & Resolutions

| # | Symptom | Probable Cause | Fix |
|---|---------|----------------|-----|
| 1 | `SDK location not found` | `ANDROID_HOME` not set | Re-export variable, restart IDE |
| 2 | `JAVA_HOME is set to an empty value` | JDK not installed or path wrong | Install JDK 17, set `JAVA_HOME` |
| 3 | Emulator stuck at boot logo | HAXM/Hypervisor missing | Enable VT-x in BIOS; install Hypervisor |
| 4 | `adb: device not found` | USB driver missing | Install OEM USB driver |
| 5 | `Could not resolve org.eclipse.paho` | No internet or wrong Maven repo | Add `mavenCentral()` to `build.gradle` |
| 6 | Gradle build hangs | Insufficient heap | Set `org.gradle.jvmargs=-Xmx4g` in `gradle.properties` |
| 7 | BLE scan returns empty | Missing location permission | Add `ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION` to manifest |
| 8 | MQTT connection refused | Wrong broker port/SSL | Verify `tcp://` vs `ssl://`; check `1883`/`8883` |
| 9 | AVD very slow | x86_64 not used | Re-create AVD with x86_64 + Hardware GLES 2.0 |
| 10 | `INSTALL_FAILED_INSUFFICIENT_STORAGE` | Low device memory | Uninstall unused apps; increase AVD RAM |

---

## 3.3 Environmental Variable Resolution Logic

When Gradle resolves dependencies, it follows this chain:

$$
\text{Gradle} \to \text{JDK (via JAVA\_HOME)} \to \text{Android SDK (via ANDROID\_HOME)} \to \text{Build-tools} \to \text{Platforms} \to \text{Repositories (Maven Central + Google Maven)}
$$

If **any link** in this chain is broken, the build fails with a chain-specific error. This is why KTU lab exams often test whether you can trace an error back to its **root environmental cause**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Layered Development Environment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph hostLayer["TIER 1 HOST OS LAYER"]
        hostOS["Windows 10 11 or Ubuntu 22.04 or macOS Sonoma"]
    end

    subgraph jdkLayer["TIER 2 JAVA RUNTIME"]
        jdk17["JDK 17 plus JAVA_HOME variable"]
    end

    subgraph ideLayer["TIER 3 IDE LAYER"]
        androidStudio["Android Studio Hedgehog or Iguana"]
        vscode["VS Code with IoT extensions"]
    end

    subgraph sdkLayer["TIER 4 ANDROID SDK"]
        platformTools["platform tools with adb and fastboot"]
        buildTools["build tools 34 with aapt2 and d8"]
        platforms34["platforms android 34"]
        systemImages["system images x86_64 with Google APIs"]
    end

    subgraph emulatorLayer["TIER 5 EMULATOR AND VIRTUAL DEVICE"]
        avdIot["AVD IoT Pixel 6 API 34 with virtual sensors"]
        extendedCtrls["Extended Controls for sensor injection"]
    end

    subgraph iotLibs["TIER 6 IOT LIBRARIES"]
        mqttPaho["Eclipse Paho MQTT client"]
        retrofitLib["Retrofit for REST and HTTP"]
        rxJavaLib["RxJava 3 for async streams"]
        firebaseLib["Firebase BoM for Auth DB FCM"]
        bleLib["Android Bluetooth LE API"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List and briefly explain any three components of a mobile app development environment for IoT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A mobile app development environment for IoT consists of several integrated components:

1. **IDE (Integrated Development Environment)** — Android Studio provides the code editor, layout designer, Gradle integration, and debugging tools. It serves as the central workspace for writing, testing, and packaging IoT applications. *(1 mark)*

2. **Android SDK & JDK** — The Android Software Development Kit (containing `platform-tools`, `build-tools`, and platform libraries) along with **JDK 17** provide the libraries and compilers needed to translate Kotlin/Java source into Android-compatible bytecode. *(1 mark)*

3. **IoT-specific Libraries** — Libraries such as **Eclipse Paho (MQTT)**, **Retrofit (HTTP/REST)**, **RxJava (asynchronous streams)**, and the **Android Bluetooth LE API** enable the mobile app to communicate with sensors, microcontrollers, and cloud platforms. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is an Android Virtual Device (AVD)? Why is it important for IoT application testing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Android Virtual Device (AVD)** is a software-emulated Android device configuration that runs on a host PC through Android Studio's emulator. *(1 mark)*

It is important for IoT application testing because:

- It **simulates a real smartphone** with virtual hardware, allowing the developer to test the IoT app's UI and behavior without needing a physical device. *(1 mark)*
- Through the **Extended Controls** panel, developers can **inject virtual sensor data** (accelerometer, GPS, gyroscope, proximity, BLE events) — which is essential for testing IoT scenarios like motion detection, location-based alerts, and Bluetooth pairing. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 (Modeled)]`

**(a) Explain the layered architecture of a complete IoT mobile app development environment. List the major software tools required at each layer. (7 marks)**

**(b) Describe the step-by-step procedure to install and configure Android Studio, including JDK 17 and the Android SDK. How do you verify that the environment is correctly set up? (7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A (a) — 7 marks**

The IoT mobile app development environment is organized into **eight logical layers**, each serving a specific purpose:

**Layer 1 — Host Operating System:** Windows 10/11, Ubuntu 22.04 LTS, or macOS. Provides the platform on which all tools run. *(0.5 marks)*

**Layer 2 — JDK (Java Development Kit):** JDK 17 with the `JAVA_HOME` environment variable configured. Needed for compiling Java/Kotlin. *(0.5 marks)*

**Layer 3 — IDE:** Android Studio (Hedgehog/Iguana) or VS Code with IoT extensions. Provides editor, debugger, profiler, and layout designer. *(1 mark)*

**Layer 4 — Android SDK:** Includes `platform-tools` (adb, fastboot), `build-tools` (aapt2, d8), `platforms/android-34`, and `system-images`. *(1 mark)*

**Layer 5 — Emulator / AVD:** Pixel 6 (or similar) virtual device with API 34, x86_64, Google APIs. Configured with virtual sensors for IoT testing. *(1 mark)*

**Layer 6 — IoT-Specific Libraries:** Eclipse Paho (MQTT), Retrofit (HTTP), RxJava 3, Firebase, AWS IoT Device SDK, Android BLE API. *(1 mark)*

**Layer 7 — Hardware Bridge Tools:** ADB, Wireshark, MQTTX, nRF Connect, Fastboot. *(1 mark)*

**Layer 8 — Version Control:** Git + GitHub/GitLab for source code management. *(1 mark)*

These layers communicate hierarchically: a higher layer depends on the proper configuration of the layer below it.

---

### **Model Answer for Question A (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Install JDK 17** *(1 mark)*
Download Temurin 17 (or use `choco install temurin17` on Windows / `apt install openjdk-17-jdk` on Ubuntu). Set the `JAVA_HOME` environment variable to the JDK installation path.

**Step 2 — Download & Install Android Studio** *(1 mark)*
Run the official installer; the Setup Wizard automatically downloads the Android SDK, build-tools, platform-tools, and a default emulator.

**Step 3 — Configure `ANDROID_HOME`** *(1 mark)*
Set the `ANDROID_HOME` environment variable to the SDK path (e.g., `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Local\Android\Sdk`).

**Step 4 — Install SDK Components** *(1 mark)*
Through **Tools → SDK Manager**, install:
- Android 14 (API 34) platform
- Build-tools 34.0.0
- Platform-tools
- System image: API 34, x86_64, Google APIs

**Step 5 — Create an AVD** *(1 mark)*
Use **Device Manager → Create Device**, select Pixel 6, system image API 34, enable virtual sensors (accelerometer, GPS, BLE).

**Step 6 — Verification** *(2 marks)*

Run the following commands in terminal:

```bash
java -version          # should report 17.x.x
echo $JAVA_HOME        # should print JDK path
echo $ANDROID_HOME     # should print SDK path
adb devices            # should list emulator as "device"
adb shell getprop ro.build.version.release  # should print 14
```

Open Android Studio → **Tools → AVD Manager** → Start AVD → confirm boot. Then create a new project and run the default "Hello World" app to confirm the entire toolchain works end-to-end.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 (Modeled)]`

**(a) What is the role of ADB and Gradle in an IoT mobile app development environment? Explain with examples. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the IoT-specific libraries and tools (MQTT, BLE, Retrofit, Wireshark) that must be integrated into the development environment. Provide a sample Gradle dependency block. (7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B (a) — 7 marks**

**Role of ADB (Android Debug Bridge):** *(3.5 marks)*

ADB is a **command-line tool** that acts as a **bridge between the developer's host PC and an Android device or emulator**. It consists of a **client** (running on the PC), a **server**, and a **daemon** (`adbd`) running on the device.

Examples of ADB usage in IoT development:
- `adb devices` — Lists all connected devices/emulators with their state (device, offline, unauthorized). *(0.7 marks)*
- `adb install app.apk` — Pushes and installs the compiled IoT app onto a device. *(0.7 marks)*
- `adb logcat` — Streams the device's system logs in real-time, used to debug MQTT connection failures, BLE pairing errors, and sensor reading issues. *(0.7 marks)*
- `adb forward tcp:8888 tcp:1883` — Forwards a local port to a remote one on the device, useful for testing local MQTT brokers. *(0.7 marks)*
- `adb shell input` — Simulates sensor events (e.g., shaking) on the emulator for testing motion-triggered IoT logic. *(0.7 marks)*

**Role of Gradle:** *(3.5 marks)*

Gradle is the **build automation tool** for Android. It manages compilation, dependency resolution, signing, packaging, and testing.

Examples:
- **`build.gradle` dependency block** — Fetches IoT libraries (Paho, Retrofit) from Maven Central. *(1 mark)*
- **Build variants (`buildTypes`)** — Allows separate Debug and Release builds with different signing configs. *(1 mark)*
- **Product flavors** — Lets you build a `lite` flavor (sensors only) and a `pro` flavor (with cloud) from the same codebase. *(0.75 marks)*
- **`gradle.properties` JVM args** — Configures memory heap (`org.gradle.jvmargs=-Xmx4g`) for large IoT projects. *(0.75 marks)*

---

### **Model Answer for Question B (b) — 7 marks**

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport):** *(1.5 marks)*
A lightweight publish/subscribe protocol ideal for IoT. The **Eclipse Paho** library provides a Java/Android client. Example usage: an Android app publishes temperature data to the topic `home/livingroom/temp` every 10 seconds; the cloud dashboard subscribes to the same topic.

$$
\text{Topic structure: } T = \text{deviceType}/\text{deviceId}/\text{sensorType}
$$

**BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy):** *(1.5 marks)*
The **Android Bluetooth LE API** (`android.bluetooth.le`) enables scanning, connecting, and reading characteristics from BLE peripherals like fitness bands, heart-rate sensors, and BLE beacons. `BluetoothLeScanner.startScan()` discovers nearby devices; `BluetoothGatt` manages the GATT profile.

**Retrofit:** *(1.5 marks)*
A type-safe HTTP client for Android, used to send REST API requests to IoT cloud platforms (e.g., AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, custom Node.js backend). It converts Java/Kotlin objects to JSON via converters like Gson/Moshi.

**Wireshark:** *(1 mark)*
A packet analyzer that captures and inspects network traffic. In IoT development, Wireshark helps verify that MQTT packets are being correctly published, that TLS handshakes succeed, and that no malformed data is being sent.

**Sample Gradle Dependency Block:** *(1.5 marks)*

```kotlin
// app/build.gradle.kts
dependencies {
    // MQTT for IoT messaging
    implementation("org.eclipse.paho:org.eclipse.paho.android.service:1.1.1")
    implementation("org.eclipse.paho:org.eclipse.paho.client.mqttv3:1.2.5")

    // REST calls to cloud
    implementation("com.squareup.retrofit2:retrofit:2.9.0")
    implementation("com.squareup.retrofit2:converter-gson:2.9.0")

    // Async / reactive streams
    implementation("io.reactivex.rxjava3:rxjava:3.1.8")
    implementation("io.reactivex.rxjava3:rxandroid:3.0.2")

    // Firebase (Auth, FCM, RTDB)
    implementation(platform("com.google.firebase:firebase-bom:32.7.0"))
    implementation("com.google.firebase:firebase-messaging")
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mentioning Android Studio without specifying JDK version** — always pair them (e.g., "JDK 17 + Android Studio Iguana"). Vague answers lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing SDK and JDK** — JDK compiles Java; SDK is the Android-specific kit. Mixing them up is a **fatal error** in long answers.
> 3. **Skipping environment variables** — answers that don't mention `JAVA_HOME` and `ANDROID_HOME` are considered incomplete. Always state the **role** of each variable.
> 4. **Omitting the IoT context** — generic Android setup answers (without MQTT, BLE, sensors) score lower than IoT-aware answers. Always tie tools back to the **IoT use case**.
> 5. **Forgetting the emulator's virtual sensor capability** — examiners specifically look for the word "virtual sensors" or "Extended Controls" in AVD-based questions.
> 6. **Missing CI/CD angle** — high-scoring answers may briefly mention Docker, GitHub Actions, or Firebase Test Lab for full marks on "production-grade" environment questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — print this and revise before exams.**

- ☐ **Development Environment = IDE + SDK + JDK + Emulator + IoT libraries + Hardware bridge tools + VCS**.
- ☐ **JDK 17** is the minimum for AGP 8.x; **Android Studio Iguana / Hedgehog** is the recommended IDE.
- ☐ Set `JAVA_HOME` → points to JDK; set `ANDROID_HOME` → points to SDK; add both to `PATH`.
- ☐ AVD = **Android Virtual Device**; configure with `x86_64`, **Google APIs**, and **virtual sensors** for IoT testing.
- ☐ **ADB** (`platform-tools`) is the bridge between PC and device/emulator: `adb devices`, `adb install`, `adb logcat`, `adb forward`.
- ☐ **Gradle** handles build automation, dependencies, build variants, and product flavors.
- ☐ IoT libraries: **Eclipse Paho** (MQTT), **Retrofit** (REST), **RxJava 3** (async), **Firebase** (push/auth/DB), **Android BLE API**.
- ☐ **MQTT** is the de-facto IoT messaging protocol; topics follow `deviceType/deviceId/sensorType`.
- ☐ **Wireshark** for packet inspection; **MQTTX / MQTTBox** for manual pub-sub testing; **nRF Connect** for BLE debugging.
- ☐ **Git + GitHub** is mandatory for source control; exclude `build/`, `.gradle/`, `local.properties` via `.gitignore`.
- ☐ Use `compileSdk 34`, `minSdk 24`, `targetSdk 34` for 2024 IoT apps.
- ☐ Always enable **virtual sensors** (accelerometer, GPS, BLE) in AVD for IoT testing.
- ☐ **Docker**-based toolchains are gaining marks in viva for "production-grade" environment questions.
- ☐ Common error → `SDK location not found` ⇒ fix by setting `ANDROID_HOME` and restarting IDE.
- ☐ Emulator performance tip → use **x86_64** with **Hardware GLES 2.0** and **Hypervisor** enabled in BIOS.
- ☐ **Continuous Integration** with GitHub Actions + Gradle + Firebase Test Lab is the industry standard for IoT mobile apps.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Building Blocks - Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basic Building Blocks - Activities # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):** > An **Activity** in Android is a single, focused, user-facing screen that the user can interact with to perform a specific task. It is one of the fundamental building blocks of any Android application and serves as the **entry point** for user interaction. Activities are subclasses of the `android.app.Activity` class (or its modern counterpart `androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity`) and provide the **window** in which the application's user interface (UI) is drawn. In the context of **Mobile App Development for IoT (PECGT415)**, Activities are particularly important because they often serve as the **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** layer that visualizes sensor data, sends control commands to IoT devices, and manages communication protocols (MQTT, HTTP, CoAP). > [!NOTE] > **Key Properties of an Activity:** > - Each Activity has its own **lifecycle** managed by the Android Framework. > - Activities are declared in the **`AndroidManifest.xml`** file. > - Activities communicate with each other via **Intents**. > - Multiple Activities are stacked in a **Back Stack** (LIFO structure). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of an **Activity** as a **page in a book** or a **slide in a PowerPoint presentation**: - Each **page/slide** is independent and shows specific content. - You can **navigate forward** (open a new page) or **navigate backward** (go to the previous page). - The **book's binding** is analogous to the **Application**, while individual **pages** are the **Activities**. - The Android system keeps track of which page you are currently reading (the **active Activity**). > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy: ATM Machine** > An ATM machine can be modeled using Activities: > - **Screen 1**: Welcome Screen (Insert Card Activity) > - **Screen 2**: PIN Entry Activity > - **Screen 3**: Main Menu Activity > - **Screen 4**: Withdrawal/Deposit Activity > - **Screen 5**: Receipt Printing Activity > > Each screen is an independent Activity. When you complete one, the next one opens, and the previous one goes into the **back stack** so you can press "Back" to return. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants | **Parameter** | **Standard Value / Description** | |---|---| | Base Class | `android.app.Activity` | | Modern Base Class | `androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity` | | Manifest Declaration | `` tag in `AndroidManifest.xml` | | Default Launch Mode | `standard` | | Back Stack Data Structure | **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** Stack | | Lifecycle Callback Count | **7** (onCreate, onStart, onResume, onPause, onStop, onDestroy, onRestart) | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Activity Lifecycle — The Core Concept The **Activity Lifecycle** is the sequence of states an Activity goes through from its creation to its destruction. The Android framework invokes specific **callback methods** at each transition. Understanding the lifecycle is **critical** for IoT apps that need to manage sensor connections, network sockets, and battery efficiently. ### 2.1.1 The Seven Lifecycle Callbacks (Ordered) | **#** | **Callback Method** | **State** | **Description** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `onCreate()` | Created | Called when Activity is **first created**. Initialize UI, bind data, set `setContentView()`. | | 2 | `onStart()` | Started | Activity becomes **visible** to the user but is not yet interactive. | | 3 | `onResume()` | Resumed | Activity is now in the **foreground** and the user can interact with it. | | 4 | `onPause()` | Paused | Another Activity is coming into the foreground. **Current Activity loses focus** but remains partially visible. | | 5 | `onStop()` | Stopped | Activity is **no longer visible** to the user. Heavy resources should be released here. | | 6 | `onDestroy()` | Destroyed | Activity is **about to be destroyed**. Final cleanup, unbind resources. | | 7 | `onRestart()` | Restarted | Called when a **stopped Activity is about to start again** (after returning from another Activity). | > [!NOTE] > **The Three Nested Loops (KTU High-Yield Concept):** > 1. **Entire Lifetime**: `onCreate()` → `onDestroy()` > 2. **Visible Lifetime**: `onStart()` → `onStop()` > 3. **Foreground Lifetime**: `onResume()` → `onPause()` --- ## 2.2 Activity States (Detailed Analysis) ### State 1: **Created / Initialized** - The Activity object is **instantiated** by the system. - `onCreate()` is called. This is where you must call `setContentView(R.layout.layout_name)`. - **Resources allocated**: View hierarchy, layouts, listeners. - **IoT Context**: Initialize MQTT client, open Bluetooth socket, register sensor listeners. ### State 2: **Started** - Activity is **visible** but not in the foreground. - `onStart()` is called. - Useful for UI preparation (animations, loading data). ### State 3: **Resumed / Running** - Activity is **active** and has user focus. - `onResume()` is called. - **CRITICAL**: Heavy I/O, network calls, and sensor data acquisition should be **active** here. - This is the **only state** where the Activity is guaranteed to be in the foreground. ### State 4: **Paused** - Activity **loses focus** but is **partially visible** (e.g., a dialog or transparent activity is on top). - `onPause()` is called. - **Best practice**: Pause animations, save drafts, release camera/sensor resources to avoid battery drain. ### State 5: **Stopped** - Activity is **completely hidden** by another Activity. - `onStop()` is called. - **Heavy cleanup**: Unregister listeners, close database connections, disconnect MQTT broker. ### State 6: **Destroyed** - `onDestroy()` is called before the Activity is removed from memory. - Final resource deallocation. - Called either because `finish()` was invoked or because the system needs memory. --- ## 2.3 Intents and Activity Communication > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** An **Intent** is a messaging object used to **request an action** from another app component. Intents are the **glue** that connects Activities, Services, and Broadcast Receivers. ### Two Types of Intents: | **Type** | **Description** | **Example** | |---|---|---| | **Explicit Intent** | Specifies the **exact target component** (Activity class name) | `startActivity(new Intent(this, SecondActivity.class))` | | **Implicit Intent** | Declares a **general action**; the system finds the best component | Opening a URL, sending an email, dialing a phone number | ### Intent Components: 1. **Action**: What to do (e.g., `ACTION_VIEW`, `ACTION_SEND`) 2. **Data**: The data to operate on (URI) 3. **Category**: Additional information about the action 4. **Extras**: Key-value pairs for passing data (Bundle) 5. **Flags**: Options to control task behavior ### Passing Data Between Activities (KTU High-Yield Pattern): ```java // In FirstActivity.java — Sending data Intent intent = new Intent(FirstActivity.this, SecondActivity.class); intent.putExtra("SENSOR_VALUE", 25.6); intent.putExtra("DEVICE_ID", "ESP32_01"); startActivity(intent); // In SecondActivity.java — Receiving data Intent intent = getIntent(); double sensorValue = intent.getDoubleExtra("SENSOR_VALUE", 0.0); String deviceId = intent.getStringExtra("DEVICE_ID"); ``` --- ## 2.4 The Back Stack and Task Management ### Back Stack Definition: The **Back Stack** is a **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** stack managed by the Android system that stores Activities in the order they were opened. Pressing the **Back button** pops the topmost Activity from the stack. ``` Back Stack Visualization: ┌─────────────────────┐ ← TOP (Current Activity) │ DisplayActivity │ ├─────────────────────┤ │ ControlActivity │ ├─────────────────────┤ │ DashboardActivity │ ← BOTTOM (Root/Launcher Activity) └─────────────────────┘ ``` --- ## 2.5 Launch Modes (KTU High-Yield) | **Launch Mode** | **Behavior** | **Use Case** | |---|---|---| | `standard` | **Default**. New instance created every time. | Most common activities. | | `singleTop` | New instance only if Activity is **not on top** of the stack. | Search activity, notification handler. | | `singleTask` | **Only one instance** in the task. Clears activities above it. | Home/Launcher activity. | | `singleInstance` | **Sole member** of its task. Always in a separate task. | Dialer, Alarm activity. | ### Declaring Launch Mode in Manifest: ```xml ``` --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Description** | |---|---|---| | Lifecycle States | $N_{states} = 7$ | Number of lifecycle callbacks | | Nested Loops | $L_{total} = L_{entire} \cup L_{visible} \cup L_{foreground}$ | All three nested lifetimes | | Back Stack Size | $S_{max} = \text{unlimited}$ (practical limit: device memory) | Activities in stack | | Intent Extras Limit | $E_{max} \approx 1\,\text{MB}$ | Binder transaction limit | | Configuration Change | `configChanges` in manifest | Prevents Activity recreation on rotation | | State Saving | `onSaveInstanceState(Bundle)` | Bundle persists across recreation | | State Restoration | `onRestoreInstanceState(Bundle)` | Restores saved state | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** > - **IoT Dashboards**: Activities display real-time sensor data; lifecycle hooks manage MQTT subscriptions efficiently. > - **Smart Home Apps**: `onPause`/`onStop` release camera streams to save bandwidth and battery. > - **Industrial Monitoring**: `singleTask` launch mode ensures only one instance of the critical control panel exists. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Lifecycle Flow & Analytical Walkthrough ## 3.1 Complete Activity Lifecycle Flow (Step-by-Step Derivation) Below is the **exhaustive analytical walkthrough** of Activity state transitions. We trace every possible path through the lifecycle state machine. ### 3.1.1 First Launch Scenario (App Opened for the First Time) **Step 1: System Instantiation** The Android system creates a new instance of the Activity class. Memory is allocated for the Activity object on the heap. **Step 2: `onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState)` Invocation** - The `savedInstanceState` parameter is `null` on first launch. - Initialize variables, inflate the layout using `setContentView(R.layout.activity_main)`. - Register listeners, initialize IoT connections (MQTT, HTTP clients). **Step 3: Transition to `onStart()`** - The Activity is now **visible** on the screen. - The system makes the window visible to the user. **Step 4: Transition to `onResume()`** - The Activity gains **user focus**. - The window is in the foreground. - All animations, sensors, and real-time data streams should be **active**. $$State_{current} = \text{RESUMED}$$ --- ### 3.1.2 User Navigates to Another Activity (Partial Foreground Loss) **Step 5: `onPause()` is called** - Another Activity is entering the foreground (or a dialog is appearing). - Commit unsaved changes (e.g., save IoT command logs). - **Stop animations** and CPU-intensive tasks. - The Activity is still **partially visible**. **Step 6: `onStop()` is called** - The Activity is **no longer visible**. - Release heavy resources: unregister sensor listeners, close database cursors, disconnect MQTT subscriptions. - Save persistent data to `SharedPreferences` or a database. $$State_{current} = \text{STOPPED}, \quad State_{previous} = \text{PAUSED}$$ --- ### 3.1.3 User Returns to the Activity (Back Navigation) **Step 7: `onRestart()` is called** - The Activity is being restarted from the **stopped** state. - Re-initialize resources that were released in `onStop()`. **Step 8: `onStart()` is called again** - The Activity becomes **visible** once more. **Step 9: `onResume()` is called again** - The Activity regains **user focus** and is interactive. $$State_{current} = \text{RESUMED} \quad (\text{cycle repeats})$$ --- ### 3.1.4 User Exits the App (Full Destruction) **Step 10: `onPause()` → `onStop()` → `onDestroy()`** -
