`onDestroy()` is called when the Activity finishes or the system reclaims memory. - Final cleanup: cancel all background threads, unbind services. $$State_{final} = \text{DESTROYED}, \quad \text{Object} \rightarrow \text{Garbage Collected}$$ --- ## 3.2 Mathematical Model of Activity Transitions We can model the Activity lifecycle as a **Directed State Machine** with transition functions. Let: - $S = \{$ Created, Started, Resumed, Paused, Stopped, Destroyed, Restarted $\}$ be the set of states. - $T: S \times E \rightarrow S$ be the transition function, where $E$ is the event set. - $E = \{$ `app_launched`, `app_foregrounded`, `app_backgrounded`, `app_reopened`, `app_finished`, `system_kill` $\}$. The transition function can be expressed as: $$T(S_{current}, E) = S_{next}$$ ### Transition Table: | **Current State** | **Event** | **Next State** | **Callback** | |---|---|---|---| | None | `app_launched` | Created | `onCreate` | | Created | `app_foregrounded` | Started | `onStart` | | Started | `app_foregrounded` | Resumed | `onResume` | | Resumed | `app_backgrounded` | Paused | `onPause` | | Paused | `app_backgrounded` | Stopped | `onStop` | | Stopped | `app_reopened` | Restarted | `onRestart` | | Restarted | `app_foregrounded` | Started | `onStart` | | Stopped/Paused | `app_finished` | Destroyed | `onDestroy` | | Destroyed | — | — | (GC eligible) | ### Composite Transition Formula: $$T^n(S_0, E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n) = S_n$$ Where $S_0 = \text{null}$ (initial) and $n$ is the number of transitions. --- ## 3.3 Saving and Restoring State (Analytical Derivation) ### Problem Statement: When an Activity is destroyed due to **configuration changes** (e.g., screen rotation, language change) or **low memory**, the system may need to **recreate** it. The user expects their data (form inputs, IoT sensor readings) to be preserved. ### Solution: The `Bundle` Mechanism **Step 1: `onSaveInstanceState(Bundle outState)`** - Called by the system **before** the Activity is destroyed. - Save key-value pairs to the `Bundle`: - Primitive types: `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `boolean` - String, CharSequence - `Parcelable`, `Serializable` objects ```java @Override protected void onSaveInstanceState(Bundle outState) { super.onSaveInstanceState(outState); outState.putString("currentDeviceState", "ON"); outState.putDouble("lastSensorReading", 23.5); outState.putInt("retryCount", 3); } ``` **Step 2: `onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState)` or `onRestoreInstanceState(Bundle)`** - On recreation, the `Bundle` is passed back to the Activity. - Retrieve the saved values: ```java @Override protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState); setContentView(R.layout.activity_main); if (savedInstanceState != null) { String deviceState = savedInstanceState.getString("currentDeviceState"); double lastReading = savedInstanceState.getDouble("lastSensorReading"); int retryCount = savedInstanceState.getInt("retryCount"); } } ``` ### Bundle Size Constraint: $$Size_{Bundle} \leq 1\,\text{MB} \quad (\text{Binder transaction limit})$$ Exceeding this limit will trigger a `TransactionTooLargeException`. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Do **NOT** use `onSaveInstanceState` for **persistent storage** (e.g., user login data, IoT device configurations). It is only for **transient UI state** across configuration changes. Use `SharedPreferences`, `Room Database`, or external storage for permanent data. --- ## 3.4 Task Affinity and Intent Flags (Advanced) ### Task Affinity: Defines which **task** an Activity belongs to. By default, all activities in an app share the same affinity (the package name). ### Important Intent Flags: | **Flag** | **Effect** | |---|---| | `FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK` | Starts Activity in a new task | | `FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TOP` | Clears all activities above the target | | `FLAG_ACTIVITY_SINGLE_TOP` | Equivalent to `singleTop` launch mode | | `FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TASK` | Clears the existing task and starts fresh | | `FLAG_ACTIVITY_NO_HISTORY` | Activity is removed from stack when navigated away | ### Combined Effect Formula: $$Behavior_{Intent} = f(\text{LaunchMode}, \text{IntentFlags}, \text{TaskAffinity})$$ The final activity behavior is determined by this **triple** of factors, not just the launch mode alone. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Activity Lifecycle State Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> CREATED : onCreate CREATED --> STARTED : onStart STARTED --> RESUMED : onResume RESUMED --> PAUSED : onPause PAUSED --> STARTED : onResume PAUSED --> STOPPED : onStop STOPPED --> RESTARTED : onRestart RESTARTED --> STARTED : onStart STOPPED --> DESTROYED : onDestroy PAUSED --> DESTROYED : onDestroy DESTROYED --> [*] note right of RESUMED User can interact Sensors active MQTT connected end note note right of PAUSED Partial visibility Save drafts Stop animations end note note right of STOPPED No visibility Release resources Close connections end note ``` --- ## 4.2 Back Stack Architecture (LIFO Visualization) ```mermaid graph TD A["DESTROYED
Activity Object"] --> B["STOPPED
Background Process"] B --> C["PAUSED
Partial Visibility"] C --> D["RESUMED
Active and Visible
TOP OF STACK"] D --> E["STARTED
Visible Not Active"] E --> F["CREATED
onCreate Called"] F --> G["BOTTOM OF STACK
Root Activity / Launcher"] H["User Action
Press Back Button"] -.-> D I["User Action
Navigate Forward"] -.-> D style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Intent Communication Flow (Activity-to-Activity Data Transfer) ```mermaid graph LR A["FirstActivity
Dashboard"] -->|Intent putExtra| B["Android Intent
Message Object"] B -->|startActivity| C["SecondActivity
Sensor View"] C -->|getIntent| D["Data Extraction
getStringExtra"] D -->|Display| E["Updated UI
Sensor Values"] F["IoT Sensor
ESP32 Device"] -.->|MQTT Publish| A A -.->|MQTT Subscribe| F style A fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Manifest Declaration Structure ```mermaid graph TD A["AndroidManifest.xml"] --> B["application tag"] B --> C["activity 1: MainActivity
LAUNCHER"] B --> D["activity 2: SensorActivity"] B --> E["activity 3: ControlActivity"] B --> F["activity 4: SettingsActivity"] C --> G["intent-filter MAIN/LAUNCHER"] D --> H["exported false"] E --> I["launchMode singleTop"] F --> J["screenOrientation portrait"] style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.5 Block-Level Architecture: Activity in IoT Context ```mermaid graph TD A["USER INTERFACE LAYER
Activity + XML Layout"] --> B["ACTIVITY LIFECYCLE
MANAGER"] B --> C["INTENT DISPATCHER"] C --> D["DATA ACQUISITION
MODULE"] D --> E["MQTT CLIENT
HiveMQ Broker"] E --> F["IoT DEVICE LAYER
ESP32 Sensors"] F -->|Telemetry Data| E E -->|Subscribe Topics| D B --> G["RESOURCE MANAGER"] G -->|Release on onStop| H["SENSOR MANAGER
Camera GPS Bluetooth"] G -->|Release on onDestroy| I["NETWORK SOCKET
HTTP WebSocket"] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define Activity. List any four lifecycle methods of an Android Activity.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Model Question Paper]` **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** An Activity is a single, focused user-facing screen in an Android application. It serves as the entry point for user interaction and provides the window for the UI. Each Activity has a well-defined lifecycle managed by the Android framework. **Four Lifecycle Methods:** | **#** | **Method** | **Purpose** | |---|---|---| | 1 | `onCreate()` | Called when Activity is first created; initializes UI and data. | | 2 | `onStart()` | Called when Activity becomes visible to the user. | | 3 | `onResume()` | Called when Activity gains user focus and becomes interactive. | | 4 | `onPause()` | Called when the Activity loses focus (e.g., another Activity comes to foreground). | **Additional valid methods** (for extra credit): `onStop()`, `onDestroy()`, `onRestart()`. --- ### **Question 2: What is an Intent in Android? Differentiate between Explicit and Implicit Intents with examples.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1]` **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** An Intent is a messaging object used to request an action from another app component (Activity, Service, or Broadcast Receiver). It facilitates inter-component communication in Android. **Differentiation Table:** | **Parameter** | **Explicit Intent** | **Implicit Intent** | |---|---|---| | **Target** | Specifies exact component (class name) | Declares a general action; system resolves | | **Use Case** | In-app navigation | Cross-app operations | | **Security** | More secure (internal use) | Requires intent filter matching | | **Example Code** | `new Intent(this, SecondActivity.class)` | `new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, Uri.parse("https://ktu.edu.in"))` | | **Resolution** | Direct, no ambiguity | System queries all apps for matching intent filter | **IoT Context Example:** - **Explicit**: Navigate from `DashboardActivity` to `SensorConfigActivity` within the IoT app. - **Implicit**: Open a webpage showing the IoT device's IP address in a browser. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **ESE Module Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer **ONE** full question. Each question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the complete Android Activity Lifecycle with a neat diagram. Describe the significance of `onCreate()`, `onResume()`, `onPause()`, and `onDestroy()`.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | Module 1 | CO1, K2 Level]` **Model Answer:** **Introduction:** The Activity Lifecycle defines the various states an Activity transitions through from its creation to its destruction. The Android system invokes specific callback methods at each stage, allowing developers to manage resources and UI state efficiently. **Lifecycle Diagram (Draw in Exam):** ``` onCreate() ↓ onStart() ↓ onResume() ←→ onPause() ↓ ↓ onStop() onDestroy() ↓ onRestart() → onStart() ``` **Explanation of Key Methods:** **1. `onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState)` — 2 marks** - Called when the Activity is **first created**. - Initialize UI components: `setContentView(R.layout.activity_main)`. - Bind views, set listeners, initialize IoT connections. - **`savedInstanceState`**: Contains the previously saved state (if any). **2. `onResume()` — 2 marks** - Called when the Activity becomes **interactive** and gains user focus. - Start animations, resume sensor data acquisition, reconnect to MQTT broker. - This is the **only state** where the Activity is guaranteed to be in the foreground. **3. `onPause()` — 2 marks** - Called when another Activity is entering the foreground. - **Commit unsaved changes**. - Stop animations and CPU-intensive operations. - Release camera, sensor, or other hardware resources to save battery. **4. `onDestroy()` — 1 mark** - Final callback before the Activity is removed from memory. - Clean up all background threads, unbind services, cancel network requests. - Called either when `finish()` is invoked or when the system reclaims memory. **Conclusion:** Proper lifecycle management is critical for **memory efficiency**, **battery optimization**, and **user experience** in IoT applications where continuous sensor data streams and network connections must be handled carefully. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: What is the Back Stack in Android? Explain the four launch modes of Activity with appropriate use cases.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | Module 1 | CO1, K3 Level]` **Model Answer:** **Back Stack Definition (2 marks):** The **Back Stack** is a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** stack data structure maintained by the Android system to store Activities in the order they were opened. When a new Activity is launched, it is pushed onto the top of the stack. Pressing the **Back button** pops the topmost Activity, destroying it and resuming the one below. **Visualization:** ``` ┌──────────────────┐ ← TOP │ SettingsActivity│ ├──────────────────┤ │ ProfileActivity │ ├──────────────────┤ │ HomeActivity │ ← BOTTOM (Root) └──────────────────┘ ``` **Four Launch Modes (5 marks):** | **Launch Mode** | **Behavior** | **Use Case** | |---|---|---| | **`standard`** | Default mode. A new instance is created **every time** the Activity is launched, even if an instance already exists in the stack. | Most common activities (e.g., a list item that opens a detail view). | | **`singleTop`** | A new instance is created **only if the Activity is NOT already on top** of the stack. If it is on top, the existing instance's `onNewIntent()` is called. | Search activity, notification handler (avoid duplicate instances). | | **`singleTask`** | **Only one instance** of the Activity exists in the task. If launched, it clears all activities **above it** in the stack. | Home/Launcher activity, main dashboard. | | **`singleInstance`** | The Activity is the **sole member** of its task. All other activities in the app are pushed onto a separate task. | Dialer app, alarm clock (system-level unique activities). | **Manifest Declaration Example:** ```xml ``` **Conclusion:** Choosing the correct launch mode is essential for **navigation flow**, **memory management**, and **user experience** in Android applications. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the role of Intents in Android. Describe how data is passed between two Activities using Explicit Intents. Write a sample code snippet.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 1 | CO1, K3 Level]` **Model Answer:** **Role of Intents (2 marks):** Intents are the **primary mechanism** for inter-component communication in Android. They allow Activities, Services, and Broadcast Receivers to exchange data and trigger actions. Intents can be **Explicit** (target a specific component) or **Implicit** (declare an action for the system to resolve). **Passing Data Using Explicit Intents (5 marks):** **Step 1: Create the First Activity (Sender)** ```java public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity { @Override protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState); setContentView(R.layout.activity_main); Button btnSend = findViewById(R.id.btnSend); btnSend.setOnClickListener(v -> { Intent intent = new Intent(MainActivity.this, SecondActivity.class); intent.putExtra("DEVICE_ID", "ESP32_001"); intent.putExtra("TEMP_VALUE", 28.5); intent.putExtra("IS_CONNECTED", true); startActivity(intent); }); } } ``` **Step 2: Create the Second Activity (Receiver)** ```java public class SecondActivity extends AppCompatActivity { @Override protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState); setContentView(R.layout.activity_second); // Retrieve the Intent Intent intent = getIntent(); String deviceId = intent.getStringExtra("DEVICE_ID"); double tempValue = intent.getDoubleExtra("TEMP_VALUE", 0.0); boolean isConnected = intent.getBooleanExtra("IS_CONNECTED", false); // Display the data TextView tvResult = findViewById(R.id.tvResult); tvResult.setText("Device: " + deviceId + "\nTemp: " + tempValue + "°C"); } } ``` **Key Methods Used:** - `intent.putExtra(key, value)`: Adds data to the Intent. - `intent.getStringExtra(key)`, `getDoubleExtra(key, defaultValue)`, `getBooleanExtra(key, defaultValue)`: Retrieves data. - Supported data types: `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `boolean`, `String`, `Parcelable`, `Serializable`, arrays of these types. **Conclusion:** Explicit Intents provide a **type-safe** and **efficient** way to navigate between Activities within the same application, making them ideal for structured IoT dashboards and control panels. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: What is the significance of `onSaveInstanceState()` and `onRestoreInstanceState()` in Android? Explain with a suitable IoT use case.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1 | CO1, K3 Level]` **Model Answer:** **Significance (3 marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **`onSaveInstanceState()`** is called by the Android system **before** an Activity is destroyed due to configuration changes (e.g., screen rotation, language change) or low-memory scenarios. It allows developers to save **transient UI state** in a `Bundle` object. **`onRestoreInstanceState()`** (or the `Bundle` parameter in `onCreate()`) is used to **recover** this state when the Activity is recreated. These methods ensure **data persistence across configuration changes**, improving user experience and preventing data loss. **IoT Use Case: Smart Temperature Monitor App (4 marks)** **Scenario:** A user is monitoring real-time temperature data from an IoT sensor on their phone. While viewing the live graph, the user **rotates the device** (landscape mode). Without state saving, the graph would reset, and the user would lose the historical temperature data. **Implementation:** **1. `onSaveInstanceState()` — Save Graph Data Points** ```java @Override protected void onSaveInstanceState(Bundle outState) { super.onSaveInstanceState(outState); outState.putString("CURRENT_DEVICE", "Living Room Sensor"); outState.putDoubleArray("TEMP_HISTORY", new double[]{22.5, 23.0, 22.8, 23.5}); outState.putInt("ALERT_THRESHOLD", 30); outState.putBoolean("MQTT_STATUS", true); } ``` **2. `onCreate()` — Restore Saved State** ```java @Override protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState); setContentView(R.layout.activity_monitor); if (savedInstanceState != null) { String device = savedInstanceState.getString("CURRENT_DEVICE"); double[] history = savedInstanceState.getDoubleArray("TEMP_HISTORY"); int threshold = savedInstanceState.getInt("ALERT_THRESHOLD"); boolean mqttStatus = savedInstanceState.getBoolean("MQTT_STATUS"); // Re-initialize the graph with historical data updateGraph(history, threshold); } else { // First launch: fetch fresh data from MQTT connectToMQTTBroker(); } } ``` **3. Manifest Configuration to Avoid Recreation (Optional)** ```xml ``` > Note: This approach **manually handles** configuration changes without relying on the Bundle mechanism. **Conclusion:** `onSaveInstanceState()` and `onRestoreInstanceState()` are **indispensable** for IoT applications where users frequently switch between activities and expect their monitoring data, control settings, and connection states to be preserved seamlessly. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing `onStop()` with `onDestroy()`**: `onStop()` is called when the Activity is no longer visible; `onDestroy()` is called when the Activity is about to be removed from memory. Many students use them interchangeably. > 2. **Forgetting `super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)`**: Always call the superclass method first in `onCreate()`, or the app will crash. > 3. **Missing the `setContentView()` call**: Without this, the Activity will have no UI and will appear as a blank screen. > 4. **Using `onSaveInstanceState()` for persistent storage**: This is only for **transient** state. Use `SharedPreferences` or `Room Database` for permanent data. > 5. **Incorrect launch mode syntax**: The correct attribute is `android:launchMode` (not `launch_mode` or `mode`). > 6. **Not declaring Activities in the Manifest**: An Activity that is not declared in `AndroidManifest.xml` will cause an `ActivityNotFoundException` at runtime. > 7. **Confusing Intent `putExtra()` with `putExtras()`**: `putExtra()` adds a single key-value pair; `putExtras(Bundle)` adds an entire Bundle. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🔑 Key Definitions - **Activity**: A single, focused, user-facing screen in an Android application. - **Intent**: A messaging object for inter-component communication. - **Back Stack**: A LIFO stack that stores Activities in navigation order. - **Lifecycle**: The sequence of states an Activity transitions through. - **Launch Mode**: Defines how a new Activity instance is created relative to the current task. - **Bundle**: A key-value container for passing data between Activities and saving state. ### 🔑 The 7 Lifecycle Methods (Memory Aid: **"CO-SR-PS-DR"**) | **Code** | **Method** | **Trigger** | |---|---|---| | **CO** | `onCreate` | First creation | | **S** | `onStart` | Becomes visible | | **R** | `onResume` | Gains focus | | **P** | `onPause` | Loses focus | | **S** | `onStop` | No longer visible | | **D** | `onDestroy` | About to be removed | | **R** | `onRestart` | Restarting after stop | ### 🔑 Three Nested Lifetimes 1. **Entire Lifetime**: `onCreate` → `onDestroy` 2. **Visible Lifetime**: `onStart` → `onStop` 3. **Foreground Lifetime**: `onResume` → `onPause` ### 🔑 Launch Modes Cheat Code - **`standard`**: New instance every time (default). - **`singleTop`**: New instance if not on top. - **`singleTask`**: Only one in the task. - **`singleInstance`**: Sole member of its own task. ### 🔑 Intent Data Types Supported `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `boolean`, `String`, `CharSequence`, `Parcelable`, `Serializable`, `Bundle`, arrays of these types. ### 🔑 Bundle Size Limit $$\text{Maximum Bundle Size} \approx 1\,\text{MB (Binder transaction limit)}$$ ### 🔑 Manifest Declaration (Minimum Required) ```xml ``` ### 🔑 IoT-Specific Best Practices - **Connect** to MQTT broker in `onStart()` or `onResume()`. - **Disconnect** in `onStop()` to save battery. - **Save** sensor history in `onSaveInstanceState()`. - **Use `singleTask`** for the main dashboard to avoid duplicate instances.

topics.title: Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Service** in the context of Android-based Mobile Application Development for IoT is an application component that represents either an application's desire to perform a longer-running operation while not interacting with the user, or to supply functionality for other applications to use. Each Service class must have a corresponding `<service>` declaration in its package's `AndroidManifest.xml`. A Service is **not** a separate process or thread by itself; it runs in the main thread of its hosting process unless explicitly configured otherwise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Highlight:** A Service is a *background* application component, not a *separate thread* by default. This distinction is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU University Examinations.

### Classification of Services (KTU Module 1 Syllabus Anchor)

| Service Type | Trigger | Termination Rule | Typical IoT Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Started Service** | `startService()` | Self-stoppable via `stopSelf()` | Continuous sensor telemetry upload |
| **Bound Service** | `bindService()` | Stops when *all* clients unbind | Remote IoT device control interface |
| **Foreground Service** | `startForeground()` | System-protected, user-visible notification | Real-time BLE/ MQTT health monitor |
| **IntentService** | `startService()` (with Intent) | Auto-stops after `onHandleIntent()` | One-shot cloud API calls (now deprecated) |

### Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Specialist

Imagine a hospital (your Android app) where patients (Activities) come and go. A **Service** is like a **Specialist Doctor** working in the background:

- The doctor doesn't need a patient to be physically present to keep working (no UI required).
- The doctor can keep doing important tasks (e.g., monitoring vitals) even after the patient leaves the room (Activity moves to background).
- Patients can either *call* the doctor to start a job (`startService`) or *request* the doctor to be on-call for personal consultation (`bindService`).
- If the doctor is doing something critical (e.g., surgery on a critical patient), the hospital puts a **persistent notification** on the board — that's a **Foreground Service**.
- The doctor only stops working when the task is complete or when explicitly told to stop.

### Key Physical / Standard Metrics to Remember

- **Default Service Lifetime:** Co-lives with the application process. A Service is **destroyed** only when system memory is critically low or `stopService()` / `stopSelf()` is called.
- **ANR (Application Not Responding) Threshold:** **5 seconds** for input events, **20 seconds** for broadcast receivers (foreground tasks). Heavy work in a Service must be offloaded to background threads.
- **Foreground Service Notification Priority:** Minimum API 26+ (Oreo) requires a notification channel; channel importance must be at least `IMPORTANCE_LOW` to avoid vibration.
- **MQTT Keep-Alive Interval (IoT context):** Typical default **60 seconds** — Service must sustain this connection in background.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Note (KTU 2024 Module 1):** Within the IoT mobile app context, Services are the *de-facto* mechanism for sustaining long-lived device-to-cloud and cloud-to-device communication channels (MQTT, CoAP, BLE GATT, HTTP polling) without draining foreground UI responsiveness.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of an Android Service

When an application component (Activity, BroadcastReceiver, or another Service) invokes a Service, the **Android Activity Manager Service (AMS)** performs the following sequence:

1. The client component creates an explicit `Intent` addressing the target Service.
2. The client calls either `startService(Intent)` or `bindService(Intent, conn, flags)`.
3. The **AMS** validates the manifest declaration, checks permissions, and (if the Service is not yet alive) instantiates it by invoking `onCreate()` on the **main UI thread**.
4. The AMS then routes the call to either `onStartCommand()` (started) or `onBind()` (bound).
5. The Service enters a managed lifecycle state, tracked by the AMS in its internal `ServiceRecord` table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for IoT:** When a mobile app is receiving continuous IoT sensor streams, the receiving Service is bound to the Activity for live UI updates, but ALSO functions as a started service for backgrounded telemetry forwarding. This *hybrid* mode is the most common exam and lab scenario.

### 2.2 Lifecycle Methods — Ordered Logical Breakdown

| Lifecycle Callback | Triggered When | Returns / Key Argument | IoT Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| `onCreate()` | Service is first instantiated | None | Initialize MQTT client, open DB |
| `onStartCommand()` | `startService()` invoked | `intent`, `flags`, `startId` | Begin sensor polling, return `START_STICKY` |
| `onBind()` | `bindService()` invoked | `Intent` from client | Return `IBinder` for IPC |
| `onUnbind()` | Last client disconnects | `Intent` | Clean up listener references |
| `onRebind()` | New client re-binds after `onUnbind()` returned `true` | `Intent` | Re-attach listener |
| `onDestroy()` | Service shutting down | None | Close MQTT, release wake locks |

### 2.3 `onStartCommand()` Return Constants — The Most-Tested Concept

These constants are the **highest-weightage** items in the KTU Module 1 question bank. Memorize them:

| Constant | Behavior on System Kill | Typical IoT Scenario |
|---|---|---|
| `START_STICKY` | System recreates Service **with null Intent** | Periodic telemetry push |
| `START_NOT_STICKY` | System does **not** recreate Service | User-initiated one-shot upload |
| `START_REDELIVER_INTENT` | System recreates and **redelivers same Intent** | Reliable command delivery to IoT device |
| `START_STICKY_COMPATIBILITY` | Legacy compatibility shim | Avoid in modern code |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (API Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for Table Rendering:** The vertical pipe `|` is replaced with `\vert` in mathematical contexts to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Item / Concept | API Signature / Value | Threading Rule |
|---|---|---|
| Start a Service | `startService(Intent)` | Main thread call |
| Stop own Service | `stopSelf(int startId)` | Use the **same startId** |
| Stop a Service externally | `stopService(Intent)` | Main thread call |
| Bind to a Service | `bindService(Intent, ServiceConnection, int)` | Asynchronous callback |
| Make Foreground | `startForeground(int id, Notification)` | API 26+ needs channel |
| Stop Foreground | `stopForeground(boolean removeNotification)` | API 24+ |
| Manifest entry | `<service android:name=".MyService"/>` | Required |
| Permission attribute | `android:permission="android.permission.BIND_*"` | Optional but tested |
| Process attribute | `android:process=":my_iot_proc"` | Creates separate process |
| Binder return | `return new LocalBinder();` (extends `Binder`) | Same-process binding |
| AIDL return | `return new MyStub();` (generated) | Cross-process binding |
| JobScheduler alternative | `JobScheduler.schedule(JobInfo)` | API 21+, replaces long services |
| WorkManager alternative | `WorkManager.getInstance(ctx).enqueue(req)` | Recommended post-2019 |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** field deployments, a mobile Service is the canonical choice for:

- Sustaining a **MQTT-over-WebSocket** connection to a broker (e.g., HiveMQ, Mosquitto Cloud) while the user is interacting with the dashboard.
- Implementing **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) GATT callbacks** that survive Activity recreation.
- Running **Geofence transition broadcasts** for asset tracking.
- Persisting **OTA (Over-The-Air) firmware download** chunks even when the app is backgrounded.

> [!NOTE]
> In production-grade IoT apps, Google now recommends **WorkManager** for deferrable, guaranteed execution, and **Foreground Services** for user-visible, latency-sensitive tasks (e.g., live GPS sharing). Pure background Services are restricted on Android 8.0+ (API 26+) when the app is in the background.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Service Lifecycle State Transitions

Let us mathematically model the **Service state machine**. Define the state set:

$$
S = \{\text{CREATED},\ \text{STARTED},\ \text{BOUND},\ \text{HYBRID},\ \text{DESTROYED}\}
$$

The state transition function is:

$$
\delta : S \times E \rightarrow S
$$

where $E$ is the set of external events:

$$
E = \{\texttt{startService},\ \texttt{bindService},\ \texttt{unbindService},\ \texttt{stopSelf},\ \texttt{stopService},\ \texttt{onLowMemory}\}
$$

The complete transition table is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(\text{CREATED},\ \texttt{startService}) &= \text{STARTED} \\
\delta(\text{CREATED},\ \texttt{bindService}) &= \text{BOUND} \\
\delta(\text{STARTED},\ \texttt{bindService}) &= \text{HYBRID} \\
\delta(\text{BOUND},\ \texttt{startService}) &= \text{HYBRID} \\
\delta(\text{HYBRID},\ \texttt{unbindService} \mid \text{lastClient}) &= \text{STARTED} \\
\delta(\text{HYBRID},\ \texttt{stopSelf}) &= \text{BOUND} \\
\delta(\text{BOUND},\ \texttt{unbindService} \mid \text{lastClient}) &= \text{DESTROYED} \\
\delta(\text{STARTED},\ \texttt{stopSelf}) &= \text{DESTROYED} \\
\delta(\text{STARTED},\ \texttt{onLowMemory}) &= \text{DESTROYED (or TRIMMED)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic, step-by-step:**

- A Service in **CREATED** state is alive but has not received either type of entry call yet. This state is transient.
- The `startService()` event transitions it to **STARTED** by invoking `onStartCommand()`.
- The `bindService()` event transitions it to **BOUND** by invoking `onBind()`.
- If *both* events have occurred, it enters the **HYBRID** state. A Service remains alive in HYBRID as long as there is **at least one** unhandled `startId` **OR** at least one active client binding.
- The destruction rule is the **logical NOR** condition:

$$
\text{Destroyed} \iff (\text{startIdCount} = 0) \ \land\ (\text{connectionCount} = 0)
$$

- The above Boolean expression is the **key insight** students must articulate in the 14-mark question.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Memory Implications of Return Flags

Consider the **expected lifetime** $E[T]$ of a Service in seconds, given a return flag $f \in \{\text{STICKY},\ \text{NOT\_STICKY},\ \text{REDELIVER\_INTENT}\}$ and the system kill rate $\lambda$ (kills per hour).

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[T \mid f = \text{STICKY}] &= \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \left(1 + e^{-\lambda \Delta t}\right) \\
E[T \mid f = \text{NOT\_STICKY}] &= \frac{1}{\lambda} \\
E[T \mid f = \text{REDELIVER\_INTENT}] &= \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \left(1 + e^{-\lambda \Delta t} + e^{-2\lambda \Delta t}\right) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\Delta t$ is the average delay before system resurrection.

**Step-by-step derivation of the STICKY case:**

Step 1 — Survival probability in one interval: $P(\text{survive}) = e^{-\lambda \Delta t}$.

Step 2 — If the Service is killed, the system *promises* to re-instantiate it; this contributes a *bonus* expected lifetime of $e^{-\lambda \Delta t} \cdot E[T]$.

Step 3 — Self-consistency equation:

$$
E[T] = \frac{1}{\lambda} + e^{-\lambda \Delta t} \cdot E[T]
$$

Step 4 — Solve for $E[T]$:

$$
E[T] \left(1 - e^{-\lambda \Delta t}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

$$
E[T] = \frac{1}{\lambda \left(1 - e^{-\lambda \Delta t}\right)}
$$

Step 5 — Apply the geometric series identity:

$$
E[T] = \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - e^{-\lambda \Delta t}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} e^{-k \lambda \Delta t}
$$

This result is approximate, but it demonstrates that **START_STICKY** yields an expected lifetime that is *amplified* by the geometric factor $\frac{1}{1 - e^{-\lambda \Delta t}}$ relative to `START_NOT_STICKY`.

### 3.3 Worked Example: IoT Telemetry Service Decision

**Problem:** A mobile app uploads temperature readings every 30 seconds to a cloud MQTT broker. The user may background the app for 10 minutes. Which return flag should the Service use?

**Step 1 — Identify the requirements:**

- Critical: Missing a reading is acceptable, but the user expects the *latest* reading on resume.
- Resource cost: Battery and network.

**Step 2 — Evaluate candidates:**

- `START_STICKY`: System respawns with `null` Intent. The Service would not know *which* sensor stream to resume without extra state.
- `START_REDELIVER_INTENT`: System respawns and redelivers the *last* Intent. This preserves the sensor target identity.
- `START_NOT_STICKY`: App might silently die during backgrounding.

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** Use `START_REDELIVER_INTENT` with persistent `SharedPreferences` state for partially completed uploads.

**Step 4 — Verification:** $E[T]$ for `REDELIVER_INTENT` exceeds `NOT_STICKY` by approximately a factor of $1 + e^{-\lambda \Delta t}$, justifying the marginal battery cost.

> [!NOTE]
> In modern Android (10+), this problem is **better solved** with **WorkManager + unique work**, but the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus still tests the raw `Service` mechanism.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Service Lifecycle Flow (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created : onCreate
    Created --> Started : startService -> onStartCommand
    Created --> Bound : bindService -> onBind
    Started --> Hybrid : bindService -> onBind
    Bound --> Hybrid : startService -> onStartCommand
    Hybrid --> Started : all clients unbind
    Hybrid --> Bound : stopSelf
    Started --> Destroyed : stopSelf or stopService
    Bound --> Destroyed : last client unbinds
    Hybrid --> Destroyed : all unbind AND stopSelf
    Destroyed --> [*] : onDestroy
```

### 4.2 IoT Service Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLIENT_DEVICE[IoT Sensor Node]
        sensorA[Sensor MCU]
        bleA[BLE Radio]
    end

    subgraph MOBILE_APP[Android Mobile App]
        actA[Main Activity]
        svcA[IoT Service]
        mqttA[MQTT Client]
        dbA[Room Database]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD[Cloud Backend]
        brokerA[MQTT Broker]
        apiA[REST API]
    end

    sensorA -->|BLE GATT Notify| svcA
    svcA -->|bindService| actA
    svcA -->|publish topic| mqttA
    mqttA -->|TLS TCP| brokerA
    brokerA -->|Command Topic| mqttA
    svcA <-->|Persist readings| dbA
    actA -->|startService| svcA
    brokerA -->|POST webhook| apiA
```

### 4.3 Threading Architecture Inside a Service

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mainT[Main UI Thread]
    workT[Worker Thread / Executor]
    handlerT[HandlerThread]
    asyncT[AsyncTask deprecated]

    mainT -->|onStartCommand| workT
    workT -->|publish onSuccess| mainT
    workT -->|publish onFailure| mainT
    mainT -->|startForeground| mainT
    handlerT -.alternative.-> workT
    asyncT -.legacy.-> workT

    classDef thread fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef worker fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    class mainT thread
    class workT,handlerT,asyncT worker
```

### 4.4 Hybrid Service Data Flow (Most-Asked Pattern)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant A as MainActivity
    participant S as IoTService
    participant M as MQTT Broker
    participant D as IoT Device

    U->>A: Open App
    A->>S: bindService Intent
    S-->>A: onServiceConnected IBinder
    A->>S: subscribe sensor topic
    S->>M: CONNECT clientId
    M-->>S: CONNACK
    S->>D: BLE Write Command
    D-->>S: GATT Notify payload
    S->>M: PUBLISH telemetry
    S-->>A: BroadcastReceiver update
    U->>A: Press Home (background)
    A->>S: onUnbind may occur
    S->>S: onStartCommand START_STICKY
    S->>M: PINGREQ keepalive
    M-->>S: PINGRESP
    U->>A: Reopen App
    A->>S: rebind
    S-->>A: Latest cached readings
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3 Mark Questions**

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**Define an Android Service. Differentiate between a Started Service and a Bound Service.**

**Model Answer:**

A Service is an application component capable of performing long-running operations in the background without a user interface.

| Aspect | Started Service | Bound Service |
|---|---|---|
| Invocation | `startService(Intent)` | `bindService(Intent, conn, flags)` |
| Lifecycle Owner | The Service itself (via `stopSelf`) | All bound clients (unbinding destroys it) |
| Communication | One-way (limited Intent extras) | Two-way via `IBinder` interface |
| Number of Clients | N/A | Multiple clients can bind simultaneously |
| Active Callback | `onStartCommand()` | `onBind()` |
| Survives Activity death | Yes | Only while clients remain bound |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for comparison table)*

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Explain the purpose of `onStartCommand()` return flags. When would you choose `START_REDELIVER_INTENT` over `START_STICKY`?**

**Model Answer:**

The return value of `onStartCommand()` informs the Android system what to do **if the Service is killed** due to resource pressure.

- `START_STICKY` → System recreates Service with a **null Intent**, suitable for services that self-manage their state (e.g., media player).
- `START_REDELIVER_INTENT` → System recreates Service and **redelivers the original Intent**, suitable for one-shot tasks whose payload is critical (e.g., uploading a specific IoT firmware blob).

Choose `START_REDELIVER_INTENT` when the **payload matters** and must be replayed verbatim. Choose `START_STICKY` when the service is **stateful** and rebuilds its work context independently.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for each constant explained, 0.5 mark for selection justification)*

---

### **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the complete **Android Service lifecycle**. Discuss all the callback methods along with their significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an **IoT Service** for a smart agriculture app that maintains a continuous MQTT connection to a cloud broker and periodically publishes soil moisture readings every 30 seconds. List the **manifest declarations**, **permissions**, and the **return flag** you would choose, with justification. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — State the lifecycle methods in invocation order:**

The Android Service lifecycle consists of six callback methods. The full state transition sequence is:

$$
\text{onCreate} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{onStartCommand} & \text{(if started)} \\ \text{onBind} & \text{(if bound)} \end{cases} \rightarrow \text{onUnbind} \rightarrow \text{onRebind} \rightarrow \text{onDestroy}
$$

**Step 2 — Explain each callback:**

- `onCreate()` — Invoked once when the Service is first instantiated. Use it for **one-time** setup: open database, instantiate MQTT client, allocate buffers.
- `onStartCommand()` — Invoked every time `startService()` is called. The returned integer dictates resurrection behavior. The `startId` argument uniquely identifies this invocation and must be passed to `stopSelf(int)` to avoid premature termination.
- `onBind()` — Invoked when the **first** client binds. Returns the `IBinder` channel.
- `onUnbind()` — Invoked when the **last** client disconnects. Returning `true` allows `onRebind()` to be called subsequently.
- `onRebind()` — Invoked when new clients bind after `onUnbind()` returned `true`.
- `onDestroy()` — Final cleanup. Release resources, close sockets, unregister listeners.

**Step 3 — Refer to the lifecycle diagram** (see SECTION 4, Diagram 4.1).

**Step 4 — Conclude** with the destruction rule:

$$
\text{Destroy} \iff (\text{no pending startIds}) \ \land\ (\text{no active bindings})
$$

*(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 3 for method explanations, 2 for destruction rule)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Manifest declaration:**

```xml
<service
    android:name=".SoilMoistureService"
    android:exported="false"
    android:foregroundServiceType="dataSync" />
```

**Step 2 — Required permissions:**

```xml
<uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET" />
<uses-permission android:name="android.permission.ACCESS_NETWORK_STATE" />
<uses-permission android:name="android.permission.FOREGROUND_SERVICE" />
<uses-permission android:name="android.permission.FOREGROUND_SERVICE_DATA_SYNC" />
<uses-permission android:name="android.permission.WAKE_LOCK" />
<uses-permission android:name="android.permission.POST_NOTIFICATIONS" />
```

**Step 3 — Return flag selection: `START_REDELIVER_INTENT`**

**Justification:** The MQTT publish intent carries the **topic** and **payload** (e.g., `farm/sector_A/moisture`). If the Service is killed, redelivering the *same* Intent guarantees the last queued reading is published. `START_STICKY` would deliver `null`, losing the payload.

**Step 4 — Architectural explanation:**

The Service will:
- Run an `ExecutorService` with a single thread (or `HandlerThread`) to offload the 30-second publish loop from the main thread.
- Call `startForeground()` with a sticky notification on `onCreate()` (API 26+ requires a notification channel of importance `LOW`).
- Use `SharedPreferences` to persist the last published timestamp for resume logic.

**Step 5 — Conclude** with the publish loop pseudocode structure (described, not coded, in KTU ESE):

The worker thread sleeps for 30 seconds using `Thread.sleep(30000)`, reads from a sensor cache, and calls `mqttClient.publish(topic, payload, qos=1, retained=false)`.

*(7 marks — 1.5 for manifest, 1.5 for permissions, 2 for flag choice + justification, 2 for architectural explanation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Started Services**, **Bound Services**, **Foreground Services**, and **IntentService** with a comparative table. State the modern recommended alternative for long-running deferrable tasks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how a **Bound Service** is used to expose a custom API to a client Activity for controlling an IoT smart light (on/off/brightness). Provide the `IBinder` interface design and the Activity-side usage. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Comparative Table:**

| Property | Started | Bound | Foreground | IntentService |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Trigger | `startService` | `bindService` | `startForeground` | `startService` (Intent) |
| UI Required | No | No | Persistent notification | No |
| Threading | Main (default) | Main (default) | Main (default) | Built-in worker thread |
| Self-stopping | Manual `stopSelf` | Auto on unbind | Manual | Auto after `onHandleIntent` |
| Multiple Instances Allowed | Yes (per Intent) | No (singleton) | Yes | No (serialized queue) |
| Long-running? | Yes (with workers) | Yes | Yes | One-shot only |
| IoT Fit | Telemetry | Device control | Live monitoring | Legacy one-shot |
| Modern Replacement | WorkManager | `Flow` / `StateFlow` | Foreground Service (still) | WorkManager |

**Modern recommended alternative:** For **deferrable, guaranteed** background work, **WorkManager** (Jetpack) is the official replacement. For **latency-critical, user-initiated** tasks, **Foreground Service** remains mandatory. For **in-Activity reactive streams**, **Kotlin Coroutines + StateFlow** replaces bound services in many UI-bound IoT use cases.

*(7 marks — 4 for table, 2 for modern alternatives, 1 for synthesis)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Define the AIDL-style interface in the Service file:**

The Service defines an inner `LocalBinder` class and a public interface, e.g., `ISmartLightControl`. The binder exposes three methods:

- `void turnOn()`
- `void turnOff()`
- `void setBrightness(int level)` where $0 \le \text{level} \le 100$

**Step 2 — Service-side `onBind()` implementation:**

The `onBind()` callback returns an instance of `LocalBinder` whose `getService()` method returns `this`, allowing direct in-process method calls without IPC overhead.

**Step 3 — Activity-side `ServiceConnection`:**

The Activity implements `ServiceConnection` with two callbacks:
- `onServiceConnected(ComponentName, IBinder)` — cast the IBinder to `LocalBinder`, store the service reference.
- `onServiceDisconnected(ComponentName)` — null out the reference (called only on unexpected disconnection, not normal unbind).

**Step 4 — Bind lifecycle in the Activity:**

The Activity calls `bindService()` in `onStart()` and `unbindService()` in `onStop()`. This guarantees the Service is alive only while the Activity is visible, conserving battery.

**Step 5 — BLE/MQTT command dispatch:**

When the user toggles a switch, the Activity calls `service.turnOn()`. The Service then translates this into a BLE GATT write (e.g., `characteristic.setValue(new byte[]{0x01})`) or an MQTT publish (e.g., `topic="home/light/cmd"`, `payload="ON"`).

**Step 6 — Conclude** with the threading caveat: All binder calls execute on the main thread of the client Activity. The Service must marshal the actual BLE/MQTT work onto a worker thread to avoid ANRs.

*(7 marks — 1.5 for interface design, 1.5 for binder pattern, 1.5 for Activity binding lifecycle, 1.5 for IoT translation, 1 for threading caveat)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. Confusing **Service** (background component) with **Thread** (concurrency primitive). A Service is **not** a thread by default. This single error has historically cost 2–3 marks.
> 2. Forgetting that `onStartCommand()` is invoked on the **main thread** — students who run a `while(true)` loop inside it without a worker thread will be marked down for the ANR risk.
> 3. Confusing `stopSelf()` with `stopSelf(int startId)`. The former stops *immediately* and may cancel pending startIds; the latter is the **safe** form.
> 4. On API 26+ (Oreo), failing to call `startForeground()` within **5 seconds** of `startService()` will cause the Service to be killed by the system. Always mention this in foreground service questions.
> 5. Writing `IntentService` examples in 2024 answers — `IntentService` was **deprecated in API 30**. Use `WorkManager` or a manual `HandlerThread` Service instead.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

- A **Service** is a background application component, **not** a separate process or thread by default.
- **Three Service flavours:** Started, Bound, Foreground. A Service can simultaneously be Started **and** Bound (HYBRID state).
- **Destruction rule:** Service dies only when there are **no pending startIds AND no active client bindings** *and* `stopSelf()` / `stopService()` is called.
- `onStartCommand()` return flags: **`START_STICKY`** (null Intent), **`START_NOT_STICKY`** (no resurrection), **`START_REDELIVER_INTENT`** (replay last Intent).
- `onBind()` returns an `IBinder` — use `LocalBinder` for same-process, **AIDL** for cross-process IPC.
- **Foreground Service is mandatory** for user-perceptible, latency-sensitive IoT tasks (live tracking, BLE streaming) on Android 8.0+.
- For **deferrable, guaranteed** background IoT work, prefer **WorkManager** over a raw Service.
- **All Service callbacks run on the main thread** — always offload blocking I/O (MQTT, BLE, HTTP) to a worker thread, `HandlerThread`, or coroutine `Dispatchers.IO`.
- **Manifest declaration** with `<service>` tag is **mandatory**; export attribute must be explicitly set from API 31+.
- **Permissions** for foreground services are split by `foregroundServiceType` from Android 14 (API 34) — choose the correct type (`dataSync`, `connectedDevice`, `location`, etc.).
- **MQTT keep-alive, BLE GATT callbacks, and HTTP polling** are the three classic IoT use cases that a Service is designed to host in a mobile IoT app.
- Remember the **5-second rule** for `startForeground()` on API 26+, and the **20-second rule** for `BroadcastReceiver` `onReceive()`.
- **Deprecated but historically asked:** `IntentService` — know its definition, the worker thread behavior, and the deprecation context.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Broadcast Receivers & Content Providers
UI Components - Views & Notifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** UI Components - Views & Notifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **UI Components** in Android constitute the visual and interactive building blocks of any application. They are broadly classified into **Views** (the atomic UI elements drawn on the screen) and **ViewGroups** (invisible containers that dictate the layout structure of their child Views).

A **View** is the fundamental building class of the Android UI hierarchy, occupying a rectangular area on the screen and being responsible for drawing itself and handling user interaction events. The `android.view.View` class is the superclass of all UI widgets such as `TextView`, `EditText`, `Button`, `ImageView`, `CheckBox`, `RadioButton`, and `Spinner`.

A **ViewGroup** (a subclass of `android.view.ViewGroup`) is a special type of View that acts as a container, defining the layout structure for other Views (children) and ViewGroups. Examples include `LinearLayout`, `RelativeLayout`, `ConstraintLayout`, and `FrameLayout`.

A **Notification** is a message that an Android application displays to the user outside of its normal UI, typically appearing in the notification shade (system tray), as a heads-up banner, or as an icon on the status bar. Notifications are managed through the `NotificationManager` system service and the `NotificationCompat` API from the AndroidX library.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of building a house 🏠:**
> - **Views** = Individual components like *doors, windows, switches, lights* (each serves a specific purpose).
> - **ViewGroups** = The *room structure* (which wall holds what, how things are arranged spatially).
> - **Layout XML** = The *architectural blueprint* that defines where everything goes before construction.
> - **Activity** = The *house itself* that hosts all these components.
> - **Notifications** = The *doorbell or letterbox* — they alert you to important events even when you are not actively looking at the house (app).

Just as a real house cannot exist without walls (ViewGroups) holding windows and doors (Views), an Android screen cannot render without a layout container holding its widgets. And just as a doorbell rings regardless of whether you are looking at the front door, notifications reach the user even when the app is in the background — a critical feature for IoT apps that monitor sensor data continuously.

---

### 1.3 Standard Units & Metrics in UI Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Density-Independent Pixels (`dp` or `dip`)** = the standard unit for measuring UI elements; **1 dp = 1 px on a 160 dpi (mdpi) screen**. Use `dp` for layout dimensions to ensure consistency across screen densities.
> - **Scale-Independent Pixels (`sp`)** = used for `textSize`; respects the user's font scale accessibility setting.
> - **Standard Notification Channel Importance Levels (API 26+)**: `IMPORTANCE_HIGH`, `IMPORTANCE_DEFAULT`, `IMPORTANCE_LOW`, `IMPORTANCE_MIN`.
> - **Material Design Tap Target Minimum**: **48 dp × 48 dp** — any clickable View must satisfy this for accessibility compliance.
> - **Default Notification Accent Color**: `#FF4081` (Material Pink A200) — the colour of the small icon background on devices running Android Lollipop and above.

---

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping

| Sub-Topic | KTU Expected Depth |
|-----------|-------------------|
| View and ViewGroup hierarchy | Conceptual + Diagram |
| Common UI Widgets (TextView, EditText, Button, etc.) | Definition + XML snippet |
| Layout Managers | Comparative table |
| Event Listeners | Code-level understanding |
| Notification Anatomy (API 26+) | Channel-based model |
| Building & Displaying Notifications | Code-level (Java/Kotlin) |
| PendingIntent & Intent Flags | Conceptual + Code |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Android UI Class Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> Every visual element in Android is part of a strict inheritance chain rooted at `android.view.View`. This is **why polymorphism** lets us handle any widget using a generic `View` reference, and why XML attributes (like `android:id`, `android:layout_width`) work universally across all widgets.

$$
\text{View} \rightarrow \text{ViewGroup} \rightarrow \text{Layout Classes} \;(\text{LinearLayout, RelativeLayout, ConstraintLayout, FrameLayout})
$$

$$
\text{View} \rightarrow \text{TextView} \rightarrow \{\text{EditText, Button, CheckBox, RadioButton}\}
$$

$$
\text{View} \rightarrow \text{ImageView, ProgressBar, SeekBar, WebView}
$$

---

### 2.2 Why & How: Operational Breakdown

#### **A. Why `View` is the Root Class**
- **Why:** Provides a uniform contract for measurement (`onMeasure`), layout positioning (`onLayout`), drawing (`onDraw`), and event handling (`onTouchEvent`).
- **How:** All widgets override these methods to render their specific appearance. For example, `Button.onDraw()` paints a rounded rectangle with text; `ImageView.onDraw()` calls `drawable.draw(canvas)`.

#### **B. Why ViewGroups Use Invalidation**
- **Why:** When a child View changes, the parent must re-render to reflect it.
- **How:** Calling `invalidate()` schedules a redraw on the next frame; `requestLayout()` triggers a fresh measure-and-layout pass.

#### **C. Why Notifications Require Channels (API 26+)**
- **Why:** Android 8.0 (Oreo) introduced channels so users can granularly control which types of notifications an app may show.
- **How:** Each `NotificationChannel` has an ID, name, description, and importance. A notification must be posted via a channel ID; otherwise, it is silently dropped by the system.

#### **D. Why PendingIntent is Required for Notifications**
- **Why:** A `Notification` lives in the system process, not the app process. When the user taps it, the system must be able to launch the target Activity even if the app is dead.
- **How:** `PendingIntent` wraps a regular `Intent` with a token, allowing the system to execute it later on behalf of the app. Flags like `FLAG_IMMUTABLE` (mandatory from API 31) and `FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT` control its behaviour.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Constants & Conversions for UI Design**

| Quantity | Formula / Value | Unit | Purpose |
|----------|----------------|------|---------|
| 1 dp to px (general) | $\text{px} = \text{dp} \times (\text{dpi} \,\vert\, 160)$ | pixels | Convert layout dims to physical pixels |
| 1 sp to px | $\text{px} = \text{sp} \times \text{fontScale} \times (\text{dpi} \,\vert\, 160)$ | pixels | Respect user font scaling |
| Density buckets | ldpi=0.75, mdpi=1.0, hdpi=1.5, xhdpi=2.0, xxhdpi=3.0, xxxhdpi=4.0 | ratio | Asset scaling factor |
| Min touch target | $\geq 48\,\text{dp} \times 48\,\text{dp}$ | dp | Accessibility |
| Notification ID | unique integer per channel | int | Replaces or cancels prior posts |
| Channel ID | immutable string per logical type | String | Routing for `notify(id, n)` |
| `IMPORTANCE_HIGH` urgency | $\leq 10\,\text{sec}$ heads-up | seconds | Foreground-style banner |
| `setTimeoutAfter()` | long ms (default = 0 = persists) | ms | Auto-dismiss timer |

---

### 2.4 Common UI Components — Comparative Reference

| Component | Inherits From | Primary Use | Key XML Attribute |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|-------------------|
| `TextView` | `View` | Display static text | `android:text` |
| `EditText` | `TextView` | Accept user text input | `android:inputType` |
| `Button` | `TextView` | Trigger an action | `android:onClick` |
| `ImageView` | `View` | Display an image resource | `android:src` |
| `CheckBox` | `Button` | Multi-selection | `android:checked` |
| `RadioButton` | `Button` | Single selection within `RadioGroup` | `android:checked` |
| `ToggleButton` | `Button` | Two-state switch | `android:textOn` / `android:textOff` |
| `Spinner` | `ViewGroup` (AdapterView) | Dropdown list | `android:entries` |
| `ProgressBar` | `View` | Show operation progress | `android:max`, `style="?android:attr/progressBarStyleHorizontal"` |
| `SeekBar` | `ProgressBar` | User-adjustable slider | `android:max` |
| `Switch` | `CompoundButton` | Material on/off switch | `android:checked` |
| `WebView` | `ViewGroup` | Render HTML content | `loadUrl()` / `loadData()` |

---

### 2.5 Layout Managers — Selection Cheat Sheet

| Layout | When to Use | Avoid When |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| `LinearLayout` | Linear stacks (rows/columns), simple hierarchies | Complex overlapping UI, deep nesting |
| `RelativeLayout` | Children relative to parent/siblings | Performance-critical, large UIs |
| `ConstraintLayout` | Flat hierarchy, responsive design, recommended by Google | Extremely trivial 2-widget screens |
| `FrameLayout` | Single child or stacked overlapping Views (e.g., fragments) | Multiple siblings needing equal weight |
| `GridLayout` | Grid arrangement without nested LinearLayouts | Free-form positioning |
| `TableLayout` | Tabular data with rows & columns | Modern UI is usually ConstraintLayout |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility in IoT

In **IoT mobile apps**, UI components are not just cosmetic — they are the human-machine interface (HMI) for sensor data.

- `TextView` displays live **temperature / humidity** readings.
- `SeekBar` lets the user adjust a target **setpoint** (e.g., fan speed 0–100%).
- `Switch` toggles a **smart bulb** ON/OFF via MQTT/HTTP call.
- `ProgressBar` shows **firmware download** progress for OTA updates.
- `Notification` alerts when a **threshold** (e.g., gas leakage, motion detected) is breached — even when the app is in the background, leveraging FCM (Firebase Cloud Messaging) for push delivery.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU exam:** Always mention **"the View is the base class of all UI components in Android"** — this is a frequently asked 2- or 3-mark question.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Because this is a CS/Programming topic (Mobile App Development), code snippets in **XML and Java/Kotlin** are integral to the explanation. Each step is annotated with the **conversion logic / rationale** as required by the KTU valuation scheme.

---

### 3.1 Derivation Walkthrough 1: Building a Simple Form Using Views

**Problem Statement:** Construct an Android form that takes a username, password, and a "Remember Me" checkbox, with a Login button. The button must display a Toast greeting the user on click.

#### **Step 1 — Define the XML Layout Blueprint**

Create a file `res/layout/activity_main.xml`. The root is a `LinearLayout` (a ViewGroup) holding the children (Views).

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="16dp">

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/headerText"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Smart Home Login"
        android:textSize="24sp"
        android:textStyle="bold" />

    <EditText
        android:id="@+id/usernameInput"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:hint="Enter Username"
        android:inputType="text" />

    <EditText
        android:id="@+id/passwordInput"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:hint="Enter Password"
        android:inputType="textPassword" />

    <CheckBox
        android:id="@+id/rememberMe"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Remember Me" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/loginButton"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Login" />

</LinearLayout>
```

**Logic of the conversion from requirement to XML:**
- `LinearLayout` is chosen as the **root ViewGroup** because children are stacked vertically — a 1D linear flow.
- `match_parent` on layout_width means the View **stretches to fill** the parent's width; `wrap_content` means it is **just big enough to hold its content**.
- `16dp` padding ensures the content is not flush against the screen edge — adheres to Material Design's **16dp gutter** standard.
- `textPassword` on `EditText` masks the input characters.
- `android:id="@+id/xxx"` is mandatory in Java/Kotlin to obtain a programmatic reference to the View via `findViewById(R.id.xxx)`.

---

#### **Step 2 — Wire Up Event Listener in `MainActivity.java`**

```java
package com.example.smarthome;

import android.os.Bundle;
import android.view.View;
import android.widget.Button;
import android.widget.CheckBox;
import android.widget.EditText;
import android.widget.Toast;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    // Step 2a: Declare View references
    private EditText usernameInput;
    private EditText passwordInput;
    private CheckBox rememberMe;
    private Button   loginButton;

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        // Step 2b: Inflate the XML layout into the Activity's content view
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);

        // Step 2c: Bind XML ids to Java objects (View lookup)
        usernameInput = findViewById(R.id.usernameInput);
        passwordInput = findViewById(R.id.passwordInput);
        rememberMe    = findViewById(R.id.rememberMe);
        loginButton   = findViewById(R.id.loginButton);

        // Step 2d: Register an anonymous OnClickListener on the Button
        loginButton.setOnClickListener(new View.OnClickListener() {
            @Override
            public void onClick(View v) {
                // Step 2e: Read values from the EditText Views
                String user = usernameInput.getText().toString().trim();
                String pass = passwordInput.getText().toString().trim();

                // Step 2f: Validate inputs and respond
                if (user.isEmpty() || pass.isEmpty()) {
                    Toast.makeText(MainActivity.this,
                            "Please enter all fields",
                            Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show();
                } else {
                    String msg = "Welcome, " + user
                               + (rememberMe.isChecked() ? " (Session Saved)" : "");
                    Toast.makeText(MainActivity.this, msg, Toast.LENGTH_LONG).show();
                }
            }
        });
    }
}
```

**Conversion logic (line by line):**
- `setContentView(R.layout.activity_main)` **parses** the XML and creates a tree of View objects in memory.
- `findViewById` **traverses** this tree to find the View whose `android:id` matches the given identifier.
- `setOnClickListener(...)` registers a **callback** invoked by the Android framework when a `MotionEvent.ACTION_UP` is detected within the button's bounds.
- `Toast.makeText(...).show()` displays a transient, non-modal message that **floats** above the UI for `LENGTH_SHORT` (~2 sec) or `LENGTH_LONG` (~3.5 sec).

---

### 3.2 Derivation Walkthrough 2: Building a Notification (API 26+ Channel-Based)

**Problem Statement:** Post a notification titled "Sensor Alert" with the message "Gas level exceeded threshold!" when the user presses a button.

#### **Step 1 — Create a Notification Channel in `onCreate()` (mandatory for API 26+)**

```java
private static final String CHANNEL_ID = "iot_sensor_alerts";

@Override
protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
    super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
    setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);

    // STEP 1: Create the NotificationChannel (one-time, idempotent)
    if (Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.O) {
        NotificationChannel channel = new NotificationChannel(
                CHANNEL_ID,
                "IoT Sensor Alerts",                       // Channel name (user-visible)
                NotificationManager.IMPORTANCE_HIGH);      // Urgency level
        channel.setDescription("Alerts from IoT sensor readings");
        channel.enableVibration(true);
        channel.setShowBadge(true);

        NotificationManager manager = getSystemService(NotificationManager.class);
        manager.createNotificationChannel(channel);
    }
}
```

**Conversion logic:**
- `Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.O` is a **runtime version guard** — `NotificationChannel` was introduced in API 26 (Oreo).
- `IMPORTANCE_HIGH` enables **heads-up display** and a default sound; the system shows the notification as a **peeking banner** on top of the current Activity for ~10 seconds.
- `createNotificationChannel()` is **idempotent** — calling it again with the same ID does not create a duplicate; it just updates the existing one.

---

#### **Step 2 — Build and Post the Notification**

```java
public void postAlert(View view) {
    // STEP 2a: Define the tap action — opens MainActivity when notification is tapped
    Intent intent = new Intent(this, MainActivity.class);
    intent.setFlags(Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK | Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TASK);

    // STEP 2b: Wrap the Intent in a PendingIntent (executed by the system, not us)
    int pendingFlags = (Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.S)
            ? PendingIntent.FLAG_IMMUTABLE | PendingIntent.FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT
            : PendingIntent.FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT;

    PendingIntent pendingIntent = PendingIntent.getActivity(
            this, 0, intent, pendingFlags);

    // STEP 2c: Construct the notification using NotificationCompat.Builder
    NotificationCompat.Builder builder = new NotificationCompat.Builder(this, CHANNEL_ID)
            .setSmallIcon(R.drawable.ic_sensor)              // Required: status-bar icon
            .setContentTitle("Sensor Alert")
            .setContentText("Gas level exceeded threshold!")
            .setPriority(NotificationCompat.PRIORITY_HIGH)  // Pre-O priority hint
            .setContentIntent(pendingIntent)                 // Tap action
            .setAutoCancel(true);                            // Dismiss on tap

    // STEP 2d: Post via NotificationManager
    NotificationManagerCompat manager = NotificationManagerCompat.from(this);
    if (ActivityCompat.checkSelfPermission(this,
            android.Manifest.permission.POST_NOTIFICATIONS) != PackageManager.PERMISSION_GRANTED
            && Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.TIRAMISU) {
        // Permission is not granted — request it or abort
        return;
    }
    manager.notify(1001, builder.build());  // 1001 = notification ID
}
```

**Conversion logic (step-by-step):**
1. `Intent` describes the **target** Activity to open.
2. `PendingIntent.getActivity()` produces a **system-tokenized** reference, granting the OS permission to launch the Activity on our behalf.
3. `FLAG_IMMUTABLE` (mandatory from API 31) prevents the **embedded Intent from being modified** by a third party — a security requirement.
4. `NotificationCompat.Builder` is the **backward-compatible** factory (works on API 14+).
5. `setSmallIcon()` is the **monochrome icon** shown in the status bar; **required**, otherwise the notification is rejected.
6. `setAutoCancel(true)` **removes** the notification from the shade when the user taps it.
7. `manager.notify(id, ...)` posts the notification; passing the same `id` again **replaces** the existing one.

---

#### **Step 3 — Add the Permission to `AndroidManifest.xml`**

```xml
<uses-permission android:name="android.permission.POST_NOTIFICATIONS" />
```

**Logic:** Since Android 13 (API 33, Tiramisu), `POST_NOTIFICATIONS` is a **runtime permission** — the user must grant it via a dialog. Without it, `notify()` is a no-op.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: View vs ViewGroup vs Notification

| Property | View | ViewGroup | Notification |
|----------|------|-----------|--------------|
| Inherits from | `Object` (root of hierarchy) | `View` | N/A (system-managed message) |
| Renders itself | ✅ Yes (`onDraw`) | ❌ Delegates to children | ✅ Yes (system UI) |
| Holds children | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (via `addView`) | ❌ No (has an `extras` Bundle) |
| Lifecycle | Attached to a Window | Manages child layouts | Created, posted, dismissed |
| Measurement role | Reports `MeasureSpec` | Calls `measure()` on children | N/A |
| Primary constructors | `View(Context)`, `View(Context, AttributeSet)` | `ViewGroup(Context, AttributeSet)` | `NotificationCompat.Builder()` |
| Survives app death? | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (stored by system) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Android UI Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeRoot["Object Java Class"]
    nodeView["android view View"]
    nodeViewGroup["android view ViewGroup"]
    nodeLayout1["LinearLayout"]
    nodeLayout2["RelativeLayout"]
    nodeLayout3["ConstraintLayout"]
    nodeLayout4["FrameLayout"]
    nodeTextView["TextView"]
    nodeEditText["EditText"]
    nodeButton["Button"]
    nodeCheckBox["CheckBox"]
    nodeRadioBtn["RadioButton"]
    nodeImageView["ImageView"]
    nodeProgressBar["ProgressBar"]
    nodeSeekBar["SeekBar"]
    nodeSpinner["Spinner"]

    nodeRoot --> nodeView
    nodeView --> nodeViewGroup
    nodeViewGroup --> nodeLayout1
    nodeViewGroup --> nodeLayout2
    nodeViewGroup --> nodeLayout3
    nodeViewGroup --> nodeLayout4

    nodeView --> nodeTextView
    nodeTextView --> nodeEditText
    nodeTextView --> nodeButton
    nodeButton --> nodeCheckBox
    nodeButton --> nodeRadioBtn
    nodeView --> nodeImageView
    nodeView --> nodeProgressBar
    nodeProgressBar --> nodeSeekBar
    nodeViewGroup --> nodeSpinner
```

**Interpretation:** `View` is the universal superclass. `ViewGroup` extends `View` to gain child-management capabilities. `TextView` branches into input-capable descendants like `EditText` and clickable descendants like `Button`/`CheckBox`/`RadioButton`.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Notification Anatomy & Posting Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    appEvent["App Logic Event e.g. Sensor Threshold Breach"]
    buildChannel["Create NotificationChannel once API 26 plus"]
    buildBuilder["Build NotificationCompat Builder"]
    setIcon["setSmallIcon mandatory"]
    setTitle["setContentTitle and setContentText"]
    setIntent["setContentIntent via PendingIntent"]
    postNotify["NotificationManagerCompat notify id, builder build"]
    sysTray["System Status Bar Icon"]
    headsUp["Heads Up Banner on lock screen"]
    shade["Notification Shade Expanded View"]
    userTap["User Taps Notification"]
    launchAct["System launches Target Activity via PendingIntent"]

    appEvent --> buildChannel
    appEvent --> buildBuilder
    buildBuilder --> setIcon
    setIcon --> setTitle
    setTitle --> setIntent
    setIntent --> postNotify
    postNotify --> sysTray
    postNotify --> headsUp
    postNotify --> shade
    userTap --> launchAct
    shade -.-> userTap
    headsUp -.-> userTap
    sysTray -.-> userTap
```

**Interpretation:** Notifications are produced through a one-time channel creation, a builder pipeline, and a `notify()` call. They surface in three visual contexts — the status bar icon, the heads-up banner, and the shade — all of which funnel into a tap-driven `PendingIntent` launch.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — UI Component Interaction

```mermaid
graph LR
    xmlFile["res layout activity_main xml"]
    inflater["LayoutInflater inflates"]
    viewTree["In Memory View Tree"]
    actWin["Activity Window"]
    eventBus["Event Dispatch Thread"]
    handler["OnClickListener / OnTouchListener"]
    callback["User Defined Callback Method"]
    toast["Toast / Snackbar / Notification"]

    xmlFile --> inflater
    inflater --> viewTree
    viewTree --> actWin
    actWin --> eventBus
    eventBus --> handler
    handler --> callback
    callback --> toast
```

**Interpretation:** The XML blueprint is inflated into a tree of View objects, attached to the Activity's Window. User input flows through the Android event dispatch thread to listeners, which invoke the developer's callback methods, which in turn produce output (Toast, Notification, or navigation).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the terms `View` and `ViewGroup` in Android. State the inheritance relationship between them with a neat diagram.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> - **View**: The base class `android.view.View` represents the fundamental building block of the Android UI. It occupies a rectangular region on the screen, is responsible for its own drawing, and handles user interaction events.
> - **ViewGroup**: A subclass of `View` that acts as an invisible container, holding and arranging child Views and ViewGroups. It extends the `View` class to inherit drawing/event capabilities and adds child-management (add/remove/measure/layout).
> - **Inheritance:** `ViewGroup extends View`. So every ViewGroup **is a** View, but with extra structural responsibility.

**Hierarchy Sketch:**
$$
\text{View} \rightarrow \text{ViewGroup} \rightarrow \{\text{LinearLayout, RelativeLayout, ConstraintLayout, FrameLayout}\}
$$

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is a Notification Channel? Why was it introduced in Android 8.0 (API 26)?*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **Notification Channel** is a logical grouping of notifications defined by an app, identified by a unique `channel_id`, with its own `IMPORTANCE` level, name, description, sound, vibration, and badge settings.
>
> **Why introduced (API 26 / Oreo):**
> - **User Control:** Users can granularly mute or customize specific types of alerts (e.g., "Mute promotional, keep alerts on").
> - **Reduced Spam:** Apps must pre-declare their alert categories, encouraging thoughtful design.
> - **Backward Compatibility:** Channels are only enforced on API 26+; older versions ignore them.
>
> Each notification posted after API 26 must be associated with a channel ID, else it is **silently dropped** by the system.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the Android UI hierarchy with a suitable diagram. Differentiate between `LinearLayout`, `RelativeLayout`, and `ConstraintLayout` in terms of nesting, performance, and use case. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write the XML and Java code to create a registration form for an IoT Smart Home app with the following fields: Name (`EditText`), Email (`EditText`), a `RadioGroup` to select User Role (Admin / Guest), a `CheckBox` to enable notifications, and a `Register` button. The Register button, on click, must show a `Toast` containing all entered values. *(7 marks)*

---

##### **Solution for (a):**

The **Android UI hierarchy** is a tree where every node is either a `View` (a leaf-like widget) or a `ViewGroup` (a container with children). The single root is attached to the `Activity`'s `Window`.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | `LinearLayout` | `RelativeLayout` | `ConstraintLayout` |
|----------|---------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Child arrangement | Single row OR column (1D) | Relative to parent/siblings (2D) | Flexible, constraint-based (2D) |
| Nesting depth | Often deep → slow | Moderate | Flat (recommended) |
| Performance | Slow when nested | Better | Best (best-in-class) |
| Best for | Simple stacks | Forms with relative positioning | Modern, responsive UIs |
| Android Studio default | No | No | **Yes** (since 2017) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why `ConstraintLayout` is recommended:** It allows flat hierarchies where every child has constraints to parent/siblings, avoiding the nested `LinearLayout` chains that cause overdraw and slow `measure`/`layout` passes.

---

##### **Solution for (b):**

**XML File — `res/layout/activity_register.xml`:**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="16dp">

    <EditText
        android:id="@+id/nameInput"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:hint="Full Name"
        android:inputType="textPersonName" />

    <EditText
        android:id="@+id/emailInput"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
```
