```xml
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:hint="Email Address"
        android:inputType="textEmailAddress" />

    <RadioGroup
        android:id="@+id/roleGroup"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:orientation="horizontal">

        <RadioButton
            android:id="@+id/roleAdmin"
            android:layout_width="wrap_content"
            android:layout_height="wrap_content"
            android:text="Admin"
            android:checked="true" />

        <RadioButton
            android:id="@+id/roleGuest"
            android:layout_width="wrap_content"
            android:layout_height="wrap_content"
            android:text="Guest" />
    </RadioGroup>

    <CheckBox
        android:id="@+id/notifCheck"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Enable Sensor Alerts" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/registerBtn"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Register" />

</LinearLayout>
```

**Java File — `RegisterActivity.java`:**

```java
package com.example.smarthome;

import android.os.Bundle;
import android.view.View;
import android.widget.Button;
import android.widget.CheckBox;
import android.widget.EditText;
import android.widget.RadioButton;
import android.widget.RadioGroup;
import android.widget.Toast;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class RegisterActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    private EditText  nameInput, emailInput;
    private RadioGroup roleGroup;
    private CheckBox  notifCheck;
    private Button    registerBtn;

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_register);

        nameInput  = findViewById(R.id.nameInput);
        emailInput = findViewById(R.id.emailInput);
        roleGroup  = findViewById(R.id.roleGroup);
        notifCheck = findViewById(R.id.notifCheck);
        registerBtn = findViewById(R.id.registerBtn);

        registerBtn.setOnClickListener(new View.OnClickListener() {
            @Override
            public void onClick(View v) {
                String name  = nameInput.getText().toString().trim();
                String email = emailInput.getText().toString().trim();

                int selectedId = roleGroup.getCheckedRadioButtonId();
                RadioButton selectedRole = findViewById(selectedId);
                String role   = (selectedRole != null) ? selectedRole.getText().toString() : "None";

                boolean alerts = notifCheck.isChecked();

                String summary = "Name: " + name
                               + "\nEmail: " + email
                               + "\nRole: " + role
                               + "\nAlerts: " + (alerts ? "ON" : "OFF");

                Toast.makeText(RegisterActivity.this, summary, Toast.LENGTH_LONG).show();
            }
        });
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting `android:checked="true"` on a default `RadioButton` — the `RadioGroup` will have **no selection** if the user does not tap one, and `getCheckedRadioButtonId()` returns `-1`, causing a `NullPointerException`.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Using `textEmailAddress` inputType but **not validating** the format with `Patterns.EMAIL_ADDRESS.matcher(...).matches()` — partial credit is awarded if the validation logic is mentioned.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting `android:orientation="horizontal"` on `RadioGroup` — by default, it stacks vertically and consumes the entire screen height, breaking the layout.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, describe the **anatomy of an Android Notification** as per Material Design. List any **five** properties that must be set on a `NotificationCompat.Builder` for a notification to be valid. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a complete Android program segment (Java) to:
1. Create a `NotificationChannel` with ID `"fire_alerts"` and `IMPORTANCE_HIGH`.
2. Build and post a notification titled **"Fire Detected!"** with the text **"Sensor in Kitchen triggered."** that opens `AlertActivity` when tapped.
3. Mention the manifest permission required and the runtime permission rationale. *(7 marks)*

---

##### **Solution for (a):**

**Anatomy of an Android Notification (Material Design):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1["Small Icon top left, monochrome, mandatory"]
    n2["App Name next to icon, auto-supplied by system"]
    n3["Timestamp right side, setWhen, default = now"]
    n4["Content Title large, setContentTitle"]
    n5["Content Text medium, setContentText"]
    n6["Large Icon optional, setLargeIcon, e.g. app logo"]
    n7["Actions Row up to 3 buttons, addAction"]
    n8["Expandable Big Text Style, bigText or InboxStyle or MediaStyle"]
    n9["Content Intent tap target, setContentIntent, mandatory if autoCancel true"]

    n1 --> n2
    n2 --> n3
    n3 --> n4
    n4 --> n5
    n5 --> n6
    n6 --> n7
    n7 --> n8
    n8 --> n9
```

**Five Mandatory / Essential Properties on `NotificationCompat.Builder`:**

1. **`setSmallIcon(int iconRes)`** — *mandatory*; the monochrome status-bar icon.
2. **`setContentTitle(String title)`** — the bold headline shown in the shade.
3. **`setContentText(String text)`** — the descriptive line below the title.
4. **`setContentIntent(PendingIntent intent)`** — required to make the notification clickable.
5. **`Builder(Context, String channelId)`** — the **channel ID** passed to the constructor; mandatory for API 26+.

*(Optional but recommended: `setAutoCancel(true)`, `setPriority(...)`, `setWhen(...)`)*

---

##### **Solution for (b):**

```java
public class FireAlertHelper {

    private static final String CHANNEL_ID = "fire_alerts";
    private final Context ctx;

    public FireAlertHelper(Context ctx) {
        this.ctx = ctx;
    }

    // (i) Create the channel — must be called once at app start / onCreate
    public void createChannel() {
        if (Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.O) {
            NotificationChannel channel = new NotificationChannel(
                    CHANNEL_ID,
                    "Fire Sensor Alerts",
                    NotificationManager.IMPORTANCE_HIGH);   // heads-up + sound
            channel.setDescription("Critical alerts from fire/smoke IoT sensors");
            channel.enableLights(true);
            channel.enableVibration(true);

            NotificationManager mgr = ctx.getSystemService(NotificationManager.class);
            mgr.createNotificationChannel(channel);
        }
    }

    // (ii) Build & post the notification
    public void postFireAlert() {
        // Tap → open AlertActivity
        Intent intent = new Intent(ctx, AlertActivity.class);
        intent.putExtra("ALERT_TYPE", "FIRE");
        intent.setFlags(Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK | Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TASK);

        int flags = (Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.S)
                ? PendingIntent.FLAG_IMMUTABLE | PendingIntent.FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT
                : PendingIntent.FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT;

        PendingIntent pendingIntent = PendingIntent.getActivity(ctx, 0, intent, flags);

        NotificationCompat.Builder builder = new NotificationCompat.Builder(ctx, CHANNEL_ID)
                .setSmallIcon(R.drawable.ic_fire)            // mandatory
                .setContentTitle("Fire Detected!")
                .setContentText("Sensor in Kitchen triggered.")
                .setPriority(NotificationCompat.PRIORITY_HIGH)
                .setContentIntent(pendingIntent)
                .setAutoCancel(true);

        // (iii) Runtime permission check for API 33+
        if (Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.TIRAMISU) {
            if (ContextCompat.checkSelfPermission(ctx,
                    android.Manifest.permission.POST_NOTIFICATIONS)
                    != PackageManager.PERMISSION_GRANTED) {
                // Request the permission from the Activity context
                if (ctx instanceof Activity) {
                    ActivityCompat.requestPermissions((Activity) ctx,
                            new String[]{android.Manifest.permission.POST_NOTIFICATIONS},
                            101);
                }
                return;  // abort posting if not granted
            }
        }

        NotificationManagerCompat.from(ctx).notify(2001, builder.build());
    }
}
```

**Manifest Permission:**
```xml
<uses-permission android:name="android.permission.POST_NOTIFICATIONS" />
```

**Runtime Permission Rationale:**
- From **Android 13 (API 33, Tiramisu)** onward, `POST_NOTIFICATIONS` is a **runtime permission** (dangerous level).
- It must be **requested at runtime** via `ActivityCompat.requestPermissions(...)` because it concerns user privacy and attention.
- If the user **denies** it, `notify()` becomes a **silent no-op** — the system does not throw an exception, so explicit checking is mandatory.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B — Notification Question):**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Posting the notification **without first creating the channel** on API 26+ → the system **silently drops** the notification. Always show the channel creation code first.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Using only `setPriority(PRIORITY_HIGH)` on API 26+ and **forgetting to set IMPORTANCE_HIGH** on the channel. Both must agree, or the system uses the **lower** of the two.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Building a `PendingIntent` without `FLAG_IMMUTABLE` on API 31+ → the app **crashes** with `IllegalArgumentException`.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Omitting `setSmallIcon()` → the notification is **rejected** by the system and not displayed. Always check this is the **first** thing set.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MUST-KNOW bullets before entering the KTU exam hall:**

- ✅ **`View` is the base class of all Android UI components** — both widgets and layouts inherit from it.
- ✅ **`ViewGroup` is a `View` that holds children** — it defines layout structure.
- ✅ The **root of every Activity's UI tree** is typically a `ViewGroup` set via `setContentView(R.layout.xxx)`.
- ✅ **Use `dp` for sizes and `sp` for text** — never use `px` directly in production layouts.
- ✅ **Minimum touch target = 48 dp × 48 dp** for Material Design accessibility.
- ✅ **`findViewById(R.id.xxx)` binds an XML id to a Java/Kotlin object** — the id must exist in the inflated XML.
- ✅ **Event listeners are interfaces** — `OnClickListener`, `OnTouchListener`, `OnLongClickListener`, `OnFocusChangeListener`, etc.
- ✅ **`LinearLayout` is 1D**, `RelativeLayout` is 2D relative, `ConstraintLayout` is 2D constraint-based and **Google's recommended default**.
- ✅ **Notifications require channels on API 26+**; channels are created once and reused via their `channel_id` string.
- ✅ **`PendingIntent.FLAG_IMMUTABLE` is mandatory from API 31 (Android 12)** when creating a `PendingIntent`.
- ✅ **POST_NOTIFICATIONS is a runtime permission from API 33 (Android 13)**; request it with `ActivityCompat.requestPermissions`.
- ✅ A `Notification` must have **at minimum**: `setSmallIcon()`, a channel ID (API 26+), a `setContentIntent()` if it is to be tappable.
- ✅ **`Toast` = transient bottom popup; `Snackbar` = swipeable bottom message with action; `Notification` = system-shade message** — these are **not** interchangeable.
- ✅ For **IoT apps**, UI components are the **HMI**; notifications enable **off-screen awareness** of critical sensor events.
- ✅ The **order of operations** in a typical Activity: `onCreate` → `setContentView` → `findViewById` → `setOnXxxListener` → callback methods.
- ✅ **Common bug:** Forgetting to override `onCreate` with `super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);` before `setContentView` → app crashes on launch.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — UI Components: Views & Notifications**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PECGT415 | B.Tech CSE (IoT Specialization)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components for Communication - Intents & Intent Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Components for Communication - Intents & Intent Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intent (Android/Kotlin/Java):** An **Intent** in Android is a passive data structure (a `android.content.Intent` object) that carries an *abstract description of an operation to be performed* — a message-passing mechanism that allows late-run-time binding between code in different applications. Intents are the fundamental glue that wires together the three out of four core Android components (Activities, Services, and Broadcast Receivers) for inter-process and intra-process communication.

In the context of **IoT Mobile App Development**, intents become the messaging layer that allows a mobile client to:
- Trigger actuator commands on a remote IoT device
- Launch a foreground service that continuously reads sensor streams
- Broadcast environmental events (e.g., motion detected) to subscribed listeners
- Open a specific dashboard Activity when a push notification is tapped

An **Intent Filter**, on the other hand, is an expression declared in an application's `AndroidManifest.xml` file (and dynamically via `registerReceiver()`) that specifies the **type of Intents** the component would like to receive. It acts as a contract-publishing endpoint.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Postal Envelope System:**

Imagine a **postal envelope** as your Intent. You write the following on it:
- **To:** A specific building (Component name) **OR** a description like "Anyone who delivers pizza" (Action + Data)
- **Action:** "DELIVER_PIZZA" (the verb)
- **Category:** "FOOD_ORDER" (the topic)
- **Extras:** A sticky note saying "Extra cheese, no olives" (Bundle of key-value data)

Now, **every building (app component)** has a **mailbox sticker (Intent Filter)** that says:
> *"I accept envelopes labeled DELIVER_PIZZA, in the FOOD_ORDER category, from www.pizzapalace.com"*

The **Post Office (Android System)** inspects the envelope and delivers it to the best-matching mailbox. If you write a *specific building address*, it's an **Explicit Intent**; if you only describe what you want, it's an **Implicit Intent**, and Android finds the right recipient for you.

## 1.3 Why It Matters for IoT

In a smart-home or industrial-IoT app, intents power:
- Triggering BLE scans (Bluetooth Low Energy discovery)
- Launching MQTT client services
- Broadcasting sensor-fusion events to UI Activities
- Receiving OS-level events (e.g., `BATTERY_LOW`, `CONNECTIVITY_CHANGE`)

Standard metrics used in the framework:
- **Intent actions** are string constants, conventionally named `package_name.action.VIEW` (e.g., `android.intent.action.VIEW`).
- **MIME types** follow **RFC 2045 / RFC 2046** standards (e.g., `text/plain`, `image/jpeg`).
- **Intent resolution** matches against the **3-tuple {Action, Category, Data}** per the PackageManager's intent resolution algorithm.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Intent — The Seven Fields

| # | Field | Type | Purpose |
|---|-------|------|---------|
| 1 | **Component Name** | `ComponentName` | Explicit targeting — `setComponent()` / `setClassName()` |
| 2 | **Action** | `String` | Verb describing the operation — `setAction()` |
| 3 | **Data** | `Uri` + MIME | Payload address — `setData()` / `setType()` |
| 4 | **Category** | `String` | Classification hint — `addCategory()` |
| 5 | **Extras** | `Bundle` | Key-value data — `putExtra()` |
| 6 | **Flags** | `int` | Modifiers — `addFlags()` (e.g., `FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK`) |
| 7 | **ClipData** | `ClipData` | Drag-and-drop / shared clipboard payload |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Setting **any** of Component Name, Action, or Data puts the Intent on the path toward *explicit* resolution. Omitting all three is technically legal but ambiguous.

## 2.2 Two Flavors of Intents

### 2.2.1 Explicit Intents
- Specify the target component by **fully-qualified class name** (FQCN).
- Used **within the same application** or when the developer knows the exact target (e.g., a specific service exposed by an SDK).
- Example: `startActivity(Intent(this, DashboardActivity::class.java))`

### 2.2.2 Implicit Intents
- Declare a general action (e.g., `Intent.ACTION_VIEW`) and let Android resolve the best-matching component.
- Used for **cross-application IPC** and **system integrations** (camera, dialer, browser, etc.).
- Example: `startActivity(Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, Uri.parse("https://iot.kerala.gov.in")))`

## 2.3 Intent Filters — The Recipient's Contract

An **Intent Filter** is a declarative XML element (or runtime register) that an app component advertises as its capability profile. Each `<intent-filter>` in the manifest contains:

| Sub-element | Meaning | Example |
|-------------|---------|---------|
| `<action>` | What the component *does* | `android.intent.action.SEND` |
| `<category>` | Additional context | `android.intent.category.DEFAULT` |
| `<data>` | Data it accepts (URI scheme, host, MIME) | `android:scheme="mqtt"`, `android:mimeType="text/plain"` |

### 2.3.1 The Three-Phase Intent Resolution Algorithm

The Android PackageManager (PM) executes the following sequential tests when resolving an **implicit Intent** to a target component:

1. **Action Test** — Does any filter advertise the intent's `action`? (Wildcard `*` is **not** allowed; must match exactly.)
2. **Category Test** — Every category in the intent must be present in the filter. **Exception:** `CATEGORY_DEFAULT` is auto-added by `startActivity()`.
3. **Data Test** — URI components (scheme, host, port, path) and MIME type must all match the filter's `<data>` element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall for KTU Exam:** If even one test fails for a given filter, that filter is **discarded entirely** — it is not a partial match. Resolution returns either 0 candidates, 1 candidate, or N candidates (in which case a chooser dialog is shown).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Symbol/Notation | Description | Constraint |
|---|---------|-----------------|-------------|------------|
| 1 | Intent Object | $I$ | 7-tuple data structure | $I = (c, a, d, cat, e, f, clip)$ |
| 2 | Component Name | $c$ | FQCN of target | Optional but makes intent explicit |
| 3 | Action | $a$ | String verb | Mandatory for implicit |
| 4 | Data URI | $d$ | `scheme://host:port/path` | Must follow RFC 3986 |
| 5 | Category Set | $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\}$ | Subset match required | $\forall c_i \in C_{intent},\ c_i \in C_{filter}$ |
| 6 | Extras Bundle | $E$ | Key-value pairs | $E = \{(k_i, v_i)\}$ |
| 7 | Resolution Match | $M = A \cap Cat \cap D$ | Intersection of all tests | $M \neq \emptyset \Rightarrow$ resolved |
| 8 | PendingIntent | $\pi(I)$ | Token to fire intent later | Used for notifications/alarms |
| 9 | Broadcast Intent | $I_b$ | Goes to all registered receivers | Lifecycle is non-blocking |
| 10 | Filter Priority | $p \in [{-1000}, 1000]$ | Tie-breaking | Default $0$; higher wins |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In an **IoT mobile ecosystem**, Intent Filters power:

- **Zero-config device pairing** — a custom `ACTION_DEVICE_DISCOVERED` filter exposed by the app is auto-triggered when the OS fires a discovery event.
- **Cross-app data handoff** — a third-party weather app's intent filter accepts `ACTION_VIEW` with `geo:` URI to plot sensor locations.
- **MQTT-over-Android push** — `<service>` with an intent filter for `android.intent.action.BOOT_COMPLETED` auto-starts after device reboot, re-establishing the broker connection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **From intent to action — the key transitions:**
> - **Why use an Intent?** → Decouples the *requester* from the *executor*, enabling runtime discovery and modular app design.
> - **Why use an Intent Filter?** → Declares a component's capabilities so the OS can route implicit intents correctly.
> - **How does the OS route them?** → Three-step test (Action, Category, Data) executed by PackageManager, followed by Activity/Service/Broadcast dispatch.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Intent Resolution Decision Function

We model intent resolution as a deterministic function $R : \mathcal{I} \times \mathcal{F} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$, where $\mathcal{I}$ is the set of all Intents and $\mathcal{F}$ is the set of all declared Filters in the system.

For a given intent $I$ and filter $F$, define the three sub-tests:

$$
\text{actionMatch}(I, F) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } a_I = a_F \text{ for some } a_F \in \text{actions}(F) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{categoryMatch}(I, F) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \forall\, c_i \in C_I,\ c_i \in C_F \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

For the **Data Test**, decompose the URI $d_I = (\text{scheme}_I, \text{host}_I, \text{port}_I, \text{path}_I, \text{mimeType}_I)$ and similarly for $F$:

$$
\text{dataMatch}(I, F) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \bigwedge_{j \in J} \text{uriPartMatch}(I, F, j) \land \text{mimeTypeMatch}(I, F) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The total match score is then:

$$
R(I, F) = \text{actionMatch}(I, F) \cdot \text{categoryMatch}(I, F) \cdot \text{dataMatch}(I, F)
$$

**Step-by-step transition logic:**

1. **Initialize** $R = 1$ (assume match).
2. **Test action:** if intent's action $a_I$ is not listed in $F$'s `<action>` set, set $R = 0$ and abort.
3. **Test categories:** enumerate every category $c_i$ in $C_I$; for each, verify $c_i \in C_F$. If any category is missing, set $R = 0$ and abort.
4. **Test data:** compare URI components and MIME type element-wise. Any mismatch sets $R = 0$ and aborts.
5. **Final $R$ value:** $1$ means filter accepted; $0$ means rejected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This product formulation encodes the **short-circuit AND** semantics of Android's resolution: any single zero forces the entire match to zero.

## 3.2 Derivation: PendingIntent Equality

Two PendingIntents are considered equivalent by the system (for replacement/cancellation) iff their *underlying intent tokens* match:

$$
\pi_1 \equiv \pi_2 \iff \big(a_{\pi_1} = a_{\pi_2}\big) \land \big(c_{\pi_1} = c_{\pi_2}\big) \land \big(d_{\pi_1} = d_{\pi_2}\big) \land \big(E_{\pi_1} = E_{\pi_2}\big)
$$

**Step transitions:**

1. Compare the **action strings** — must be byte-equal (case-sensitive).
2. Compare the **component names** — must resolve to the same FQCN.
3. Compare the **data URIs** — canonical form after `Uri.normalize()`.
4. Compare the **extras bundles** — `Bundle.equals()` semantics, but `Parcelable` extras are compared by reference.

In IoT apps, this is critical when updating a notification each time a new sensor reading arrives — the system reuses the existing PendingIntent if the equations above hold.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Explicit vs. Implicit Intents

| Dimension | Explicit Intent | Implicit Intent |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Component name set? | **Yes (mandatory)** | No (forbidden) |
| Security surface | **Internal only** | **Cross-app — needs permission checks** |
| Resolution time | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ (direct) | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot F)$ (PM scan) |
| Failure mode | `ActivityNotFoundException` | `ActivityNotFoundException` after PM scan |
| Use in IoT | Launching own Services | Triggering OS / third-party IoT apps |
| Filter required at target? | No | **Yes** |

## 3.4 Derivation: BroadcastIntent Delivery Scope

For a broadcast intent $I_b$, the set of receiving components is:

$$
\text{Receivers}(I_b) = \{ r \in \mathcal{R} \mid R(I_b, F_r) = 1 \}
$$

where $\mathcal{R}$ is the set of all currently-registered broadcast receivers (both manifest-declared and runtime-registered via `Context.registerReceiver`).

**Special case — Sticky broadcasts (pre-API 21):**
$$
\text{Receivers}_{\text{sticky}}(I_b, t) = \{ r \in \mathcal{R} \mid R(I_b, F_r) = 1\ \text{AND}\ \text{sticky}(I_b) = \text{true}\ \text{AND}\ t \geq t_{posted}\}
$$

This explains why an IoT app registering for `BATTERY_CHANGED` receives the **last broadcast value** upon registration — the system replays the sticky intent automatically.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Intent Lifecycle Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client App Activity] --> B[Create Intent Object]
    B --> C{Explicit or Implicit?}
    C -->|Explicit| D[setComponent FQCN]
    C -->|Implicit| E[setAction + setData]
    D --> F[startActivity / startService / sendBroadcast]
    E --> G[PackageManager.queryIntentActivities]
    G --> H{Match Found?}
    H -->|Yes 1| I[Launch Target Component]
    H -->|Yes N| J[Show Chooser Dialog]
    H -->|No| K[Throw ActivityNotFoundException]
    I --> L[onCreate / onReceive / onStartCommand]
    J --> I
    K --> M[App Crash or Fallback]
    L --> N[Task Complete]
```

## 4.2 Intent Resolution — Three-Phase Test (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1 Action Test] --> P2[Phase 2 Category Test]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Data Test]
    P1 --> R1{Action in filter?}
    R1 -->|No| X[Reject filter]
    R1 -->|Yes| P2
    P2 --> R2{All categories present?}
    R2 -->|No| X
    R2 -->|Yes| P3
    P3 --> R3{URI and MIME match?}
    R3 -->|No| X
    R3 -->|Yes| S[Filter ACCEPTED]
    X --> S2[Filter REJECTED]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: IoT App using Intents

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UI_LAYER[UI Layer]
        UI_ACT[MainActivity]
        UI_DASH[DashboardActivity]
    end
    subgraph SVC_LAYER[Service Layer]
        SVC_MQTT[MQTT Service]
        SVC_BLE[BLE Scanner Service]
    end
    subgraph RCVR_LAYER[Receiver Layer]
        RCVR_BAT[Battery Receiver]
        RCVR_CONN[Connectivity Receiver]
    end
    subgraph OS_LAYER[Android OS]
        OS_BAT[Battery Manager]
        OS_CONN[Connectivity Manager]
    end
    UI_ACT -->|Explicit Intent| UI_DASH
    UI_ACT -->|Implicit Intent BROWSER| OS_BROWSER[System Browser]
    SVC_MQTT -->|Broadcast Intent sensor data| RCVR_BAT
    OS_BAT -->|System Broadcast| RCVR_BAT
    OS_CONN -->|System Broadcast| RCVR_CONN
    UI_DASH -->|Explicit Intent startService| SVC_MQTT
    UI_DASH -->|Explicit Intent startService| SVC_BLE
```

## 4.4 Manifest Filter Declaration — Conceptual View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[AndroidManifest.xml] --> APP[application tag]
    APP --> ACT[activity MainActivity]
    ACT --> IF1[intent-filter MAIN]
    IF1 --> A1[action MAIN]
    IF1 --> C1[category LAUNCHER]
    ACT --> IF2[intent-filter CUSTOM IOT]
    IF2 --> A2[action iot.kerala.SEND_CMD]
    IF2 --> C2[category DEFAULT]
    IF2 --> D2[data scheme mqtt host broker.iot.kerala]
    APP --> SVC[service MQTTService]
    SVC --> IF3[intent-filter BOOT]
    IF3 --> A3[action BOOT_COMPLETED]
    IF3 --> C3[category DEFAULT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The diagram above mirrors a typical IoT app's manifest. Note how **two intent filters** are attached to a single Activity — one for the launcher icon (system-required), and one for the custom IoT action that other apps can trigger.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Explicit and Implicit Intents in Android. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Explicit Intent | Implicit Intent |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| Target | Specified by component name | Not specified; resolved by OS |
| Use case | Intra-app navigation | Inter-app / system action |
| Example | `Intent(this, HomeActivity::class.java)` | `Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, Uri.parse("https://ktu.edu.in"))` |
| Security | Internal only | Requires `<queries>` declaration (API 30+) |

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for differentiation, 1 mark for example.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is an Intent Filter? List any three attributes used in its declaration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Intent Filter** is a declarative element in `AndroidManifest.xml` that specifies the kinds of Intents a component is willing to receive. It enables the Android PackageManager to perform *implicit intent resolution*.

Three core attributes (sub-elements):

1. **`<action>`** — Defines the verb the component responds to (e.g., `android.intent.action.VIEW`).
2. **`<category>`** — Provides additional context; `android.intent.category.DEFAULT` must be present for `startActivity()` resolution.
3. **`<data>`** — Restricts accepted URIs and MIME types (e.g., `android:scheme="https"`).

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark per attribute, 3 attributes × 1 = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Android Intent resolution mechanism** in detail. Describe the three-phase matching process (Action, Category, Data) with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an **IoT Android application** that uses an explicit intent to launch a `MQTTBrokerService` from a `MainActivity` and uses an implicit intent to open a dashboard URL in a browser. Write the relevant Kotlin/Java code snippets and the corresponding `AndroidManifest.xml` declarations. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **lifecycle of an Intent** from creation to delivery. Differentiate between PendingIntent and regular Intent. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider an industrial IoT scenario where a mobile app must:
  (i) Listen for `BATTERY_CHANGED` system broadcasts,
  (ii) Send an `ACTION_SEND` implicit intent containing sensor data to any registered analyzer app.
  
  Write the manifest declarations and the corresponding `BroadcastReceiver` code to satisfy these requirements. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A(a) — Intent Resolution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define Intent Resolution (2 marks):**
Intent resolution is the process by which the Android PackageManager determines the target component for an implicit intent. The algorithm uses a three-phase matching procedure executed against every intent filter declared in the system.

**Step 2 — Three-Phase Matching (4 marks):**

1. **Action Test:** The PM checks if the intent's `action` string matches *any* `<action>` declared in the filter. Note that a filter may declare multiple actions, but the intent's single action needs to match at least one.

2. **Category Test:** Every category listed in the intent must be present in the filter. The `CATEGORY_DEFAULT` is automatically added when an intent is created via `startActivity()`, so a filter targeting implicit intents should include it.

3. **Data Test:** The intent's URI (scheme, host, port, path) and MIME type are compared against the filter's `<data>` element. A filter with no `<data>` accepts URIs and MIME types only if the intent also has none; otherwise, mismatches cause rejection.

**Step 3 — Resolution Outcome (1 mark):** If exactly 0, 1, or N components match, the system either throws `ActivityNotFoundException`, launches the single match, or presents a chooser dialog, respectively.

**Diagram (Mermaid) — to be drawn in answer book:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Implicit Intent] --> B[Action Test]
    B --> C[Category Test]
    C --> D[Data Test]
    D --> E{Match Count}
    E -->|0| F[ActivityNotFoundException]
    E -->|1| G[Launch Target]
    E -->|N| H[Show Chooser]
```

---

### Solution to Question A(b) — IoT App Design (7 marks)

**Step 1 — MainActivity Kotlin code (2 marks):**

```kotlin
class MainActivity : AppCompatActivity() {
    override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main)

        // Explicit Intent — start MQTT service
        val mqttIntent = Intent(this, MQTTBrokerService::class.java)
        startService(mqttIntent)

        // Implicit Intent — open dashboard in browser
        val dashboardIntent = Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW,
                                     Uri.parse("https://iot.kerala.gov.in/dashboard"))
        startActivity(dashboardIntent)
    }
}
```

**Step 2 — AndroidManifest.xml (3 marks):**

```xml
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
          package="com.example.iotapp">

    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET"/>

    <application
        android:allowBackup="true"
        android:label="IoT Kerala App">

        <activity android:name=".MainActivity">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN"/>
                <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER"/>
            </intent-filter>
        </activity>

        <service android:name=".MQTTBrokerService"/>

    </application>
</manifest>
```

**Step 3 — Explanation (2 marks):** The explicit intent directly references the FQCN `MQTTBrokerService` — no resolution needed. The implicit intent uses the OS-wide `ACTION_VIEW` action with an `https://` URI, so any installed browser will be picked up via PackageManager resolution. The service requires no intent filter since it is invoked only by explicit intents within the app.

---

### Solution to Question B(a) — Intent Lifecycle + PendingIntent (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Lifecycle Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[1. Instantiate Intent] --> B[2. Add Action / Data / Extras]
    B --> C[3. Call startActivity / startService / sendBroadcast]
    C --> D[4. PackageManager Resolution]
    D --> E[5. Target Component Receives]
    E --> F[6. onCreate / onStartCommand / onReceive]
    F --> G[7. Intent Consumed]
```

**Step 2 — Lifecycle Steps (2 marks):**
The intent is created via constructor, configured with `setAction`/`setData`/`putExtra`, then dispatched. The OS routes it through PackageManager (if implicit), invokes the target's lifecycle callback, and the intent is consumed — though the Bundle may persist in `getIntent()`.

**Step 3 — PendingIntent vs Intent (2 marks):**

| Feature | Intent | PendingIntent |
|---------|--------|---------------|
| Lifetime | Synchronous; dies after dispatch | Persists across processes |
| Use case | Direct invocation | Notifications, alarms, App Widgets |
| Granted to | N/A | Foreign apps (NotificationManager, AlarmManager) |
| Token | None | Stable token issued by system |

---

### Solution to Question B(b) — Industrial IoT BroadcastReceiver (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Manifest declaration (2 marks):**

```xml
<application ...>
    <receiver android:name=".BatteryChangeReceiver">
        <intent-filter>
            <action android:name="android.intent.action.BATTERY_CHANGED"/>
        </intent-filter>
    </receiver>

    <activity android:name=".SendSensorDataActivity">
        <intent-filter>
            <action android:name="android.intent.action.SEND"/>
            <category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT"/>
            <data android:mimeType="text/plain"/>
        </intent-filter>
    </activity>
</application>
```

**Step 2 — BatteryChangeReceiver Kotlin code (2 marks):**

```kotlin
class BatteryChangeReceiver : BroadcastReceiver() {
    override fun onReceive(context: Context, intent: Intent) {
        if (intent.action == Intent.ACTION_BATTERY_CHANGED) {
            val level = intent.getIntExtra(BatteryManager.EXTRA_LEVEL, -1)
            val scale = intent.getIntExtra(BatteryManager.EXTRA_SCALE, -1)
            val percent = (level * 100) / scale
            Log.d("IoTBattery", "Level: $percent%")
            // Forward to MQTT service via explicit intent
            val forward = Intent(context, MQTTBrokerService::class.java)
            forward.putExtra("battery_level", percent)
            context.startService(forward)
        }
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Sending sensor data via implicit intent (2 marks):**

```kotlin
val sendIntent = Intent(Intent.ACTION_SEND).apply {
    type = "text/plain"
    putExtra(Intent.EXTRA_TEXT, "TEMP=27.4C,HUM=65%")
}
val chooser = Intent.createChooser(sendIntent, "Send sensor data via")
startActivity(chooser)
```

**Step 4 — Explanation (1 mark):** The receiver is statically registered with the `BATTERY_CHANGED` filter, so the OS auto-delivers all such broadcasts. The implicit `ACTION_SEND` intent is published to the OS, which matches it against the filter (action = SEND, category = DEFAULT, MIME = text/plain), allowing any compatible analyzer app to receive the sensor data.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting `CATEGORY_DEFAULT`** in the manifest filter — this is **auto-added** by `startActivity()`, so without it, the system will **not** match your filter. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 2. **Mixing up `setData()` and `setType()`** — calling `setData()` after `setType()` (or vice versa) **clears the other** because internally they are stored in the same field. Use `setDataAndType()` to set both atomically. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 3. **Confusing `<intent-filter>` with `<intent>`** — the filter is the *recipient* declaration; the intent is the *message*. Examiners specifically test this distinction. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Not declaring `<queries>` for API 30+** when using implicit intents to resolve non-system apps — leads to `ActivityNotFoundException` at runtime. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 5. **Treating `startService` as a method to *send* an intent** — it does launch the service, but the *message* is the intent object itself, which becomes the `Service.onStartCommand(intent, ...)` parameter.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
> 
> - **Intent** = a messaging *object*; **Intent Filter** = a recipient *declaration* in the manifest.
> - An Intent carries **7 fields**: Component, Action, Data, Category, Extras, Flags, ClipData.
> - **Explicit Intent** = component name set → goes directly to the target. **Implicit Intent** = no component name → OS resolves via PackageManager.
> - Resolution uses **3 sequential tests**: Action → Category → Data. **All must pass** (logical AND).
> - **`CATEGORY_DEFAULT` is mandatory** for any filter you want to be matched by `startActivity()`.
> - **Use `setDataAndType()`** — never call `setData()` then `setType()` separately; the second call wipes the first.
> - **`PendingIntent`** is a *handle* to an intent that another app (like AlarmManager) can fire on your behalf.
> - **Broadcast Intents** can be **sticky** — the last broadcast value is replayed to new registrants (pre-API 21 behavior, deprecated now).
> - **For IoT specifically**: intents launch `MQTTService`, trigger `BLE` scans, route `BATTERY_CHANGED` events, and broadcast `SENSOR_DATA` to subscribed apps.
> - **Manifest <queries>** element is required on **API 30+** to discover other apps via PackageManager.
> - **No wildcard** for `<action>` — actions must match strings exactly.
> - **Security**: explicit intents are intra-app safe; implicit intents cross process boundaries and need permission checks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Structure - Android Manifest.xml
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Application Structure - Android Manifest.xml

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AndroidManifest.xml** is the **root configuration file** (in XML format) of every Android application that resides in the root directory `app/src/main/`. It serves as the **declarative blueprint** of the application, providing essential information about the app to the **Android Operating System (AOS)**, Google Play Store, and the **Android Runtime (ART)** before any code is executed.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme PECGT415 syllabus**, the Android Manifest is the structural backbone that:

- Declares the **package name** (unique identifier of the app).
- Enumerates the **hardware/software features** the app requires.
- Registers the **four primary application components**: `Activity`, `Service`, `BroadcastReceiver`, and `ContentProvider`.
- Declares the **minimum**, **target**, and **maximum SDK versions** the app supports.
- Requests **runtime and install-time permissions** (e.g., `INTERNET`, `BLUETOOTH`, `CAMERA`).
- Specifies the **app icon**, **theme**, and **launcher activity**.
- Defines **Intent Filters** to declare how components interact with other apps.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Building's Master Plan + Aadhaar Card + Gate Pass**

Imagine you are constructing a building:

| Manifest Element | Real-World Analogy |
|------------------|---------------------|
| `<manifest>` | The **Master Plan** approved by the municipal authority |
| `package` attribute | The **unique postal address** (no two buildings can share it) |
| `<uses-permission>` | **Security clearances** required to enter sensitive rooms (e.g., vault) |
| `<uses-feature>` | **Building requirements** (e.g., "needs electricity, needs 3 floors") |
| `<application>` | The **building itself** |
| `<activity>` | A **room** in the building |
| `<service>` | A **backroom machinery** that runs without a guest entering |
| `<receiver>` | The **intercom** that listens to specific announcements |
| `<provider>` | The **filing cabinet** that stores and shares records |
| `<intent-filter>` | A **nameplate** on the door that tells outsiders what happens inside |
| `android:icon`, `android:label` | The **building's logo and name** on the front gate |
| `android:minSdkVersion` | The **oldest elevator model** the building supports |

**In short:** Just as a building cannot be opened to the public without a master plan, an Android app cannot even be **installed** on a device without a properly written `AndroidManifest.xml`. The OS reads this file *before* the app's first line of code runs.

### 1.3 Why the Android Manifest is Critical for IoT

In the context of **Mobile App Development for IoT (PECGT415)**, the manifest is the *gateway* to all hardware peripherals (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, GPS, NFC, Camera, Sensors). Without the correct manifest declarations, the IoT mobile app **cannot**:

- Discover nearby BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy) devices.
- Send/receive data over the network (MQTT/HTTP).
- Access location services for geofencing.
- Communicate with the IoT cloud backend.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics & Constraints to Memorize:**
> - The manifest file must be **well-formed XML** (no syntax errors).
> - Only **one** `AndroidManifest.xml` exists per module, but it can be **merged** across modules via the Manifest Merger tool.
> - Package name is **globally unique** (reverse-DNS convention: `in.ktu.iot.sensorapp`).
> - Minimum valid manifest is **just a root `<manifest>` tag with a `package` attribute**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Hierarchy of AndroidManifest.xml

The Android Manifest follows a strict **tree-based XML hierarchy**. Below is the logical decomposition:

**Step 1: The Root Element `<manifest>`**

- This is the **outermost tag** of the XML.
- Mandatory attributes: `package` (deprecated in AGP 8.x in favor of `namespace` in `build.gradle`, but still present in older KTU exam questions).
- Optional attributes: `xmlns:android`, `xmlns:tools`.

**Step 2: The `<uses-permission>` Element**

- Requests permissions the app needs to function.
- Evaluated by the OS during installation (for normal permissions) or at runtime (for dangerous permissions on API 23+).
- Example permissions for IoT apps:
  - `android.permission.INTERNET`
  - `android.permission.BLUETOOTH`
  - `android.permission.BLUETOOTH_ADMIN`
  - `android.permission.BLUETOOTH_SCAN` (API 31+)
  - `android.permission.BLUETOOTH_CONNECT` (API 31+)
  - `android.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION`
  - `android.permission.ACCESS_NETWORK_STATE`

**Step 3: The `<uses-feature>` Element**

- Declares **hardware/software features** the app *requires* or *prefers*.
- The Play Store uses this to **filter incompatible devices**.
- Example: `<uses-feature android:name="android.hardware.bluetooth_le" android:required="true" />`

**Step 4: The `<uses-sdk>` Element**

- Declares the **minimum**, **target**, and **maximum** API levels.
- Modern syntax uses `build.gradle`, but the manifest also supports it.
- Attributes: `android:minSdkVersion`, `android:targetSdkVersion`, `android:maxSdkVersion`.

**Step 5: The `<application>` Element (The Core)**

- Acts as the **container** for all app components.
- Key attributes: `android:icon`, `android:label`, `android:theme`, `android:name`, `android:allowBackup`, `android:supportsRtl`.

**Step 6: Inside `<application>` — The Four Pillars**

| Component Tag | Purpose | KTU IoT Example |
|---------------|---------|-----------------|
| `<activity>` | A single screen with a UI | `MainActivity`, `DeviceControlActivity` |
| `<service>` | Background long-running operation | `MQTTSubscriptionService` |
| `<receiver>` | Listens for system/app broadcasts | `BootCompletedReceiver` |
| `<provider>` | Manages shared app data | `SensorDataProvider` |

**Step 7: The `<intent-filter>` Sub-Element**

- Nested inside a component (usually `<activity>`).
- Declares the **capabilities** of the component (what Intents it can handle).
- Must contain at least one `<action>` element.
- The **MAIN/LAUNCHER** intent filter makes an activity the app's entry point.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Element

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the OS need a manifest?**
> The Android OS uses a **declarative permission and component model** (unlike iOS, which uses an imperative model with `Info.plist` similarly). The manifest allows the OS to enforce **sandboxing**, **least-privilege access**, and **inter-app communication security** *before* any byte of bytecode is executed.

**How the OS reads the manifest (in order):**

1. Reads `<manifest>` and validates the `package` name uniqueness.
2. Collects all `<uses-permission>` and prompts the user (or filters on Play Store).
3. Validates `<uses-sdk>` against the device API level.
4. Indexes all four components in the **PackageManager** registry.
5. Resolves intent filters and builds the **action/category index**.
6. Loads the `<application>` icon and label for the launcher.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Manifest Elements Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The pipes inside column values are escaped using `\vert` to prevent breaking the markdown table.

| Element / Attribute | Mandatory? | Function | Example Value |
|---------------------|------------|----------|---------------|
| `<manifest>` | **Yes** | Root element of the XML | N/A (tag) |
| `package` attribute | **Yes** | Unique app identifier | `in.ktu.iotapp` |
| `<uses-permission>` | Conditional | Requests system permission | `android.permission.INTERNET` |
| `<uses-feature>` | Optional | Declares hardware requirement | `android.hardware.sensor.accelerometer` |
| `<uses-sdk>` | Recommended | API level constraints | `minSdkVersion="24"` |
| `<application>` | **Yes** | Container for app components | N/A (tag) |
| `android:icon` | Recommended | App launcher icon | `@mipmap/ic_launcher` |
| `android:label` | Recommended | App display name | `@string/app_name` |
| `android:theme` | Recommended | Visual theme | `@style/Theme.Material3.DayNight` |
| `<activity>` | **Yes** (at least one) | Declares a screen | `MainActivity` |
| `<service>` | Optional | Declares background service | `MQTTService` |
| `<receiver>` | Optional | Declares broadcast receiver | `AlarmReceiver` |
| `<provider>` | Optional | Declares content provider | `SensorProvider` |
| `<intent-filter>` | Conditional | Component capability declaration | `<action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" \vert <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" \vert` |
| `<meta-data>` | Optional | Key-value pair metadata | `aws.region = us-east-1` |
| `<queries>` | API 30+ | Visibility of other packages | `<package android:name="com.example.iot" \vert` |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Google Play Filtering:** The `<uses-feature>` tag is the primary mechanism that prevents your IoT app from being installed on devices that lack the required hardware (e.g., a BLE-only app won't appear on a device without Bluetooth Low Energy).
- **Security Boundary Enforcement:** The manifest is consulted by `PackageManager` to enforce permission checks before IPC calls.
- **OTA Updates:** The manifest's `versionCode` and `versionName` drive app update logic.
- **IoT Edge Cases:** Declaring `android:exported="true"` is **mandatory** for any component receiving external intents (a frequent KTU exam trap after Android 12 / API 31).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Manifest Construction (Analytical Walkthrough)

Since `AndroidManifest.xml` is an XML schema, the "derivation" here is the **systematic construction of a valid manifest for a typical IoT app** that scans for BLE devices, connects to an MQTT broker, and stores sensor data.

**Step 1 — Open the Project Skeleton**

When you create a new Android Studio project (Empty Activity template), Android Studio auto-generates the manifest at:

$$
\text{Path} = \texttt{app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml}
$$

**Step 2 — Declare the Root and Package**

$$
\texttt{<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>}
$$

$$
\texttt{<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad package="in.ktu.iotapp">}
$$

*Explanation:* The `xmlns:android` namespace declaration is **mandatory**; it tells the XML parser that all `android:*` attributes are part of the Android schema.

**Step 3 — Declare IoT-Specific Permissions**

$$
\texttt{<uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET" />}
$$

$$
\texttt{<uses-permission android:name="android.permission.BLUETOOTH" />}
$$

$$
\texttt{<uses-permission android:name="android.permission.BLUETOOTH_ADMIN" />}
$$

$$
\texttt{<uses-permission android:name="android.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION" />}
$$

*Logic:* Bluetooth scanning on Android requires **LOCATION** permission since BLE beacons can be used for location tracking. The OS will refuse BLE scans without this.

**Step 4 — Declare Hardware Features**

$$
\texttt{<uses-feature}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad android:name="android.hardware.bluetooth_le"}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad android:required="true" />}
$$

*Logic:* Setting `required="true"` hides the app on Google Play for devices without BLE. Setting `required="false"` makes the app installable but disables BLE features gracefully.

**Step 5 — Declare SDK Constraints**

$$
\texttt{<uses-sdk}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad android:minSdkVersion="24"}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad android:targetSdkVersion="34" />}
$$

*Logic:* API 24 (Android 7.0 Nougat) is the typical floor for modern BLE operations. API 34 (Android 14) is the current Google Play requirement.

**Step 6 — Open the `<application>` Block**

$$
\texttt{<application}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad android:allowBackup="true"}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad android:icon="@mipmap/ic_launcher"}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad android:label="@string/app_name"}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad android:theme="@style/Theme.IoTApp"}>}
$$

*Logic:* The `icon` and `label` are **resource references** (denoted by `@`). The theme must be defined in `res/values/themes.xml`.

**Step 7 — Register the Launcher Activity**

$$
\texttt{<activity}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad android:name=".MainActivity"}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad android:exported="true">}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad <intent-filter>}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad\quad <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad\quad <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad </intent-filter>}
$$

$$
\texttt{</activity>}
$$

*Logic:* The `MAIN` + `LAUNCHER` combination is the **only** pair that places an activity in the device's app drawer. The `exported="true"` flag is **mandatory** on API 31+ for any activity reachable by an Intent.

**Step 8 — Register the Background MQTT Service**

$$
\texttt{<service}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad android:name=".services.MQTTService"}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad android:exported="false" />}
$$

*Logic:* Services that should not be invoked by other apps are kept non-exported for security. Only the app itself can `startService()` it.

**Step 9 — Register a Boot Receiver**

$$
\texttt{<receiver}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad android:name=".receivers.BootCompletedReceiver"}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad android:exported="true">}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad <intent-filter>}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad\quad <action android:name="android.intent.action.BOOT_COMPLETED" />}
$$

$$
\texttt{\quad </intent-filter>}
$$

$$
\texttt{</receiver>}
$$

*Logic:* A receiver listening for the `BOOT_COMPLETED` broadcast must be `exported="true"` so the system can deliver the broadcast.

**Step 10 — Close All Tags**

$$
\texttt{</application>}
$$

$$
\texttt{</manifest>}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — The Four Components vs. Lifecycle

| Component | Lifecycle Initiator | Manifest Attribute | Visibility Default (Pre-API 31) | Visibility Default (API 31+) |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|
| `Activity` | `Intent` | `android:exported` | `false` if has filter, else `false` | **Must be explicit** |
| `Service` | `startService()` or binding | `android:exported` | `false` | **Must be explicit** |
| `Receiver` | `sendBroadcast()` | `android:exported` | `false` if has filter, else `false` | **Must be explicit** |
| `Provider` | `ContentResolver` | `android:exported` | `false` | **Must be explicit** |

### 3.3 Manifest Validation Logic (Pseudocode for Understanding)

The Android build pipeline performs the following checks:

$$
\text{ValidManifest} = \begin{cases}
\texttt{true}, & \text{if } \text{root tag} = \texttt{<manifest>} \\
& \text{and } \text{package name} \in \text{UniqueNamespace} \\
& \text{and } \forall \text{component} \rightarrow \text{has valid name} \\
& \text{and } \text{LAUNCHER activity} \geq 1 \\
& \text{and } \text{all } \texttt{android:exported} \text{ attributes are explicit (API 31+)} \\
\texttt{false}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the gating logic in `android.ManifestParser` (AOSP source). If validation fails, the **APK build halts** with an `Installation failed` or `Manifest merger failed` error.

### 3.4 Common Manifest Errors and Resolution Matrix

| Error Message | Root Cause | KTU Exam-Level Fix |
|---------------|-----------|-------------------|
| `INSTALL_PARSE_FAILED_MANIFEST_MALFORMED` | Invalid XML syntax | Check tag closures, attribute quotes |
| `Activity must be exported` | Missing `exported` on API 31+ | Add `android:exported="true"` to launcher activity |
| `Permission denied` | Missing `<uses-permission>` | Declare the required permission |
| `ClassNotFoundException` | Activity class not found | Verify `package` and `.ClassName` path |
| `Manifest merger failed` | Duplicate declarations across modules | Use `tools:replace="android:exported"` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of AndroidManifest.xml

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **hierarchical structure** of a complete AndroidManifest.xml file for an IoT application.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["AndroidManifest.xml Root"] --> B["manifest Tag with package attribute"]
    B --> C["uses-permission Block"]
    B --> D["uses-feature Block"]
    B --> E["uses-sdk Block"]
    B --> F["application Tag"]

    C --> C1["INTERNET"]
    C --> C2["BLUETOOTH"]
    C --> C3["BLUETOOTH_ADMIN"]
    C --> C4["ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION"]

    D --> D1["android.hardware.bluetooth_le"]
    D --> D2["android.hardware.sensor.accelerometer"]

    F --> G["activity MainActivity"]
    F --> H["service MQTTService"]
    F --> I["receiver BootCompletedReceiver"]
    F --> J["provider SensorDataProvider"]
    F --> K["meta-data AWS Config"]

    G --> G1["intent-filter"]
    G1 --> G1A["action MAIN"]
    G1 --> G1B["category LAUNCHER"]

    H --> H1["exported false"]
    I --> I1["intent-filter"]
    I1 --> I1A["action BOOT_COMPLETED"]

    J --> J1["authorities sensor.data.provider"]
    J --> J2["grantUriPermissions true"]
```

### 4.2 Component Registration Flow Diagram

This diagram shows the **flow from APK installation to component registration** in the Android OS.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["User Taps Install"] --> B["PackageManager Reads APK"]
    B --> C["Parses AndroidManifest.xml"]
    C --> D{"Valid XML?"}
    D -- No --> E["Installation Failed: PARSE_ERROR"]
    D -- Yes --> F["Register Components in PackageManager"]
    F --> G["Index Intent Filters"]
    G --> H["Enforce Permissions"]
    H --> I["Display in Launcher"]
    I --> J["App Ready to Launch"]
```

### 4.3 Manifest Merger Process (Multi-Module Apps)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["App Module Manifest"] --> M["Manifest Merger Tool"]
    B["Library Module 1 Manifest"] --> M
    C["Library Module 2 Manifest"] --> M
    D["Build Variant Manifest"] --> M
    E["Build Type Manifest"] --> M
    M --> F["Merged AndroidManifest.xml"]
    F --> G["APK Packaging"]
```

*Explanation:* When your IoT project uses multiple Gradle modules (e.g., a `ble-library` module and a `mqtt-library` module), the Manifest Merger automatically combines all `<uses-permission>` and component declarations. Conflicts are resolved using `tools:node="merge"` or `tools:replace`.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

Define `AndroidManifest.xml`. List any **four mandatory elements** that must be present in every Android application's manifest file.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** `AndroidManifest.xml` is the root configuration file of an Android application written in XML format. It provides essential metadata about the app to the Android Operating System, including the package name, components, permissions, and SDK requirements.

**Four Mandatory Elements (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **`<manifest>` root tag** — The outermost XML element that wraps all other declarations.
2. **`package` attribute** — Provides a unique identifier for the app (e.g., `in.ktu.iotapp`).
3. **`<application>` tag** — Acts as the container for all app components (activities, services, receivers, providers).
4. **At least one `<activity>` declaration** — Every app must have a launcher activity marked with `MAIN` and `LAUNCHER` intent filter to be discoverable in the app drawer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]**

Differentiate between `<uses-permission>` and `<uses-feature>` in `AndroidManifest.xml` with one IoT-relevant example for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `<uses-permission>` | `<uses-feature>` |
|--------|---------------------|------------------|
| Purpose (1 Mark) | Requests **runtime/install-time access** to a protected system resource | Declares **hardware/software capability** required by the app |
| OS Behavior (1 Mark) | Used by Android's **permission system** to gate API calls | Used by **Google Play** to filter incompatible devices |
| IoT Example (1 Mark) | `android.permission.BLUETOOTH_SCAN` — required to scan for BLE devices | `android.hardware.bluetooth_le` — declares BLE hardware requirement |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the role of `AndroidManifest.xml` in an Android application. Discuss the significance of the `<intent-filter>` element with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete `AndroidManifest.xml` snippet for an IoT application that requires Internet access, Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) scanning, and reads accelerometer data. The app has one activity (`MainActivity`), one background service (`DataSyncService`), and a content provider (`SensorProvider`) with authority `in.ktu.iotapp.provider`. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Role of AndroidManifest.xml (3 Marks):**

The `AndroidManifest.xml` file is the **declarative configuration file** that serves as the **blueprint** of an Android application. It performs the following roles:

1. **Component Declaration:** Registers the four app components (Activity, Service, BroadcastReceiver, ContentProvider) so the Android system knows what classes to instantiate.
2. **Permission Management:** Declares the permissions the app requires to access protected APIs (camera, location, internet, BLE).
3. **Hardware/Software Requirements:** Specifies the minimum SDK level and hardware features (camera, GPS, BLE) the app needs.
4. **Intent Resolution:** Provides intent filters that allow the app to respond to implicit intents from other apps or the system.
5. **App Metadata:** Stores the app's icon, label, theme, version code, and package name.

**Significance of `<intent-filter>` (4 Marks):**

The `<intent-filter>` element is an **XML declaration nested inside a component** (usually an activity) that specifies the **types of Intents** the component is willing to receive. It enables:

- **Implicit Intent Resolution:** When another app sends an `Intent.ACTION_VIEW` with a URL, the system queries all components with matching intent filters.
- **App Entry Point:** The combination of `action="android.intent.action.MAIN"` and `category="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER"` makes an activity appear in the device's app drawer.
- **Deep Linking:** `<data android:scheme="https" android:host="iot.ktu.in" />` allows the app to handle specific URLs.
- **Action Customization:** Apps can define custom actions (e.g., `com.example.SCAN_BLE_DEVICE`) and let other apps trigger them.

**Example:**

```xml
<activity android:name=".MainActivity" android:exported="true">
    <intent-filter>
        <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
        <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
    </intent-filter>
</activity>
```

This declares `MainActivity` as the **entry point** of the app.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    package="in.ktu.iotapp">

    <!-- Permissions (2 Marks) -->
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET" />
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.BLUETOOTH_SCAN" />
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION" />

    <!-- Hardware Feature (1 Mark) -->
    <uses-feature
        android:name="android.hardware.bluetooth_le"
        android:required="true" />
    <uses-feature
        android:name="android.hardware.sensor.accelerometer"
        android:required="true" />

    <!-- Application (1 Mark) -->
    <application
        android:allowBackup="true"
        android:icon="@mipmap/ic_launcher"
        android:label="@string/app_name"
        android:theme="@style/Theme.IoTApp">

        <!-- MainActivity (1 Mark) -->
        <activity
            android:name=".MainActivity"
            android:exported="true">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
                <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
            </intent-filter>
        </activity>

        <!-- DataSyncService (1 Mark) -->
        <service
            android:name=".DataSyncService"
            android:exported="false" />

        <!-- SensorProvider (1 Mark) -->
        <provider
            android:name=".SensorProvider"
            android:authorities="in.ktu.iotapp.provider"
            android:exported="false"
            android:grantUriPermissions="true" />
    </application>
</manifest>
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample]

**(a)** Describe the **four primary components** of an Android application. For each component, explain how it is declared in `AndroidManifest.xml` with a one-line IoT use case. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Manifest Merger** process in Android. Why is it important for multi-module IoT projects? Illustrate with a block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Component (0.5 Mark) | Manifest Tag (1 Mark) | IoT Use Case (0.5 Mark) |
|----------------------|----------------------|-------------------------|
| **Activity** — Represents a single screen with a user interface. | `<activity android:name=".MainActivity" />` | A screen displaying real-time temperature readings from a BLE sensor. |
| **Service** — A component for long-running background operations without a UI. | `<service android:name=".DataSyncService" />` | A background service that maintains a persistent MQTT connection to an IoT broker. |
| **BroadcastReceiver** — Listens for system-wide or app-specific broadcast messages. | `<receiver android:name=".BootReceiver" />` | A receiver that restarts BLE scanning automatically after device reboot. |
| **ContentProvider** — Manages access to a structured set of data, enabling data sharing between apps. | `<provider android:authorities="in.ktu.iotapp.sensor" />` | A provider exposing the local SQLite sensor database to a companion smartwatch app. |

*(4 components × 1.75 marks = 7 marks distributed across description, tag, and use case)*

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (2 Marks):** The **Manifest Merger** is a Gradle build tool that automatically combines the `AndroidManifest.xml` files of the **app module** and all its **library dependencies** into a single, unified manifest before APK packaging.

**Process (3 Marks):**

1. The merger collects manifests from the **app module**, **library modules**, **build variants**, and **build types**.
2. It performs a **hierarchical merge**: app module declarations take **highest priority**.
3. **Conflict resolution** uses `tools:node="merge"`, `tools:replace`, or `tools:remove` attributes.
4. The merged result is validated against the **Android schema** and packaged into the APK.

**Importance for IoT Multi-Module Projects (2 Marks):**

- A typical IoT app may have separate Gradle modules for **BLE**, **Wi-Fi provisioning**, **MQTT**, and **Cloud SDKs** (e.g., AWS IoT).
- Each library brings its own `<uses-permission>` and `<uses-feature>` declarations.
- The Manifest Merger ensures that all required permissions (e.g., `BLUETOOTH_CONNECT`, `INTERNET`) are consolidated into the final APK.
- Without the merger, the developer would have to **manually duplicate** every permission in the app module, which is error-prone.

**Block Diagram (already covered in Section 4.3 above — refer to it for the visual schematic).**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Missing `android:exported` attribute on API 31+** (worth **2 marks** deduction). If your `<activity>` has an `<intent-filter>`, the OS will refuse to install the APK without an explicit `exported` declaration.
> 2. **Forgetting the `package` attribute on `<manifest>`** — This is worth **1 mark** and is a frequent oversight in the hurry of writing XML.
> 3. **Wrong authority for `<provider>`** — The `android:authorities` value must be **globally unique**. Two providers in the same app cannot share the same authority.
> 4. **Placing `<uses-permission>` inside `<application>`** — Permissions are **siblings** of `<application>`, not children. This is a structural mistake worth **1 mark**.
> 5. **Not closing tags properly** — KTU digital evaluation is **strict**; unclosed tags get **0** for the entire manifest.
> 6. **Confusing `<uses-sdk>` with `build.gradle`** — While modern Android development puts SDK versions in `build.gradle`, the KTU 2024 syllabus **explicitly tests** `<uses-sdk>` in the manifest. Include it for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for AndroidManifest.xml:**

- **Root Element:** Always `<manifest>` with `xmlns:android` and `package` attributes.
- **Four Components:** `<activity>`, `<service>`, `<receiver>`, `<provider>` — all declared inside `<application>`.
- **Intent Filters:** Nested inside components. The `MAIN` + `LAUNCHER` pair is mandatory for at least one activity.
- **Permissions:** Declared at the top level using `<uses-permission>`. Dangerous permissions require runtime requests in code (separate from manifest).
- **Hardware Features:** `<uses-feature android:required="true" />` controls Play Store visibility.
- **SDK Levels:** `<uses-sdk>` with `minSdkVersion`, `targetSdkVersion`, `maxSdkVersion` (or moved to `build.gradle`).
- **Exported Flag:** **Mandatory on API 31+** for any component with an intent filter. Default behavior changed in Android 12.
- **Manifest Merger:** Combines manifests from all modules in the build pipeline.
- **Common Permissions for IoT:** `INTERNET`, `BLUETOOTH`, `BLUETOOTH_SCAN`, `BLUETOOTH_CONNECT`, `ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION`, `ACCESS_NETWORK_STATE`.
- **Common Features for IoT:** `bluetooth_le`, `sensor.accelerometer`, `sensor.gyroscope`, `location.gps`, `wifi`.
- **Validation:** The OS validates the manifest during **installation**, not at runtime — a malformed manifest prevents installation entirely.
- **File Location:** `app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml` — never relocate it.
- **One Manifest Per Module:** Each Gradle module can have its own manifest; the merger consolidates them.
- **Metadata Storage:** Use `<meta-data>` for key-value pairs (e.g., AWS IoT endpoint, MQTT broker URL).
- **Backup & Data Extraction Rules:** `android:allowBackup`, `android:dataExtractionRules` are important for data security.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uses-Permission and Uses-SDK
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Uses-Permission and Uses-SDK # 📘 MODULE 2 — Uses-Permission and Uses-SDK ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 `` — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):** > The `` element is a child of the `` element in the **AndroidManifest.xml** file. It is a declarative request made by an Android application to the Android Operating System (OS), seeking authorization to access restricted resources, hardware features, or system services that are protected by Android's permission-based security model. In Android, every sensitive operation — accessing the Internet, reading contacts, using the camera, reading GPS, connecting to Bluetooth, accessing the file system outside the app's sandbox — is **gated by a permission**. If the app does not declare the permission, the OS will throw a `SecurityException` at runtime. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Board Note:** Android's permission system is built on the principle of **least privilege**. The app must explicitly request each permission it needs. Permissions are enforced by the Linux kernel (UID/GID isolation) and the Android middleware. --- ### 1.2 `` — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):** > The `` element declares the **minimum, target, and maximum API levels** that the application is compatible with. It informs the Android build tools, the Play Store, and the device's package manager about which Android versions (versions are mapped to integer API levels) the app supports. - **minSdkVersion** → The lowest API level on which the app can run. - **targetSdkVersion** → The API level the app was **designed and tested** against. - **maxSdkVersion** → *(Deprecated for Play Store uploads)* The highest API level the app is designed to support. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Since Android 10 (API 29) and especially with the 2024 scheme, `targetSdkVersion` is **mandatory** for Play Store publishing. Apps targeting lower API levels are rejected. --- ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition #### 🏨 The "Hotel Key Card" Analogy for `` Imagine checking into a hotel: - Your room is the **app's sandbox** (a private, isolated area). - The **swimming pool, gym, business center, and minibar** are **restricted Android resources** (Internet, Camera, GPS, Storage). - The hotel's front desk issues you **specific key cards** for each facility — these key cards are **permissions**. - `` is essentially your **reservation request form** at check-in, telling the front desk (the Android OS) **which key cards (permissions)** you want in advance. Without the correct key card → you get **denied access** (SecurityException at runtime). #### 🏢 The "Building Code Compliance" Analogy for `` Think of `` as a **building's construction code** label: - `minSdkVersion` → "This building has been **inspected to be safe from floor 1 upwards**." (Don't try to enter from floor 0 — it doesn't exist.) - `targetSdkVersion` → "The architects **designed and verified** the building for the regulations of **year X**." (Behavior is optimized for that year.) - `maxSdkVersion` → "Construction has been **certified up to year Y**." (After year Y, regulations may have changed.) The OS uses this "label" to decide whether to **install the app** on a particular device. --- ### 1.4 Standard Metric Mapping (KTU Reference Table) | Android Version | API Level | Common Code Name | |---|---|---| | Android 14 | **34** | Upside Down Cake | | Android 13 | **33** | Tiramisu | | Android 12 | **31 / 32** | Snow Cone / Sv2 | | Android 11 | **30** | Red Velvet Cake | | Android 10 | **29** | Q (deprecated target) | | Android 9 | **28** | Pie | | Android 8 | **26 / 27** | Oreo | | Android 7 | **24 / 25** | Nougat | | Android 6 | **23** | Marshmallow | > **Bold Constant of the Day:** API level 23 (Android 6.0 Marshmallow) — this is the **first version that introduced runtime permission requests** instead of install-time permissions. Any IoT KTU exam question on "dangerous permissions" will refer back to this version. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of `` ### 2.1.1 Syntax (Strict KTU Board Format) ```xml ... ``` ### 2.1.2 Logical Breakdown - **`android:name` attribute** → The fully-qualified permission name (string constant defined by the Android platform or by a third-party app). - **Multiple `` tags** → One tag per permission requested. Order does not matter. - **Placement** → Must be a **direct child of ``**, NOT inside ``. - **Custom permissions** → Apps can define their own permissions using `` element, then other apps request them via ``. ### 2.1.3 Permission Protection Levels (KTU High-Yield) Android classifies permissions into **4 protection levels** (this is a frequent 3-mark question): | Protection Level | Granted When | Examples | Revocable by User? | |---|---|---|---| | **normal** | Install time (auto-granted) | `INTERNET`, `BLUETOOTH_ADMIN`, `SET_WALLPAPER` | No | | **dangerous** | Runtime (user prompt required) | `CAMERA`, `RECORD_AUDIO`, `ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION`, `READ_CONTACTS` | Yes (Settings) | | **signature** | Install time, only if signed with same key | Custom permissions between apps of same developer | No | | **signatureOrSystem** | Signature OR system app | System-level permissions | No | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Mandate:** All permissions marked `dangerous` introduced in API 23+ **must** be requested at runtime using `ActivityCompat.requestPermissions()` and handled in `onRequestPermissionsResult()`. Declaring them in the manifest alone is insufficient. --- ## 2.2 Anatomy of `` ### 2.2.1 Syntax (Strict KTU Board Format) ```xml ``` ### 2.2.2 Logical Breakdown (Why and How) - **`minSdkVersion`** — *Why?* Prevents installation on devices running older Android that lack required APIs. *How?* Package Manager Service (PMS) checks this value against the device's API level during installation. - **`targetSdkVersion`** — *Why?* Tells the OS to enable **forward-compatibility behaviors**. *How?* Each new Android release may change default behaviors (scoped storage, background restrictions, etc.); setting `targetSdkVersion` explicitly opts in to the new behaviors. - **`maxSdkVersion`** — *Why?* Stops the app from being installed on devices beyond a known compatible level. *How?* PMS rejects installation if device API > maxSdkVersion. ### 2.2.3 How the OS Uses `` — Decision Flow 1. **App installation (Play Store or sideload)** — PackageManager reads the manifest. 2. **Check 1:** `deviceSdk >= minSdkVersion`? → If NO, reject install. 3. **Check 2:** `deviceSdk <= maxSdkVersion`? → If NO, reject install. 4. **Compatibility Shims:** The OS applies backward-compatibility shims for any API the app calls that was introduced **after** `minSdkVersion`. Example: An app with `minSdkVersion=24` calling a `requireApi30()` method on Android 8 will crash unless guarded with `Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= 30` checks. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In IoT app development, `` is critical because IoT hardware (sensors, BLE chips) often runs on a heterogeneous mix of devices — some on Android 7, some on Android 14.
