### Step 2 — Select Hardware Profile (Chassis)
The **Device Picker** window lists hardware profiles grouped by category:

$$
\text{Category} \in \{\text{Phone, Tablet, Wear OS, TV, Automotive}\}
$$

You can either **pick a stock profile** (e.g., Pixel 7, Nexus 5X) or click **"New Hardware Profile"** to define a custom chassis. For the custom dialog, fields include:
- Device Name (alphanumeric)
- Screen size (inches): typical $3.2$ to $13.0$
- Resolution (px × px): e.g., $1080 \times 2400$
- Round screen (Wear): true/false
- Supported sensors: checkbox list

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students confuse *Screen Size* (diagonal, inches) with *Resolution* (px × px). The diagonal is computed via:
> $$\text{Diagonal (in)} = \sqrt{\frac{w_{px}^2 + h_{px}^2}{\text{dpi}^2}}$$
> where $w_{px}, h_{px}$ are width/height in pixels and dpi is the dots-per-inch.

### Step 3 — Select System Image
A **System Image** is chosen next. The list is filtered by:
- **Recommended tab** (✓ icon) — Google-tested images.
- **x86 Images** tab — fastest on Intel/AMD hosts.
- **ARM Images** tab — required when testing ARM-only native libraries.
- **Other Images** tab — beta/canary/Atd (Automated Test Device) images.

Each row shows: **API Level → Version → ABI → Target (Google Play / Google APIs / AOSP)**.

### Step 4 — Verify Configuration
The **AVD Configuration dialog** displays the *Verify Configuration* screen with the full set of chosen and overridable properties. Click **"Show Advanced Settings"** to edit:

| Property | Default | Editable |
|---|---|---|
| RAM | 1536 MB | ✓ |
| VM Heap | 192 MB | ✓ |
| Internal Storage | 8192 MB | ✓ |
| SD Card | None | ✓ |
| Graphics | Automatic | Hardware / Software / Automatic |
| Multi-Core CPU | 4 | 1 – 8 |
| Boot Animation | On | On / Off |
| Front Camera | None | Webcam0 / Emulated / None |
| Back Camera | None | Webcam0 / Emulated / None |
| GPS | ✓ | ✓ |
| Accelerometer | ✓ | ✓ |
| Network Speed | Full | Full / GSM / HSCSD / GPRS / EDGE / UMTS / HSDPA |
| Network Latency | None | None / GPRS / EDGE / UMTS |

### Step 5 — Name and Create
A unique AVD name (no spaces, no special characters) is provided. KTU exam examples:
- `Pixel_7_API_34`
- `IoT_Tablet_API_33`

Click **Finish** → AVD is created. AVD metadata is stored in:
`~/.android/avd/<avd_name>.avd/config.ini`

### Step 6 — Boot the AVD
- **From Studio:** Click ▶ next to the AVD.
- **From CLI:** `emulator -avd Pixel_7_API_34 -no-snapshot-load`
- **Headless CI:** `emulator -avd Pixel_7_API_34 -no-window -no-audio -no-boot-anim &`

### Step 7 — Interact with the Booted Emulator
- ADB auto-connects to `emulator-5554`.
- Drag-drop an APK onto the emulator window to install.
- Or from terminal: `adb install app-debug.apk`.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Computing AVD Memory Footprint (Examiner-Favorite Problem)

> [!TIP]
> **Sample KTU-style question:** *"Calculate the total RAM reserved by 3 AVDs running concurrently, each configured with 2 GB RAM, 256 MB VM heap, and 8 GB internal storage. How much disk space is consumed if snapshots are enabled at 1.5 GB per AVD?"*

### Given Data
$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{AVD}} &= 3 \\
\text{RAM per AVD} &= 2 \text{ GB} = 2048 \text{ MB} \\
\text{VM Heap per AVD} &= 256 \text{ MB} \\
\text{Internal Storage per AVD} &= 8 \text{ GB} \\
\text{Snapshot per AVD} &= 1.5 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step (a) — Total RAM Consumed
The VM heap is a **subset** of the configured RAM, not additive. So:

$$
\text{RAM}_{\text{per AVD}} = \text{User RAM} + \text{Kernel Reserved}
$$
$$
\text{RAM}_{\text{per AVD}} = 2048 \text{ MB} \;(\text{heap already inside})
$$

For 3 AVDs:

$$
\text{RAM}_{\text{total}} = n_{\text{AVD}} \times \text{RAM}_{\text{per AVD}}
$$
$$
\text{RAM}_{\text{total}} = 3 \times 2048 \text{ MB} = 6144 \text{ MB} \approx 6 \text{ GB}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The host OS also needs ~2–4 GB, so total system RAM required ≈ **10 GB** for smooth concurrent emulation.

### Step (b) — Total Disk Consumed
Internal storage and snapshots are **independent** of each other and of RAM.

$$
\text{Disk}_{\text{per AVD}} = \text{Internal Storage} + \text{Snapshot Size}
$$
$$
\text{Disk}_{\text{per AVD}} = 8 \text{ GB} + 1.5 \text{ GB} = 9.5 \text{ GB}
$$

For 3 AVDs:

$$
\text{Disk}_{\text{total}} = 3 \times 9.5 \text{ GB} = 28.5 \text{ GB}
$$

Plus system images:
$$
\text{Disk}_{\text{system images}} \approx 3 \times 1.2 \text{ GB (avg)} = 3.6 \text{ GB}
$$

Grand total:
$$
\text{Disk}_{\text{grand total}} = 28.5 + 3.6 = 32.1 \text{ GB}
$$

> **Final Answer:** 3 concurrent AVDs need ~**6 GB RAM** and ~**32.1 GB disk** on the host.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Emulator vs. Physical Device vs. Genymotion

This comparison matrix is the **most-asked 14-mark question** in KTU Module 2.

| Feature | Android Studio Emulator | Physical Device | Genymotion (3rd-party) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | Free | $200 – $1200 | Free (Personal) / Paid |
| Speed | Slow without HAXM/WHPX | Native | Fast (uses VirtualBox) |
| Realism | High (90%) | 100% | Medium (80%) |
| Sensor Accuracy | Synthetic (perfect) | Real | Synthetic |
| Battery Drain Test | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Network Latency Simulation | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Multi-Device Matrix | ✓ (unlimited AVDs) | ✗ (limited devices) | ✓ |
| Bluetooth Low Energy | Limited | Full | Limited |
| Camera | Emulated scene | Real | Emulated |
| Suitable for IoT Apps | ✓ (MQTT, Wi-Fi) | ✓✓ (best) | ✓ |
| CI/CD Integration | ✓ (headless mode) | ✗ | ✓ (plugin) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: ADB Command Sequence to Launch & Test an IoT App

A complete exam-style answer for *"Demonstrate ADB commands to install and launch an IoT app on an AVD"*.

```text
# Step 1: List available AVDs
$ emulator -list-avds
Pixel_7_API_34
IoT_Tablet_API_33

# Step 2: Boot AVD headless
$ emulator -avd Pixel_7_API_34 -no-window -no-snapshot-load &

# Step 3: Verify ADB connection
$ adb devices
emulator-5554   device

# Step 4: Install the IoT APK
$ adb install -r app-debug.apk
Success

# Step 5: Launch the main activity
$ adb shell am start -n com.example.iot/.MainActivity
Starting: Intent { cmp=com.example.iot/.MainActivity }

# Step 6: Capture logs (MQTT publish confirmation)
$ adb logcat -s MQTT:V

# Step 7: Push a config file to the AVD's external storage
$ adb push mqtt_config.json /sdcard/Download/

# Step 8: Uninstall (cleanup)
$ adb uninstall com.example.iot
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When writing ADB commands, always precede them with `adb` (not `>>`) and use shell-escaped paths on Windows.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Android Emulator Architecture (Top-to-Bottom)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appA[IoT Mobile App] --> guestA[Guest Android OS]
    guestA --> sysImg[Android System Image API 34]
    sysImg --> qemuLayer[QEMU Machine Emulator Engine]
    qemuLayer --> accelLayer[Hardware Accelerator Layer]
    accelLayer --> hostOS[Host OS Windows Linux macOS]
    hostOS --> hostHW[Host Hardware CPU RAM Disk]

    cfgA[AVD config.ini] --> qemuLayer
    adbA[ADB Daemon Port 5555] --> guestA
    consA[Emulator Console Port 5554] --> qemuLayer
    snapA[Snapshot File] --> qemuLayer

    subgraph "Acceleration Backends"
        haxmA[Intel HAXM]
        whpxA[Windows Hypervisor Platform]
        kvmA[Linux KVM]
        hvfA[macOS Hypervisor Framework]
    end

    accelLayer --> haxmA
    accelLayer --> whpxA
    accelLayer --> kvmA
    accelLayer --> hvfA
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — AVD Lifecycle (Creation to Deletion)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Open Device Manager] --> s2[Select Hardware Profile]
    s2 --> s3{Profile OK?}
    s3 -- no --> s2
    s3 -- yes --> s4[Choose System Image API + ABI]
    s4 --> s5[Verify Configuration]
    s5 --> s6[Name and Save AVD]
    s6 --> s7[AVD Stored in userhome.androidavd]
    s7 --> s8[Boot via Studio or CLI]
    s8 --> s9[Emulator Window Appears]
    s9 --> s10[Install APK via ADB]
    s10 --> s11{More Testing?}
    s11 -- yes --> s10
    s11 -- no --> s12[Save Snapshot optional]
    s12 --> s13[Shutdown Emulator]
    s13 --> s14{Delete AVD?}
    s14 -- yes --> s15[Wipe AVD Directory]
    s14 -- no --> s16[Keep for Future Use]
```

## 4.3 Component Diagram — Three Pillars of an AVD

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph avd["AVD Android Virtual Device"]
        hw["Hardware Profile: Screen RAM CPU Sensors"]
        sys["System Image: Android Version API Level ABI"]
        stor["Storage + Advanced: Internal SD VM Heap GPU Mode"]
    end

    hw --> combined["AVD Manifest config.ini"]
    sys --> combined
    stor --> combined

    combined --> emu["Emulator Engine: QEMU + Hypervisor"]
    emu --> boot["Booted Virtual Phone on Host Screen"]
```

## 4.4 Sequence Diagram — App Deployment to AVD via ADB

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Developer
    participant AS as Android Studio
    participant AVD as AVD Emulator
    participant ADB as ADB Daemon
    participant Sys as Virtual Android OS

    Dev->>AS: Click Run
    AS->>AVD: Start Emulator if not running
    AVD->>Sys: Boot Android System Image
    Sys-->>AVD: Boot complete
    AS->>ADB: adb install app-debug.apk
    ADB->>Sys: Push APK to data app
    Sys-->>ADB: Install success
    AS->>ADB: adb shell am start MainActivity
    ADB->>Sys: Launch Intent
    Sys-->>Dev: App UI appears on emulator window
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree — Which Accelerator Should I Enable?

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[Check Host CPU Brand] --> intel{Intel CPU?}
    intel -- yes --> win1{Host OS is Windows?}
    win1 -- yes --> whpx1[Use WHPX if Win10 Pro or enable HAXM]
    win1 -- no --> lin1{Host OS is Linux?}
    lin1 -- yes --> kvm1[Use KVM]
    lin1 -- no --> mac1[Use HAXM or HVF]

    intel -- no --> amd{AMD CPU?}
    amd -- yes --> win2{Host OS is Windows?}
    win2 -- yes --> whpx2[Use WHPX]
    win2 -- no --> lin2[Use KVM on Linux]
    amd -- no --> arm{Apple Silicon M1 M2 M3?}
    arm -- yes --> hvf2[Use HVF with arm64 image]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams comply with the alpha-numeric node ID rule (e.g., `haxmA`, `whpxA`, `kvmA`, `hvfA`) and avoid reserved keywords as node names.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECGT415]**
**Q: Define Android Virtual Device (AVD). List any two advantages of using an emulator over a physical device for IoT app testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
An **Android Virtual Device (AVD)** is a configuration profile that defines the characteristics of an emulated Android device — including system image (Android version + ABI), hardware profile (screen, RAM, CPU, sensors), and storage options. The AVD is interpreted by the Android Emulator (built on QEMU) to spin up a virtual phone on the developer's host machine.

**Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
1. **Cost-effective:** No need to purchase multiple physical devices to test across screen sizes and Android versions.
2. **Reproducible:** AVD configurations can be version-controlled and shared across the team, ensuring every developer tests against identical virtual hardware.
3. **Automation-friendly:** Emulators can run in headless mode on CI/CD servers, enabling large-scale matrix testing.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECGT415]**
**Q: What is the role of QEMU in the Android Emulator architecture? Name the accelerator technologies supported on Intel and AMD hosts.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**QEMU Role (1.5 marks):**
QEMU (Quick Emulator) is the **core machine emulator** at the heart of the Android Emulator. It emulates the CPU, memory, disk, and peripheral hardware of a virtual phone motherboard and translates guest ARM/x86 instructions to host instructions. It pairs with a hypervisor to achieve near-native execution speed.

**Accelerators (1.5 marks):**
- **Intel hosts:** Intel HAXM (Hardware Accelerated Execution Manager) — now legacy, replaced by **WHPX** (Windows Hypervisor Platform).
- **AMD hosts:** **WHPX** on Windows 10+; **KVM** on Linux.
- **Apple Silicon:** **HVF** (Hypervisor.framework) on macOS.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Regulation:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PECGT415]**

**A (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the layered architecture of the Android Emulator. **(7 marks)**
**A (b)** Compare the Android Studio Emulator with a physical device for IoT app development across at least six parameters. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — A (a): Layered Architecture of the Android Emulator (7 marks)

The Android Emulator is organized as a **layered stack** that translates Android-app instructions into host-machine operations. From top to bottom, the layers are:

1. **Application Layer (top)** — The IoT app (e.g., MQTT dashboard) running as an Android APK. It calls Android framework APIs without knowing it is on a virtual device. **(0.5 mark)**

2. **Guest Android OS** — The full Android system image containing the framework, system services, and kernel. It believes it is running on real phone hardware. **(0.5 mark)**

3. **AVD Configuration Layer** — Reads `config.ini` from the AVD directory and overlays hardware properties (screen, RAM, sensors) onto the system image at boot. **(0.5 mark)**

4. **QEMU Engine** — The machine emulator that emulates the phone's CPU, memory map, and peripherals. It uses **TCG (Tiny Code Generator)** to translate guest instructions to host instructions on the fly. **(1 mark)**

5. **Hardware Accelerator Layer** — Sits beneath QEMU. On Intel hosts it is **HAXM** or **WHPX**; on AMD hosts it is **WHPX** (Windows) or **KVM** (Linux); on Apple Silicon it is **HVF**. This layer bypasses TCG by executing guest instructions directly on the host CPU, achieving **near-native speed**. **(1 mark)**

6. **Host Operating System** — Windows / macOS / Linux, which schedules emulator processes and provides I/O. **(0.5 mark)**

7. **Host Hardware (bottom)** — The physical CPU, RAM, disk, and GPU of the developer's laptop. **(0.5 mark)**

8. **Control Interfaces (sidecar):**
   - **ADB Daemon** on port **5555** for app install / log capture.
   - **Emulator Console** on port **5554** for telnet-style commands (e.g., `geo fix`, `power ac off`).
   - **Snapshot subsystem** for fast boot/rollback. **(0.5 mark)**

> **Diagram (must be drawn on answer sheet, 1 mark):** Refer to the **Block Diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes. Redraw it neatly on the answer booklet.

#### Model Solution — A (b): Emulator vs. Physical Device Comparison (7 marks)

| Parameter | Android Studio Emulator | Physical Device |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Free — bundled with Android Studio. | $200 – $1200 per device, multiplied across screen-size matrix. |
| **Performance** | Slower than physical; can be near-native with HAXM/WHPX. | Native CPU/GPU speed. |
| **Sensor Realism** | Synthetic — values are mathematically generated; perfect, but unrealistic edge cases. | Real-world noise, drift, and failure modes. |
| **Network Simulation** | Configurable latency (GPRS, EDGE, 3G, 4G) and packet loss. | Real network behavior only. |
| **Battery & Thermal** | No battery drain; emulator runs on host power. | Real battery consumption testing possible. |
| **Hardware APIs** | Most APIs (camera, GPS, accelerometer) are emulated. Bluetooth LE and NFC are limited. | Full support for all hardware. |
| **Scalability & Automation** | Can run many AVDs concurrently; ideal for CI/CD and matrix testing. | Limited by physical device count. |
| **IoT BLE/802.15.4 Testing** | Limited; HCI commands can be injected via ADB. | Native, full-stack BLE/Mesh/Thread support. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For *early-stage UI testing, cross-device matrix testing, and CI/CD automation*, the emulator is superior. For *final validation, BLE pairing, NFC tag reading, and real-world sensor edge cases*, a physical device is mandatory. The two are **complementary, not substitutable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often claim the emulator is "always slower than a physical device." Half-credit. State the *qualifier*: only **without** HAXM/WHPX/HVF acceleration. With acceleration, the gap narrows to 1.2–1.5× for most workloads.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PECGT415]**

**B (a)** List and explain the steps to create an AVD in Android Studio. Discuss the role of system images and ABIs in the process. **(7 marks)**
**B (b)** Three AVDs are created for an IoT project: each has 2 GB RAM, 256 MB VM heap, 8 GB internal storage, and snapshots of 1.5 GB. Calculate the total RAM and disk footprint on the host. Comment on the practical implications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — B (a): AVD Creation Steps + System Image / ABI Role (7 marks)

**Steps to Create an AVD (4.5 marks):**

1. **Open Device Manager:** `Tools → Device Manager → Create Device`. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Select Hardware Profile:** Choose Phone/Tablet/Wear; pick stock profile or define custom (screen size, resolution, sensors). **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Select System Image:** Pick API level (e.g., 34 for Android 14) and ABI. Recommended tab is preferred. **(1 mark)**
4. **Verify Configuration:** Open *Advanced Settings* to tune RAM, VM heap, internal storage, GPU mode, multi-core CPU. **(1 mark)**
5. **Name and Finish:** Provide a unique AVD name (e.g., `IoT_Phone_API34`). The AVD is stored at `~/.android/avd/<name>.avd/config.ini`. **(0.5 mark)**
6. **Boot:** Click ▶ in Device Manager, or run `emulator -avd <name>` from CLI. **(0.5 mark)**
7. **Install & Test:** Drag-drop APK or use `adb install`. **(0.5 mark)**

**Role of System Images and ABIs (2.5 marks):**

- **System Image** is a **disk image** containing a complete Android OS — kernel, framework, system apps — for a given API level. It is the *software identity* of the AVD: choosing API 34 means the AVD runs Android 14.
- **ABI (Application Binary Interface)** specifies the **CPU instruction set** the system image is compiled for:
  - `armeabi-v7a` → 32-bit ARM (older phones).
  - `arm64-v8a` → 64-bit ARM (modern phones, recommended for Apple Silicon hosts).
  - `x86` → 32-bit Intel (legacy).
  - `x86_64` → 64-bit Intel (fastest on Intel/AMD hosts with HAXM/WHPX).
- **Why does ABI matter?** Native libraries in your app (e.g., `libmqtt.so`, `libtensorflowlite.so`) are compiled for a specific ABI. If the AVD's ABI doesn't match the app's native libs, installation will fail with `INSTALL_FAILED_NO_MATCHING_ABIS`. **Always match the AVD ABI to the app's native-library ABIs.**

#### Model Solution — B (b): Memory Footprint Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
$$
n_{\text{AVD}} = 3, \quad \text{RAM} = 2\text{ GB}, \quad \text{VM Heap} = 256\text{ MB}, \quad \text{Storage} = 8\text{ GB}, \quad \text{Snapshot} = 1.5\text{ GB}
$$

**Step 1 — Total RAM (1.5 marks):**
$$
\text{RAM}_{\text{per AVD}} = 2 \text{ GB} = 2048 \text{ MB}
$$
$$
\text{RAM}_{\text{total}} = 3 \times 2048 = 6144 \text{ MB} \approx 6 \text{ GB}
$$
*(The 256 MB VM heap is **included within** the 2 GB configured RAM and is not additive.)*

**Step 2 — Total Disk (2 marks):**
$$
\text{Disk}_{\text{per AVD}} = 8 \text{ GB (storage)} + 1.5 \text{ GB (snapshot)} = 9.5 \text{ GB}
$$
$$
\text{Disk}_{\text{AVDs}} = 3 \times 9.5 = 28.5 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 3 — System Image Overhead (0.5 mark):**
$$
\text{System Images} \approx 3 \times 1.2 \text{ GB} = 3.6 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 4 — Grand Total (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Disk}_{\text{grand}} = 28.5 + 3.6 = 32.1 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 5 — Practical Implications (2 marks):**
- **RAM:** 6 GB for AVDs + ~3 GB for host OS = **~9 GB system RAM required**. Laptops with 8 GB RAM will struggle; **16 GB recommended** for parallel IoT testing.
- **Disk:** 32 GB is significant on a 256 GB SSD. Use **smaller storage (2 GB) for throwaway AVDs** and **delete snapshots** for AVDs not in active use.
- **Concurrency:** Running all 3 AVDs simultaneously will throttle the host CPU; consider **sequential testing** or use **Genymotion Cloud** for parallel matrix runs.
- **CI/CD:** In a Jenkins/GitHub Actions runner, allocate a dedicated VM with ≥16 GB RAM and ≥50 GB disk to host the AVD matrix.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** A common error is **adding the VM heap to the RAM** when computing total memory. Remember: **VM heap is a *partition* of the configured RAM**, not an additional allocation. Total AVD RAM = configured RAM. (Examiner will deduct 1 mark for this mistake.)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this section — it is your last-minute revision sheet for the KTU exam.**

- ✅ **AVD = configuration profile.** **Emulator = the QEMU-based software that runs the AVD.**
- ✅ An AVD is defined by **3 orthogonal dimensions:** Hardware Profile + System Image + Storage/Advanced Settings.
- ✅ **QEMU** is the core machine emulator. **HAXM / WHPX / KVM / HVF** are the hardware accelerators.
- ✅ **Emulator Console port = 5554** (odd AVDs) / 5556 (even AVDs). **ADB port = console + 1** (i.e., 5555, 5557).
- ✅ **Default AVD RAM = 1536 MB**; **Default VM Heap = 192 MB**; **Default Internal Storage = 8192 MB**.
- ✅ **ABIs to remember:** `armeabi-v7a` (32-bit ARM), `arm64-v8a` (64-bit ARM), `x86`, `x86_64`.
- ✅ **First cold boot = 30–90 s**; **Snapshot resume = 2–5 s** — a major speed optimization.
- ✅ **Headless command:** `emulator -avd <name> -no-window -no-audio -no-snapshot-load` for CI/CD.
- ✅ **VM heap ⊂ configured RAM** — never add them when computing total memory footprint.
- ✅ **Disk footprint = Internal Storage + Snapshot + System Image**; per AVD, plan ~10–12 GB.
- ✅ **Emulator ≠ Physical Device:** Emulator wins for *matrix testing + automation*; physical wins for *BLE/NFC + battery + sensor noise*.
- ✅ **Storage path:** `~/.android/avd/<name>.avd/config.ini` on the host.
- ✅ **IoT exam hook:** Always mention **MQTT testing + sensor injection via Extended Controls + headless CI runs** to score bonus marks.
- ✅ **API level vs. Android version mapping (must memorize for viva):** API 30 → Android 11, API 31 → 12, API 33 → 13, API 34 → 14.
- ✅ **Graphics rendering modes:** Automatic (default) / Hardware (uses host GPU — fast) / Software (SwiftShader — slow but compatible).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic UI Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic UI Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Basic UI Design** in the context of **Mobile App Development for IoT** refers to the systematic process of creating the **graphical user interface (GUI)** of an Android application using a hierarchical tree of **View** and **ViewGroup** objects, declared primarily through **XML layout files** stored in the `res/layout/` directory. It encompasses the selection of appropriate **layout managers**, **UI widgets**, **Material Design components**, and **responsive design strategies** to ensure that the application interface is intuitive, accessible, and effective at presenting IoT data and controlling connected devices.

In Android terminology, every UI element on the screen is either a **`View`** (a leaf node such as a button, text, or image) or a **`ViewGroup`** (a container that holds and arranges other Views and ViewGroups). The combination forms a **View Hierarchy** — a tree structure rooted at the Activity's content view.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Empty House and Furniture":**
> Imagine you are moving into an empty house (the **Activity / Screen**). Before you can live in it, you need:
>
> 1. **A blueprint** of the house (the **XML Layout File**) — it tells you where the doors, windows, and walls go.
> 2. **Rooms** to organize things (the **ViewGroups / Layouts** like `LinearLayout`, `RelativeLayout`, `ConstraintLayout`).
> 3. **Furniture and appliances** (the **Views / Widgets** like `TextView`, `Button`, `ImageView`, `EditText`).
> 4. **A decorator** who places everything according to the blueprint at runtime (the **LayoutInflater**).
> 5. **A design language** ensuring the house looks modern and consistent (Google's **Material Design**).
>
> In an **IoT app**, the UI is the **control panel** of the smart home — the sliders for fan speed, toggles for lights, and gauges for temperature. A bad design means the user cannot switch on the light; a good design means the user does it in one tap without thinking.

## 1.3 Key UI Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **View** | Basic building block; rectangular area on screen that handles drawing and events. |
| **ViewGroup** | Subclass of `View`; acts as a container for child Views. |
| **Widget** | Pre-built interactive View (e.g., `Button`, `Switch`, `SeekBar`). |
| **Layout** | A `ViewGroup` that defines the visual structure of other Views. |
| **XML Layout** | Declarative file (`*.xml`) under `res/layout/` describing UI structure. |
| **Material Design** | Google's design system (typography, color, motion, components) — version **Material 3** is current. |
| **dp (Density-independent Pixel)** | Standard UI unit; **1 dp = 1 px on a 160 dpi screen**. |
| **sp (Scale-independent Pixel)** | Font-size unit; respects user accessibility settings. |
| **DPI / Density** | Pixels per inch; Android buckets: **mdpi (160)**, **hdpi (240)**, **xhdpi (320)**, **xxhdpi (480)**, **xxxhdpi (640)**. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Android UI Architecture (Layered View)

Android UI is built in **three conceptual layers**:

1. **Activity / Fragment Layer (Java/Kotlin)** — Acts as the controller. Calls `setContentView(R.layout.filename)` to inflate the XML layout into the screen.
2. **Layout XML Layer (`res/layout/*.xml`)** — Declares what Views exist, their IDs, attributes, and positions.
3. **Resource Layer (`res/values/*.xml`)** — Holds colors, strings, dimensions, styles, and themes — **separated from layout** for internationalization and theme consistency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule (KTU favourite question):** *Separate your concerns — never hard-code strings, colors, or dimensions inside the layout XML.* Use `@string/`, `@color/`, `@dimen/` references.

## 2.2 View Hierarchy — The "Tree of UI"

When Android renders the screen, it walks the **View tree** and performs three passes per node:

- **Measure pass** — Parent asks each child: *"How big do you want to be?"* Each child reports its `MeasureSpec` (mode + size).
- **Layout pass** — Parent places each child at the correct `(left, top, right, bottom)` coordinates.
- **Draw pass** — Each `View.onDraw(Canvas)` is invoked from root downward.

```
Activity
   └── DecorView (PhoneWindow's root FrameLayout)
        └── ContentFrame (android.R.id.content)
             └── Your Root ViewGroup (e.g., ConstraintLayout)
                  ├── View 1 (e.g., TextView)
                  ├── View 2 (e.g., Button)
                  └── ViewGroup 3 (e.g., LinearLayout)
                       ├── Child A
                       └── Child B
```

## 2.3 Core Layout Managers

### 2.3.1 LinearLayout
Arranges children in a **single horizontal or vertical row**. Uses `android:orientation`, `layout_weight`, and `gravity`.

### 2.3.2 RelativeLayout (Legacy)
Positions children **relative to parent or sibling** via attributes like `android:layout_below`, `android:layout_toRightOf`.

### 2.3.3 ConstraintLayout (Recommended)
The **official Google recommendation** since 2017. Uses **Constraints** — flat hierarchy, no nesting — leading to fewer overdraws and better performance. Anchors: `app:layout_constraintTop_toTopOf`, `app:layout_constraintStart_toEndOf`, etc.

### 2.3.4 FrameLayout
Stacks children on top of each other (z-ordered). Useful for fragments, splash overlays, FABs.

### 2.3.5 TableLayout / GridLayout
Tabular grid arrangement.

## 2.4 Essential UI Widgets (Common Views)

| Widget | Class | Use in IoT App |
|---|---|---|
| `TextView` | `android.widget.TextView` | Display sensor values (e.g., "Temp: 28.5 °C") |
| `Button` / `ImageButton` | `android.widget.Button` | Trigger commands (e.g., "Turn ON Bulb") |
| `Switch` / `ToggleButton` | CompoundButton derivatives | Binary on/off device states |
| `SeekBar` | `android.widget.SeekBar` | Fan speed / dimmer level slider |
| `EditText` | `android.widget.EditText` | User input (e.g., threshold value) |
| `ImageView` | `android.widget.ImageView` | Device icons, status indicators |
| `ProgressBar` | `android.widget.ProgressBar` | Loading sensor data from cloud |
| `Spinner` / `AutoCompleteTextView` | Dropdown selectors | Choose device from list |
| `RecyclerView` | `androidx.recyclerview.widget.RecyclerView` | List of IoT devices / logs |
| `CardView` | `androidx.cardview.widget.CardView` | Material card wrapping each device |
| `FloatingActionButton (FAB)` | Material Components | Primary action (e.g., "Add Device") |

## 2.5 Material Design Principles (Material 3)

Material Design is built on **four pillars**:
- **Material is a metaphor** — surfaces and elevation mimic physical paper.
- **Bold, graphic, intentional** — strong typography and color.
- **Motion provides meaning** — animations guide attention.
- **Flexible foundation** — adaptive across phone, tablet, foldable, Wear OS.

Key attributes: **Color roles** (Primary, Secondary, Surface, On-Surface), **Typography scale** (Display, Headline, Title, Body, Label), **Shape system** (small/medium/large corner radii).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (UI Metrics & Conversions)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **mathematical conversions and design equations** frequently asked in KTU university exams for Android UI topics.

| Quantity | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| dp → px | $\text{px} = \text{dp} \times \dfrac{\text{dpi}}{160}$ | pixels | At **mdpi (160)**, 1 dp = 1 px |
| px → dp | $\text{dp} = \text{px} \times \dfrac{160}{\text{dpi}}$ | dp | Use `TypedValue.applyDimension()` in code |
| sp → px | $\text{px} = \text{sp} \times \dfrac{\text{dpi}}{160} \times \text{fontScale}$ | pixels | `fontScale` = user accessibility setting |
| Density Bucket | $\text{density} = \dfrac{\text{dpi}}{160}$ | unitless | mdpi=1, hdpi=1.5, xhdpi=2, xxhdpi=3, xxxhdpi=4 |
| Aspect Ratio | $r = \dfrac{w}{h}$ | ratio | ConstraintLayout `app:layout_constraintDimensionRatio="w,16:9"` |
| Safe Area | $\text{TopInset} = 24 \text{ dp (status bar)}$ | dp | Material spec |
| Touch Target | $\text{minSize} \ge 48 \times 48 \text{ dp}$ | dp | Accessibility Material guideline |
| Elevation | $\text{resting\_elev} \in [1, 12] \text{ dp}$ | dp | Cards=1dp, FAB=6dp, TopAppBar=4dp |
| Grid Columns | $n = \left\lfloor \dfrac{W - 2M}{(C + G)} \right\rfloor$ | integer | W=screen width, M=margins, C=cell, G=gutter |
| Weight Distribution | $\text{width}_i = \dfrac{w_i}{\sum w_j} \times \text{parentWidth}$ | dp/px | LinearLayout `layout_weight` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Any time you write a layout in your answer, declare `xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"` and the root tag must be a ViewGroup. Always specify **both** `layout_width` and `layout_height` — forgetting this is the #1 reason XML fails to compile.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Designing an IoT Control Screen (XML Layout)

**Scenario:** Build the home screen of an IoT app that shows:
- A title "**Smart Home**"
- A status `TextView` showing "Living Room Light: ON"
- A `Switch` widget to toggle the light
- A `Button` labelled "Refresh Sensors"

We use **`ConstraintLayout`** (Google's recommended choice).

### Step 1 — Create the XML file

Save as `res/layout/activity_main.xml`.

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<androidx.constraintlayout.widget.ConstraintLayout
    xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    xmlns:app="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res-auto"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:padding="16dp">

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/tvTitle"
        android:layout_width="0dp"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="@string/app_title"
        android:textSize="24sp"
        android:textStyle="bold"
        app:layout_constraintTop_toTopOf="parent"
        app:layout_constraintStart_toStartOf="parent"
        app:layout_constraintEnd_toEndOf="parent" />

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/tvStatus"
        android:layout_width="0dp"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="@string/light_status"
        android:textSize="18sp"
        android:layout_marginTop="24dp"
        app:layout_constraintTop_toBottomOf="@id/tvTitle"
        app:layout_constraintStart_toStartOf="parent"
        app:layout_constraintEnd_toStartOf="@id/switchLight" />

    <Switch
        android:id="@+id/switchLight"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        app:layout_constraintTop_toTopOf="@id/tvStatus"
        app:layout_constraintBottom_toBottomOf="@id/tvStatus"
        app:layout_constraintEnd_toEndOf="parent" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/btnRefresh"
        android:layout_width="0dp"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="@string/refresh"
        android:minHeight="48dp"
        android:layout_marginTop="32dp"
        app:layout_constraintTop_toBottomOf="@id/tvStatus"
        app:layout_constraintStart_toStartOf="parent"
        app:layout_constraintEnd_toEndOf="parent" />

</androidx.constraintlayout.widget.ConstraintLayout>
```

### Step 2 — Create supporting string resources (`res/values/strings.xml`)

```xml
<resources>
    <string name="app_title">Smart Home</string>
    <string name="light_status">Living Room Light: ON</string>
    <string name="refresh">Refresh Sensors</string>
</resources>
```

### Step 3 — Inflate in `MainActivity.java`

```java
public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {
    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);

        Switch lightSwitch = findViewById(R.id.switchLight);
        Button btnRefresh  = findViewById(R.id.btnRefresh);
        TextView tvStatus  = findViewById(R.id.tvStatus);

        lightSwitch.setOnCheckedChangeListener((b, isChecked) -> {
            tvStatus.setText("Living Room Light: " + (isChecked ? "ON" : "OFF"));
        });
    }
}
```

### Step 4 — Logical Walkthrough

- `setContentView(R.layout.activity_main)` tells Android to inflate `activity_main.xml`.
- `LayoutInflater` parses the XML, creates `ConstraintLayout` and child View objects, and assigns them the IDs.
- `findViewById()` walks the inflated tree using depth-first search and returns references to the Java objects.
- The listener lambda updates the `TextView` whenever the switch state changes.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Deriving `layout_weight` Distribution

**Problem:** A horizontal `LinearLayout` has parent width = **360 dp**, with three children of weights $w_1 = 1$, $w_2 = 2$, $w_3 = 1$. Find each child's actual width.

**Step 1 — Total weight**

$$\sum w_j = w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4$$

**Step 2 — Apply the weight formula**

$$\text{width}_i = \frac{w_i}{\sum w_j} \times \text{parentWidth}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each child**

$$\text{width}_1 = \frac{1}{4} \times 360 = 90 \text{ dp}$$

$$\text{width}_2 = \frac{2}{4} \times 360 = 180 \text{ dp}$$

$$\text{width}_3 = \frac{1}{4} \times 360 = 90 \text{ dp}$$

**Step 4 — Verify**

$$90 + 180 + 90 = 360 \text{ dp} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> If any child has `android:layout_width="match_parent"` (i.e., pre-claims all space) AND a `layout_weight`, Android treats the `match_parent` as `0dp` for measurement purposes. This is a common bug — always set `layout_width="0dp"` inside a weighted `LinearLayout`.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — DPI Bucket Conversion

**Problem:** A designer provides a **24 dp × 24 dp** icon. The target device is **xhdpi (320 dpi)**. What is the bitmap size in **px**?

**Step 1 — Compute density**

$$\text{density} = \frac{\text{dpi}}{160} = \frac{320}{160} = 2$$

**Step 2 — Convert dp to px**

$$\text{px} = \text{dp} \times \frac{\text{dpi}}{160} = 24 \times 2 = 48 \text{ px}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion**

The icon bitmap for `drawable-xhdpi/` should be **48 × 48 pixels**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Layout

| Scenario | Best Layout | Why |
|---|---|---|
| IoT dashboard with form-like inputs | `LinearLayout` (vertical) | Predictable, easy stacking |
| Complex screen with many relative positions | `ConstraintLayout` | Flat hierarchy, performant |
| Need overlap (e.g., FAB over a list) | `FrameLayout` or `CoordinatorLayout` | Z-axis stacking |
| Settings list with rows | `RecyclerView` + `LinearLayoutManager` | Memory-efficient scrolling |
| Tiles/dashboard grid | `RecyclerView` + `GridLayoutManager` (n=2 or 3) | Material spec for grid |
| Master-detail on tablet/foldable | `FragmentContainerView` | Adaptive UI |
| Tab navigation | `TabLayout` + `ViewPager2` | Material navigation pattern |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Android UI Layered Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Taps Screen] --> B[Activity onCreate]
    B --> C[setContentView R.layout.main]
    C --> D[LayoutInflater parses XML]
    D --> E[Build View Tree]
    E --> F[Measure Pass]
    F --> G[Layout Pass]
    G --> H[Draw Pass on Canvas]
    H --> I[Rendered Pixels on Screen]
    I --> J{User Interaction}
    J --> K[Event Dispatch onTouchEvent]
    K --> L[Listener Callback in Activity]
    L --> M[Update View via setText setVisibility etc]
    M --> H
```

## 4.2 View Hierarchy for an IoT Device Card

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[CardView rootCard] --> img[ImageView imgDeviceIcon]
    root --> tvName[TextView tvDeviceName]
    root --> tvState[TextView tvDeviceState]
    root --> sw[Switch switchPower]
    root --> btn[Button btnConfigure]

    style root fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000
    style img fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000
    style tvName fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000
    style tvState fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000
    style sw fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000
    style btn fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000
```

## 4.3 ConstraintLayout Constraint Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Parent Container]:::parent
    V[View A]:::view
    W[View B]:::view
    X[View C]:::view

    P ---|top to top| V
    V ---|start to end| W
    V ---|end to end| P
    W ---|top to bottom of A| X

    classDef parent fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef view fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

## 4.4 XML → Java Object Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Developer
    participant XML as res/layout/activity_main.xml
    participant Infl as LayoutInflater
    participant Tree as View Hierarchy
    participant Act as Activity

    Dev->>XML: Define Views and attributes
    Dev->>Act: Call setContentView R.layout.activity_main
    Act->>Infl: Request inflation
    Infl->>XML: Parse using XmlPullParser
    XML-->>Infl: Tags and attributes
    Infl->>Tree: Instantiate ViewGroup and Views
    Tree-->>Act: Attached to Activity window
    Act->>Tree: findViewById id switchLight
    Tree-->>Act: Returns Switch reference
    Act->>Tree: setOnCheckedChangeListener
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define `View` and `ViewGroup` in Android. How are they related?
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A `View` is the base class for all UI components in Android; it occupies a rectangular area on the screen, is responsible for drawing itself, and handles user interaction events. Examples: `TextView`, `Button`, `ImageView`.
- A `ViewGroup` is a subclass of `View` that acts as a **container** for other Views (and even other ViewGroups). It defines the layout structure. Examples: `LinearLayout`, `ConstraintLayout`, `FrameLayout`.
- **Relationship:** ViewGroup **is-a** View (inheritance), but **has-a** collection of child Views (composition). Together they form a **tree structure** called the View Hierarchy, with the Activity's content view as the root.

### Q2. What is the difference between `dp` and `sp`? Why are they preferred over `px`?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `dp` (density-independent pixel) is an abstract unit based on a **160 dpi** reference screen. It scales uniformly across devices of different screen densities, ensuring consistent physical size.
- `sp` (scale-independent pixel) is the same as `dp` but additionally **respects the user's font-size accessibility settings** (font scale). It is the recommended unit for **`textSize`**.
- `px` (pixel) is a raw device pixel whose physical size varies across devices — a 100 px element is tiny on a 4K tablet and huge on a small phone. Hence Android recommends `dp` for sizes/margins and `sp` for fonts.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the different types of Layout Managers available in Android. Compare `LinearLayout` and `ConstraintLayout` with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Design the XML layout for an IoT app screen that displays a `TextView` showing "Soil Moisture: 45%", a `SeekBar` (0–100) to set a moisture threshold, a `Switch` to enable auto-irrigation, and a `Button` labelled "Save Settings". Mention the role of `strings.xml`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Layout Managers in Android (3 marks):**

1. **LinearLayout** — Arranges children in a single row (`horizontal`) or column (`vertical`). Supports `layout_weight` for proportional sizing.
2. **RelativeLayout** — Positions children relative to parent or siblings. Considered legacy; produces deeply nested trees.
3. **ConstraintLayout** — Flat, flexible, anchor-based. Recommended for all new Android projects. Each View has at least 2 constraints (e.g., top, start).
4. **FrameLayout** — Stacks children on the z-axis; used for fragments and FABs.
5. **TableLayout / GridLayout** — Tabular grid arrangement.
6. **CoordinatorLayout** — Super-power FrameLayout enabling Material behaviors (snackbar, appbar scroll, FAB hide-on-scroll).

**Comparison Table: LinearLayout vs ConstraintLayout (4 marks):**

| Feature | LinearLayout | ConstraintLayout |
|---|---|---|
| Hierarchy depth | Nested for complex UI → slow | Flat → fast |
| Performance | More overdraw | Less overdraw |
| Flexibility | Limited to row/column | Arbitrary positioning + chains + guidelines + barriers |
| Recommended by Google | Older apps | New apps (since 2017) |
| Animations | Difficult | Easy via ConstraintSet |

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L["LinearLayout nested (deep)"]
        L1[Outer Linear vertical] --> L2[Inner Linear horizontal]
        L2 --> L3[TextView]
        L2 --> L4[EditText]
    end

    subgraph C["ConstraintLayout (flat)"]
        C1[ConstraintLayout root] --> C2[TextView anchored start and top]
        C1 --> C3[EditText anchored end of TextView]
    end

    style L1 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**XML Layout: `res/layout/activity_irrigation.xml` (5 marks):**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<androidx.constraintlayout.widget.ConstraintLayout
    xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    xmlns:app="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res-auto"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:padding="16dp">

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/tvMoisture"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="@string/moisture_label"
        android:textSize="20sp"
        app:layout_constraintTop_toTopOf="parent"
        app:layout_constraintStart_toStartOf="parent" />

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/tvThresholdLabel"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="@string/threshold_label"
        android:layout_marginTop="24dp"
        app:layout_constraintTop_toBottomOf="@id/tvMoisture"
        app:layout_constraintStart_toStartOf="parent" />

    <SeekBar
        android:id="@+id/seekThreshold"
        android:layout_width="0dp"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:max="100"
        android:progress="45"
        android:layout_marginTop="8dp"
        app:layout_constraintTop_toBottomOf="@id/tvThresholdLabel"
        app:layout_constraintStart_toStartOf="parent"
        app:layout_constraintEnd_toEndOf="parent" />

    <Switch
        android:id="@+id/switchAuto"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="@string/auto_irrigation"
        android:layout_marginTop="24dp"
        android:minHeight="48dp"
        app:layout_constraintTop_toBottomOf="@id/seekThreshold"
        app:layout_constraintStart_toStartOf="parent" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/btnSave"
        android:layout_width="0dp"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="@string/save_settings"
        android:minHeight="48dp"
        android:layout_marginTop="32dp"
        app:layout_constraintTop_toBottomOf="@id/switchAuto"
        app:layout_constraintStart_toStartOf="parent"
        app:layout_constraintEnd_toEndOf="parent" />

</androidx.constraintlayout.widget.ConstraintLayout>
```

**Role of `strings.xml` (2 marks):**
- Centralizes **all user-facing text** in `res/values/strings.xml`.
- Enables **internationalization (i18n)** — just add `res/values-es/strings.xml` for Spanish.
- Keeps layout XML clean and free of hard-coded strings.
- Example: `<string name="moisture_label">Soil Moisture: 45%</string>`

```xml
<!-- res/values/strings.xml -->
<resources>
    <string name="moisture_label">Soil Moisture: 45%</string>
    <string name="threshold_label">Threshold: 45%</string>
    <string name="auto_irrigation">Enable Auto Irrigation</string>
    <string name="save_settings">Save Settings</string>
</resources>
```

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Alternative]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Android UI View Hierarchy**. Describe the three passes (Measure, Layout, Draw) performed during rendering. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain Material Design principles. List at least **six** Material components used in modern Android UI design with their use cases. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**View Hierarchy Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Window PhoneWindow] --> B[DecorView]
    B --> C[LinearLayout vertical]
    C --> D[ActionBar]
    C --> E[ContentFrame id content]
    E --> F[ConstraintLayout res layout activity_main]
    F --> G1[TextView tvTitle]
    F --> G2[Switch switchLight]
    F --> G3[Button btnRefresh]
    F --> G4[LinearLayout horizontal]
    G4 --> H1[ImageView]
    G4 --> H2[TextView]
    G4 --> H3[Button]

    style A fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style F fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

**Three Rendering Passes (4 marks):**

1. **Measure Pass** — Top-down. The root ViewGroup asks each child for its desired size by passing a `MeasureSpec` (mode: `EXACTLY`, `AT_MOST`, `UNSPECIFIED` + size). Each child returns its measured dimensions. Use `onMeasure(int, int)` to override.
2. **Layout Pass** — Top-down. Each parent places its children using `layout(l, t, r, b)` based on the measured sizes. Override `onLayout()` in custom ViewGroups.
3. **Draw Pass** — Top-down (or as invalidated). Each View's `onDraw(Canvas)` is called to render itself. Hardware acceleration is enabled by default since Android 4.0.

> [!NOTE]
> Invalidating one View (e.g., via `setText`) only re-triggers Draw for that subtree — it does **not** re-measure the whole hierarchy. This is why hierarchical optimization matters.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Material Design Principles (3 marks):**

1. **Material is a metaphor** — Surfaces have elevation and cast shadows, mimicking physical paper stacked in 3D space.
2. **Bold, graphic, intentional** — Strong typography hierarchy, deliberate use of color, generous whitespace.
3. **Motion provides meaning** — Animations describe spatial relationships (e.g., shared element transitions between activities) and direct user attention.
4. **Flexible foundation** — A single design system adapts across phones, tablets, foldables, Wear OS, and TV.

**Six Material Components (4 marks):**

| Component | Class | Use Case in IoT App |
|---|---|---|
| **MaterialButton** | `com.google.android.material.button.MaterialButton` | Primary action like "Connect Device" |
| **TextInputLayout + TextInputEditText** | `com.google.android.material.textfield.*` | Threshold input with floating label and validation |
| **MaterialCardView** | `com.google.android.material.card.MaterialCardView` | Container for each IoT device tile |
| **FloatingActionButton (FAB)** | `com.google.android.material.floatingactionbutton.FloatingActionButton` | "Add New Device" primary action |
| **SwitchMaterial** | `com.google.android.material.switchmaterial.SwitchMaterial` | Toggle appliance ON/OFF with Material styling |
| **BottomNavigationView** | `com.google.android.material.bottomnavigation.BottomNavigationView` | Top-level navigation: Home / Devices / Profile |
| **Snackbar** | `com.google.android.material.snackbar.Snackbar` | Lightweight feedback: "Sensor data updated" |
| **Chip** | `com.google.android.material.chip.Chip` | Filter tags (e.g., "Living Room", "Kitchen") |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting `xmlns:android` namespace** on the root tag — XML will fail to inflate. (-2 marks)
> 2. **Mixing up `px`, `dp`, and `sp`** — using `px` for margins or `dp` for `textSize` is a design anti-pattern. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Not specifying `layout_width` and `layout_height`** on every View — runtime crash. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Hard-coding strings inside XML** instead of using `@string/` — violates Android best practices. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Drawing a deep nested `LinearLayout` instead of a flat `ConstraintLayout`** — flagged as poor design in valuation. (-1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Every Android UI is a tree** of `View` (leaf) and `ViewGroup` (container) objects.
- ✅ **Three rendering passes**: Measure → Layout → Draw. Top-down traversal.
- ✅ **Layout Managers**: `LinearLayout`, `RelativeLayout`, `ConstraintLayout` (recommended), `FrameLayout`, `TableLayout`, `GridLayout`, `CoordinatorLayout`.
- ✅ **Always use `ConstraintLayout`** for new apps — flat hierarchy = better performance.
- ✅ **Use `dp` for sizes/margins, `sp` for fonts, `px` rarely.** Conversion: $\text{px} = \text{dp} \times \dfrac{\text{dpi}}{160}$.
- ✅ **Touch targets must be ≥ 48 × 48 dp** for accessibility (Material guideline).
- ✅ **Minimize overdraw** — avoid deep nesting and opaque overlapping backgrounds.
- ✅ **Externalize resources**: `@string/`, `@color/`, `@dimen/`, `@drawable/` — never hard-code.
- ✅ **`setContentView(R.layout.filename)`** links XML to Activity. `findViewById(id)` retrieves a reference at runtime.
- ✅ **Material Design** = Google's design system; **MaterialButton**, **CardView**, **FAB**, **TextInputLayout**, **Snackbar**, **BottomNavigationView** are must-know components.
- ✅ For **IoT apps**, typical widgets: `Switch` (device state), `SeekBar` (thresholds), `TextView` (sensor values), `ProgressBar` (loading), `RecyclerView` (device list).
- ✅ **`layout_weight` formula:** $\text{width}_i = \dfrac{w_i}{\sum w_j} \times \text{parentWidth}$; use `layout_width="0dp"` with weights.
- ✅ **Density buckets:** mdpi(160), hdpi(240), xhdpi(320), xxhdpi(480), xxxhdpi(640).
- ✅ Always declare `xmlns:android` on the **root** ViewGroup of every layout XML.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Styles & Themes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Styles & Themes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Styles

In the context of **Android Application Development for IoT**, a **Style** is a collection of attributes (properties) that collectively specify the visual appearance and formatting for a single `View` widget, a layout container, or a logical group of UI elements. A style is defined as an XML resource inside the `res/values/` directory, conventionally in a file named `styles.xml` (or `themes.xml`).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECGT415 — Module 2):**
> A *Style* in Android is a set of one or more formatting attributes applied to the visual components (Views) of an application. It is analogous to a **CSS stylesheet** in web development — it separates the *presentation layer* from the *application logic layer*, promoting **reusability**, **consistency**, and **maintainability** of the User Interface (UI).

A style does not modify the **behavior** of a View — it only alters its **appearance** (color, font, size, padding, margin, background, border, etc.).

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Themes

A **Theme** is a specialized type of Style that is applied to an entire **Activity**, **Application**, or a specific screen-level hierarchy (such as an `AppCompatActivity` or the `application` tag inside the `AndroidManifest.xml`). While a Style scopes its attributes to one View, a Theme cascades its attributes to **all child elements** within the scope where it is applied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Difference (High-Yield for KTU Board Exams):**
> - **Style** → Applied to **individual View widgets** (e.g., one `TextView`, one `Button`).
> - **Theme** → Applied to an **Activity or whole Application** and **inherits downward** to every View inside it.
>
> Technically, a Theme IS a Style — but a Style applied at the screen/app scope is called a Theme.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The "Interior Designer" Intuition

Imagine you are building a smart-home IoT control panel. You have **20 different buttons** on the dashboard: "Turn on Living Room Light", "Adjust AC", "Lock Door", etc.

**Without Styles** (Bad Practice):
You would have to manually set the background color, text color, font size, padding, and corner radius for **each of the 20 buttons** in 20 separate XML layout files. If the designer says *"change all button colors to dark blue"*, you must edit 20 files.

**With Styles** (Good Practice):
You define **one** style called `<style name="IoTButton">` that contains all the visual properties. You reference it from all 20 buttons using `style="@style/IoTButton"`. To change the color, you edit **one** place.

**With Themes** (Application-Wide):
You define one Theme `AppTheme.IoT` inside `styles.xml` and apply it to the **whole application** in `AndroidManifest.xml`. Every `Button`, `TextView`, and `EditText` in your entire IoT app automatically receives the same "look and feel" — just like how a single interior design theme (e.g., "Modern Minimalist") is applied to every room in a house, so every room feels consistent.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exam:** "**S**tyle = **S**ingle widget. **T**heme = **T**otal app." 🎯

## 1.4 Why Styles & Themes Are Critical for IoT Apps

IoT mobile apps (such as those controlling smart bulbs, thermostats, or industrial sensors) frequently need:

- **Consistent Branding** — every screen must look like it belongs to the same product family.
- **Dynamic Theming** — light/dark mode switching for OLED battery saving on devices.
- **Material Design Compliance** — Google's standard for modern Android UIs.
- **Rapid Prototyping** — quickly re-skin the entire app without touching layout files.

**Standard XML Resource Location:** `app/src/main/res/values/styles.xml`
**Standard Naming Convention:** PascalCase or dot-notation (e.g., `Theme.MyApp`, `TextAppearance.IoT`)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Style XML Block

A style resource is defined using the `<style>` root element with the following structural anatomy:

```xml
<resources>
    <style name="StyleName" parent="ParentStyleName">
        <item name="android:attributeName">value</item>
        <item name="android:attributeName">value</item>
        ...
    </style>
</resources>
```

### Structural Breakdown:

| Component | Purpose | Mandatory? |
|---|---|---|
| `<resources>` | Root container for all style resources in the file | **Yes** |
| `<style name="...">` | Unique identifier for the style | **Yes** |
| `parent="..."` | Inherits attributes from an existing style/theme | No (but recommended) |
| `<item name="android:...">` | Individual visual property and its value | **Yes** (at least one) |

> [!NOTE]
> The `name` attribute traditionally uses **dot-notation** (e.g., `Theme.MyApp.Light`) which Android internally converts to a Java-style inheritance hierarchy. Alternatively, the `parent` attribute can be used explicitly for clarity. **The `parent` attribute is preferred in modern Android development** because it is more readable.

## 2.2 Common Style Attributes Used in IoT App UI

| Attribute Name | What It Controls | Typical Value (IoT Use Case) |
|---|---|---|
| `android:textColor` | Text color | `@color/iot_accent` |
| `android:textSize` | Font size in `sp` | `18sp` |
| `android:textStyle` | Bold, italic, normal | `bold` |
| `android:background` | View background | `@drawable/button_bg` |
| `android:padding` | Inner spacing | `12dp` |
| `android:layout_margin` | Outer spacing | `8dp` |
| `android:fontFamily` | Font typeface | `sans-serif-medium` |
| `android:minHeight` | Minimum tap-target size | `48dp` (accessibility standard) |
| `android:elevation` | Shadow depth (Material) | `6dp` |
| `android:cornerRadius` | Rounded corners | `8dp` |

> **Important Constant for KTU:** The recommended minimum touch target size for IoT control buttons is **48dp × 48dp** (per Google Material Design accessibility guidelines). Always define this inside a reusable style to ensure uniform usability across your IoT app.

## 2.3 Common Theme Attributes (Application-Wide)

Themes use special attributes that affect the **entire window or activity**, not just one view. These are defined in the Android framework under the `android:theme` namespace.

| Theme Attribute | Scope of Effect | Real-World IoT Use |
|---|---|---|
| `android:windowBackground` | Background of the entire screen | Dark theme for smart-home dashboard |
| `android:colorPrimary` | Top app bar, primary buttons | Brand color of IoT product |
| `android:colorPrimaryDark` | Status bar | Status bar color |
| `android:colorAccent` | Floating buttons, switches, checkboxes | Toggle switch for "Smart Plug ON/OFF" |
| `android:textColorPrimary` | All text in the app | Legibility on dark backgrounds |
| `android:statusBarColor` | Status bar tint | Immersive mode for full-screen sensor graphs |
| `android:windowLightStatusBar` | Light/dark status bar icons | Visibility on light backgrounds |

## 2.4 The `parent` Attribute — Style Inheritance

The `parent` attribute is the most powerful concept in Android styles. It allows a style to **inherit all attributes from another style** and override only the properties it needs to change.

```xml
<!-- Base style -->
<style name="BaseButton" parent="android:Widget.Button">
    <item name="android:background">@color/blue</item>
    <item name="android:textColor">@color/white</item>
    <item name="android:padding">16dp</item>
</style>

<!-- Child style inheriting from BaseButton -->
<style name="BaseButton.Emergency" parent="BaseButton">
    <item name="android:background">@color/red</item>  <!-- overrides blue -->
    <!-- padding and textColor are INHERITED -->
</style>
```

### How Inheritance Works (KTU High-Yield Concept):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Inheritance Resolution:**
> 1. The system first looks at the **child style's** explicitly defined attributes.
> 2. If an attribute is **not found** in the child, it traverses **upward** to the `parent`.
> 3. If still not found, it goes to the **grandparent**, and so on.
> 4. The final fallback is the **Android framework's default theme**.
>
> This is the same **depth-first search (DFS)** traversal concept used in Object-Oriented Programming class inheritance.

## 2.5 Applying Styles and Themes

### Application Site 1: Inside a Layout XML (Per-View Style)

```xml
<Button
    android:id="@+id/btnToggleLight"
    style="@style/IoTButton"
    android:layout_width="wrap_content"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:text="Toggle Light" />
```

### Application Site 2: In `AndroidManifest.xml` (Per-Activity or Per-Application Theme)

```xml
<application
    android:allowBackup="true"
    android:theme="@style/AppTheme">
    
    <activity android:name=".MainActivity"
              android:theme="@style/AppTheme.NoActionBar" />
</application>
```

### Application Site 3: Programmatically (Rare, Runtime Theme Switching)

```java
// In an Activity, for dynamic theme switching (e.g., dark mode toggle)
setTheme(R.style.AppTheme_Dark);
setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);
```

> [!WARNING]
> The programmatic `setTheme()` call **MUST** happen **before** `setContentView()`. This is a common source of bugs in IoT apps that support light/dark mode toggling.

## 2.6 The "Nine-Patch" and Drawable Reference Pattern

In production IoT apps, styles often reference **drawable resources** (e.g., custom button backgrounds) instead of solid colors, to support gradients, rounded corners, and state-based visuals (pressed, focused, disabled).

```xml
<style name="IoTButton" parent="Widget.AppCompat.Button">
    <item name="android:background">@drawable/bg_iot_button_selector</item>
    <item name="android:textColor">@color/button_text_selector</item>
    <item name="android:stateListAnimator">@null</item>
</style>
```

The `stateListAnimator` being `@null` disables the default Material elevation animation — a common requirement for IoT apps that need flat, industrial-looking dashboards.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Quick-Reference Summary Table

| Feature | Style | Theme |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Single View or ViewGroup | Activity or Application |
| **Defined in** | `res/values/styles.xml` | `res/values/themes.xml` (or same file) |
| **Applied via** | `style="@style/X"` in layout XML | `android:theme="@style/X"` in manifest |
| **Inheritance** | Via `parent` attribute | Via `parent` attribute |
| **Can change `colorPrimary`?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Can change `windowBackground`?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Typical use case** | Custom button, text appearance | Light/dark mode, brand identity |
| **KTU exam frequency** | Very High | Very High |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Building a Complete IoT App Style System

This section provides an **exhaustive, line-by-line construction** of a real-world styles.xml file for an IoT smart-home control application, as it would appear in a KTU practical examination. Each step explains the *reasoning* (the "why") behind the design decision.

### STEP 1: Define the Color Palette

The first step in any styled IoT app is to extract the color values into a separate `colors.xml` file. This is a **best practice** that KTU examiners specifically test.

```xml
<!-- res/values/colors.xml -->
<resources>
    <color name="iot_primary">#1976D2</color>         <!-- Trust Blue -->
    <color name="iot_primary_dark">#0D47A1</color>    <!-- Darker Blue for status bar -->
    <color name="iot_accent">#FF5722</color>          <!-- Emergency Orange -->
    <color name="iot_bg_dark">#121212</color>         <!-- OLED-friendly background -->
    <color name="iot_text_primary">#FFFFFF</color>    <!-- White text on dark -->
    <color name="iot_text_secondary">#B0BEC5</color>  <!-- Greyish secondary text -->
    <color name="iot_success">#4CAF50</color>         <!-- "Device Online" green -->
    <color name="iot_warning">#FFC107</color>         <!-- "Low Battery" yellow -->
</resources>
```

**Reasoning:** Centralizing colors allows you to swap the entire app's color scheme by editing **one** file. This is critical for IoT apps that often re-brand per client (e.g., a smart-bulb manufacturer vs. a smart-lock manufacturer).

### STEP 2: Define the Base Application Theme

```xml
<!-- res/values/themes.xml -->
<resources xmlns:tools="http://schemas.android.com/tools">

    <!-- BASE THEME — Inherits from AppCompat DayNight -->
    <style name="Theme.IoTApp" parent="Theme.AppCompat.DayNight">
        <!-- Primary brand color (app bar, FAB, switches) -->
        <item name="colorPrimary">@color/iot_primary</item>
        <!-- Darker variant of primary (status bar) -->
        <item name="colorPrimaryDark">@color/iot_primary_dark</item>
        <!-- Accent color (floating buttons, switches) -->
        <item name="colorAccent">@color/iot_accent</item>
        <!-- Default background for all windows/activities -->
        <item name="android:windowBackground">@color/iot_bg_dark</item>
        <!-- Default text color across the app -->
        <item name="android:textColorPrimary">@color/iot_text_primary</item>
        <item name="android:textColorSecondary">@color/iot_text_secondary</item>
        <!-- Status bar configuration -->
        <item name="android:statusBarColor">?attr/colorPrimaryDark</item>
        <!-- Compatibility attribute for older API levels -->
        <item name="android:colorBackground">@color/iot_bg_dark</item>
    </style>

</resources>
```

**Reasoning (line-by-line for KTU):**
- `parent="Theme.AppCompat.DayNight"` → This is the **parent theme**. The `DayNight` suffix means the app **automatically switches between light and dark** based on the system setting — a critical feature for IoT dashboards on OLED displays.
- `colorPrimary`, `colorPrimaryDark`, `colorAccent` → These are the **three core Material Design colors**. They propagate to *every* component that uses them (e.g., the Floating Action Button automatically uses `colorAccent`).
- `?attr/colorPrimaryDark` → The `?attr/` prefix is a **theme attribute reference**. It tells Android: *"Look up the value of `colorPrimaryDark` from the currently active theme."* This is dynamic and follows inheritance.

### STEP 3: Define a Variant Theme (No Action Bar)

For IoT dashboards, the top action bar is often removed to maximize the screen area for sensor graphs.

```xml
    <!-- VARIANT THEME — No top bar, edge-to-edge feel -->
    <style name="Theme.IoTApp.NoActionBar">
        <item name="windowActionBar">false</item>
        <item name="windowNoTitle">true</item>
    </style>
```

**Reasoning:** This theme *implicitly* inherits from `Theme.IoTApp` (Android uses **dot-notation inheritance** here: `Theme.IoTApp.NoActionBar` is a child of `Theme.IoTApp`). The two `<item>`s override the action bar visibility only, retaining all other brand colors.

### STEP 4: Define a Custom View Style (IoT Toggle Button)

```xml
    <!-- STYLE for the smart-device toggle button -->
    <style name="Widget.IoT.ToggleButton" parent="Widget.AppCompat.Button">
        <item name="android:minWidth">120dp</item>
        <item name="android:minHeight">48dp</item>
        <item name="android:padding">16dp</item>
        <item name="android:background">@drawable/bg_toggle_selector</item>
        <item name="android:textColor">@color/iot_text_primary</item>
        <item name="android:textSize">16sp</item>
        <item name="android:textAllCaps">false</item>
        <item name="android:fontFamily">sans-serif-medium</item>
        <item name="android:gravity">center</item>
    </style>
```

**Reasoning:**
- `minHeight="48dp"` → Adheres to the Material Design **accessibility touch target** standard.
- `textAllCaps="false"` → IoT apps often have mixed-case labels like "Turn On Light" rather than all-caps "TURN ON LIGHT".
- `fontFamily="sans-serif-medium"` → A medium-weight font for better legibility on small dashboard buttons.

### STEP 5: Define a Text Appearance Style

```xml
    <!-- STYLE for sensor data labels (e.g., "Temperature: 24°C") -->
    <style name="TextAppearance.IoT.SensorLabel" parent="TextAppearance.AppCompat">
        <item name="android:textColor">@color/iot_text_secondary</item>
        <item name="android:textSize">14sp</item>
        <item name="android:letterSpacing">0.05</item>
        <item name="android:textAllCaps">true</item>
    </style>

    <!-- STYLE for sensor data values (e.g., the "24°C" number itself) -->
    <style name="TextAppearance.IoT.SensorValue" parent="TextAppearance.AppCompat">
        <item name="android:textColor">@color/iot_text_primary</item>
        <item name="android:textSize">36sp</item>
        <item name="android:fontFamily">sans-serif-light</item>
    </style>
```

**Reasoning:** This pattern — separating *labels* (small, secondary color) from *values* (large, prominent color) — is the **hallmark of every IoT dashboard** (think of the Google Home app, Philips Hue app, etc.). Splitting them into named styles makes them easy to reuse across 10+ sensor cards.

### STEP 6: Apply the Theme to the Manifest

```xml
<!-- AndroidManifest.xml -->
<application
    android:allowBackup="true"
    android:icon="@mipmap/ic_launcher"
    android:label="@string/app_name"
    android:theme="@style/Theme.IoTApp"
    android:supportsRtl="true">
    
    <activity
        android:name=".MainActivity"
        android:theme="@style/Theme.IoTApp.NoActionBar">
        <intent-filter>
            <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
            <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
        </intent-filter>
    </activity>
    
</application>
```

**Reasoning:**
- `android:theme="@style/Theme.IoTApp"` → Sets the **default** theme for **all** activities in the app.
- `android:theme="@style/Theme.IoTApp.NoActionBar"` → **Overrides** for `MainActivity` only, hiding the action bar for the dashboard.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow

The following table summarizes *when to use what* during an IoT app's style architecture design:

| Question | Use Style | Use Theme |
|---|---|---|
| I want to change the color of **one specific button**? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| I want to change the color of **all buttons in the app**? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (via `colorAccent` or `?attr/` references) |
| I want a **rounded corner** on a card? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| I want a **dark background for the whole app**? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (`android:windowBackground`) |
| I want **custom font on a single TextView**? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| I want to **inherit from a base style**? | ✅ Yes (via `parent`) | ✅ Yes (via `parent`) |
| I want to support **light/dark mode**? | ⚠️ Limited | ✅ Yes (`DayNight` parent) |

## 3.3 Inheritance Resolution Algorithm (Inferred)

When Android encounters `style="@style/MyStyle"`, it executes the following resolution:

$$\text{Resolve}(S) = \begin{cases} \text{Items in } S & \text{if defined in } S \\ \text{Resolve}(S.\text{parent}) & \text{if not defined, and } S \text{ has a parent} \\ \text{Framework default} & \text{if no parent and no match} \end{cases}$$

This is mathematically a **recursive depth-first lookup** — the same algorithm used in resolving CSS specificity and Java class method resolution.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"Explain style inheritance in Android"*, draw a **tree diagram** with arrows pointing from child to parent, and mention that **child overrides parent on conflict** (this is the "last-merged-wins" rule of Android's resource merger).

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **complete inheritance chain** for a typical IoT app's themes and styles:

```mermaid
graph TD

    F["android:Widget.Button\n(Framework Default Button)"] --> G["Widget.AppCompat.Button\n(AppCompat Button)"]
    G --> H["Widget.IoT.ToggleButton\n(Custom IoT Button)"]
    
    I["android:TextAppearance\n(Framework Text)"] --> J["TextAppearance.AppCompat\n(AppCompat Text)"]
    J --> K["TextAppearance.IoT.SensorLabel\n(Label Style)"]
    J --> L["TextAppearance.IoT.SensorValue\n(Value Style)"]
    
    C -.applies to.> M["MainActivity (Launcher)"]
    D -.applies to.> M
    E -.applies to.> N["SettingsActivity"]
    H -.applied via style attr.> O["Button in activity_main.xml"]
    K -.applied via android:textAppearance.> P["TextView in sensor_card.xml"]
    L -.applied via android:textAppearance.> P
    
    style A fill:#37474F,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#1976D2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#0D47A1,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#0D47A1,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#0D47A1,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#5D4037,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#FF5722,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#E64A19,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#5D4037,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style J fill:#FF9800,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K fill:#FFA726,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L fill:#FFB74D,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- **Solid arrows (`-->`)** = `parent` attribute inheritance (child takes attributes from parent).
- **Dotted arrows (`-.->`)** = application sites (where the style/theme is *used*).
- **Dark blue** = Theme hierarchy (Activity/Application scope).
- **Orange** = Widget/View Style hierarchy (per-View scope).
- **Brown** = Framework defaults (Android OS level).

## 4.2 Runtime Style Resolution Flow

This diagram shows what happens *internally* when the Android layout inflater encounters a styled View:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Layout XML: style='@style/Widget.IoT.ToggleButton'"] --> B["LayoutInflater reads style attribute"]
    B --> C{"Is the style a\nfully qualified name?"}
    C -->|"Yes (@style/X)"| D["Look up X in res/values/styles.xml"]
    C -->|"No"| E["Use framework default"]
    D --> F{"Does X have a\nparent attribute?"}
    F -->|"Yes"| G["Recursively load parent style"]
    F -->|"No"| H["Use only items in X"]
    G --> I["Merge parent items with child items"]
    I --> J{"Conflict on\nsame attribute?"}
    J -->|"Yes"| K["Child value WINS (overrides parent)"]
    J -->|"No"| L["Keep both values"]
    K --> M["Final attribute set applied to View"]
    L --> M
    H --> M
    E --> M
    M --> N["View is rendered on screen"]
    
    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style N fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#4CAF50
    style K fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#D32F2F
    style J fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#FBC02D
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — IoT App Theming Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Input["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1["colors.xml\n(Color Palette)"]
        A2["dimens.xml\n(Size Tokens)"]
        A3["strings.xml\n(Text Content)"]
    end
    
    subgraph StyleLayer["STYLE DEFINITION LAYER"]
        B1["styles.xml /\nthemes.xml"]
        B1 --> B2["Theme.IoTApp\n(App-wide Theme)"]
        B1 --> B3["Widget.IoT.*\n(Custom Widget Styles)"]
        B1 --> B4["TextAppearance.IoT.*\n(Text Styles)"]
    end
    
    subgraph ApplicationLayer["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        C1["AndroidManifest.xml\n(android:theme)"]
        C2["activity_main.xml\n(style= attr)"]
    end
    
    subgraph Runtime["RUNTIME LAYER"]
        D1["LayoutInflater\n(Reads style refs)"]
        D2["Theme.applyStyle()\n(Resolves inheritance)"]
        D3["View.render()\n(Applies final attrs)"]
    end
    
    subgraph Output["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        E1["Rendered IoT Dashboard\n(Consistent UI)"]
    end
    
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> C2
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> C2
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1
    
    style A1 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1976D2
    style B1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#F57C00
    style C1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C
    style D1 fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#C2185B
    style E1 fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#689F38
```

**Interpretation of Block Architecture:**
- The **Input Layer** holds atomic design tokens (colors, dimensions, strings).
- The **Style Definition Layer** composes these tokens into reusable styles and themes.
- The **Application Layer** is where styles are *referenced* (manifest for theme, layout for view style).
- The **Runtime Layer** is Android's internal machinery that resolves inheritance and applies final attributes.
- The **Output Layer** is the rendered, consistent UI that the IoT app user sees.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define **Styles** and **Themes** in Android. List any **two differences** between them.
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> - A **Style** in Android is a collection of attributes that define the visual format and appearance of a single `View` or a group of `View` widgets. *(1 mark)*
> - A **Theme** is a type of style that is applied to an entire `Activity` or `Application` and whose attributes are inherited by all child Views in that scope. *(1 mark)*
> - **Differences:** *(1 mark — any two of the following)*
>   - (i) Style is applied via the `style="@style/X"` attribute on a View; Theme is applied via the `android:theme="@style/X"` attribute in the `AndroidManifest.xml` or programmatically using `setTheme()`.
>   - (ii) Style affects only the View it is attached to; Theme affects the entire window/Activity and cascades to all its children.
>   - (iii) Style cannot change window-level attributes (like `windowBackground`); Theme can.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Explain the role of the **`parent` attribute** inside an Android `<style>` element. How does it support **code reusability**?
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> - The `parent` attribute of a `<style>` element is used to **inherit** all the formatting attributes from an existing style (the "parent style") into the current "child" style. *(1 mark)*
> - This allows developers to define a **base style** with common properties (e.g., `BaseButton`) and then create multiple **specialized child styles** (e.g., `BaseButton.Emergency`, `BaseButton.Normal`) that inherit everything but override only the differing attributes. *(1 mark)*
> - This supports the **DRY principle** (Don't Repeat Yourself), reduces code duplication, enables easier maintenance, and ensures **UI consistency** across the application. *(1 mark)*

---
