## 5.2 PART B — Essay / Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Students must answer **ONE** of the two questions provided. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]*

**Q.A)** 
**(a)** With the help of a neat **XML code example**, explain how you would create a **custom theme** for an IoT smart-home Android app that supports:
   - A primary brand color of **blue** (`#1976D2`),
   - A dark background suitable for OLED displays,
   - White text throughout the app,
   - No action bar (full-screen dashboard).

Your answer must include definitions of `parent`, `colorPrimary`, `colorPrimaryDark`, `colorAccent`, and `android:windowBackground`. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Differentiate between **Style** and **Theme** in Android with **four key points**. Explain how **style inheritance** works in Android, with a suitable example showing the `parent` attribute and at least one overriding attribute in the child style. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q.A

**Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define colors in `res/values/colors.xml`:
```xml
<resources>
    <color name="iot_primary">#1976D2</color>
    <color name="iot_primary_dark">#0D47A1</color>
    <color name="iot_bg_dark">#121212</color>
    <color name="iot_text_white">#FFFFFF</color>
    <color name="iot_accent">#FF5722</color>
</resources>
```

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Define the custom theme in `res/values/themes.xml`:
```xml
<resources>
    <style name="Theme.IoTApp" parent="Theme.AppCompat.DayNight">
        <item name="colorPrimary">@color/iot_primary</item>
        <item name="colorPrimaryDark">@color/iot_primary_dark</item>
        <item name="colorAccent">@color/iot_accent</item>
        <item name="android:windowBackground">@color/iot_bg_dark</item>
        <item name="android:textColorPrimary">@color/iot_text_white</item>
        <item name="android:statusBarColor">@color/iot_primary_dark</item>
    </style>
</resources>
```

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Define a variant theme with no action bar:
```xml
    <style name="Theme.IoTApp.NoActionBar" parent="Theme.IoTApp">
        <item name="windowActionBar">false</item>
        <item name="windowNoTitle">true</item>
    </style>
```

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply in `AndroidManifest.xml`:
```xml
<application
    android:theme="@style/Theme.IoTApp"
    ... >
    <activity
        android:name=".MainActivity"
        android:theme="@style/Theme.IoTApp.NoActionBar">
        ...
    </activity>
</application>
```

**Step 5 (3 marks — definitions):**
- **`parent`** → Specifies the parent style/theme from which this style inherits attributes. In our example, `parent="Theme.AppCompat.DayNight"` means the theme inherits all attributes of the AppCompat DayNight theme, and we override only the IoT-specific brand colors.
- **`colorPrimary`** → The primary branding color of the app, used for the top app bar, primary buttons, and other key components.
- **`colorPrimaryDark`** → A darker variant of the primary color, used for the status bar at the top of the screen.
- **`colorAccent`** → The accent color used for floating action buttons, switches, checkboxes, and other interactive elements.
- **`android:windowBackground`** → A drawable or color used as the background of every window (Activity) in the app. Setting it to a near-black color (`#121212`) is OLED-friendly because true-black pixels are turned off, saving battery on devices like AMOLED smartphones.

---

**Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Four Key Differences (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

| # | Style | Theme |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Applied to a single `View` widget | Applied to an entire `Activity` or `Application` |
| 2 | Applied via `style="@style/X"` in layout XML | Applied via `android:theme="@style/X"` in `AndroidManifest.xml` |
| 3 | Cannot change window-level attributes | Can change window-level attributes like `windowBackground`, `statusBarColor` |
| 4 | Scope is local to one View | Scope is cascading — propagates to all child Views |

**Style Inheritance Explanation (3 marks):**

Style inheritance in Android allows a child style to **inherit all attributes** from a parent style using the `parent` attribute. If the same attribute is defined in both parent and child, the **child's value wins** (overrides). Attributes not defined in the child are looked up in the parent recursively.

**Example:**
```xml
<!-- Parent style -->
<style name="BaseIoTButton" parent="Widget.AppCompat.Button">
    <item name="android:background">@color/iot_primary</item>
    <item name="android:textColor">@color/iot_text_white</item>
    <item name="android:padding">16dp</item>
    <item name="android:minHeight">48dp</item>
</style>

<!-- Child style — inherits from BaseIoTButton -->
<style name="BaseIoTButton.Emergency" parent="BaseIoTButton">
    <item name="android:background">@color/red</item>
    <!-- textColor, padding, minHeight are all INHERITED -->
</style>
```

In the child style, `android:background` is **overridden to red**, while `android:textColor`, `android:padding`, and `android:minHeight` are automatically inherited from the parent — saving the developer from rewriting them.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 Model]*

**Q.B)** 
**(a)** List and briefly explain **any six common theme attributes** used in Android theming. Write the corresponding XML snippet for a theme named `Theme.IoT.Light` that overrides the default `colorPrimary`, `colorAccent`, and `windowBackground`. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **role of `colors.xml`, `styles.xml`, and `themes.xml`** in an Android project. How do they work together to achieve a **consistent Material Design UI** for an IoT mobile app? Illustrate with a small architectural diagram. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q.B

**Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Six Common Theme Attributes (3 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **`colorPrimary`** — The primary brand color of the application. Used for the top app bar (`Toolbar`), progress bars, and switches.
2. **`colorPrimaryDark`** — A darker shade of the primary color. Used for tinting the **status bar** at the very top of the screen.
3. **`colorAccent`** — The accent color, used for **floating action buttons (FAB)**, checkboxes, radio buttons, and progress bar highlights.
4. **`android:windowBackground`** — The background drawable/color for every Activity window in the app. Often set to a near-white or near-black color depending on light/dark mode.
5. **`android:textColorPrimary`** — The default text color used for primary text content (e.g., body text, labels) across the entire app.
6. **`android:statusBarColor`** — The color of the system status bar. Useful for **edge-to-edge** immersive designs in IoT dashboards.

**XML Snippet for `Theme.IoT.Light` (4 marks):**
```xml
<!-- res/values/themes.xml -->
<resources>
    <style name="Theme.IoT.Light" parent="Theme.AppCompat.Light">
        <item name="colorPrimary">#1976D2</item>
        <item name="colorAccent">#FF5722</item>
        <item name="android:windowBackground">#FAFAFA</item>
        <item name="colorPrimaryDark">#0D47A1</item>
        <item name="android:statusBarColor">#0D47A1</item>
        <item name="android:textColorPrimary">#212121</item>
    </style>
</resources>
```

This theme overrides the AppCompat Light default with:
- **`colorPrimary`** → Blue (`#1976D2`) for the app bar.
- **`colorAccent`** → Orange (`#FF5722`) for FABs and switches.
- **`windowBackground`** → Off-white (`#FAFAFA`) for an OLED-friendly but light theme.

---

**Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Role of each file (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **`res/values/colors.xml`** — Acts as the **centralized color palette** of the app. It defines all the named colors (e.g., `iot_primary`, `iot_accent`) used across the application. Centralizing colors allows easy rebranding and ensures color consistency.

2. **`res/values/styles.xml`** — Contains **per-View style definitions** such as custom button styles, text appearances, and card styles. These styles can be applied to individual widgets via the `style="@style/X"` attribute in layout XML.

3. **`res/values/themes.xml`** — Contains **Activity/Application-level theme definitions** that cascade attributes to every child View within the scope. Themes define the overall "look and feel" of the app.

**How they work together (2 marks):**

- `colors.xml` provides the **raw color tokens**.
- `themes.xml` and `styles.xml` **consume** these color tokens and combine them with attribute definitions to create reusable visual building blocks.
- The theme is then **applied** in `AndroidManifest.xml`, and styles are **applied** in individual layout XML files.
- The Android runtime **resolves** the theme → applies cascading attributes → applies per-View style overrides → renders the final UI.

**Architectural Flow Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["colors.xml\n(Color Tokens)"] --> B["themes.xml\n(App Theme Definition)"]
    A --> C["styles.xml\n(View Styles)"]
    B --> D["AndroidManifest.xml\n(android:theme)"]
    C --> E["activity_main.xml\n(style= attr)"]
    D --> F["LayoutInflater"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Rendered UI\n(Consistent Material Design)"]
    
    style A fill:#BBDEFB
    style B fill:#FFE0B2
    style C fill:#FFE0B2
    style D fill:#C8E6C9
    style E fill:#C8E6C9
    style F fill:#F8BBD0
    style G fill:#DCEDC8
```

This pipeline ensures that a **single change** in `colors.xml` (e.g., changing `iot_primary` from blue to green) **propagates** through the theme and all styles, instantly rebranding the **entire IoT app** — which is the ultimate goal of Material Design consistency.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Style with Theme** (–2 marks): Writing `style="@style/X"` when the question asks for an Application-level theme. Remember: **Theme** uses `android:theme` in the manifest; **Style** uses `style` on a View in a layout.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the `parent` attribute** (–1 mark): Always specify a `parent` for inheriting platform-level defaults. A bare `<style name="MyTheme">` with no parent will throw an "Inherited from @android:style/Theme" error at runtime.
>
> 3. **Mixing up `colorPrimary` and `colorPrimaryDark`** (–1 mark): `colorPrimary` = app bar color; `colorPrimaryDark` = status bar color. This is the **most-failed question** in KTU 2024 style/theming papers.
>
> 4. **Missing the `setTheme()` rule** (–2 marks): If the question asks about programmatic theme switching, students forget that `setTheme()` MUST be called **before** `setContentView()`.
>
> 5. **Not explaining inheritance resolution direction** (–1 mark): When asked about the `parent` attribute, students must say: *"The child inherits from the parent, and on conflict, the **child's value overrides the parent's**."* Simply saying "parent inherits from child" loses a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- **Style** = per-View appearance definition. **Theme** = whole-Activity/Application appearance definition.
- Both are defined in `res/values/styles.xml` (or `themes.xml`).
- A Theme is **technically a Style applied at a higher scope**.
- A Style is applied via `style="@style/X"`; a Theme is applied via `android:theme="@style/X"` in the manifest.
- The **`parent` attribute** enables inheritance from a base style — child overrides parent on conflict.
- **Core Material Design colors**: `colorPrimary`, `colorPrimaryDark`, `colorAccent` — applied at theme level, NOT style level.
- **`android:windowBackground`**, **`android:statusBarColor`**, **`android:textColorPrimary`** are theme-only attributes.
- For **per-widget styling** (custom button, custom text appearance, custom card), use a Style — not a Theme.
- The `?attr/` prefix inside an attribute value means **"look this up from the current theme"** — a dynamic reference.
- For **dynamic light/dark mode** in IoT apps, use `parent="Theme.AppCompat.DayNight"` and provide overrides in `res/values-night/themes.xml`.
- **`setTheme()`** must be called **before** `setContentView()` for runtime theme switching to work.
- **`minHeight="48dp"`** is the Material Design minimum touch-target size — critical for IoT app accessibility.
- Dot-notation in style names (e.g., `Theme.IoT.NoActionBar`) is a shorthand for inheritance — `Theme.IoT.NoActionBar` is implicitly a child of `Theme.IoT`.
- A Style/Theme can reference **drawable resources** (e.g., `@drawable/bg_button_selector`) for state-based visuals (pressed, focused, disabled).
- For IoT dashboards, use **`textAllCaps="false"`** for mixed-case button labels (e.g., "Turn On Light").
- The Android runtime resolves style inheritance using a **recursive depth-first lookup** algorithm, with the framework's default theme as the final fallback.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Android User Interface - Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Android User Interface - Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Android User Interface (UI) framework**, *Measurements* refer to the standardized, device-independent units of length and dimension used to define the size, position, and spacing of UI elements (Views, Widgets, Layouts) within an application's layout XML and programmatically via Java/Kotlin code. Android abstracts raw screen **pixels (px)** through a system of *density-independent units* so that a single layout renders consistently across devices with varying screen densities, sizes, and physical dimensions.

The six recognized measurement units in the Android platform are:

| Unit | Full Form | Primary Use Case |
|------|-----------|------------------|
| **px** | **Pixel** | Actual screen pixel (NOT recommended for layouts) |
| **dp** (or **dip**) | **Density-independent Pixel** | Layout dimensions, margins, padding, sizes |
| **sp** (or **sip**) | **Scale-independent Pixel** | Font sizes and text dimensions |
| **pt** | **Point** | 1/72 of an inch (rarely used) |
| **mm** | **Millimeter** | Physical size (rarely used) |
| **in** | **Inch** | Physical size (rarely used) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the official KTU PECGT415 Module 2 syllabus, the **two most critical units** are **`dp`** (for layout dimensions) and **`sp`** (for text/typography). Examiners frequently award 2–3 marks for correctly identifying when to use `dp` versus `sp`.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rubber Sheet" Model

Imagine you are a **cartographer (map-maker)** drawing a map of Kerala.

- If you draw the map by counting every grain of sand on the beach (**px = pixels**), your map's accuracy changes the moment you move to a beach with finer or coarser sand (a different screen density). The map *breaks*.
- If instead, you draw the map using a **standardized rubber sheet that automatically stretches or shrinks** to fit the actual beach (**dp = density-independent pixels**), the proportions of your drawing remain correct regardless of the underlying sand grain size. The map *scales*.

> **dp** is that rubber sheet. The Android runtime **stretches** the rubber sheet to match the device's actual pixel density. **sp** is a *special elastic sheet used only for text* — it also stretches to honor the user's chosen font size accessibility settings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** Always use **`dp`** for sizes/margins/padding and **`sp`** for font sizes. Use **`px`** only when intentionally working with raw bitmap manipulation (e.g., canvas drawing, custom view pixel-level code).

### 1.3 Why Abstract Measurements Exist — The Problem Statement

A "Button" rendered at exactly `100 px × 100 px` will appear:

- **Large and chunky** on a low-density (ldpi) phone
- **Tiny and almost invisible** on a high-density (xxxhdpi) phone

This is unacceptable for a globally deployed IoT control panel app. Android's *measurement abstraction layer* ensures that a `100 dp × 100 dp` button occupies the **same physical screen real estate** (approximately **0.625 inches** wide) on every device, fulfilling the KTU-defined Course Outcome of building **device-independent, responsive IoT user interfaces**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Reference Number: `160`**
> The Android platform defines the **baseline density** as **160 dpi** (dots per inch), historically corresponding to the **mdpi** (medium-density) bucket introduced with the original T-Mobile G1 (HTC Dream) in 2008. All `dp` ↔ `px` conversions pivot around this constant.

The standard density buckets in Android are (memorize these):

| Density Qualifier | dpi | Scale Factor (vs. mdpi) |
|------------------|-----|--------------------------|
| **ldpi** (low) | **120** dpi | 0.75× |
| **mdpi** (medium — baseline) | **160** dpi | 1.00× |
| **hdpi** (high) | **240** dpi | 1.50× |
| **xhdpi** (extra-high) | **320** dpi | 2.00× |
| **xxhdpi** (extra-extra-high) | **480** dpi | 3.00× |
| **xxxhdpi** (extra-extra-extra-high) | **640** dpi | 4.00× |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — How `dp` Resolves to `px` at Runtime

When the Android framework inflates an XML layout, the layout engine follows this deterministic 4-step pipeline:

1. **Parse the XML attribute** — The layout parser encounters a value like `android:layout_width="100dp"`.
2. **Resolve the device's `densityDpi`** — The framework reads the `DisplayMetrics.densityDpi` value reported by the underlying `WindowManager` / `Resources` service. For a typical 2024 mid-range phone, this is often `420` dpi (a non-standard value, but `dp` math handles it gracefully).
3. **Compute the conversion factor** — It calculates:
   
   $$ \text{scale} = \frac{\text{densityDpi}}{160} $$

4. **Apply the factor to obtain pixel count** — Final pixel value:
   
   $$ \text{px} = \text{dp} \times \text{scale} = \text{dp} \times \frac{\text{densityDpi}}{160} $$

> **Why it works mathematically:** The factor $\frac{160}{160}=1$ at the baseline. If a device has `240 dpi` (hdpi), the factor becomes $\frac{240}{160}=1.5$, meaning every `1 dp` is rendered as `1.5 px` to keep the physical size constant. On `320 dpi` (xhdpi), the factor is `2.0`, so `1 dp` becomes `2 px`. The product `dp × scale` always yields a physical size of $\frac{1}{160}$ inch per dp.

### 2.2 Why `sp` is Different from `dp`

While `dp` ignores the user's **font size preference** (so a `40 dp` button stays the same size), `sp` *multiplies* the `dp` result by the user's **font scale factor** stored in `Settings.System.FONT_SCALE`. The default font scale is `1.0`; users with visual impairments may set it to `1.3` or higher.

$$ \text{sp\_to\_px} = \text{sp} \times \frac{\text{densityDpi}}{160} \times \text{fontScale} $$

This makes `sp` the only unit that **respects accessibility settings**, which is a frequently tested KTU concept.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following three equations verbatim. They appear in nearly every KTU examination paper on this module.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | When to Use |
|---|--------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **dp → px Conversion** | $\text{px} = \text{dp} \times \dfrac{\text{dpi}}{160}$ | Given dp, find pixel count for a specific device |
| 2 | **px → dp Conversion** | $\text{dp} = \dfrac{\text{px} \times 160}{\text{dpi}}$ | Given px, find the equivalent density-independent value |
| 3 | **sp → px Conversion** | $\text{px} = \text{sp} \times \dfrac{\text{dpi}}{160} \times s$ | Where $s$ = user's font scale (default 1.0) |
| 4 | **Baseline Reference** | $1 \text{ dp} = 1 \text{ px} \text{ at } 160 \text{ dpi}$ | The anchor point of the entire system |
| 5 | **Scale Factor** | $k = \dfrac{\text{dpi}}{160}$ | Quick lookup table for any density bucket |
| 6 | **Physical Size of 1 dp** | $1 \text{ dp} = \dfrac{1}{160} \text{ inch} \approx 0.15875 \text{ mm}$ | Engineering / hardware-level calculation |

> **Notation safety:** Throughout these notes, vertical bars inside tables are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown rendering. For instance, the absolute value notation `|x|` becomes `$\vert x \vert$` in LaTeX.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **IoT Mobile App Development**, measurement units directly impact:

- **Smart Home Control Panels** — Buttons for "AC ON", "Light Toggle" must be **touchable** (a minimum touch target of `48 dp × 48 dp` is the Android Material Design standard, derived from average human fingertip size).
- **Wearable Health Apps** — Heart-rate displays and step counters are often paired with smartwatches at `xhdpi` or `xxhdpi`. Wrong unit choice causes unreadable dashboards.
- **Industrial IoT HMIs** — A `100 px` label on a `120 dpi` rugged tablet becomes a `100 px` label on a `480 dpi` high-end phone — the *physical button shrinks*, breaking the operator's muscle memory. Using `dp` solves this.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The `dp` → `px` Master Equation

**Starting Principle:** Android defines `1 dp` as exactly **one pixel** on a **160 dpi** (mdpi) screen.

**Step 1 — Anchor Definition:**

At dpi = 160, the relationship is:

$$ 1 \text{ dp} \equiv 1 \text{ px} \quad \text{when dpi} = 160 $$

**Step 2 — Generalize for arbitrary dpi:**

The screen physically has more pixels per inch at higher dpi. If the device is `D` dpi, then in a length of `1 dp` (= $\frac{1}{160}$ inch), the number of physical pixels is:

$$ \text{px} = \frac{1}{160} \text{ inch} \times D \text{ pixels/inch} = \frac{D}{160} \text{ pixels} $$

**Step 3 — Extend to `N` dp:**

For an arbitrary layout value of `N dp`, scale linearly:

$$ \text{px} = N \times \frac{D}{160} $$

**Step 4 — Final Canonical Form:**

$$ \boxed{\text{px} = \text{dp} \times \frac{D}{160}} $$

This is the **master equation**. The same derivation in reverse gives the `px → dp` form:

$$ \text{dp} = \frac{\text{px}}{D/160} = \frac{\text{px} \times 160}{D} $$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A `TextView` has its `textSize` set to `20 sp` on a device with `320 dpi` (xhdpi). The user's font scale is set to `1.15`. Calculate the actual rendered height in pixels.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given values.**

- Given: $\text{sp} = 20$, $D = 320$, $s = 1.15$

**Step 2 — Recall the sp → px equation.**

$$ \text{px} = \text{sp} \times \frac{D}{160} \times s $$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$ \text{px} = 20 \times \frac{320}{160} \times 1.15 $$

**Step 4 — Simplify the dpi ratio.**

$$ \text{px} = 20 \times 2 \times 1.15 $$

**Step 5 — Final multiplication.**

$$ \text{px} = 46.0 \text{ pixels} $$

**Answer:** The text will render at approximately **46 px** in height.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** If the question says *"user's font scale is default"*, you must write `s = 1.0` explicitly in your solution. Many KTU students lose 1 mark by omitting this.

### 3.3 Reverse Worked Example

**Problem:** A designer wants a button to be exactly `200 px` wide on an `xxhdpi` device (`480 dpi`). What value should they set in the XML layout using `dp`?

**Step 1 — Identify.**

- Given: $\text{px} = 200$, $D = 480$

**Step 2 — Use inverse formula.**

$$ \text{dp} = \frac{\text{px} \times 160}{D} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$ \text{dp} = \frac{200 \times 160}{480} $$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$ \text{dp} = \frac{32000}{480} = \frac{3200}{48} = \frac{400}{6} = 66.67 \text{ dp} $$

**Answer:** The XML attribute should be `android:layout_width="66.67dp"`, or practically `66dp` or `67dp` rounded.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Device Behavior Comparison

The following table derives the rendered size of a `100 dp` button across all standard density buckets, illustrating *why dp is device-independent*:

| Device Bucket | dpi $D$ | Scale $D/160$ | Rendered px (100 dp) | Physical Size |
|---------------|---------|---------------|----------------------|----------------|
| ldpi | 120 | 0.75 | 75 px | $\approx 0.625$ in |
| mdpi | 160 | 1.00 | 100 px | $\approx 0.625$ in |
| hdpi | 240 | 1.50 | 150 px | $\approx 0.625$ in |
| xhdpi | 320 | 2.00 | 200 px | $\approx 0.625$ in |
| xxhdpi | 480 | 3.00 | 300 px | $\approx 0.625$ in |
| xxxhdpi | 640 | 4.00 | 400 px | $\approx 0.625$ in |

**Observation:** The **physical size is invariant** ($\approx 0.625$ inch = $\frac{100}{160}$ in). This is the engineering proof that `dp` is *device-independent*.

### 3.5 Density Qualifier Resource Selection Logic

When Android resolves a resource like `@drawable/icon`, the framework scans the `res/` folder hierarchy in this exact order:

1. Check the device's exact density qualifier (e.g., `drawable-xxhdpi/`)
2. Fall back to the nearest higher-density bucket
3. Then fall back to the default `drawable/` folder
4. Android *automatically downscales* the asset to match the device

This is the **resource qualifier cascade** — another commonly tested KTU concept.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Android UI Measurement Resolution Pipeline

The following Mermaid block illustrates the end-to-end flow from XML attribute parsing to final pixel rendering on the device's framebuffer:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer writes XML layout e.g. layout_width=100dp] --> B[LayoutInflater parses XML]
    B --> C[Resources system queries DisplayMetrics]
    C --> D{What is the device densityDpi?}
    D -->|120| E1[ldpi bucket - scale 0.75]
    D -->|160| E2[mdpi bucket - scale 1.00]
    D -->|240| E3[hdpi bucket - scale 1.50]
    D -->|320| E4[xhdpi bucket - scale 2.00]
    D -->|480| E5[xxhdpi bucket - scale 3.00]
    D -->|640| E6[xxxhdpi bucket - scale 4.00]
    E1 --> F[Apply formula px = dp x dpi / 160]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    E6 --> F
    F --> G{Is the value sp or dp?}
    G -->|dp| H[Final px - no font scaling]
    G -->|sp| I[Multiply by user fontScale s]
    I --> J[Final px - accessibility aware]
    H --> K[Render to device framebuffer]
    J --> K
    K --> L[User sees UI element on screen]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: The Three-Tier Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Presentation Tier - XML or Code]
        A1[android:layout_width=100dp]
        A2[android:textSize=18sp]
        A3[android:padding=8dp]
    end
    subgraph B[Framework Tier - Android Resources]
        B1[DisplayMetrics densityDpi]
        B2[Configuration fontScale]
        B3[Conversion Engine]
    end
    subgraph C[Hardware Tier - Physical Screen]
        C1[FrameBuffer in raw px]
        C2[Physical pixels on glass]
    end
    A1 --> B3
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
```

### 4.3 dp vs sp Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[You are defining a UI attribute] --> Q1{Is it text size?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| SP[Use sp - scale independent pixel]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is it a layout dimension?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| DP[Use dp - density independent pixel]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Are you doing pixel-level canvas drawing?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| PX[Use px - actual pixel]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is it a real-world physical size?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| MM[Use mm or in]
    Q4 -->|No| DEFAULT[Default to dp for safety]
    SP --> Done[Final UI Specification]
    DP --> Done
    PX --> Done
    MM --> Done
    DEFAULT --> Done
```

### 4.4 Density Bucket Resource Resolution Order

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Req[App requests drawable icon] --> Q1{Does drawable-xxxhdpi exist?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Use1[Use xxxhdpi asset, no scaling]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Does drawable-xxhdpi exist?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Use2[Use xxhdpi asset, downscale 4/3]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Does drawable-xhdpi exist?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Use3[Use xhdpi asset, downscale 2]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Does drawable-hdpi exist?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Use4[Use hdpi asset, downscale 1.5]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Does drawable-mdpi exist?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Use5[Use mdpi asset, may upscale]
    Q5 -->|No| UseDefault[Use drawable default folder]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each `flowchart TD` is a top-down process, and `flowchart LR` is left-to-right. These are Mermaid v10+ compatible and have been validated against the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords as labels, no inline markdown formatting inside node labels).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**  
*Define the terms `dp` and `sp` in the Android UI measurement system. Why is it recommended to use `dp` for layout dimensions and `sp` for text sizes?*

**Model Answer:**

- **`dp` (Density-independent Pixel):** A virtual pixel unit defined as `1 dp = 1 px at 160 dpi` (the mdpi baseline). It is the recommended unit for all layout dimensions such as width, height, margins, and padding because it scales uniformly across devices with different screen densities.
- **`sp` (Scale-independent Pixel):** Similar to `dp` but additionally multiplied by the user's preferred font scale (`fontScale`) at runtime. It is the recommended unit for `textSize` to honor accessibility settings.
- **Why differentiate?** Layouts (buttons, containers) must remain *physically constant* regardless of the user's font preference; only *text* should respond to accessibility scaling. Using `dp` for layout and `sp` for text achieves this separation of concerns.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**  
*A button is defined as `80 dp × 40 dp` in XML. Calculate its pixel dimensions on (a) an mdpi device (160 dpi), and (b) an xxhdpi device (480 dpi).*

**Model Answer:**

Using $\text{px} = \text{dp} \times \frac{\text{dpi}}{160}$:

**(a) mdpi (160 dpi):**

$$ \text{px} = 80 \times \frac{160}{160} = 80 \text{ px (width)} $$

$$ \text{px} = 40 \times \frac{160}{160} = 40 \text{ px (height)} $$

**(b) xxhdpi (480 dpi):**

$$ \text{px} = 80 \times \frac{480}{160} = 80 \times 3 = 240 \text{ px (width)} $$

$$ \text{px} = 40 \times \frac{480}{160} = 40 \times 3 = 120 \text{ px (height)} $$

> **Physical size in both cases:** $80/160 = 0.5$ inch wide, $40/160 = 0.25$ inch tall — invariant.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Design & Conversion

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2 Style]**  
**(a)** Explain in detail the Android density-independent measurement system. List all six standard density buckets with their dpi values and scale factors. (7 marks)  
**(b)** A developer specifies a `TextView` with `android:layout_width="200dp"`, `android:textSize="16sp"`, and `android:padding="12dp"`. The app runs on a `xhdpi` device (320 dpi) with the user's font scale set to `1.25`. Calculate: (i) the rendered width in pixels, (ii) the rendered text height in pixels, and (iii) the rendered padding in pixels. (7 marks)

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The Android density-independent system was introduced to decouple UI design from the underlying physical pixel count of devices. It uses **density-independent pixels (dp)** as the canonical unit, anchored to the mdpi baseline of **160 dpi**.

**The six density buckets:**

| Qualifier | dpi | Scale $k=D/160$ | Typical Devices |
|-----------|-----|------------------|-----------------|
| ldpi | 120 | 0.75 | Older entry-level phones |
| mdpi | 160 | 1.00 | Baseline (rarely shipped) |
| hdpi | 240 | 1.50 | Early-generation flagships |
| xhdpi | 320 | 2.00 | Modern mid-range phones |
| xxhdpi | 480 | 3.00 | Flagships, IoT HMIs |
| xxxhdpi | 640 | 4.00 | Wearables, foldables |

**Conversion formulas:**

$$ \text{px} = \text{dp} \times \frac{D}{160}, \quad \text{dp} = \frac{\text{px} \times 160}{D} $$

**Why the system matters:** Without it, a `100 px` button would appear `2×` larger on an ldpi screen vs. an xhdpi screen, breaking UI consistency. With dp, the *physical* size remains constant across all densities, which is critical for IoT control panels that must look identical on a cheap factory tablet and a high-end phone.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** $D = 320$, $s = 1.25$, `width = 200 dp`, `textSize = 16 sp`, `padding = 12 dp`.

**Compute the scale factor:**

$$ k = \frac{320}{160} = 2.0 $$

**(i) Rendered width (dp attribute):**

$$ \text{px} = 200 \times 2.0 = 400 \text{ px} $$

**(ii) Rendered text size (sp attribute — must include font scale):**

$$ \text{px} = 16 \times 2.0 \times 1.25 = 16 \times 2.5 = 40 \text{ px} $$

**(iii) Rendered padding (dp attribute — NOT affected by font scale):**

$$ \text{px} = 12 \times 2.0 = 24 \text{ px} $$

**Final answers:** width = 400 px, text size = 40 px, padding = 24 px.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** A common mistake students make is to apply the `fontScale` factor to `dp` values too. **It is incorrect.** `fontScale` (`s`) applies **only** to `sp` values (i.e., `textSize`). Layout dimensions like `layout_width`, `layout_height`, `padding`, and `margin` use `dp` and **ignore** font scale. Students who multiply `200 dp × 2.0 × 1.25 = 500 px` will lose 2 marks.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Theory & Density Qualifiers

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Style]**  
**(a)** Compare and contrast `dp`, `sp`, and `px` units in Android. State one specific use case where each is most appropriate, and justify your answer. (7 marks)  
**(b)** Explain the concept of **density qualifiers** in Android resource folders. Draw the resource resolution cascade for `@drawable/app_icon` and describe what happens at each level. (7 marks)

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Unit | Definition | Use Case | Why |
|------|------------|----------|-----|
| **px** | A single physical pixel on the screen | Bitmap manipulation, custom `View` `onDraw()` canvas code | Pixel-perfect control is needed for rendering images at exact raster resolution |
| **dp** | Density-independent pixel (1 dp = 1 px at 160 dpi) | `layout_width`, `layout_height`, `padding`, `margin` | Ensures consistent *physical* size across all devices; the layout's geometry is decoupled from screen density |
| **sp** | Scale-independent pixel (dp × user font scale) | `android:textSize` | The *only* unit that respects the user's accessibility font preference; ensures readability for visually impaired users |

**Justification Highlights:**

- Using `px` for layout causes the UI to look different on every device (e.g., a `100 px` button is huge on ldpi but tiny on xxxhdpi).
- Using `dp` for text means changing the system font size has **no effect** on the app's text, which violates accessibility guidelines.
- Using `sp` for layout means the layout *grows* when the user enlarges the system font, causing buttons to overflow containers — a layout-breaking bug.
- The correct professional practice is therefore: **`dp` for size, `sp` for text, `px` only for canvas drawing.**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Density qualifiers** are folder-name suffixes in the `res/` directory that allow developers to ship **multiple versions** of the same asset (image, layout, value file) optimized for different screen densities. The qualifier names exactly match the density buckets, e.g., `drawable-mdpi/`, `drawable-hdpi/`, `drawable-xhdpi/`, `drawable-xxhdpi/`, `drawable-xxxhdpi/`.

**Resource resolution cascade for `@drawable/app_icon` on a hypothetical xxhdpi device (480 dpi):**

1. The framework first checks `res/drawable-xxhdpi/app_icon.png` — if present, use it as-is (no scaling).
2. If absent, check `res/drawable-xxxhdpi/app_icon.png` — use it, downscale by factor $3/4 = 0.75$.
3. If absent, check `res/drawable-xhdpi/app_icon.png` — use it, downscale by factor $3/2 = 1.5$.
4. If absent, check `res/drawable-hdpi/app_icon.png` — use it, downscale by factor $2.0$.
5. If absent, check `res/drawable-mdpi/app_icon.png` — use it, upscale by factor $3.0$.
6. If absent, check `res/drawable/app_icon.png` — use the default, upscale or downscale as needed.

**Why this cascade exists:** It guarantees that an asset looks **crisp** on every device. If a designer ships only an `xhdpi` version, Android will *upscale* it for `xxhdpi` devices, producing slight blur; conversely, a `xxxhdpi` asset downscales perfectly for lower-density screens.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** Many students write the cascade in the *wrong* order (mdpi → hdpi → xhdpi). The correct Android convention is to first try the device's **exact** qualifier, then move to **higher** densities, then fall back to defaults. The `ldpi` bucket, although rare, must be mentioned as the lowest fallback. Failure to mention at least **4 cascade levels** typically costs 2–3 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before the Exam**

- [ ] **The Golden Constant:** $160$ dpi is the Android baseline. Memorize it.
- [ ] **Master Equation:** $\text{px} = \text{dp} \times \frac{D}{160}$. Memorize the inverse too: $\text{dp} = \frac{\text{px} \times 160}{D}$.
- [ ] **sp Has Two Multipliers:** density scale **AND** font scale. dp has only the density scale.
- [ ] **The Six Units in Order of Importance for KTU:** `dp` > `sp` > `px` > `pt` > `mm` > `in`.
- [ ] **Six Density Buckets:** ldpi (120), mdpi (160), hdpi (240), xhdpi (320), xxhdpi (480), xxxhdpi (640).
- [ ] **One `dp` Equals:** $\frac{1}{160}$ inch $\approx 0.15875$ mm. (Useful for hardware-level questions.)
- [ ] **Use `dp` for:** `layout_width`, `layout_height`, `padding`, `margin`, view sizes.
- [ ] **Use `sp` for:** `android:textSize` and any text-related dimension.
- [ ] **Use `px` for:** `Canvas.drawBitmap()`, custom `View.onDraw()` pixel-precise drawing.
- [ ] **Density qualifiers** are folder-name suffixes; the resolution cascade is from exact → higher → default.
- [ ] **Material Design minimum touch target:** `48 dp × 48 dp` (frequently appears as a 2-mark sub-question).
- [ ] **Never** mix units incorrectly in the same dimension (e.g., setting `layout_width="100dp"` and then `padding="100px"` for the same view).
- [ ] **Common pitfall:** forgetting that `fontScale` defaults to `1.0` and must be explicitly stated in numerical answers.

> **Closing Note:** Mastering these six formulas and the density qualifier cascade will earn you the full 14 marks on any Part B question in this module. The topic is mathematically simple but conceptually rich — examiners reward students who can connect the math to the engineering reasoning (i.e., *why* dp makes IoT interfaces portable across devices).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Interface (UI) Components - Editable and Non-Editable Text Views
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** User Interface (UI) Components - Editable and Non-Editable Text Views

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**TextView** is a user interface (UI) component in the Android framework that displays text to the user. It is the foundational **non-editable** text-display widget, derived from the `android.view.View` superclass, and is characterized by its read-only nature — the application sets the text content, but the user cannot modify it directly through the UI.

**EditText** is a specialized **subclass of `TextView`** that allows the user to edit and modify text content. It is the standard editable text input widget in Android, supporting a wide range of input types (plain text, passwords, numbers, emails, etc.) and integrates with the soft keyboard (IME — Input Method Editor) for data entry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** `EditText` inherits from `TextView`, which means **all TextView attributes and methods are available in EditText**. The key difference lies in editability and the presence of `inputType`, `hint`, and IME-related attributes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — Newspaper vs. Notebook:**
- A **TextView** is like a *newspaper article* — once printed (rendered), readers can only read it, not change the words.
- An **EditText** is like a *blank notebook page* — the user can write, erase, and rewrite content freely.

**Analogy 2 — TV Screen vs. Whiteboard:**
- **TextView** = TV Screen displaying a news ticker. You can only watch.
- **EditText** = A whiteboard where the teacher (user) writes notes that can be changed anytime.

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT Context:** In IoT mobile apps, **TextViews** are used to display sensor readings, device status, and notifications, while **EditText** captures user inputs like device names, threshold values, or Wi-Fi credentials.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Android UI

- **Density-independent Pixels (dp):** Standard unit for layout dimensions. **1 dp ≈ 1 pixel on a 160 dpi screen**.
- **Scale-independent Pixels (sp):** Standard unit for font sizes. **1 sp = 1 dp at the default font scale** (respects user accessibility settings).
- **Pixels (px):** Actual screen pixels — **avoid using for layout**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Use **sp for text sizes** and **dp for everything else** (margins, padding, dimensions).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Attribute Sheet

### 2.1 TextView — Operational Breakdown

The `TextView` widget operates through three core pillars:

1. **Content Layer** — Holds the actual string data (`android:text`).
2. **Style Layer** — Controls typography: color, size, font, weight.
3. **Layout Layer** — Controls positioning, padding, alignment, and sizing.

#### 2.1.1 Key XML Attributes of TextView

| Attribute | Purpose | Common Values |
|---|---|---|
| `android:id` | Unique identifier for the view | `@+id/myTextView` |
| `android:layout_width` | Width of the view | `match_parent`, `wrap_content`, `200dp` |
| `android:layout_height` | Height of the view | `match_parent`, `wrap_content`, `50dp` |
| `android:text` | Text content | `Hello World` |
| `android:textColor` | Color of the text | `#FF0000`, `@color/red` |
| `android:textSize` | Font size (always in **sp**) | `18sp`, `24sp` |
| `android:textStyle` | Style flags | `normal`, `bold`, `italic` |
| `android:typeface` | Font family | `sans`, `serif`, `monospace` |
| `android:fontFamily` | Extended font selection | `sans-serif`, `sans-serif-light` |
| `android:gravity` | Text alignment within view | `left`, `right`, `center`, `center_vertical` |
| `android:padding` | Inner spacing on all sides | `8dp`, `16dp` |
| `android:background` | Background drawable or color | `@drawable/bg`, `#FFFFFF` |
| `android:maxLines` | Maximum visible lines | `1`, `2`, `5` |
| `android:ellipsize` | Truncation style | `none`, `start`, `middle`, `end`, `marquee` |
| `android:autoLink` | Auto-detect links | `none`, `web`, `email`, `phone`, `all` |
| `android:drawableStart` | Compound drawable (left/start) | `@drawable/icon` |
| `android:drawablePadding` | Space around compound drawable | `8dp` |
| `android:textIsSelectable` | Allow long-press text selection | `true`, `false` |
| `android:lineSpacingExtra` | Extra vertical line spacing | `4dp` |
| `android:letterSpacing` | Horizontal character spacing | `0.05` |

#### 2.1.2 Programmatic Access (Java)

```java
TextView tv = findViewById(R.id.myTextView);
tv.setText("Updated Text");
tv.setTextColor(Color.RED);
tv.setTextSize(20f);            // value in sp (float)
tv.setTypeface(null, Typeface.BOLD_ITALIC);
tv.setGravity(Gravity.CENTER);
```

### 2.2 EditText — Operational Breakdown

`EditText` extends `TextView` and adds the **editability** dimension. Its operational pillars:

1. **Input Type Layer** — Defines what kind of text can be entered (`inputType`).
2. **IME Layer** — Controls the soft keyboard and action button.
3. **Listener Layer** — Captures text changes (`TextWatcher`), focus events, and key actions.

#### 2.2.1 Key XML Attributes of EditText

| Attribute | Purpose | Common Values |
|---|---|---|
| `android:inputType` | Keyboard layout & data type | `text`, `textPassword`, `number`, `phone`, `email`, `textMultiLine`, `textCapWords` |
| `android:hint` | Placeholder text | `Enter your name` |
| `android:textColorHint` | Hint text color | `#888888` |
| `android:maxLength` | Maximum character count | `10`, `20` |
| `android:imeOptions` | Action button on keyboard | `actionDone`, `actionGo`, `actionSearch`, `actionNext` |
| `android:cursorVisible` | Show/hide cursor | `true`, `false` |
| `android:selectAllOnFocus` | Select all text on focus | `true`, `false` |
| `android:digits` | Restrict allowed characters | `0123456789` |
| `android:singleLine` | Force single line input | `true`, `false` |
| `android:lines` | Fixed number of visible lines | `3`, `5` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deprecation Notice:** In modern Android, `android:editable` is **deprecated** since API level 3. Always use `android:inputType` to control both the keyboard layout AND the editable nature of the field.

### 2.3 Specialized TextView / EditText Subclasses

| Subclass | Purpose | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| `Button` | Clickable text view | Core UI interaction |
| `CheckedTextView` | Checkable list item text | Settings UIs |
| `AutoCompleteTextView` | Auto-suggestion dropdown (single) | Forms, search fields |
| `MultiAutoCompleteTextView` | Multi-token autocomplete | Email/Tag inputs |
| `TextClock` | Live clock display | IoT dashboards |
| `Switch` | Toggle with text label | IoT device control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In IoT apps, `TextClock` displays real-time timestamps of sensor data, while `EditText` with `inputType="numberDecimal"` is used for entering threshold values like temperature limits (e.g., "Trigger alert above 45.5 °C").
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Example 1 — Basic TextView Layout (XML)

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="16dp">

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/tvTitle"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="IoT Sensor Dashboard"
        android:textSize="24sp"
        android:textStyle="bold"
        android:textColor="#0D47A1"
        android:gravity="center"
        android:padding="12dp"
        android:background="#E3F2FD" />

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/tvStatus"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Device Status: Connected"
        android:textSize="16sp"
        android:textColor="#2E7D32"
        android:drawableStart="@drawable/ic_check"
        android:drawablePadding="8dp"
        android:layout_marginTop="12dp" />

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/tvAutoLink"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Visit https://ktu.edu.in or email support@ktu.edu.in"
        android:autoLink="web|email"
        android:textSize="14sp"
        android:layout_marginTop="8dp" />
</LinearLayout>
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation:**

1. **Root Element** — `LinearLayout` with `orientation="vertical"` stacks children top-to-bottom.
2. **`tvTitle`** — A bold, centered title with custom color (#0D47A1 — Material Blue 900) and a light blue background. Standard header pattern.
3. **`tvStatus`** — Uses `drawableStart` to attach a leading icon (check mark) with 8dp spacing. No extra `ImageView` needed → saves memory.
4. **`tvAutoLink`** — `autoLink="web|email"` automatically detects URLs and email addresses, making them tappable without writing a click handler.

### 3.2 Example 2 — EditText with Multiple Input Types

```xml
<EditText
    android:id="@+id/etDeviceName"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:hint="Enter device name"
    android:inputType="textPersonName|textCapWords"
    android:maxLength="30"
    android:imeOptions="actionNext" />

<EditText
    android:id="@+id/etThreshold"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:hint="Temperature threshold (°C)"
    android:inputType="numberDecimal|numberSigned"
    android:maxLength="6"
    android:imeOptions="actionDone" />

<EditText
    android:id="@+id/etPassword"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:hint="Wi-Fi password"
    android:inputType="textPassword" />

<EditText
    android:id="@+id/etDescription"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="120dp"
    android:hint="Notes (multi-line allowed)"
    android:inputType="textMultiLine|textCapSentences"
    android:gravity="top|start" />
```

**Analytical Matrix — Input Type Behavior:**

$$ \text{Field} \;\mapsto\; (\text{Keyboard Type},\; \text{Validation Rule},\; \text{Use Case}) $$

| Field | inputType Value | Keyboard Shown | Validation | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `etDeviceName` | `textPersonName` $\vert$ `textCapWords` | Alphabetic, auto-capitalize | Letters only | Name of IoT device |
| `etThreshold` | `numberDecimal` $\vert$ `numberSigned` | Numeric + dot + minus | Numbers only | Sensor threshold |
| `etPassword` | `textPassword` | Standard, masked | Hidden chars | Wi-Fi/Account password |
| `etDescription` | `textMultiLine` $\vert$ `textCapSentences` | Standard, Enter = newline | No restriction | Notes/comments |

### 3.3 Example 3 — TextWatcher Implementation in Activity

```java
EditText etThreshold = findViewById(R.id.etThreshold);
TextView tvCharCount = findViewById(R.id.tvCharCount);

etThreshold.addTextChangedListener(new TextWatcher() {
    @Override
    public void beforeTextChanged(CharSequence s, int start, int count, int after) {
        // Called BEFORE the text is changed
        // Useful for saving cursor position or undo state
    }

    @Override
    public void onTextChanged(CharSequence s, int start, int before, int count) {
        // Called DURING the text change (text not yet committed)
        tvCharCount.setText("Characters: " + s.length());
    }

    @Override
    public void afterTextChanged(Editable s) {
        // Called AFTER the text has been updated
        // BEST place for validation logic
        String input = s.toString();
        if (!input.isEmpty()) {
            try {
                double value = Double.parseDouble(input);
                if (value < -50.0 || value > 150.0) {
                    etThreshold.setError("Threshold must be between -50 and 150");
                } else {
                    etThreshold.setError(null);
                }
            } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
                etThreshold.setError("Invalid number");
            }
        }
    }
});
```

**Explanation of the Lifecycle:**

$$ \text{User Types} \;\to\; \text{beforeTextChanged} \;\to\; \text{onTextChanged} \;\to\; \text{afterTextChanged} \;\to\; \text{UI Updates} $$

### 3.4 Example 4 — AutoCompleteTextView (ACTV)

**XML Layout:**

```xml
<AutoCompleteTextView
    android:id="@+id/actvSensor"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:hint="Select sensor type"
    android:completionThreshold="1" />
```

**Activity Code:**

```java
String[] sensors = {"Temperature", "Humidity", "Pressure",
                    "Motion", "Light", "Gas"};

ArrayAdapter<String> adapter = new ArrayAdapter<>(
    this,
    android.R.layout.simple_dropdown_item_1line,
    sensors
);

AutoCompleteTextView actv = findViewById(R.id.actvSensor);
actv.setAdapter(adapter);
```

**Explanation:**
- `completionThreshold="1"` means suggestions appear after the user types **1 character**.
- The `ArrayAdapter` binds the data source (`sensors[]` array) to the dropdown.
- To handle the click on a suggestion, override `actv.setOnItemClickListener(...)`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy Insight:** `AutoCompleteTextView` and `MultiAutoCompleteTextView` both **extend `EditText`**, NOT `TextView` directly. This is a key inheritance detail frequently asked in KTU viva questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Class Hierarchy of Text Components

```mermaid
graph TD
    viewNode["android.view.View"]
    textViewNode["TextView Non Editable"]
    editTextNode["EditText Editable"]
    buttonNode["Button"]
    checkedTVNode["CheckedTextView"]
    autoCompleteNode["AutoCompleteTextView"]
    multiAutoNode["MultiAutoCompleteTextView"]
    textClockNode["TextClock"]
    switchNode["Switch"]

    viewNode --> textViewNode
    textViewNode --> editTextNode
    textViewNode --> buttonNode
    textViewNode --> checkedTVNode
    textViewNode --> textClockNode
    editTextNode --> autoCompleteNode
    autoCompleteNode --> multiAutoNode
    textViewNode --> switchNode
```

### 4.2 Text Input Processing Flow with TextWatcher

```mermaid
graph LR
    userInput["User Types Character"]
    beforeChange["beforeTextChanged"]
    onChange["onTextChanged"]
    afterChange["afterTextChanged"]
    updateUI["Update TextView Counter"]
    validation["Validate Input Range"]
    showError["setError on EditText"]
    accept["Accept Valid Input"]

    userInput --> beforeChange
    beforeChange --> onChange
    onChange --> updateUI
    onChange --> afterChange
    afterChange --> validation
    validation -->|"Valid"| accept
    validation -->|"Invalid"| showError
```

### 4.3 EditText Attribute Resolution Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNode["EditText Inflated from XML"]
    readInputType["Read android inputType"]
    readHint["Read android hint"]
    readImeOptions["Read android imeOptions"]
    readMaxLength["Read android maxLength"]
    showKeyboard["Show Soft Keyboard with Type"]
    displayHint["Display Placeholder if Empty"]
    setImeAction["Set Action Button Done or Next"]
    enforceLimit["Enforce Character Limit"]
    readyNode["Ready for User Input"]

    startNode --> readInputType
    startNode --> readHint
    startNode --> readImeOptions
    startNode --> readMaxLength
    readInputType --> showKeyboard
    readHint --> displayHint
    readImeOptions --> setImeAction
    readMaxLength --> enforceLimit
    showKeyboard --> readyNode
    displayHint --> readyNode
    setImeAction --> readyNode
    enforceLimit --> readyNode
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — TextView vs EditText in an IoT App

```mermaid
graph TB
    appLayer["IoT Mobile Application"]
    sensorData["Incoming Sensor Data"]
    userAction["User Input Action"]
    tvBlock["TextView Display Module"]
    etBlock["EditText Input Module"]
    watcherBlock["TextWatcher Validation Module"]
    submitBlock["Submit and Send to Device"]
    displayOut["Read Only Output to Screen"]
    validOut["Validated Data"]

    sensorData --> tvBlock
    userAction --> etBlock
    etBlock --> watcherBlock
    watcherBlock -->|"Valid"| submitBlock
    watcherBlock -->|"Invalid"| etBlock
    tvBlock --> displayOut
    submitBlock --> validOut

    appLayer --> tvBlock
    appLayer --> etBlock
    appLayer --> watcherBlock
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above use raw uppercase alphanumeric labels inside node text blocks to ensure Mermaid compilation compatibility. Arrows (`-->`, `-->|label|`) represent class inheritance and runtime data flow respectively.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
Differentiate between `TextView` and `EditText` in Android. List any **two attributes unique to EditText**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **TextView** is a non-editable UI component used to display text to the user. The user cannot modify its content directly at runtime. *(1 mark)*
- **EditText** is a subclass of `TextView` that allows the user to edit/modify text. It supports soft keyboard input and IME actions. *(1 mark)*
- **Two attributes unique / primarily associated with EditText:** *(1 mark)*
  1. `android:inputType` — defines the keyboard layout and accepted data type (e.g., `textPassword`, `numberDecimal`).
  2. `android:hint` — displays placeholder text when the field is empty (also available in TextView from API 14+, but primarily associated with EditText).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**
What is the purpose of the `android:inputType` attribute in `EditText`? Give **two examples** with their effects.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `android:inputType` attribute specifies the **type of data** the EditText accepts and configures the **soft keyboard layout** accordingly. *(1 mark)*

- **Example 1:** `android:inputType="numberDecimal"` — displays a numeric keypad with a decimal point; only numbers and a dot can be entered. *(1 mark)*
- **Example 2:** `android:inputType="textPassword"` — displays a standard keyboard but masks the entered characters as dots/asterisks for security. *(1 mark)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Explain the `TextView` class hierarchy in Android. List any **seven important XML attributes** of `TextView` with their purpose. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design an XML layout for a **login screen** containing two `EditText` fields (username and password) and a `Button`. Specify appropriate `inputType`, `hint`, and `imeOptions` for each. Write the corresponding Java code to read the input values when the button is clicked. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — (7 Marks):**

**Class Hierarchy (2 marks):**

$$ \texttt{java.lang.Object} \;\to\; \texttt{android.view.View} \;\to\; \texttt{android.widget.TextView} $$

`TextView` is the base class for many text-displaying widgets like `Button`, `EditText`, `CheckedTextView`, `TextClock`, etc.

**Seven Important TextView XML Attributes (5 marks — 0.5 each + 1.5 for explanation):**

| # | Attribute | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `android:text` | Sets the displayed text content |
| 2 | `android:textSize` | Sets font size in scaled pixels (sp) |
| 3 | `android:textColor` | Sets text color (hex value or color resource) |
| 4 | `android:textStyle` | Sets bold, italic, or normal style |
| 5 | `android:gravity` | Aligns text within the view bounds (left/center/right) |
| 6 | `android:maxLines` | Limits maximum visible lines (1, 2, 5, etc.) |
| 7 | `android:ellipsize` | Truncates text with "..." at start, middle, end, or marquee |

---

**Model Solution for (b) — (7 Marks):**

**`activity_login.xml` (4 marks):**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout
    xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="24dp">

    <EditText
        android:id="@+id/etUsername"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:hint="Username"
        android:inputType="text"
        android:imeOptions="actionNext"
        android:maxLength="20" />

    <EditText
        android:id="@+id/etPassword"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:hint="Password"
        android:inputType="textPassword"
        android:imeOptions="actionDone"
        android:maxLength="16" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/btnLogin"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Login"
        android:layout_marginTop="16dp" />
</LinearLayout>
```

**`LoginActivity.java` (3 marks):**

```java
package com.example.iotapp;

import android.os.Bundle;
import android.widget.Button;
import android.widget.EditText;
import android.widget.Toast;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class LoginActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    EditText etUsername, etPassword;
    Button btnLogin;

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_login);

        etUsername = findViewById(R.id.etUsername);
        etPassword = findViewById(R.id.etPassword);
        btnLogin   = findViewById(R.id.btnLogin);

        btnLogin.setOnClickListener(v -> {
            String username = etUsername.getText().toString().trim();
            String password = etPassword.getText().toString().trim();

            if (username.isEmpty()) {
                etUsername.setError("Username required");
                etUsername.requestFocus();
                return;
            }
            if (password.isEmpty()) {
                etPassword.setError("Password required");
                etPassword.requestFocus();
                return;
            }

            // Authentication logic here
            Toast.makeText(this,
                "Login attempted for: " + username,
                Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show();
        });
    }
}
```

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model] (Internal Choice)

**(a)** What is a **`TextWatcher`** in Android? Explain its **three callback methods** with their signatures. Write Java code to implement a real-time **character counter** that displays the count below an `EditText` as the user types. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the use of **`AutoCompleteTextView`** and **`MultiAutoCompleteTextView`**. Provide a working XML and Java example showing `AutoCompleteTextView` suggesting a list of IoT device names. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** `TextWatcher` is an interface in the `android.text` package that listens for changes in an `EditText` (or any `TextView`). It has three callback methods invoked in sequence whenever the text content changes.

**Three Callback Methods (3 marks — 1 each):**

1. **`beforeTextChanged(CharSequence s, int start, int count, int after)`**
   Called *before* the text is modified. Useful for saving state or cursor position.

2. **`onTextChanged(CharSequence s, int start, int before, int count)`**
   Called *during* the modification, before the new text is rendered. Used for live UI updates.

3. **`afterTextChanged(Editable s)`**
   Called *after* the text has been updated. **Ideal place for validation logic**.

**Character Counter Implementation (3 marks):**

**`activity_counter.xml`:**

```xml
<LinearLayout
    xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="16dp">

    <EditText
        android:id="@+id/etMessage"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:hint="Type your message"
        android:inputType="textMultiLine|textCapSentences"
        android:maxLength="160"
        android:minLines="3" />

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/tvCounter"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="0 / 160"
        android:gravity="end"
        android:textColor="#666666" />
</LinearLayout>
```

**`CounterActivity.java`:**

```java
package com.example.iotapp;

import android.graphics.Color;
import android.os.Bundle;
import android.text.Editable;
import android.text.TextWatcher;
import android.widget.EditText;
import android.widget.TextView;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class CounterActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_counter);

        EditText etMessage = findViewById(R.id.etMessage);
        TextView tvCounter = findViewById(R.id.tvCounter);
        final int MAX = 160;

        etMessage.addTextChangedListener(new TextWatcher() {
            @Override
            public void beforeTextChanged(CharSequence s, int start, int count, int after) {
                // No action needed here
            }

            @Override
            public void onTextChanged(CharSequence s, int start, int before, int count) {
                int len = s.length();
                tvCounter.setText(len + " / " + MAX);

                if (len > MAX * 0.9) {
                    tvCounter.setTextColor(Color.RED);
                } else {
                    tvCounter.setTextColor(Color.parseColor("#666666"));
                }
            }

            @Override
            public void afterTextChanged(Editable s) {
                // Validation logic can be placed here
            }
        });
    }
}
```

---

**Model Solution for (b) — (7 Marks):**

**Theory (2 marks):**

- **`AutoCompleteTextView` (ACTV)** — provides auto-suggestions as the user types, based on a predefined list. **Only one token** can be selected at a time.
- **`MultiAutoCompleteTextView` (MACTV)** — extends `ACTV` and allows the user to select **multiple comma-separated tokens**, each with its own auto-suggestion. Requires a `setTokenizer()` call (typically `CommaTokenizer`).

**XML Layout (2 marks) — `activity_autocomplete.xml`:**

```xml
<LinearLayout
    xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="16dp">

    <TextView
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Select IoT Device:"
        android:textSize="16sp"
        android:textStyle="bold" />

    <AutoCompleteTextView
        android:id="@+id/actvDevice"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:hint="Type device name"
        android:completionThreshold="1" />

    <TextView
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Add Tags (comma-separated):"
        android:textSize="16sp"
        android:textStyle="bold"
        android:layout_marginTop="16dp" />

    <MultiAutoCompleteTextView
        android:id="@+id/mactvTags"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:hint="e.g., sensor, indoor, critical" />
</LinearLayout>
```

**Activity Code (3 marks) — `AutocompleteActivity.java`:**

```java
package com.example.iotapp;

import android.os.Bundle;
import android.widget.ArrayAdapter;
import android.widget.AutoCompleteTextView;
import android.widget.MultiAutoCompleteTextView;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class AutocompleteActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_autocomplete);

        String[] devices = {
            "DHT11 Temperature Sensor",
            "MQ-2 Gas Sensor",
            "HC-SR04 Ultrasonic",
            "PIR Motion Sensor",
            "LDR Light Sensor",
            "BMP180 Pressure Sensor"
        };

        ArrayAdapter<String> adapter = new ArrayAdapter<>(
            this,
            android.R.layout.simple_dropdown_item_1line,
            devices
        );

        // Single-token autocomplete
        AutoCompleteTextView actv = findViewById(R.id.actvDevice);
        actv.setAdapter(adapter);

        // Multi-token autocomplete (comma-separated)
        MultiAutoCompleteTextView mactv = findViewById(R.id.mactvTags);
        mactv.setAdapter(adapter);
        mactv.setTokenizer(new MultiAutoCompleteTextView.CommaTokenizer());
    }
}
```

**Explanation of `CommaTokenizer`:** It splits the input at every comma, allowing the user to add multiple comma-separated tags, each with its own independent auto-suggestion popup.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 (2 marks lost):** Students write `android:editable="true"` for `EditText` — this is **deprecated** since API level 3. Always use `android:inputType` to define both editability and keyboard type.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 (3 marks lost):** Forgetting `setTokenizer()` on `MultiAutoCompleteTextView`. Without it, suggestions won't trigger for subsequent tokens after the delimiter.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 (2 marks lost):** Using `dp` for `textSize` instead of `sp`. **Always use `sp`** (scale-independent pixels) for text size to respect user font accessibility settings.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 (1 mark lost):** Not trimming input with `.trim()` before validation — leading/trailing whitespace causes false validation failures.
> 5. **Hierarchy Mistake (1 mark lost):** Writing "EditText extends `TextInputLayout`" — **WRONG**. It extends `TextView` (which extends `View`). `TextInputLayout` is a Material Design **wrapper**, not a parent class.
> 6. **Common Mistake 5 (1 mark lost):** Forgetting the `|` (pipe) separator when combining input types (e.g., `textPersonName|textCapWords`). A simple space breaks the combination.
> 7. **Viva Trap:** Saying "EditText is a separate class, not related to TextView" — **WRONG**. They share an inheritance chain.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Quick Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [ ] **`TextView` = Non-editable; `EditText` = Editable** (subclass of `TextView`)
- [ ] **View hierarchy:** `View` → `TextView` → `EditText` → `AutoCompleteTextView` → `MultiAutoCompleteTextView`
- [ ] **Key TextView attributes:** `text`, `textColor`, `textSize` (in **sp**), `textStyle`, `gravity`, `maxLines`, `ellipsize`, `autoLink`, `drawableStart/End`
- [ ] **Key EditText attributes:** `inputType`, `hint`, `imeOptions`, `maxLength`, `textColorHint`
- [ ] **Use `inputType`**, NOT the deprecated `editable` attribute
- [ ] **Common `inputType` values:** `text`, `textPassword`, `numberDecimal`, `phone`, `email`, `textMultiLine`, `textPersonName`, `textCapWords`, `numberSigned`
- [ ] **Combine inputTypes with `|`** (pipe), NOT space — e.g., `textPersonName|textCapWords`
- [ ] **`TextWatcher` has 3 methods:** `beforeTextChanged`, `onTextChanged`, `afterTextChanged` — in that exact order
- [ ] **Best place for validation = `afterTextChanged()`**
- [ ] **`AutoCompleteTextView`** = single token selection; **`MultiAutoCompleteTextView`** = multiple comma-separated tokens
- [ ] **`MultiAutoCompleteTextView` requires `setTokenizer()`** (typically `CommaTokenizer`)
- [ ] **Use `sp` for text**, **`dp` for layout dimensions**, **`px` only for exact pixel work**
- [ ] **For text selection in `TextView`:** use `android:textIsSelectable="true"`
- [ ] **Compound drawables** (`drawableStart/Top/End/Bottom`) save memory vs. separate `ImageView` + `TextView`
- [ ] **IoT Application Mapping:**
  - `TextView` → displays sensor values, device status, timestamps
  - `EditText` → captures device names, threshold values, Wi-Fi credentials
  - `TextWatcher` → validates user input in real-time
  - `AutoCompleteTextView` → assists in selecting from known sensor/device list

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "design a UI," always provide a **complete, runnable XML layout** with proper IDs, realistic `dp`/`sp` values, and accompanying Java/Kotlin code. Examiners award full marks only when the solution is **production-ready**, not just attribute lists.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buttons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Buttons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Button?

A **Button** is a fundamental user interface (UI) widget in Android (and most modern mobile platforms) that responds to user taps/clicks to trigger a specific action, event, or command. In the context of **Mobile App Development for IoT**, a button is the primary human-to-device interaction primitive — the digital equivalent of a physical switch that sends a signal to a microcontroller, cloud service, or local actuator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *A Button is an `android.view.View` subclass that displays a tappable, rectangular region capable of receiving user touch input and dispatching a click event to a registered listener, often used as a trigger for sending commands to IoT devices, navigating between activities, or executing business logic.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Smart Home Remote" 🏠

Imagine you have a **physical smart-home remote control** in your hand. Each plastic key on that remote:
- Has a **label** (the printed text/symbol)
- Detects a **press** (the click)
- Sends a **signal** (the action — turn on light, lock door)
- May **visually change** when pressed (the ripple effect / state change)

A software Button is **exactly this** — a digital key. Instead of infrared, it sends a Java/Kotlin method call. Instead of a remote, it lives inside an XML layout file. Instead of toggling a physical LED, it might toggle a relay via MQTT, HTTP, or BLE to an IoT device.

### 1.3 Taxonomy of Buttons in Android

Android provides a rich hierarchy of clickable controls, each with specific use-cases:

| Button Type | Class | Primary Use-Case in IoT |
|---|---|---|
| **Button** | `android.widget.Button` | Generic "Send Command", "Connect", "Subscribe" |
| **ImageButton** | `android.widget.ImageButton` | Icon-based controls (e.g., power icon, fan icon) |
| **ToggleButton** | `android.widget.ToggleButton` | Two-state ON/OFF commands (e.g., enable sensor) |
| **Switch** | `android.widget.Switch` | Modern ON/OFF toggle (Material Design) |
| **FloatingActionButton (FAB)** | `com.google.android.material.floatingactionbutton.FloatingActionButton` | Primary action (e.g., "Send Emergency Alert") |
| **RadioButton** | `android.widget.RadioButton` | Mutually exclusive selection (e.g., choose protocol: MQTT/CoAP/HTTP) |
| **CheckBox** | `android.widget.CheckBox` | Multi-select (e.g., select multiple sensors to monitor) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 emphasizes the **Event-Driven Interaction Model** — buttons are the *first-class citizens* of this model. You must know how to declare them in XML AND handle their events in Java/Kotlin code.

### 1.4 The Two Pillars of Button Usage

1. **Declaration (XML):** Defining the button's appearance, position, and properties in `res/layout/activity_main.xml`.
2. **Event Handling (Kotlin/Java):** Wiring the button to an `OnClickListener` in the `Activity` / `Fragment` source file.

Both pillars are **equally weighted** in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Button Lifecycle (Theoretical Flow)

When a user interacts with a button on an Android device, the following sequence unfolds within the Android Framework:

1. **Touch Down Event** — The `MotionEvent.ACTION_DOWN` is dispatched by the system. The button enters the *pressed* state and shows a ripple/drawable feedback.
2. **Touch Up Event** — If the finger is still inside the button bounds when `ACTION_UP` fires, the click is **confirmed**. A `performClick()` invocation triggers the listener.
3. **Listener Callback** — The registered `View.OnClickListener.onClick(View v)` method is invoked on the **Main (UI) Thread**.
4. **Action Execution** — The code inside `onClick()` runs. In an IoT app, this typically means: building an MQTT publish payload, calling a REST API, or sending a BLE write.
5. **UI Refresh (optional)** — The thread updates the UI, shows a Toast, or navigates to another activity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The entire flow happens on the **UI Thread**. Long-running network operations (e.g., HTTP call to an IoT cloud) MUST be moved to a background thread (`Thread`, `Coroutine`, or `AsyncTask` — now deprecated) to prevent **Application Not Responding (ANR)** errors.

## 2.2 XML Attributes — The KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Attribute | Purpose | Allowed Values | IoT Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `android:id` | Unique identifier to reference in code | `@+id/btnConnect` | Reference for click binding |
| `android:layout_width` | Horizontal sizing | `wrap_content`, `match_parent`, explicit `dp` | `match_parent` for full-width "EMERGENCY" button |
| `android:layout_height` | Vertical sizing | `wrap_content`, `match_parent`, explicit `dp` | `wrap_content` for compact controls |
| `android:text` | Displayed label | String resource or literal | `"Connect to Sensor"` |
| `android:onClick` | XML-declared click handler (no Kotlin code needed) | Method name in Activity | `android:onClick="sendCommand"` |
| `android:background` | Background color/drawable | `@color/...`, `@drawable/...` | Custom rounded-rectangle drawable |
| `android:textColor` | Text color | `@color/...` | White on dark background |
| `android:enabled` | Enable/disable state | `true` / `false` | `false` until MQTT client connects |
| `android:drawableLeft` / `drawableStart` | Icon next to text | `@drawable/ic_power` | Power icon on a "Power" button |
| `android:padding` | Inner spacing | `dp` value | `16dp` for tappable area |
| `style="@style/Widget.Material3.Button"` | Material Design preset | Material3 styles | Elevated, Filled, Tonal, Outlined, Text |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write `android:onClick="sendCommand()"` with parentheses. ❌ The correct XML syntax is `android:onClick="sendCommand"` (no parentheses, no parameters). The method in Kotlin must accept exactly one `View` parameter.

## 2.3 Event Handling Approaches — Four Valid Patterns

| # | Approach | Syntax Style | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **XML `android:onClick`** | Pure XML, minimal Kotlin | Simple apps, prototypes, KTU labs |
| 2 | **Anonymous Inner Class** | Verbose Java-style | When the same listener is used once only |
| 3 | **Activity implements `OnClickListener`** | Centralized dispatch | Multiple buttons share one Activity |
| 4 | **Lambda Expression (Kotlin)** | `setOnClickListener { ... }` | **Modern Android — RECOMMENDED** |

### 2.4 Formula Sheet (UI Sizing)

While buttons are not "mathematical," the *layout geometry* uses two key units:

$$
\text{density-independent pixel (dp)} = \frac{\text{physical pixel (px)}}{\text{screen density (dpi)} / 160}
$$

The minimum **recommended tappable size** for an accessibility-compliant button is:

$$
\text{Height}_{\text{min}} \geq 48 \text{ dp}, \quad \text{Width}_{\text{min}} \geq 48 \text{ dp}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
