> **Material Design Spec:** The official touch target is **48 × 48 dp** (≈ 9 mm). Buttons smaller than this are flagged in accessibility audits. KTU may ask you to justify this dimension in a design question.

## 2.5 Real-World IoT Utility

In an IoT mobile app, buttons are used to:

- **Send MQTT publish messages** → e.g., `btnToggle` → publishes `"ON"` to topic `home/livingroom/light`.
- **Trigger HTTP REST POSTs** → e.g., `btnLockDoor` → POSTs `{ "command": "lock" }` to cloud endpoint.
- **Initiate BLE GATT writes** → e.g., `btnCalibrate` → writes `0x01` to characteristic UUID.
- **Toggle device state UI** → e.g., `btnRefresh` → re-queries sensor telemetry and updates the dashboard.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From XML Declaration to a Working Click Handler

We will now **exhaustively derive** a complete, working button — step-by-step — combining the XML layout, the Kotlin Activity, and the IoT action.

### Step 1 — Create the Layout File

File: `res/layout/activity_main.xml`

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout
    xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="24dp">

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/tvStatus"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Device: Disconnected"
        android:textSize="18sp"
        android:layout_marginBottom="24dp" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/btnConnect"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Connect to IoT Device"
        android:minHeight="48dp" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/btnToggle"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Toggle LED"
        android:enabled="false"
        android:minHeight="48dp" />
</LinearLayout>
```

**Logic of each line:**

- `LinearLayout` is the parent container with vertical orientation.
- The first `Button` (`btnConnect`) establishes a connection; it is **always enabled**.
- The second `Button` (`btnToggle`) is the action button; it starts `enabled="false"` because the device is not yet connected.
- `minHeight="48dp"` enforces the **accessibility rule** discussed in §2.4.

### Step 2 — Reference the Buttons in Kotlin (Inflation)

File: `MainActivity.kt`

```kotlin
package com.example.iotcontrol

import android.os.Bundle
import android.widget.Button
import android.widget.TextView
import android.widget.Toast
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity

class MainActivity : AppCompatActivity() {

    private lateinit var btnConnect: Button
    private lateinit var btnToggle: Button
    private lateinit var tvStatus: TextView

    private var isConnected: Boolean = false

    override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main)

        // Inflation step — bridge XML <-> Kotlin
        btnConnect = findViewById(R.id.btnConnect)
        btnToggle  = findViewById(R.id.btnToggle)
        tvStatus   = findViewById(R.id.tvStatus)
    }
}
```

**Why this step?** `setContentView()` parses the XML and creates the in-memory View tree. `findViewById()` walks that tree using the unique `android:id` to hand you a Kotlin reference. Without this, the button exists on screen but is inaccessible to your code.

### Step 3 — Attach the Click Listener (Lambda Pattern — Recommended)

```kotlin
override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
    super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)
    setContentView(R.layout.activity_main)

    btnConnect = findViewById(R.id.btnConnect)
    btnToggle  = findViewById(R.id.btnToggle)
    tvStatus   = findViewById(R.id.tvStatus)

    // Lambda — the modern Kotlin way
    btnConnect.setOnClickListener {
        connectToDevice()
    }

    btnToggle.setOnClickListener {
        toggleLed()
    }
}
```

**Mechanism explained:** `setOnClickListener { ... }` is a higher-order Kotlin function that accepts a lambda. Internally, the framework wraps the lambda into an anonymous `View.OnClickListener` instance. When the user taps, Android calls `onClick(view)` on the UI thread, which executes your lambda body.

### Step 4 — Implement the IoT Logic

```kotlin
private fun connectToDevice() {
    // Simulated MQTT / HTTP connection
    isConnected = true
    tvStatus.text = "Device: Connected ✅"
    btnConnect.isEnabled = false
    btnToggle.isEnabled  = true
    Toast.makeText(this, "Connected to broker", Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show()
}

private fun toggleLed() {
    // In production: client.publish("home/led", "TOGGLE")
    Toast.makeText(this, "Command 'TOGGLE' sent to device", Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show()
}
```

**Flow analysis:**

$$
\text{User Tap} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{setOnClickListener} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{connectToDevice()/toggleLed()} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{IoT Action}
$$

### Step 5 — Equivalent: XML `android:onClick` Approach (No Listener)

**XML:**
```xml
<Button
    android:id="@+id/btnConnect"
    android:onClick="onConnectClick"
    android:text="Connect" />
```

**Kotlin:**
```kotlin
fun onConnectClick(view: View) {
    connectToDevice()
}
```

**Rule:** The method name in XML must **exactly** match a public method in the hosting Activity, and the method **must** accept a single `View` parameter (or be parameterless in some legacy Java variants — but KTU prefers the `View` signature).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Four Event-Handling Patterns

| Criterion | XML `onClick` | Anonymous Class | Activity implements | Lambda (Kotlin) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lines of code** | 1 (XML) + 1 (Kotlin) | 5–7 | 3 + override | 1 |
| **Readability** | High | Low | High | **Highest** |
| **Reusability** | Per-view | Per-instance | Activity-wide | Per-instance |
| **KTU-recommended?** | ✅ (for quick demos) | ❌ (verbose) | ✅ (many buttons) | ✅✅ (modern) |
| **Risk of leak** | None | Low (must remove in `onDestroy`) | None | Low (auto-cleared with view) |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Customizing the Button Appearance

A KTU question may ask you to create a **rounded, colored button** programmatically. The derivation:

**Step A:** Define a drawable in `res/drawable/bg_rounded_button.xml`:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<shape xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:shape="rectangle">
    <solid android:color="#1976D2" />
    <corners android:radius="12dp" />
    <stroke
        android:width="2dp"
        android:color="#0D47A1" />
</shape>
```

**Step B:** Apply to the button:
```xml
<Button
    android:id="@+id/btnSend"
    android:layout_width="wrap_content"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:background="@drawable/bg_rounded_button"
    android:text="SEND COMMAND"
    android:textColor="#FFFFFF"
    android:padding="16dp" />
```

**Mathematical relationship of corner radius to perceived "softness":**

$$
\text{Visual Softness} \;\propto\; \text{corner radius (dp)}
$$

A radius of **0 dp** → sharp square; **≥ 50% of height** → fully circular (pill shape).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Hierarchy of Clickable Widgets

This Mermaid diagram shows where `Button` sits in the Android `View` inheritance tree — a frequent KTU 2-mark question.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["android.view.View"] --> B["android.widget.TextView"]
    B --> C["android.widget.Button"]
    C --> D["android.widget.CompoundButton"]
    D --> E["android.widget.ToggleButton"]
    D --> F["android.widget.CheckBox"]
    D --> G["android.widget.RadioButton"]
    D --> H["android.widget.Switch"]
    C --> I["android.widget.ImageButton"]
    C --> J["MaterialButton"]
    J --> K["FloatingActionButton"]
    J --> L["ExtendedFloatingActionButton"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `Button` extends `TextView` (so it inherits all text-rendering capabilities).
- `CompoundButton` is the *abstract* parent of all two-state buttons (toggle, checkbox, radio, switch).
- `MaterialButton` (from Material Components library) extends `Button` to add elevation, ripple, and corner-shape customization.

## 4.2 End-to-End Button Event Flow (IoT Context)

This flowchart traces a single button press all the way from the user's finger to the IoT device and back.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User taps Button"] --> B["ACTION_DOWN event"]
    B --> C{"Touch released inside bounds?"}
    C -- No --> D["Event cancelled — no click"]
    C -- Yes --> E["performClick() invoked"]
    E --> F["onClickListener.onClick(view) runs"]
    F --> G["Execute business logic e.g. MQTT publish"]
    G --> H{"Long-running network call?"}
    H -- No --> I["Update UI directly"]
    H -- Yes --> J["Dispatch to coroutine or thread"]
    J --> K["Send payload via MQTT / HTTP / BLE"]
    K --> L["Receive device ACK"]
    L --> M["Update UI on main thread"]
    I --> N["Show Toast or navigate"]
    M --> N
```

**Key insight:** Every click results in **one** `onClick()` call on the **UI thread**. Network I/O must be offloaded to a background dispatcher to avoid ANR (Application Not Responding) errors.

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Button → IoT Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UI["UI Layer (Activity)"]
        U1["XML Layout"]
        U2["Kotlin findViewById"]
        U3["setOnClickListener lambda"]
    end

    subgraph LOGIC["Logic Layer"]
        L1["CommandBuilder"]
        L2["NetworkDispatcher"]
    end

    subgraph IO["IO Layer"]
        I1["MQTT Client"]
        I2["HTTP Client"]
        I3["BLE GATT"]
    end

    subgraph DEVICE["Physical / Cloud IoT"]
        D1["ESP32 / Arduino"]
        D2["AWS IoT Core"]
        D3["Raspberry Pi"]
    end

    U1 --> U2 --> U3 --> L1 --> L2
    L2 --> I1
    L2 --> I2
    L2 --> I3
    I1 --> D2
    I2 --> D2
    I1 --> D1
    I3 --> D1
    I2 --> D3
```

**Reading guide:** The button lives in the **UI Layer**. Its click triggers a **Logic Layer** command, which is dispatched by a **Network Layer** to whichever transport is appropriate, finally reaching a physical or cloud device.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a Button widget in Android. List any FOUR XML attributes used to configure a Button with an example value for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Button** is a UI widget derived from `android.widget.Button` that displays a tappable region and triggers an action upon user click. It is used in IoT apps to send commands to connected devices.

Four XML attributes:

| # | Attribute | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `android:id` | `android:id="@+id/btnConnect"` |
| 2 | `android:text` | `android:text="Connect"` |
| 3 | `android:layout_width` | `android:layout_width="match_parent"` |
| 4 | `android:onClick` | `android:onClick="onConnectClick"` |

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for attribute + example = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a `Button` and a `CompoundButton` in Android. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `Button` | `CompoundButton` |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Concrete class | Abstract class |
| **State** | Stateless (single click action) | Two-state (checked / unchecked) |
| **Example** | `android.widget.Button` | `android.widget.ToggleButton` |
| **Inherits from** | `TextView` | `Button` |
| **Checkable** | No | Yes (has `isChecked()` method) |

*(1.5 marks for tabular difference + 1.5 marks for examples = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 📌 Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **event-driven interaction model** in Android with a neat diagram. How does a `Button` fit into this model? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write the **XML layout** and corresponding **Kotlin Activity code** to create a button labeled `"Send Command"` that, when clicked, displays a Toast message `"Command Sent to IoT Device"`. Use the **lambda** event-handling approach. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The **event-driven interaction model** is a programming paradigm where the flow of the program is determined by **events** — user actions (taps, swipes), system events (battery low), or sensor events. The application defines **listeners** (event handlers) and waits for the framework to dispatch events to them.

**Key participants:**
- **Event Source:** The widget that generates the event (e.g., the `Button`).
- **Event Listener:** An interface implemented by the app to handle the event (e.g., `View.OnClickListener`).
- **Event Handler:** The actual method that runs when the event occurs (e.g., `onClick()`).
- **Main Looper / UI Thread:** The thread on which all view events are queued and dispatched.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["User touches screen"] --> B["InputManager / WindowManager"]
    B --> C["View.dispatchTouchEvent()"]
    C --> D["Button.onTouchEvent()"]
    D --> E["performClick()"]
    E --> F["OnClickListener.onClick(view)"]
    F --> G["Application code executes"]
```

**Role of a Button:** A button is a *passive* event source — it does nothing until the framework confirms a valid tap, at which point it activates its registered `OnClickListener`. The button encapsulates both the **visual state** (pressed, focused, ripple animation) and the **event-dispatch logic**, making it the simplest event-source in the Android framework.

*(2 marks: definition + 4 key participants listed above) + (2 marks: diagram) + (2 marks: button's role) + (1 mark: conclusion) = 7 marks*

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: XML — `res/layout/activity_main.xml`** *(3 marks)*

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout
    xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:gravity="center"
    android:padding="24dp">

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/btnSend"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Send Command"
        android:textSize="18sp"
        android:minHeight="48dp" />
</LinearLayout>
```

**Step 2: Kotlin — `MainActivity.kt`** *(3 marks)*

```kotlin
package com.example.iotapp

import android.os.Bundle
import android.widget.Button
import android.widget.Toast
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity

class MainActivity : AppCompatActivity() {
    override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main)

        val btnSend: Button = findViewById(R.id.btnSend)

        // Lambda event-handling approach
        btnSend.setOnClickListener {
            Toast.makeText(
                this,
                "Command Sent to IoT Device",
                Toast.LENGTH_SHORT
            ).show()
        }
    }
}
```

**Step 3: Explanation** *(1 mark)*

- `findViewById(R.id.btnSend)` obtains a reference to the button declared in XML using its `android:id`.
- `setOnClickListener { ... }` registers an `OnClickListener` whose `onClick()` method runs the lambda body on every valid click.
- `Toast.makeText(...).show()` creates and displays a transient popup message at the bottom of the screen.

*(Distribution: 3 + 3 + 1 = 7 marks)*

---

### 📌 Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the **four approaches** to handle button click events in Android (XML onClick, anonymous class, Activity-implements, Kotlin lambda). State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design an IoT control app interface with **three buttons** — `Connect`, `Toggle Light`, `Disconnect` — arranged vertically. Provide the complete XML layout and the Kotlin code that handles the three clicks, ensuring the `Toggle Light` button is enabled **only after** `Connect` is clicked. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

| # | Approach | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **XML `android:onClick`** | Minimal code, easy to maintain UI; click handler visible in the layout. | Couples view and logic; method name is a string (no compile-time check). |
| 2 | **Anonymous inner class** | Localized to the specific view — no need to share the listener. | Verbose; risk of memory leak if holding outer-class reference. |
| 3 | **Activity implements `OnClickListener`** | One `onClick(view)` handles many buttons via `view.id` switch. | Reduces cohesion — the Activity becomes a "god class" for click logic. |
| 4 | **Kotlin lambda** | Concise (1 line); SAM conversion is automatic. | Cannot easily reference `this` of the outer Activity (use `this@MainActivity`). |

*(1.5 marks for each row × 4 rows = 6 marks + 1 mark for overall conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**XML Layout — `res/layout/activity_main.xml`** *(3 marks)*

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout
    xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="24dp">

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/tvStatus"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Status: Disconnected"
        android:textSize="18sp"
        android:layout_marginBottom="24dp" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/btnConnect"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Connect"
        android:minHeight="48dp" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/btnToggle"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Toggle Light"
        android:enabled="false"
        android:minHeight="48dp" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/btnDisconnect"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Disconnect"
        android:enabled="false"
        android:minHeight="48dp" />
</LinearLayout>
```

**Kotlin Code — `MainActivity.kt`** *(3 marks)*

```kotlin
package com.example.iotlight

import android.os.Bundle
import android.widget.Button
import android.widget.TextView
import android.widget.Toast
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity

class MainActivity : AppCompatActivity() {

    private lateinit var btnConnect: Button
    private lateinit var btnToggle: Button
    private lateinit var btnDisconnect: Button
    private lateinit var tvStatus: TextView

    override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main)

        btnConnect    = findViewById(R.id.btnConnect)
        btnToggle     = findViewById(R.id.btnToggle)
        btnDisconnect = findViewById(R.id.btnDisconnect)
        tvStatus      = findViewById(R.id.tvStatus)

        btnConnect.setOnClickListener {
            tvStatus.text = "Status: Connected ✅"
            btnConnect.isEnabled    = false
            btnToggle.isEnabled     = true
            btnDisconnect.isEnabled = true
            Toast.makeText(this, "MQTT client connected", Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show()
        }

        btnToggle.setOnClickListener {
            // In production: client.publish("home/light", "TOGGLE")
            Toast.makeText(this, "Light toggled", Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show()
        }

        btnDisconnect.setOnClickListener {
            tvStatus.text = "Status: Disconnected ❌"
            btnConnect.isEnabled    = true
            btnToggle.isEnabled     = false
            btnDisconnect.isEnabled = false
            Toast.makeText(this, "Disconnected", Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show()
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation** *(1 mark)*:
- Initial state: only `Connect` is enabled.
- After `Connect` click: `Connect` becomes disabled, `Toggle Light` and `Disconnect` become enabled (state transition).
- After `Disconnect` click: state reverts, mirroring a typical MQTT client lifecycle.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls**
> 1. **Missing `lateinit` keyword** — You will lose 1 mark per `findViewById` that uses `val` without `lateinit` (causes compile errors or unsafe casts).
> 2. **Forgetting `setContentView()`** — A button declared in XML but never inflated via `setContentView(R.layout.activity_main)` is a common copy-paste error (-1 mark).
> 3. **Putting parentheses in `android:onClick`** — Writing `android:onClick="sendCommand()"` is incorrect; only the method name is allowed (-0.5 mark).
> 4. **Click logic on the UI thread for network I/O** — KTU may deduct 1–2 marks if you place a blocking `Thread.sleep()` or synchronous HTTP call directly in `onClick()`.
> 5. **Not enabling/disabling dependent buttons** — For the "Toggle Light" question, if you forget to set `android:enabled="false"` initially, you lose 1 mark on state-management logic.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

| # | Concept | Key Point |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Button class** | Extends `TextView`; resides in `android.widget.Button`. |
| 2 | **Mandatory XML attributes** | `id`, `layout_width`, `layout_height`, `text` — all four are required for a usable button. |
| 3 | **`android:onClick`** | Method name only (no parentheses). Kotlin method must accept a `View` parameter. |
| 4 | **Lambda listener** | `btn.setOnClickListener { /* code */ }` is the modern, recommended approach. |
| 5 | **Touch target size** | Minimum **48 × 48 dp** per Material Design / accessibility guidelines. |
| 6 | **UI Thread rule** | All `onClick` callbacks run on the main thread; offload network I/O to a coroutine/thread. |
| 7 | **CompoundButton family** | `ToggleButton`, `CheckBox`, `RadioButton`, `Switch` — all two-state, all extend `Button`. |
| 8 | **Enable/disable state** | Use `btn.isEnabled = true / false` to gate interactions dynamically. |
| 9 | **MaterialButton** | Preferred over legacy `Button` in modern apps — supports elevation, ripple, and shape theming. |
| 10 | **IoT pattern** | Button click → command builder → network dispatcher (MQTT/HTTP/BLE) → physical device → ACK → UI update. |
| 11 | **Drawable customization** | Use `<shape>` XML in `res/drawable/` for rounded, colored, or stroked buttons. |
| 12 | **`findViewById` vs ViewBinding** | ViewBinding is type-safe and the modern alternative; KTU still accepts `findViewById`. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** Always draw the **Mermaid flow diagram** of the click event flow in long answers — examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for visual representation, and it is the single highest-impact upgrade you can make to a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radio and Toggle Buttons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Radio and Toggle Buttons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Radio Button — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radio Button (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **RadioButton** is a two-state selection widget in Android (a subclass of `android.widget.CompoundButton`) that can be either *checked* or *unchecked*. When grouped inside a **`RadioGroup`**, it enforces **mutually exclusive (single-choice)** selection — only one RadioButton in the group can be in the checked state at any given time.

In the Android View hierarchy:

```
android.view.View
   └── android.widget.TextView
         └── android.widget.Button
               └── android.widget.CompoundButton
                     └── android.widget.RadioButton
```

The class `RadioGroup` extends `android.widget.LinearLayout` and is responsible for managing the mutual-exclusion logic among its child `RadioButton` instances.

### 1.2 Toggle Button — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Toggle Button (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **ToggleButton** is a two-state user-interface element (subclass of `android.widget.CompoundButton`) that visually flips between an **ON** and **OFF** state, often accompanied by a textual indicator (e.g., `"ON"` / `"OFF"`). It is functionally a *binary switch* used to capture boolean input from the user.

Class hierarchy:

```
android.view.View
   └── android.widget.TextView
         └── android.widget.Button
               └── android.widget.CompoundButton
                     └── android.widget.ToggleButton
```

A closely related alternative is the **`Switch`** widget, which behaves identically but renders a *slider-style* UI.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### 🎯 Analogy for Radio Buttons
> Imagine a **multiple-choice exam question** in which you must pick *exactly one correct answer*. The options are the **RadioButtons**, and the question-paper itself is the **RadioGroup**. You simply cannot tick two answers — the paper enforces "only one." This is the **single-choice paradigm**.

### 🔘 Analogy for Toggle Buttons
> Picture a **light switch on a wall**. It is either flipped **UP (ON)** or **DOWN (OFF)** — there is no third state. A ToggleButton in Android is a digital version of this wall switch, used to capture a **boolean state** (true/false, enabled/disabled, dark mode/light mode, Wi-Fi ON/OFF, etc.).

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Default Values

| Property | Default Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| `android:checked` | `false` | Initial state of the button |
| `android:textOn` | `"ON"` | Text shown when checked |
| `android:textOff` | `"OFF"` | Text shown when unchecked |
| `android:enabled` | `true` | Interactivity toggle |
| RadioGroup `android:orientation` | `vertical` | Stack orientation of children |

> [!NOTE]
> Both Radio and Toggle buttons emit the same core signal: a **boolean checked state**. The difference is *contextual* — a RadioButton lives inside a single-choice group, whereas a ToggleButton is a *standalone* binary input.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 RadioButton — Operational Breakdown

A RadioButton operates on a **state machine** with two nodes: `CHECKED` and `UNCHECKED`.

**Step-by-step logic:**

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** The RadioButton is created either declaratively (XML layout) or programmatically (`new RadioButton(context)`).
- **Step 2 — Grouping:** If placed inside a `<RadioGroup>` XML element, it joins a *mutual-exclusion set*. The RadioGroup internally tracks the currently checked child using a `CheckedStateTracker`.
- **Step 3 — User Interaction:** A tap/click invokes `toggle()` on the `CompoundButton` superclass, which flips the boolean `mChecked` field.
- **Step 4 — Group Logic:** If the tapped button is now `CHECKED`, the `RadioGroup`'s `PassThroughHierarchyChangeListener` automatically unchecks the previously selected child.
- **Step 5 — Event Dispatch:** A `CompoundButton.OnCheckedChangeListener` fires for the individual button, while a `RadioGroup.OnCheckedChangeListener` fires for the group.
- **Step 6 — State Persistence:** To survive configuration changes (e.g., screen rotation), the boolean state should be saved in `onSaveInstanceState()`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The `RadioGroup` is what gives RadioButtons their *single-choice semantics*. A bare RadioButton outside a group behaves like an independent checkbox.

---

## 2.2 ToggleButton — Operational Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Definition:** A ToggleButton (or `Switch`) is instantiated with optional `textOn` and `textOff` strings.
- **Step 2 — State Initialization:** The initial `isChecked()` value is read from `android:checked` in XML.
- **Step 3 — User Tap:** Tapping invokes `toggle()`; the view re-renders the `text` property to either `textOn` or `textOff` and refreshes the thumb position.
- **Step 4 — Listener Callback:** `CompoundButton.OnCheckedChangeListener.onCheckedChanged(buttonView, isChecked)` is invoked with the new boolean.
- **Step 5 — State Propagation:** Application logic reacts to the boolean — e.g., enabling Wi-Fi, switching themes, muting audio.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **essential XML attributes, listeners, and APIs** that KTU examiners expect students to memorize for the ESE (End Semester Examination).

| Component | XML Element | Key XML Attributes | Primary Listener | State Accessor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| RadioButton | `<RadioButton>` | `android:id`, `android:text`, `android:checked`, `android:enabled` | `CompoundButton.OnCheckedChangeListener` | `isChecked()` |
| RadioGroup | `<RadioGroup>` | `android:orientation`, `android:checkedButton` | `RadioGroup.OnCheckedChangeListener` | `getCheckedRadioButtonId()` |
| ToggleButton | `<ToggleButton>` | `android:textOn`, `android:textOff`, `android:checked` | `CompoundButton.OnCheckedChangeListener` | `isChecked()` |
| Switch | `<Switch>` | `android:textOn`, `android:textOff`, `android:showText` | `CompoundButton.OnCheckedChangeListener` | `isChecked()` |

### 2.3.1 State-Transition Notation

Let $S_t$ denote the checked state at time $t$. The toggle operation is:

$$
S_{t+1} \;=\; \lnot S_t
$$

where $S_t \in \{0, 1\}$ and $\lnot$ is logical negation.

For a `RadioGroup` containing $n$ RadioButtons with states $s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i \;=\; 1
$$

> This is the **mutual-exclusion invariant** — exactly one button is checked at any moment.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in IoT

| Scenario | Widget Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Selecting a Wi-Fi network | RadioGroup of RadioButtons | Only one network is active at a time |
| Turning on a smart bulb | ToggleButton | Binary state — ON or OFF |
| Choosing home/away/auto mode on a thermostat | RadioGroup | Mutually exclusive modes |
| Enabling Bluetooth Low Energy scanning | Switch/ToggleButton | Boolean feature flag |
| Selecting sensor sampling rate (1Hz/10Hz/100Hz) | RadioGroup | Single-choice numeric parameter |

> The **boolean state** captured by these widgets is typically transmitted to the IoT cloud (MQTT/HTTP) to actuate physical hardware — making them **the most fundamental input primitives** in any IoT mobile app.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a **CS / Mobile App Development** course (PECGT415), code snippets in **XML (layout)** and **Java (logic)** are integral to the syllabus and are explicitly required for full marks. The implementation below is exhaustive and shows every step.

---

## 3.1 Example: Building a "Device Control Panel"

We will build a small panel that lets the user:
- **Select a fan speed** using a `RadioGroup` (Low / Medium / High).
- **Toggle the device power** using a `ToggleButton`.
- Display the current selection in a `TextView`.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — `activity_main.xml` (Layout)

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="16dp">

    <!-- Step 1a: TextView to show the result -->
    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/tvResult"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Status: OFF"
        android:textSize="18sp"
        android:padding="8dp" />

    <!-- Step 1b: ToggleButton to control device power -->
    <ToggleButton
        android:id="@+id/tbPower"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:textOn="POWER ON"
        android:textOff="POWER OFF"
        android:checked="false" />

    <!-- Step 1c: RadioGroup containing 3 RadioButtons -->
    <RadioGroup
        android:id="@+id/rgSpeed"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:orientation="vertical">

        <RadioButton
            android:id="@+id/rbLow"
            android:layout_width="wrap_content"
            android:layout_height="wrap_content"
            android:text="Low Speed" />

        <RadioButton
            android:id="@+id/rbMed"
            android:layout_width="wrap_content"
            android:layout_height="wrap_content"
            android:text="Medium Speed" />

        <RadioButton
            android:id="@+id/rbHigh"
            android:layout_width="wrap_content"
            android:layout_height="wrap_content"
            android:text="High Speed" />
    </RadioGroup>

</LinearLayout>
```

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — `MainActivity.java` (Logic)

```java
package com.example.iotcontrol;

import android.os.Bundle;
import android.widget.CompoundButton;
import android.widget.RadioButton;
import android.widget.RadioGroup;
import android.widget.TextView;
import android.widget.Toast;
import android.widget.ToggleButton;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    private TextView tvResult;
    private ToggleButton tbPower;
    private RadioGroup rgSpeed;
    private String selectedSpeed = "None";

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);

        // Step 2a: Wire up the views using findViewById()
        tvResult = findViewById(R.id.tvResult);
        tbPower  = findViewById(R.id.tbPower);
        rgSpeed  = findViewById(R.id.rgSpeed);

        // Step 2b: Attach listener for the ToggleButton
        tbPower.setOnCheckedChangeListener(new CompoundButton.OnCheckedChangeListener() {
            @Override
            public void onCheckedChanged(CompoundButton buttonView, boolean isChecked) {
                // Step 2c: React to the boolean state
                if (isChecked) {
                    tvResult.setText("Status: ON | Speed: " + selectedSpeed);
                    Toast.makeText(MainActivity.this, "Device Powered ON", Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show();
                } else {
                    tvResult.setText("Status: OFF");
                    Toast.makeText(MainActivity.this, "Device Powered OFF", Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show();
                }
            }
        });

        // Step 2d: Attach listener for the RadioGroup
        rgSpeed.setOnCheckedChangeListener(new RadioGroup.OnCheckedChangeListener() {
            @Override
            public void onCheckedChanged(RadioGroup group, int checkedId) {
                // Step 2e: Identify which RadioButton was selected
                RadioButton rb = findViewById(checkedId);
                if (rb != null) {
                    selectedSpeed = rb.getText().toString();
                    tvResult.setText("Status: " + (tbPower.isChecked() ? "ON" : "OFF")
                                     + " | Speed: " + selectedSpeed);
                }
            }
        });
    }
}
```

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Logical Flow Explanation

- **Initialization:** `onCreate()` inflates the XML layout via `setContentView()`.
- **View Binding:** `findViewById(R.id.xxx)` returns a typed reference to each widget.
- **ToggleButton Event:** When the user taps, the boolean `isChecked` flips; our lambda updates the `TextView` and shows a `Toast`.
- **RadioGroup Event:** The framework passes the `R.id` of the newly selected child to `onCheckedChanged()`. We use `findViewById(checkedId)` to retrieve the corresponding `RadioButton` and read its label.
- **Coupling Logic:** The `TextView` is updated from *both* listeners to maintain a single source of truth, demonstrating how multiple event sources interact.

---

## 3.2 State-Transition Derivation

We will formally derive the **mutual-exclusion invariant** for a RadioGroup.

Let the RadioGroup contain $n$ RadioButtons. Let $s_i$ be the boolean state of the $i$-th button.

**Assumption A1:** The RadioGroup enforces that selecting a new button automatically unselects the previously selected one.

**Assumption A2:** The initial state (after `onCreate`) has at most one button checked, because:
- The default `android:checked` is `false` for all buttons.
- If the developer explicitly sets `android:checked="true"` on more than one button, only the *last-encountered* checked button remains selected (documented Android behavior).

**Logical Consequence:**

$$
\forall t \in \mathbb{N}, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i^{(t)} \;\in\; \{0, 1\}
$$

This means at any time $t$, the sum of all checked states is either 0 (no selection) or 1 (exactly one selection).

**Derivation of the toggle operator:**

When the user taps button $k$:

$$
s_k^{(t+1)} \;=\; 1
$$

$$
s_j^{(t+1)} \;=\; 0, \quad \forall j \neq k
$$

**Verification of conservation:** Let $T = \sum s_i^{(t)}$.

- *Case 1:* $T = 0$ before tap → $T' = 1$ after tap.  
- *Case 2:* $T = 1$ before tap (say $s_m = 1$, $m \neq k$) → $T' = 1$ after tap.  
- *Case 3:* $T = 1$ before tap (say $s_m = 1$, $m = k$) → $T' = 0$ (user un-tapped the same one).  

In all cases, $T' \in \{0, 1\}$, **preserving the invariant**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Programmatic Creation (No XML)

For completeness, here is the *same* UI built entirely in Java:

```java
ToggleButton tb = new ToggleButton(this);
tb.setTextOn("ON");
tb.setTextOff("OFF");
tb.setChecked(false);

RadioGroup rg = new RadioGroup(this);
rg.setOrientation(LinearLayout.VERTICAL);

String[] labels = {"Low", "Medium", "High"};
for (String label : labels) {
    RadioButton rb = new RadioButton(this);
    rb.setText(label);
    rg.addView(rb);
}
```

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often award **partial credit** for knowing programmatic alternatives. Always mention both XML and Java approaches in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 State Machine — RadioButton (Inside a Group)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unchecked : onCreate()
    Unchecked --> Checked : userTaps() [ group.size > 0 ]
    Checked --> Unchecked : userTapsSame()
    Checked --> Unchecked : siblingButtonTapped()
    Unchecked --> [*]
    Checked --> [*]
```

## 4.2 State Machine — ToggleButton (Standalone)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OffState : android:checked = false
    [*] --> OnState : android:checked = true
    OffState --> OnState : userTaps() / setChecked(true)
    OnState --> OffState : userTaps() / setChecked(false)
    OnState --> OnState : persists across configChange (via onSaveInstanceState)
    OffState --> OffState : persists across configChange (via onSaveInstanceState)
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — IoT Device Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Opens App] --> B[Render XML Layout]
    B --> C{RadioGroup present?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Initialize RadioButtons]
    C -- No --> E[Skip RadioGroup]
    D --> F[Initialize ToggleButton]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Attach OnCheckedChangeListener]
    G --> H{User Taps Widget}
    H -- RadioButton --> I[RadioGroup clears previous selection]
    I --> J[Update selectedSpeed variable]
    H -- ToggleButton --> K[Flip isChecked boolean]
    K --> L[Update TextView status]
    J --> L
    L --> M{Power is ON?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Publish MQTT message to IoT broker]
    M -- No --> O[Send OFF command]
    N --> P[Update TextView with ACK]
    O --> P
```

## 4.4 Class-Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[android.view.View] --> T[android.widget.TextView]
    T --> B[android.widget.Button]
    B --> C[android.widget.CompoundButton]
    C --> R[android.widget.RadioButton]
    C --> TB[android.widget.ToggleButton]
    C --> S[android.widget.Switch]
    C --> CB[android.widget.CheckBox]
```

## 4.5 Event-Listener Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UI_Layer
        U1[User Tap]
    end
    subgraph View_Layer
        VB[RadioButton / ToggleButton]
    end
    subgraph Listener_Layer
        CL1[CompoundButton.OnCheckedChangeListener]
        CL2[RadioGroup.OnCheckedChangeListener]
    end
    subgraph App_Logic_Layer
        AL[Activity / ViewModel]
    end
    U1 --> VB
    VB --> CL1
    VB --> CL2
    CL1 --> AL
    CL2 --> AL
    AL --> TV[Update TextView / Toast / MQTT]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing `RadioGroup` as a *layout* rather than a *container* with mutual-exclusion logic. Always state the **invariant** $\sum s_i \in \{0,1\}$.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using `OnClickListener` for a `CompoundButton` (Radio/Toggle). KTU's correct answer requires **`OnCheckedChangeListener`**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to call `setContentView()` before `findViewById()` — this causes a runtime crash.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not distinguishing between `RadioButton` and `CheckBox`. RadioButton = single choice (in a group); CheckBox = multiple choice (independent).
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In 14-mark answers, omitting the **programmatic (Java)** counterpart of the XML approach. KTU 2024 expects both.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a RadioButton and a CheckBox in Android. When would you prefer a RadioGroup of RadioButtons over individual CheckBoxes?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | RadioButton | CheckBox |
|---|---|---|
| Inheritance | `CompoundButton` | `CompoundButton` |
| Grouping | Requires `RadioGroup` for single-choice | Independent; multiple can be checked |
| Typical Use | Mutually exclusive options (mode, frequency) | Independent toggles (filters, settings) |
| Listener | `CompoundButton.OnCheckedChangeListener` | `CompoundButton.OnCheckedChangeListener` |

A **RadioGroup of RadioButtons** is preferred when **only one option can be active** (e.g., choosing a Wi-Fi network). **CheckBoxes** are preferred for **independent multi-select** scenarios (e.g., subscribing to multiple notification topics). *(1 mark for the table, 1 mark for the use-case, 1 mark for the conclusion.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the `android:checked`, `android:textOn`, and `android:textOff` attributes with respect to a `ToggleButton`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- `android:checked` (Boolean, default `false`): specifies the **initial boolean state** of the button when the layout is inflated. *(1 mark)*
- `android:textOn` (String, default `"ON"`): the **label displayed when the button is in the checked state**. *(1 mark)*
- `android:textOff` (String, default `"OFF"`): the **label displayed when the button is in the unchecked state**. *(1 mark)*

These three attributes together define both the **state** and the **visual feedback** of the toggle.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 ESE Part B for a 14-mark question expects a **two-part answer** (typically 7 + 7 marks). Choose **either** Option A **or** Option B.

---

### ✅ Option A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** *(Module 2, 14 marks)*

**(a)** With a neat XML layout and corresponding Java code, demonstrate how a `RadioGroup` containing three `RadioButton`s can be used to let a user select a **sensor sampling rate** (Low = 1 Hz, Medium = 10 Hz, High = 100 Hz). Explain the listener mechanism used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **mutual-exclusion invariant** of a RadioGroup mathematically. Show, with a worked example of 3 buttons, why $\sum s_i \in \{0, 1\}$ holds under user interaction. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**XML Layout (3 marks):**

```xml
<RadioGroup
    android:id="@+id/rgSamplingRate"
    android:layout_width="wrap_content"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:orientation="vertical">

    <RadioButton
        android:id="@+id/rbLow"
        android:text="Low (1 Hz)" />
    <RadioButton
        android:id="@+id/rbMed"
        android:text="Medium (10 Hz)" />
    <RadioButton
        android:id="@+id/rbHigh"
        android:text="High (100 Hz)" />
</RadioGroup>
```

**Java Listener (3 marks):**

```java
RadioGroup rg = findViewById(R.id.rgSamplingRate);
rg.setOnCheckedChangeListener((group, checkedId) -> {
    String rate;
    if (checkedId == R.id.rbLow)        rate = "1 Hz";
    else if (checkedId == R.id.rbMed)   rate = "10 Hz";
    else                                rate = "100 Hz";
    Log.d("IoTApp", "Sampling rate set to: " + rate);
});
```

**Listener Explanation (1 mark):** The `RadioGroup.OnCheckedChangeListener` interface is invoked whenever the user selects a new child. The `checkedId` parameter is the `R.id` of the newly selected `RadioButton`. Inside the lambda, we map this ID to the corresponding sampling-rate string and log it (which, in a real IoT app, would be published to the cloud via MQTT).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Definition of Variables (1 mark):**
Let $s_1, s_2, s_3 \in \{0, 1\}$ be the checked states of the three RadioButtons. The RadioGroup enforces the **mutual-exclusion invariant**:

$$
s_1 + s_2 + s_3 \;\in\; \{0, 1\}
$$

**Worked Example — Initial State (2 marks):**
All buttons start as `false`:

$$
s_1^{(0)} = 0, \quad s_2^{(0)} = 0, \quad s_3^{(0)} = 0
$$

$$
S^{(0)} \;=\; s_1^{(0)} + s_2^{(0)} + s_3^{(0)} \;=\; 0 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Worked Example — User Taps Button 2 (2 marks):**
The RadioGroup sets $s_2 = 1$ and forces the others to remain 0:

$$
s_1^{(1)} = 0, \quad s_2^{(1)} = 1, \quad s_3^{(1)} = 0
$$

$$
S^{(1)} \;=\; 0 + 1 + 0 \;=\; 1 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Worked Example — User Taps Button 3 (2 marks):**
The RadioGroup un-checks button 2 and checks button 3:

$$
s_1^{(2)} = 0, \quad s_2^{(2)} = 0, \quad s_3^{(2)} = 1
$$

$$
S^{(2)} \;=\; 0 + 0 + 1 \;=\; 1 \;\;\checkmark
$$

The invariant $S^{(t)} \in \{0, 1\}$ is preserved at every step because the RadioGroup's `PassThroughHierarchyChangeListener` ensures that *exactly one* button is checked after every user action.

---

### ✅ Option B — Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** *(Module 2, 14 marks)*

**(a)** Design an Android UI fragment that uses a **`ToggleButton`** to control an IoT **smart plug**. The button should display `"PLUG ON"` when checked and `"PLUG OFF"` when unchecked. Write the XML layout and the Java handler that publishes an MQTT message (`"ON"` or `"OFF"`) when the state changes. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the `ToggleButton` and the `Switch` widget in terms of: (i) visual appearance, (ii) default text behavior, (iii) API, and (iv) ideal use-case. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**XML Layout (3 marks):**

```xml
<ToggleButton
    android:id="@+id/tbPlug"
    android:layout_width="wrap_content"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:textOn="PLUG ON"
    android:textOff="PLUG OFF"
    android:checked="false" />
```

**Java Handler (4 marks):**

```java
ToggleButton tbPlug = findViewById(R.id.tbPlug);
tbPlug.setOnCheckedChangeListener((buttonView, isChecked) -> {
    String mqttPayload = isChecked ? "ON" : "OFF";
    String topic = "home/livingroom/plug/cmd";

    // Pseudocode for the actual publish call:
    // mqttClient.publish(topic, new MqttMessage(mqttPayload.getBytes()));
    Log.d("IoTApp", "Published to " + topic + " : " + mqttPayload);
});
```

**Explanation:** The `CompoundButton.OnCheckedChangeListener` provides a `boolean isChecked` parameter that directly maps to the desired MQTT payload. The `String topic` follows the KERBEROS-style hierarchical IoT topic convention `home/room/device/cmd`.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

| Dimension | ToggleButton | Switch |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Visual Appearance | Rectangular button with text indicator | Slider-style thumb with track |
| (ii) Default Text | Shows `textOn` / `textOff` strings | Shows `textOn` / `textOff` only if `android:showText="true"` (API 14+) |
| (iii) API | `setTextOn()`, `setTextOff()` | `setShowText(boolean)`, `setThumbTextPadding()` |
| (iv) Ideal Use-Case | Legacy apps, compact UIs | Material Design, modern settings panels |

**(1.5 marks per row × 4 rows = 6 marks; 1 mark for the concluding recommendation.)**

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For *modern Material Design* IoT control apps, prefer **`Switch`**; for *compact, legacy-compatible* UIs, use **`ToggleButton`**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **RadioButton** is a `CompoundButton` subclass; it is *single-choice only when grouped inside a `RadioGroup`*.
- ✅ The **mutual-exclusion invariant** of a RadioGroup is $\sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i \in \{0, 1\}$.
- ✅ A **ToggleButton** captures a **boolean state**, rendering `textOn` when checked and `textOff` when unchecked.
- ✅ The correct listener for *both* widgets is `CompoundButton.OnCheckedChangeListener` (NOT `OnClickListener`).
- ✅ `RadioGroup.OnCheckedChangeListener` is a *secondary* listener that provides the `R.id` of the currently checked child — useful for group-level logic.
- ✅ State should be persisted via `onSaveInstanceState()` to survive configuration changes.
- ✅ For modern UIs, prefer the **`Switch`** widget; for compact layouts, prefer **`ToggleButton`**.
- ✅ In IoT apps, the boolean captured by these widgets is typically **published to an MQTT broker** as a control command.
- ✅ Programmatically, the toggle operation is $S_{t+1} = \lnot S_t$, where $S_t \in \{0, 1\}$.
- ✅ Always include **both** XML and **programmatic (Java)** examples in 14-mark answers for full credit.
- ✅ Remember the class hierarchy: `View → TextView → Button → CompoundButton → {RadioButton, ToggleButton, Switch, CheckBox}`.
- ✅ Distinguish clearly between **RadioButton** (single-choice group) and **CheckBox** (multi-choice independent) — a frequent KTU trick question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Checkboxes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Checkboxes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Android Mobile Application Development for IoT**, a **Checkbox** is a specific type of two-state button (a subclass of `CompoundButton`) that can be either **checked (true)** or **unchecked (false)**. It is one of the foundational input controls (UI Widgets) defined under the `android.widget.Checkbox` class hierarchy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECGT415 - Module 2):**
> A Checkbox is a UI component that allows the user to make a binary (binary-state) selection. The state is mutually independent from other checkboxes, meaning multiple checkboxes in a group can be simultaneously checked. This is fundamentally different from RadioButtons, which enforce mutual exclusion.

The class signature follows the standard Android SDK hierarchy:

```
android.view.View
   └── android.widget.TextView
         └── android.widget.Button
               └── android.widget.CompoundButton
                     └── android.widget.CheckBox
```

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Survey Form:**
> Imagine you receive a paper feedback form at a hotel reception asking:
> *"Which amenities did you use during your stay? (You may tick multiple)"*
> * [ ] Swimming Pool
> * [ ] Gym
> * [ ] Free Wi-Fi
> * [ ] Spa
>
> Each box can be **ticked or left empty**, and you can tick as many as apply. This is exactly how a Checkbox behaves in an Android app. Each Checkbox is an **independent decision-maker** — ticking the "Gym" box has no effect on the "Wi-Fi" box. This independence is the defining feature of a Checkbox in IoT dashboards (e.g., selecting which sensors to monitor, which appliances to switch ON).

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Standard Size (Material Design):** 48 dp × 48 dp touch target area (minimum)
- **State Count:** 2 logical states (`true` / `false`)
- **Default Padding:** 8 dp internal
- **State Persistence:** Stored via `SharedPreferences`, `SQLite`, or remote IoT cloud (e.g., Firebase, AWS IoT Core)
- **Event Listener Interface:** `CompoundButton.OnCheckedChangeListener`

> [!TIP]
> In **IoT applications**, Checkboxes are extensively used for *device control panels* (e.g., "Turn ON Living Room Light", "Activate Motion Sensor"). The checked state corresponds to a `HIGH` or `ON` signal transmitted over MQTT/HTTP to the IoT node.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy of an Android Checkbox

A Checkbox operates as a **stateful view** that maintains a boolean property and triggers callbacks upon state transition. The underlying machinery can be broken down as follows:

### Step 1: The Visual Component
- A small **square box** is rendered on screen.
- When checked: a **check mark (✓)** appears inside the box.
- When unchecked: the box remains empty.

### Step 2: The Boolean State Model
- Internally, Android stores the state as a **boolean flag** `isChecked()`.
- The state can be:
  - `true` → Checked (✓ visible)
  - `false` → Unchecked (empty)

### Step 3: The User Interaction Loop
1. User taps the checkbox area (touch event captured by `View.onTouchEvent`).
2. The system toggles the internal boolean state.
3. The view is invalidated and re-drawn to reflect the new state.
4. The `OnCheckedChangeListener` callback is fired with the new state.

### Step 4: The Event Notification
- The application code receives `(buttonView, isChecked)` via the listener.
- In an IoT app, this callback typically triggers an **MQTT publish** or **HTTP POST** to the IoT broker/server.

## 2.2 Why and How Behind Each Step

| Step | Why? | How? |
|------|------|------|
| 1. Visual Box | Provides clear user affordance | `drawable/btn_check.xml` selector |
| 2. Boolean State | Binary logic maps directly to digital electronics (ON/OFF) | `mChecked` field in `CompoundButton` |
| 3. Touch Loop | Decouples user input from app logic | `View.onTouchEvent()` overridden |
| 4. Callback | Enables reactive programming pattern | `setOnCheckedChangeListener` |

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Property / Method | Type | Purpose / Description |
|-------------------|------|------------------------|
| `android:id` | XML Attribute | Unique identifier: `@+id/chkSensor` |
| `android:text` | String | Label text: "Activate Temperature Sensor" |
| `android:checked` | Boolean | Initial state: `true` or `false` |
| `isChecked()` | Method | Returns current state (`Boolean`) |
| `setChecked(boolean)` | Method | Programmatically sets state |
| `toggle()` | Method | Flips the current state |
| `setOnCheckedChangeListener` | Method | Registers state-change callback |
| `android:button` | Drawable | Custom checkbox icon |
| `android:padding` | Dimension | Internal padding in `dp` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Key Formula:**
> The state transition for a Checkbox can be expressed logically as:
>
> $$S_{new} = \neg S_{old}$$
>
> where $S_{new}$ is the new state, $S_{old}$ is the previous state, and $\neg$ denotes logical NOT (toggle operation).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in IoT Engineering

In IoT systems, checkboxes are pivotal for:

- **Multi-Sensor Activation Panels:** Selecting multiple sensors to subscribe to (temperature + humidity + gas).
- **Device Grouping:** Choosing which smart devices to include in a "Scene" (e.g., "Good Morning" scene = Light ON + Coffee Maker ON + Curtains OPEN).
- **Notification Filters:** Selecting alert types (SMS + Email + Push).
- **Data Logging Preferences:** Choosing which sensor streams to log to the cloud.

The **state of each Checkbox** is often serialized as a **JSON payload** and transmitted to the IoT backend:

$$\text{Payload} = \{ "device_1": \text{true}, \text{ "device_2": \text{false}, \ldots \}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Checkbox vs. RadioButton vs. Switch vs. ToggleButton

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **frequently tested comparison** in KTU exams. Students often confuse these four widgets.

| Feature | Checkbox | RadioButton | Switch | ToggleButton |
|---------|----------|-------------|--------|--------------|
| **States** | 2 (Checked/Unchecked) | 2 (Selected/Unselected) | 2 (ON/OFF) | 2 (ON/OFF) |
| **Mutual Exclusion** | ❌ No (Independent) | ✅ Yes (Group-based) | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Multi-Select** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Parent Class** | `CompoundButton` | `CompoundButton` | `CompoundButton` | `CompoundButton` |
| **Typical Use** | Multi-choice | Single-choice (form) | Settings toggle | Settings toggle |
| **IoT Use Case** | Sensor selection | Mode selection | Light ON/OFF | Wi-Fi enable |

## 3.2 Exhaustive XML Implementation (Step-by-Step)

### Step 1: Define the Checkbox in the Layout XML

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout
    xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="16dp">

    <TextView
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Select IoT Sensors to Monitor:"
        android:textSize="18sp"
        android:textStyle="bold" />

    <CheckBox
        android:id="@+id/chkTemperature"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Temperature Sensor"
        android:checked="true" />

    <CheckBox
        android:id="@+id/chkHumidity"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Humidity Sensor" />

    <CheckBox
        android:id="@+id/chkMotion"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Motion Sensor" />

    <CheckBox
        android:id="@+id/chkGas"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Gas Sensor" />

</LinearLayout>
```

### Step 2: Logic Conversion in the Activity (Java)

```java
public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    private CheckBox chkTemperature, chkHumidity, chkMotion, chkGas;

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);

        // Step 1: Initialize views using findViewById()
        chkTemperature = findViewById(R.id.chkTemperature);
        chkHumidity    = findViewById(R.id.chkHumidity);
        chkMotion      = findViewById(R.id.chkMotion);
        chkGas         = findViewById(R.id.chkGas);

        // Step 2: Attach OnCheckedChangeListener
        chkTemperature.setOnCheckedChangeListener((buttonView, isChecked) -> {
            if (isChecked) {
                Log.d("IoT_PANEL", "Temperature Sensor ACTIVATED");
                // MQTT publish: {"sensor":"temp","state":"ON"}
            } else {
                Log.d("IoT_PANEL", "Temperature Sensor DEACTIVATED");
            }
        });

        chkHumidity.setOnCheckedChangeListener((buttonView, isChecked) -> {
            // Symmetric handling for humidity
        });
    }

    // Step 3: Method to read all checkbox states
    public String buildSensorConfigPayload() {
        StringBuilder payload = new StringBuilder("{");
        payload.append("\"temperature\":").append(chkTemperature.isChecked()).append(",");
        payload.append("\"humidity\":").append(chkHumidity.isChecked()).append(",");
        payload.append("\"motion\":").append(chkMotion.isChecked()).append(",");
        payload.append("\"gas\":").append(chkGas.isChecked());
        payload.append("}");
        return payload.toString();
    }
}
```

### Step 3: Step-by-Step State Aggregation Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** *"Explain how you would collect and transmit the state of multiple checkboxes to an IoT cloud."*

Let $n$ = number of checkboxes, indexed $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.

For each checkbox $i$, define the state variable:
$$S_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if checkbox } i \text{ is checked} \\ 0 & \text{if checkbox } i \text{ is unchecked} \end{cases}$$

The aggregated JSON payload sent to the IoT broker is constructed as:

$$
P = \Big\{ \text{"chk"} \oplus i : S_i \;\; \big\vert \;\; i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\} \Big\}
$$

where $\oplus$ denotes string concatenation and $\big\vert$ denotes the *such that* quantifier. In expanded form:

$$
P = \{\text{"chk1"}: S_1, \; \text{"chk2"}: S_2, \; \ldots, \; \text{"chkN"}: S_n\}
$$

**Numerical Example (4 Checkboxes):**

Assume states:
- $S_1 = 1$ (Temperature ON)
- $S_2 = 0$ (Humidity OFF)
- $S_3 = 1$ (Motion ON)
- $S_4 = 0$ (Gas OFF)

Substituting into the formula:

$$
P = \{\text{"chk1"}: 1, \; \text{"chk2"}: 0, \; \text{"chk3"}: 1, \; \text{"chk4"}: 0\}
$$

Serialized as JSON:

$$
P_{JSON} = \texttt{\{"chk1":true,"chk2":false,"chk3":true,"chk4":false\}}
$$

This payload is then published to the **MQTT topic** `home/sensors/configure`:

$$
\text{topic} = \text{"home/"} \oplus \text{roomId} \oplus \text{"/sensors/configure"}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the boolean-to-bit mapping is a direct homomorphism between Android UI state and digital logic. This makes checkboxes ideal for representing **binary control signals** in IoT firmware.

### Step 4: Programmatic Creation (Without XML)

```java
CheckBox dynamicChk = new CheckBox(this);
dynamicChk.setText("Dynamic IoT Sensor");
dynamicChk.setChecked(false);
dynamicChk.setOnCheckedChangeListener((btn, isChecked) -> {
    // Handle event
});
linearLayout.addView(dynamicChk);
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end flow** when a user interacts with a Checkbox in an IoT mobile app:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Taps Checkbox on Screen] --> B{Touch Event Captured}
    B --> C[CompoundButton Internal Toggle]
    C --> D[State Updated: isChecked true or false]
    D --> E[Re-draw View with New Visual]
    D --> F[Fire OnCheckedChangeListener]
    F --> G{Checkbox ID matched}
    G -- chkTemperature --> H1[Publish MQTT: temp ON or OFF]
    G -- chkHumidity --> H2[Publish MQTT: humid ON or OFF]
    G -- chkMotion --> H3[Publish MQTT: motion ON or OFF]
    G -- chkGas --> H4[Publish MQTT: gas ON or OFF]
    H1 --> I[MQTT Broker: HiveMQ or Mosquitto]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> J[IoT Cloud: AWS IoT Core or Firebase]
    J --> K[Rule Engine Triggers]
    K --> L[Actuator Response: Fan, Light, Alarm]
```

## 4.2 Class Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[View]
    T[TextView]
    B[Button]
    CB[CompoundButton]
    CHK[CheckBox]
    RB[RadioButton]
    SW[Switch]
    TB[ToggleButton]

    V --> T
    T --> B
    B --> CB
    CB --> CHK
    CB --> RB
    CB --> SW
    CB --> TB
```

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram of a Checkbox

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unchecked
    Unchecked --> Checked: User taps checkbox or toggle method
    Checked --> Unchecked: User taps checkbox or toggle method
    Checked --> Checked: setChecked true
    Unchecked --> Unchecked: setChecked false
```

## 4.4 Component Interaction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UI_LAYER [UI Layer XML]
        X1[activity_main.xml]
        X2[CheckBox Declaration]
    end

    subgraph LOGIC_LAYER [Logic Layer Java or Kotlin]
        Y1[MainActivity.java]
        Y2[findViewById]
        Y3[setOnCheckedChangeListener]
    end

    subgraph DATA_LAYER [Data Layer IoT]
        Z1[JSON Payload Builder]
        Z2[MQTT Client Publisher]
        Z3[Cloud Broker]
    end

    X1 --> X2
    X2 --> Y2
    Y1 --> Y2
    Y2 --> Y3
    Y3 --> Z1
    Z1 --> Z2
    Z2 --> Z3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Define a Checkbox in Android. List any two attributes used to configure it in XML.*

#### ✅ Model Answer (Step-by-Step):
**Definition:** A Checkbox is a two-state UI widget in Android (extending `CompoundButton`) that allows the user to make an **independent binary selection** (checked or unchecked). Multiple checkboxes in a group can be selected simultaneously, making it suitable for multi-choice input in IoT control panels.

**Two Key XML Attributes:**

1. `android:text` — Defines the label displayed next to the checkbox (e.g., `android:text="Temperature Sensor"`).
2. `android:checked` — Specifies the initial state of the checkbox (boolean: `true` or `false`).

*(Alternative attributes accepted: `android:id`, `android:padding`, `android:button` for custom icon.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
*Write a short note on the difference between a Checkbox and a RadioButton with respect to multi-selection capability.*

#### ✅ Model Answer:
| Aspect | Checkbox | RadioButton |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| Multi-Selection | ✅ Allows multiple selections simultaneously | ❌ Allows only one selection within a `RadioGroup` |
| State Independence | Each checkbox is independent | Mutually exclusive within a group |
| Use Case | Selecting multiple IoT sensors | Selecting one communication mode (Wi-Fi/BLE) |

In an IoT context, a **Checkbox** is preferred when a user must activate multiple sensors (e.g., Temperature + Humidity), whereas a **RadioButton** is preferred for choosing a single operational mode.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**
**(a)** Explain the concept of Checkbox in Android with its class hierarchy. Mention any three important properties. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Design an XML layout for a Smart Home IoT app that contains **four checkboxes** representing: Living Room Light, Bedroom Fan, Kitchen Appliance, and AC. Write the corresponding Java code to display a **Toast message** indicating which device was toggled. *(7 Marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**(a) Concept, Hierarchy, and Properties (7 Marks):**

A **Checkbox** is an `android.widget.CheckBox` instance that represents a binary toggle state. It is widely used in **multi-select scenarios** common in IoT dashboards (e.g., selecting which appliances to control).

**Class Hierarchy (Write in Exam):**

```
java.lang.Object
   └── android.view.View
         └── android.widget.TextView
               └── android.widget.Button
                     └── android.widget.CompoundButton
                           └── android.widget.CheckBox
```

**Three Important Properties:**

1. **`android:id`** — Unique identifier to reference the checkbox in Java code (e.g., `@+id/chkLight`).
2. **`android:text`** — The label visible to the user (e.g., "Living Room Light").
3. **`android:checked`** — Initial state (boolean). Defaults to `false` if omitted.

**Programmatic Methods:**
- `isChecked()` — Returns `Boolean` indicating current state.
- `setChecked(boolean)` — Sets the state.
- `toggle()` — Switches the state automatically.

---

**(b) XML Layout and Java Code (7 Marks):**

**`res/layout/activity_main.xml`**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout
    xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="16dp">

    <TextView
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Smart Home Control Panel"
        android:textSize="20sp"
        android:textStyle="bold"
        android:paddingBottom="12dp" />

    <CheckBox
        android:id="@+id/chkLight"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Living Room Light" />

    <CheckBox
        android:id="@+id/chkFan"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Bedroom Fan" />

    <CheckBox
        android:id="@+id/chkKitchen"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Kitchen Appliance" />

    <CheckBox
        android:id="@+id/chkAC"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Air Conditioner" />

</LinearLayout>
```

**`MainActivity.java`**

```java
package com.example.smarthome;

import android.os.Bundle;
import android.widget.CheckBox;
import android.widget.CompoundButton;
import android.widget.Toast;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    CheckBox chkLight, chkFan, chkKitchen, chkAC;

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);

        chkLight   = findViewById(R.id.chkLight);
        chkFan     = findViewById(R.id.chkFan);
        chkKitchen = findViewById(R.id.chkKitchen);
        chkAC      = findViewById(R.id.chkAC);

        attachListener(chkLight,   "Living Room Light");
        attachListener(chkFan,     "Bedroom Fan");
        attachListener(chkKitchen, "Kitchen Appliance");
        attachListener(chkAC,      "Air Conditioner");
    }

    private void attachListener(CheckBox cb, String deviceName) {
        cb.setOnCheckedChangeListener((CompoundButton buttonView, boolean isChecked) -> {
            String status = isChecked ? "ON" : "OFF";
            Toast.makeText(getApplicationContext(),
                deviceName + " turned " + status,
                Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show();
            // IoT action: publish to MQTT topic "home/<deviceName>/set"
        });
    }
}
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
**(a)** Compare and contrast Checkbox, RadioButton, Switch, and ToggleButton in Android. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Write a complete Android program (XML + Java) that uses **three checkboxes** to let the user select IoT notification channels: **Email, SMS, and Push Notification**. When a checkbox is checked, the corresponding channel should be added to a `StringBuilder` and displayed in a `TextView`. *(7 Marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

| Feature | Checkbox | RadioButton | Switch | ToggleButton |
|---------|----------|-------------|--------|--------------|
| Class | `CheckBox` | `RadioButton` | `Switch` | `ToggleButton` |
| States | Checked / Unchecked | Selected / Unselected | ON / OFF | ON / OFF |
| Multi-Select | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (mutually exclusive) | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| Visual Style | Square box with ✓ | Circular radio with dot | Sliding track | Button with text |
| Listener | `OnCheckedChangeListener` | `OnCheckedChangeListener` | `OnCheckedChangeListener` | `OnCheckedChangeListener` |
| IoT Example | Select sensors | Choose protocol (MQTT/HTTP) | Wi-Fi ON/OFF | Bluetooth enable |

---

**(b) Complete Program (7 Marks):**

**`activity_main.xml`**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout
    xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="16dp">

    <TextView
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Choose Notification Channels:"
        android:textSize="18sp"
        android:textStyle="bold" />

    <CheckBox android:id="@+id/chkEmail"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Email Alerts" />

    <CheckBox android:id="@+id/chkSMS"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="SMS Alerts" />

    <CheckBox android:id="@+id/chkPush"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Push Notifications" />

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/txtResult"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:paddingTop="20dp"
        android:text="Selected: None"
        android:textSize="16sp" />

</LinearLayout>
```

**`MainActivity.java`**

```java
package com.example.iotnotify;

import android.os.Bundle;
import android.widget.CheckBox;
import android.widget.TextView;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    CheckBox chkEmail, chkSMS, chkPush;
    TextView txtResult;
    StringBuilder selected = new StringBuilder();

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);

        chkEmail = findViewById(R.id.chkEmail);
        chkSMS   = findViewById(R.id.chkSMS);
        chkPush  = findViewById(R.id.chkPush);
        txtResult = findViewById(R.id.txtResult);

        chkEmail.setOnCheckedChangeListener((b, c) -> updateResult());
        chkSMS.setOnCheckedChangeListener((b, c)   -> updateResult());
        chkPush.setOnCheckedChangeListener((b, c)  -> updateResult());
    }

    private void updateResult() {
        selected.setLength(0);
        if (chkEmail.isChecked()) selected.append("Email, ");
        if (chkSMS.isChecked())   selected.append("SMS, ");
        if (chkPush.isChecked())  selected.append("Push, ");

        if (selected.length() == 0) {
            txtResult.setText("Selected: None");
        } else {
            // Remove trailing comma
            String result = selected.substring(0, selected.length() - 2);
            txtResult.setText("Selected: " + result);
        }
    }
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Checkbox with RadioButton** — Students often write code for `RadioGroup` when the question asks for multi-select. Read carefully: *"multiple can be selected"* = Checkbox; *"only one"* = RadioButton.
> 2. **Forgetting `findViewById()` initialization** — A frequent mistake is using the checkbox without binding it. The view ID must be resolved in `onCreate()`.
> 3. **Missing `setContentView(R.layout.activity_main)`** — Without this, the XML layout is never loaded and the app crashes.
> 4. **Writing `setOnClickListener` instead of `setOnCheckedChangeListener`** — The correct listener for a Checkbox is `OnCheckedChangeListener` (not the generic click listener).
> 5. **Not handling initial state** — If `android:checked="true"` is set in XML, the listener fires once on creation; account for this in IoT synchronization logic.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ A **Checkbox** is a two-state (`true`/`false`) UI widget extending `CompoundButton`.
- ✅ Multiple checkboxes can be **simultaneously checked** (independent state).
- ✅ Defined in XML using `<CheckBox>` tag; configured via `android:text`, `android:checked`, `android:id`.
- ✅ Programmatic class: `android.widget.CheckBox`.
- ✅ State inspection: `isChecked()` returns boolean.
- ✅ State modification: `setChecked(boolean)` and `toggle()`.
- ✅ Event handling: `setOnCheckedChangeListener` with `(CompoundButton, boolean)` callback.
- ✅ In **IoT apps**, checkbox state maps directly to **ON/OFF** digital control signals.
- ✅ Checkbox ≠ RadioButton: Checkbox is for **multi-select**; RadioButton is for **single-select**.
- ✅ Checkbox ≠ Switch: Both are 2-state, but Switch is a *settings-style* toggle; Checkbox is a *form-style* selection.
- ✅ Common IoT use cases: sensor activation, notification channel selection, device group selection, data-logging filter.
- ✅ State aggregation formula: $P = \{\text{"chk"} \oplus i : S_i \mid i \in \{1,\ldots,n\}\}$ — used to build MQTT/JSON payloads.
- ✅ Default initial state is `false` (unchecked) unless `android:checked="true"` is set.
- ✅ Always call `setContentView()` before `findViewById()` to avoid `NullPointerException`.
- ✅ Touch target area per Material Design: minimum **48 dp × 48 dp**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spinners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Spinners

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spinner (Android UI Component):** A `Spinner` is a view in the Android framework that provides a quick way to select one value from a pre-defined set of values. It presents the currently selected value in a compact form and exposes a dropdown list when tapped, allowing the user to pick a single item from the available list. It is essentially a subclass of `AdapterView` and is the Android equivalent of a drop-down list or combo box found in desktop GUI environments.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Mobile App Development for IoT*, Spinners are classified under the `android.widget` package and are pivotal for collecting discrete user input in IoT control applications — for instance, selecting a sensor channel, choosing a device ID, or picking a command mode (e.g., ON/OFF/AUTO) to be transmitted over MQTT/HTTP.

### Class Hierarchy
The `Spinner` class inherits from the following hierarchy:

$$
\text{java.lang.Object} \rightarrow \text{android.view.View} \rightarrow \text{android.view.ViewGroup} \rightarrow \text{android.widget.AdapterView} \rightarrow \text{android.widget.AbsSpinner} \rightarrow \text{android.widget.Spinner}
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Menu Wheel:**
> Imagine you walk into a restaurant and the waiter hands you a small folded card that only displays the chef's special (the *currently selected value*). When you tap on the card, it flips open like a small wheel, revealing the full menu (the *dropdown list* of all available dishes). You scroll, pick one dish, the wheel collapses, and the card now displays your chosen dish. That entire interactive card is a **Spinner**.

For an **IoT dashboard application**, think of a smart-home control panel where the user taps a tiny widget labeled "Living Room" — tapping it reveals all rooms (Bedroom, Kitchen, Garage). Selecting a room updates the widget, and the IoT app then queries sensor data for the newly selected room.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics

| Property | Value / Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Package** | `android.widget` |
| **Parent Class** | `AbsSpinner` |
