It is the **safe place to access child views** (e.g., `findViewById`, set listeners, configure adapters). It receives the inflated `View` and the `savedInstanceState` bundle.
>
> **Key Difference:** `onCreateView()` *creates* the view; `onViewCreated()` *interacts* with it.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question is 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. You must answer **one** of two internal choices.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Full Fragment Creation Walkthrough*

**Q.A. (a) [7 Marks]**
*Explain the Fragment lifecycle with a neat diagram. Compare it with the Activity lifecycle.*

**Model Solution:**

The Fragment lifecycle extends the Activity lifecycle with additional states that reflect the creation and destruction of the Fragment's view hierarchy.

| Fragment Callback | Equivalent Activity Callback | Extra Detail |
|---|---|---|
| `onAttach()` | — | Fragment links to Activity |
| `onCreate()` | `onCreate()` | Object created, no UI yet |
| `onCreateView()` | `onCreate()` (UI part) | Inflates layout XML |
| `onViewCreated()` | — | Views are accessible |
| `onStart()` | `onStart()` | Fragment visible |
| `onResume()` | `onResume()` | Fragment interactive |
| `onPause()` | `onPause()` | Losing focus |
| `onStop()` | `onStop()` | No longer visible |
| `onDestroyView()` | — | View tree destroyed |
| `onDestroy()` | `onDestroy()` | Fragment object destroyed |
| `onDetach()` | — | No longer linked to Activity |

**Key Differences (3 marks for the comparison):**

1. **Granularity:** Fragment has *more* states (e.g., `onCreateView`, `onDestroyView`) than Activity, giving finer control.
2. **Nesting:** Fragment lifecycle is **nested within** the Activity's lifecycle — the Fragment cannot be in `Resumed` state when the Activity is `Paused`.
3. **Re-creation:** On configuration change, the Fragment's `onCreateView` may fire multiple times within the same Fragment object lifetime (unlike Activity, which is fully re-created).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often draw the Fragment lifecycle as **identical** to the Activity lifecycle. That is **wrong**. The Fragment has *unique* states: `onAttach`, `onCreateView`, `onViewCreated`, `onDestroyView`, `onDetach`. Listing these explicitly will earn full marks.

---

**Q.A. (b) [7 Marks]**
*Write the complete code (Kotlin) to create a Fragment that displays a temperature value in a TextView. Show both the Fragment class and the host Activity that loads it dynamically.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Fragment Layout (`res/layout/fragment_temperature.xml`):**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<LinearLayout xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="20dp">

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/tvTempLabel"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Current Temperature"
        android:textSize="18sp" />

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/tvTempValue"
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="25.0"
        android:textSize="42sp"
        android:textColor="#D32F2F" />
</LinearLayout>
```

**Step 2 – Fragment Class (`TemperatureFragment.kt`):**

```kotlin
package com.example.iotexam

import android.os.Bundle
import android.view.LayoutInflater
import android.view.View
import android.view.ViewGroup
import android.widget.TextView
import androidx.fragment.app.Fragment

class TemperatureFragment : Fragment() {

    private var tvTempValue: TextView? = null

    override fun onCreateView(
        inflater: LayoutInflater,
        container: ViewGroup?,
        savedInstanceState: Bundle?
    ): View {
        return inflater.inflate(
            R.layout.fragment_temperature,
            container,
            false
        )
    }

    override fun onViewCreated(view: View, savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
        super.onViewCreated(view, savedInstanceState)
        tvTempValue = view.findViewById(R.id.tvTempValue)
        tvTempValue?.text = "26.3"
    }

    override fun onDestroyView() {
        super.onDestroyView()
        tvTempValue = null
    }
}
```

**Step 3 – Host Activity (`MainActivity.kt`):**

```kotlin
package com.example.iotexam

import android.os.Bundle
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity

class MainActivity : AppCompatActivity() {

    override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main)

        if (savedInstanceState == null) {
            supportFragmentManager.beginTransaction().apply {
                replace(R.id.fragmentContainer, TemperatureFragment())
                commit()
            }
        }
    }
}
```

**Step 4 – Activity Layout (`res/layout/activity_main.xml`):**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<androidx.fragment.app.FragmentContainerView
    xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:id="@+id/fragmentContainer"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent" />
```

**Explanation of the code (for the remaining 1 mark):** The `if (savedInstanceState == null)` guard prevents the Fragment from being added twice when the Activity is recreated after rotation. The `commit()` schedules the transaction to run on the next UI frame.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Common mistakes that **cost marks**:
> 1. Forgetting to call `setContentView()` in the Activity → `IllegalStateException`.
> 2. Using the old `<fragment>` tag instead of `FragmentContainerView` → loses modern-lifecycle marks.
> 3. Not setting the Fragment's `arguments` for data passing → not in this question, but a frequent exam trap.
> 4. Forgetting to **null out view references** in `onDestroyView()` → memory-leak penalty.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice: Static vs Dynamic + Back Stack*

**Q.B. (a) [7 Marks]**
*Differentiate between static and dynamic Fragment creation. When would you prefer each in a real IoT mobile application?*

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Static Creation | Dynamic Creation |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Declared in XML using `<fragment>` or `FragmentContainerView` with `android:name` | Programmatic via `FragmentManager.beginTransaction()` |
| **Lifecycle Control** | Automatic — system inflates on Activity start | Manual — developer calls `add`, `replace`, `remove` |
| **When Loaded** | During Activity `setContentView()` | At runtime, on user action or condition |
| **Replacement** | Cannot be swapped without recreating Activity | Easy to swap via `replace()` |
| **Back Stack** | Not added by default | Can use `addToBackStack()` |
| **Use Case** | Persistent UI sections (e.g., header, footer) | Tabs, navigation drawers, swappable IoT dashboards |

**IoT Application Context (3 marks):**

- **Static** — Use for a **fixed sensor-readout panel** that always shows on the main IoT dashboard. Example: a permanent temperature gauge at the top of the screen.
- **Dynamic** — Use for **swappable control panels** when the user navigates between rooms (Living Room, Kitchen, Bedroom) in a smart-home app. Each room is a Fragment; tapping a tab replaces the current Fragment.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "static is better because it's simpler." That is **not** the right engineering answer. The correct framing is: *static is appropriate for fixed UI that does not change; dynamic is required when the UI must adapt to user navigation, network state, or device configuration.*

---

**Q.B. (b) [7 Marks]**
*With a suitable code example, explain how the Fragment back stack works. What is the role of `addToBackStack()` and `popBackStack()`?*

**Model Solution:**

The **Fragment back stack** is a stack data structure (LIFO – Last In, First Out) managed by `FragmentManager` that records the history of Fragment transactions, allowing the user to reverse navigation by pressing the **Back** button.

**Key Methods:**

| Method | Role |
|---|---|
| `addToBackStack(name)` | Pushes the current transaction onto the back stack. If `name` is `null`, the system auto-assigns one. |
| `popBackStack()` | Pops the most recent transaction, reversing it. |
| `popBackStackImmediate()` | Synchronous version of `popBackStack()`. |
| `getBackStackEntryCount()` | Returns the current depth of the back stack. |
| `addOnBackStackChangedListener` | Callback when the back stack changes. |

**Code Example:**

```kotlin
// Add a Fragment and record it in the back stack
supportFragmentManager.beginTransaction().apply {
    replace(R.id.fragmentContainer, TemperatureFragment())
    addToBackStack("temp")
    commit()
}

// Add another Fragment (e.g., user navigates to humidity)
supportFragmentManager.beginTransaction().apply {
    replace(R.id.fragmentContainer, HumidityFragment())
    addToBackStack("humidity")
    commit()
}

// Pop the most recent transaction when "Back" is pressed
supportFragmentManager.popBackStack()
// This restores the TemperatureFragment
```

**Sequence (for the remaining 1 mark):**

1. User opens Temperature → back stack: `[temp]`
2. User opens Humidity → back stack: `[temp, humidity]`
3. User presses Back → `popBackStack()` runs → Humidity is destroyed → Temperature is restored → back stack: `[temp]`
4. User presses Back again → back stack is empty → Android exits the Activity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:**
> 1. Forgetting to call `addToBackStack()` → Back button exits the app directly, **no marks for back-stack demonstration**.
> 2. Confusing `commit()` with `commitNow()`: `commit()` is async (the transaction may not be on the stack immediately). Use `commitNow()` only when you need synchronous behaviour.
> 3. Calling `popBackStack()` multiple times in a single method → only the *outermost* call is effective in many cases.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this the night before the exam):**

- ✅ A **Fragment** is a reusable UI component with its **own lifecycle**, hosted inside an **Activity**.
- ✅ Always use **AndroidX** (`androidx.fragment.app.Fragment`), not the legacy `android.app.Fragment`.
- ✅ Always host Fragments in a **`FragmentContainerView`**, not the old `<fragment>` tag.
- ✅ The mandatory override is **`onCreateView()`** — it must inflate the layout XML and return the root `View`.
- ✅ Use **`onViewCreated()`** to bind views and set listeners (NOT `onCreateView`).
- ✅ Always **null out View references** in `onDestroyView()` to prevent memory leaks.
- ✅ Pass data using **`setArguments(Bundle)`**, never via custom constructors.
- ✅ Use **`FragmentManager.beginTransaction()`** with `add`, `replace`, `remove`, `hide`, `show`.
- ✅ Call **`addToBackStack(name)`** to make the transaction reversible with the Back button.
- ✅ **`commit()` is asynchronous; `commitNow()` is synchronous.**
- ✅ Guard your `replace()` calls with `if (savedInstanceState == null)` to avoid duplicates on rotation.
- ✅ The Fragment lifecycle is **nested within** the Activity lifecycle but is **more granular**.
- ✅ Use **`viewLifecycleOwner`** (not `this`) as the LifecycleOwner for observers, LiveData, and Flow collection inside a Fragment.
- ✅ For **IoT dashboards**, use one Fragment per sensor/control panel inside a single Activity — this is the canonical KTU Module-3 use case.
- ✅ `requireContext()`, `requireActivity()`, `requireView()` throw `IllegalStateException` if called when the Fragment is detached — prefer them over the nullable `getContext()`, `getActivity()`, `getView()`.
- ✅ **ViewModel + LiveData** is the recommended way to share data between Fragments, NOT direct Fragment-to-Fragment calls.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lifecycle of Fragments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lifecycle of Fragments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Fragment** in Android represents a reusable portion of UI that can be embedded in an Activity. A Fragment has its **own lifecycle**, receives its **own input events**, and can be added or removed while the Activity is running, making it behave like a **mini-Activity** that lives inside an Activity.

The **Fragment Lifecycle** is the complete set of **callback methods** invoked by the Android framework as a Fragment transitions through various states — from the moment it is *attached* to its host Activity, through the *creation* of its view, *visibility*, *interaction*, and finally *destruction* and *detachment*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** The Fragment Lifecycle is a state-driven, callback-based execution model defined by the `androidx.fragment.app.Fragment` class. It consists of **three nested lifecycles**:
> 1. **Fragment Lifecycle** (entire existence of the Fragment instance)
> 2. **Fragment View Lifecycle** (the UI hierarchy's existence)
> 3. **Fragment Data Lifecycle** (SavedInstanceState behavior)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Apartment Building"

Imagine a **Fragment** as a **rental apartment inside a large building (the Activity)**.

| Android Concept | Real-World Analogy | Lifecycle Stage |
|---|---|---|
| Fragment instantiation | Tenant signs the lease | `onAttach()` |
| View creation | Furniture is moved in | `onCreateView()` |
| View becomes visible | Lights turned on, blinds open | `onStart()` |
| User interacts with UI | Tenant entertaining guests | `onResume()` |
| User receives a phone call | Brief pause in activity | `onPause()` |
| User navigates away | Tenant leaves the apartment | `onStop()` |
| View destroyed | Furniture removed | `onDestroyView()` |
| Fragment destroyed | Lease terminated | `onDestroy()` |
| Fragment detached | Tenant officially moves out | `onDetach()` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as an apartment's "furniture" (view) can be removed before the "lease" (fragment) ends, the **View Lifecycle can be destroyed while the Fragment still exists**. This is critical for KTU exam questions on memory leaks and configuration changes.

## 1.3 Why Fragments Have a Separate Lifecycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fragments were introduced in **Android 3.0 (Honeycomb, API 11)** primarily to support **responsive UI design** — allowing apps to dynamically rearrange UI components on tablets vs. phones. Their lifecycle decouples UI management from Activity management, enabling **modular, reusable, and back-stack-aware** components.

The Fragment lifecycle is **bound to** but **not identical to** the host Activity's lifecycle — this is the most important concept for KTU exams.

## 1.4 Fragment States (KTU High-Yield Concept)

A Fragment can be in exactly one of **three primary states** at any given time:

- **RESUMED** — Fragment is visible and actively running (highest priority)
- **STARTED** — Fragment is visible but not in focus (e.g., partially obscured)
- **STOPPED** — Fragment is not visible (lowest priority, retains state)

> [!NOTE]
> The Fragment's state is **promoted** as it moves toward `onResume()` and **demoted** as it moves toward `onDestroy()`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Nested Lifecycle Tracks

The Fragment lifecycle is best understood as **three parallel, overlapping tracks**:

### Track 1: Fragment Instance Lifecycle (Entire Existence)
```
onAttach() → onCreate() → onCreateView() → onViewCreated() →
onStart() → onResume() → [RUNNING] → onPause() → onStop() →
onDestroyView() → onDestroy() → onDetach()
```

### Track 2: Fragment View Lifecycle (UI Hierarchy)
```
onCreateView() → onViewCreated() → onViewStateRestored() →
[VIEW ACTIVE] → onPause() → onStop() → onDestroyView()
```

### Track 3: Fragment Data Lifecycle (State Preservation)
```
onSaveInstanceState() → onViewStateRestored()
```

## 2.2 Exhaustive Callback Method Breakdown

| # | Callback Method | Trigger Condition | Purpose | View Available? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `onAttach(Context)` | Fragment associated with Activity | Get Activity reference, initialize Activity-dependent resources | ❌ No |
| 2 | `onCreate(Bundle)` | Fragment first created | Non-UI initialization (variables, retain state) | ❌ No |
| 3 | `onCreateView(LayoutInflater, ViewGroup, Bundle)` | UI inflation required | **Return inflated View** — most critical method | ❌ No (returning) |
| 4 | `onViewCreated(View, Bundle)` | View created, not yet visible | Initialize UI widgets, attach listeners | ✅ Yes |
| 5 | `onActivityCreated(Bundle)` | *Deprecated in API 28* — host Activity's `onCreate()` finished | Use `onViewCreated()` instead | ✅ Yes |
| 6 | `onViewStateRestored(Bundle)` | View state restored from saved bundle | Read saved state into UI | ✅ Yes |
| 7 | `onStart()` | Fragment visible | Start visible-only operations (animations) | ✅ Yes |
| 8 | `onResume()` | Fragment in focus, interactive | Resume paused operations (sensors, GPS) | ✅ Yes |
| 9 | `onPause()` | Another fragment/activity takes focus | Pause UI-only operations, commit edits | ✅ Yes |
| 10 | `onSaveInstanceState(Bundle)` | Before destruction for state save | Save state to bundle (key-value pairs) | ✅ Yes |
| 11 | `onStop()` | Fragment no longer visible | Stop heavy operations | ✅ Yes |
| 12 | `onDestroyView()` | View hierarchy removed | **Clean up view references** (prevent memory leaks) | ❌ Destroying |
| 13 | `onDestroy()` | Fragment instance being destroyed | Final cleanup (threads, listeners) | ❌ No |
| 14 | `onDetach()` | Fragment no longer associated with Activity | Release Activity reference | ❌ No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** `onCreateView()` and `onDestroyView()` can be called **multiple times** during a Fragment's existence (e.g., when navigating with back stack). The `onCreate()` and `onDestroy()` are called **only once**.

## 2.3 Lifecycle State Diagram (Logical Flow)

```
                    ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
                    │        FRAGMENT INSTANCE LIFECYCLE    │
                    └──────────────────────────────────────┘
                                      │
                  ┌───────────────────┴───────────────────┐
                  ▼                                        ▼
            [CREATED]                                 [DESTROYED]
            onAttach()                                onDetach()
            onCreate()                                onDestroy()
                  │                                        ▲
                  ▼                                        │
            [VIEW CREATED]  ───onDestroyView()──►  [VIEW DESTROYED]
            onCreateView()                          (Fragment still alive)
            onViewCreated()
            onViewStateRestored()
                  │
                  ▼
            [STARTED]  ◄──────onStop()──────  [STOPPED]
                  │
                  ▼
            [RESUMED]  ◄──────onPause()─────  [PAUSED]
                  │
                  ▼
              [ACTIVE/RUNNING]
```

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Fragment Lifecycle Decision Matrix

| Scenario | Callbacks Invoked (Forward) | Callbacks Invoked (Reverse) |
|---|---|---|
| **App first launched** | `onAttach → onCreate → onCreateView → onViewCreated → onStart → onResume` | — |
| **User presses Home** | — | `onPause → onStop` |
| **User returns to app** | `onStart → onResume` | — |
| **Configuration change (rotation)** | `onPause → onStop → onDestroyView → onDestroy → onDetach` → **NEW** `onAttach → ... → onResume` | — |
| **Fragment replaced in back stack** | — | `onPause → onStop → onDestroyView` |
| **Back stack popped** | `onCreateView → onViewCreated → onStart → onResume` | — |
| **Activity finishes** | — | `onPause → onStop → onDestroyView → onDestroy → onDetach` |
| **System kills process (low memory)** | — | `onPause → onStop → onSaveInstanceState` (then process killed) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT Context (PECGT415 Specific):** In IoT mobile apps, Fragment lifecycles are crucial for managing **sensor subscriptions, MQTT connections, and BLE device links**. For example:
> - `onResume()` → Subscribe to sensor data stream
> - `onPause()` → Unsubscribe (battery saving)
> - `onDestroyView()` → Release BLE GATT references
> - `onSaveInstanceState()` → Cache last-known sensor readings

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Walkthrough: A Fragment's Complete Life (Analytical Derivation)

Let's trace a Fragment's lifecycle using a **logical state-transition model**. Define:

- $S_f$ = Current Fragment instance state
- $S_v$ = Current Fragment view state
- $S_a$ = Host Activity state
- $C_i$ = Callback method $i$ where $i \in \{1, 2, ..., 14\}$

The Fragment's life can be modeled as a **state machine transition sequence**:

$$
T_{seq} = \big\langle S_{f0}, S_{v0}, S_{a0} \big\rangle \xrightarrow{C_1} \big\langle S_{f1}, S_{v0}, S_{a0} \big\rangle \xrightarrow{C_2} \cdots \xrightarrow{C_{14}} \big\langle S_{fN}, S_{v0}, S_{a0} \big\rangle
$$

Where $S_{f0}$ = `NOT_ATTACHED` and $S_{fN}$ = `DETACHED`.

### Step 1: Initial Attachment

$$
\text{FragmentManager calls } C_1 = \texttt{onAttach(context)}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow S_f = \texttt{ATTACHED}, \quad S_v = \texttt{NULL}, \quad \text{Activity ref obtained}
$$

**Logic:** The framework first binds the Fragment to its host Activity. At this point, `getActivity()` returns a valid reference, but no view exists yet.

### Step 2: Fragment Created

$$
\text{Framework calls } C_2 = \texttt{onCreate(savedInstanceState)}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow S_f = \texttt{CREATED}, \quad S_v = \texttt{NULL}
$$

**Logic:** The Fragment's non-UI instance is created. Initialize variables, retrieve arguments via `getArguments()`. **Do not** touch UI here.

### Step 3: View Inflated

$$
\text{Framework calls } C_3 = \texttt{onCreateView(inflater, container, savedInstanceState)}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow S_v = \texttt{INFLATED}, \quad \text{return } v \text{ where } v = \texttt{inflater.inflate(R.layout.fragment\_my, container, false)}
$$

**Logic:** This is the **only** method where the View hierarchy is created. The return value is the root view of the Fragment's UI.

### Step 4: View State Restored

$$
\text{Framework calls } C_4 = \texttt{onViewCreated(view, savedInstanceState)}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \text{view.findViewById()} \text{ is now safe to call}
$$

**Logic:** Post-view creation hook. Bind adapters, set click listeners, configure RecyclerViews here.

### Step 5: Visibility Achieved

$$
\text{Framework calls } C_7 = \texttt{onStart()}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow S_f = \texttt{STARTED}
$$

**Logic:** Fragment is now visible but may not have focus. Begin animations or visibility-dependent work.

### Step 6: Resumed (Active)

$$
\text{Framework calls } C_8 = \texttt{onResume()}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow S_f = \texttt{RESUMED} \quad \text{(Fragment receives input events)}
$$

**Logic:** Fragment is fully active. **Start resource-intensive tasks** like sensor listeners, network subscriptions, or IoT device connections.

### Step 7: Paused

$$
\text{Framework calls } C_9 = \texttt{onPause()}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow S_f = \texttt{STARTED}
$$

**Logic:** Another component has focus. **Commit unsaved changes**, stop animations that should not continue in background.

### Step 8: Saved State (if process may be killed)

$$
\text{Framework calls } C_{10} = \texttt{onSaveInstanceState(outState)}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \text{outState.putString("key", "value")}
$$

**Logic:** Persist transient state. Note: `onSaveInstanceState()` is called **between `onPause()` and `onStop()`**.

### Step 9: Stopped

$$
\text{Framework calls } C_{11} = \texttt{onStop()}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow S_f = \texttt{CREATED}
$$

**Logic:** Fragment is no longer visible. **Stop heavy operations** — IoT polling, location updates, etc.

### Step 10: View Destroyed

$$
\text{Framework calls } C_{12} = \texttt{onDestroyView()}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow S_v = \texttt{NULL}
$$

**Logic:** The View hierarchy is being torn down. **Critical:** Set all view references to `null` to prevent memory leaks.

### Step 11: Fragment Destroyed

$$
\text{Framework calls } C_{13} = \texttt{onDestroy()}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow S_f = \texttt{DESTROYED}
$$

**Logic:** Fragment instance is being permanently removed. Clean up threads, broadcast receivers, and coroutine scopes.

### Step 12: Detached

$$
\text{Framework calls } C_{14} = \texttt{onDetach()}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \text{Activity reference released}
$$

**Logic:** Fragment is no longer associated with any Activity. Final cleanup hook.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — View vs. Instance Lifecycle

| Property | View Lifecycle | Instance Lifecycle |
|---|---|---|
| **Starts at** | `onCreateView()` | `onAttach()` |
| **Ends at** | `onDestroyView()` | `onDetach()` |
| **Can repeat?** | ✅ Yes (back-stack pop) | ❌ No (once per instance) |
| **State preserved** | In `onSaveInstanceState()` | In `onSaveInstanceState()` |
| **Triggered on rotation** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (unless `setRetainInstance(true)`) |
| **Use for UI cleanup** | ✅ `onDestroyView()` | ❌ Wrong place |
| **Use for thread cleanup** | ❌ Too early | ✅ `onDestroy()` |

## 3.3 Mathematical Model — Configuration Change Behavior

Define $R$ as a configuration change event (e.g., screen rotation). The Fragment transition can be expressed as:

$$
\text{Pre-}R: \quad S_f = \texttt{RESUMED}, \quad S_v = \texttt{INFLATED}
$$

$$
\text{Post-}R: \quad S_f = \texttt{RESUMED}, \quad S_v = \texttt{INFLATED} \quad \text{(new instance)}
$$

The transition sequence is:

$$
\texttt{RESUMED} \xrightarrow{\texttt{onPause}} \texttt{STARTED} \xrightarrow{\texttt{onStop}} \texttt{CREATED} \xrightarrow{\texttt{onDestroyView}} \texttt{VIEW\_DESTROYED} \xrightarrow{\texttt{onDestroy}} \texttt{DESTROYED} \xrightarrow{\texttt{onDetach}} \texttt{DETACHED}
$$

$$
\rightarrow \text{NEW INSTANCE} \rightarrow \texttt{onAttach} \rightarrow \texttt{onCreate} \rightarrow \texttt{onCreateView} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \texttt{onResume}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Without** `setRetainInstance(true)` (deprecated in AndroidX), the **Fragment instance is destroyed** and **recreated** on configuration change. **With** `ViewModel`, data survives across configuration changes without retaining the Fragment.

# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Differentiate between the lifecycle of an Activity and a Fragment. List any THREE fragment-specific lifecycle methods that do not exist in the Activity lifecycle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Activity Lifecycle | Fragment Lifecycle |
|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | Single linear lifecycle | Three nested lifecycles (Fragment, View, Data) |
| **Attachment** | Independent | Must be attached to a host Activity |
| **View creation** | `setContentView()` in `onCreate()` | Separate `onCreateView()` method |
| **Detachment** | `onDestroy()` only | `onDetach()` after `onDestroy()` |

**Three Fragment-specific lifecycle methods (not in Activity):**
1. **`onAttach(Context)`** — Called when Fragment is associated with host Activity.
2. **`onCreateView(LayoutInflater, ViewGroup, Bundle)`** — Returns the View hierarchy for the Fragment's UI.
3. **`onDestroyView()`** — Cleans up view resources; called separately from `onDestroy()`.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Explain the significance of `onCreateView()` and `onDestroyView()` callbacks in the Fragment lifecycle. Why is `onDestroyView()` critical for memory management?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**`onCreateView()` (1 mark):**
- This callback is responsible for **inflating the Fragment's UI layout**.
- It receives a `LayoutInflater`, a parent `ViewGroup`, and a `Bundle` of saved state.
- It must return a `View` object that becomes the Fragment's root view.
- This method can be **called multiple times** during the Fragment's lifetime (especially when using back-stack navigation).

**`onDestroyView()` (1 mark):**
- Called when the **View hierarchy is being removed** (e.g., when the Fragment is replaced or popped from back stack).
- It is critical for **memory management** because view references must be explicitly nullified.
- Without proper cleanup, view references may leak, causing **memory leaks** — especially when holding references to context-bound resources like `Context`, `Activity`, or `Drawable` objects.

**Significance in IoT Apps (1 mark):**
- In IoT apps, fragments often hold **sensor listeners, BLE connections, and database cursors**. Cleaning these up in `onDestroyView()` prevents resource leaks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete Fragment lifecycle with a neat diagram. Describe each callback method and its purpose in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between the **Fragment lifecycle**, **Fragment View lifecycle**, and **Fragment Data lifecycle**. Discuss why it is essential to understand these distinctions when developing IoT applications that use fragments to display real-time sensor data.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
A Fragment is a reusable portion of UI embedded in an Activity. It has its own lifecycle that closely mirrors but extends the Activity lifecycle. The Fragment lifecycle consists of **14 callback methods** invoked by the Android framework.

**2. Lifecycle Diagram (2 marks):**

```
onAttach() → onCreate() → onCreateView() → onViewCreated() 
   → onActivityCreated() [deprecated] → onStart() → onResume()
   → [RUNNING] → onPause() → onStop() → onDestroyView() 
   → onDestroy() → onDetach()
```

**3. Detailed Explanation of Key Callbacks (4 marks):**

| Method | Purpose | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| `onAttach()` | Called when Fragment is first attached to Activity. Use to get Activity reference. | 0.5 |
| `onCreate()` | Non-UI initialization. Receive saved state bundle. | 0.5 |
| `onCreateView()` | **Returns the inflated View** — the only method that must return a View. | 0.5 |
| `onViewCreated()` | View is created; safe to bind views and set listeners. | 0.5 |
| `onStart()` | Fragment is visible but may not have focus. | 0.5 |
| `onResume()` | Fragment is in focus and receiving user input. | 0.5 |
| `onPause()` | Another component has focus. Commit pending changes. | 0.5 |
| `onSaveInstanceState()` | Save state before potential destruction. | 0.5 |
| `onDestroyView()` | Cleanup view-related resources. | 0.5 |
| `onDestroy()` | Final cleanup of Fragment instance. | 0.5 |

**4. Conclusion (covered in marks above):**
The lifecycle ensures fragments are properly initialized, displayed, paused, and destroyed — preventing memory leaks and providing responsive UI behavior.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**1. The Three Nested Lifecycles (3 marks):**

**(i) Fragment Instance Lifecycle:**
- Spans from `onAttach()` to `onDetach()`.
- Covers the **entire existence** of the Fragment object in memory.
- Called **only once** per Fragment instance.

**(ii) Fragment View Lifecycle:**
- Spans from `onCreateView()` to `onDestroyView()`.
- Covers the **existence of the UI hierarchy**.
- Can be called **multiple times** for the same Fragment instance (e.g., when added to back stack and later restored).

**(iii) Fragment Data Lifecycle:**
- Spans across `onSaveInstanceState()` and `onViewStateRestored()`.
- Manages **state preservation** across configuration changes and process death.

**2. Differences (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Instance Lifecycle | View Lifecycle | Data Lifecycle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Fragment object | View hierarchy | Saved state |
| **Repetition** | Once | Multiple times | Per destruction |
| **Purpose** | Object management | UI management | State preservation |

**3. Importance in IoT Applications (2 marks):**

In IoT apps, fragments commonly display **real-time sensor data** (temperature, humidity, GPS, etc.). Understanding the three lifecycle types is critical for:

- **Subscribing/unsubscribing to sensors:** Use `onResume()` to subscribe and `onPause()` to unsubscribe — saving battery.
- **Cleaning up view references:** In IoT, fragments may hold references to BLE GATT objects or MQTT clients. Cleanup in `onDestroyView()` is essential.
- **Preserving sensor data:** Use `onSaveInstanceState()` to cache last sensor readings before configuration changes.
- **Avoiding null pointer exceptions:** Recognizing that the View may be destroyed while the Fragment instance still exists helps prevent crashes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the different states a Fragment can be in? Explain with a state diagram. Discuss what happens to a Fragment's state during a configuration change (such as screen rotation).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Android Fragment class (in Java or Kotlin) that:
- Logs each lifecycle callback as it is invoked.
- Properly cleans up a simulated "IoT sensor listener" in the correct lifecycle methods.
- Demonstrates the difference between the View and Instance lifecycles.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Fragment States (3 marks):**

A Fragment can exist in one of these states at any time:

- **RESUMED:** The fragment is visible, in the foreground, and receiving user input. This is the **active state**.
- **STARTED:** The fragment is visible but not in the foreground. For example, partially obscured by another fragment or dialog.
- **STOPPED:** The fragment is not visible to the user. The instance and state information are retained, but the view may or may not be destroyed.

**2. State Diagram (2 marks):**

```
   ┌──────────────┐
   │   RESUMED    │ ◄──── onResume()
   └──────┬───────┘
          │ onPause()
          ▼
   ┌──────────────┐
   │   STARTED    │ ◄──── onStart()
   └──────┬───────┘
          │ onStop()
          ▼
   ┌──────────────┐
   │   STOPPED    │
   └──────────────┘
```

**3. Configuration Change Behavior (2 marks):**

When a configuration change occurs (e.g., screen rotation), Android performs the following sequence:

1. `onPause()` → `onStop()` → `onSaveInstanceState()` → `onDestroyView()` → `onDestroy()` → `onDetach()`
2. A **new Fragment instance** is created.
3. `onAttach()` → `onCreate()` → `onCreateView()` → `onViewCreated()` → `onStart()` → `onResume()`

The saved `Bundle` is passed to the new instance so that state can be restored.

> [!NOTE]
> To preserve data across configuration changes without recreating the Fragment, use **`ViewModel`** (recommended) or the deprecated `setRetainInstance(true)`.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Java Implementation:**

```java
public class IoTFragment extends Fragment {
    private static final String TAG = "IoTFragment";
    private TextView sensorDataView;
    private IoTSensorListener sensorListener; // Simulated IoT sensor
    
    // 1. Instance Lifecycle: Called when Fragment is attached to Activity
    @Override
    public void onAttach(@NonNull Context context) {
        super.onAttach(context);
        Log.d(TAG, "onAttach: Fragment attached to Activity");
    }
    
    // 2. Instance Lifecycle: Non-UI initialization
    @Override
    public void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        Log.d(TAG, "onCreate: Fragment instance created");
        // Initialize non-UI components here
        sensorListener = new IoTSensorListener();
    }
    
    // 3. View Lifecycle: Inflate the layout
    @Override
    public View onCreateView(LayoutInflater inflater, ViewGroup container, 
                             Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        Log.d(TAG, "onCreateView: View being created");
        return inflater.inflate(R.layout.fragment_iot, container, false);
    }
    
    // 4. View Lifecycle: Bind views after creation
    @Override
    public void onViewCreated(@NonNull View view, Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onViewCreated(view, savedInstanceState);
        Log.d(TAG, "onViewCreated: View is ready");
        sensorDataView = view.findViewById(R.id.sensor_data);
    }
    
    // 5. View Lifecycle: Restore saved state
    @Override
    public void onViewStateRestored(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onViewStateRestored(savedInstanceState);
        Log.d(TAG, "onViewStateRestored: View state restored");
        if (savedInstanceState != null) {
            String lastReading = savedInstanceState.getString("sensor_data");
            if (lastReading != null) sensorDataView.setText(lastReading);
        }
    }
    
    // 6. Visibility: Fragment becomes visible
    @Override
    public void onStart() {
        super.onStart();
        Log.d(TAG, "onStart: Fragment is visible");
    }
    
    // 7. Resumed: Fragment in focus - START IoT sensor here
    @Override
    public void onResume() {
        super.onResume();
        Log.d(TAG, "onResume: Fragment is active - Starting sensor");
        sensorListener.start(); // Begin real-time IoT data collection
    }
    
    // 8. Paused: STOP heavy operations
    @Override
    public void onPause() {
        super.onPause();
        Log.d(TAG, "onPause: Pausing sensor to save battery");
        sensorListener.stop();
    }
    
    // 9. Save state before potential destruction
    @Override
    public void onSaveInstanceState(@NonNull Bundle outState) {
        super.onSaveInstanceState(outState);
        Log.d(TAG, "onSaveInstanceState: Saving sensor reading");
        outState.putString("sensor_data", sensorDataView.getText().toString());
    }
    
    // 10. Stopped: Fragment no longer visible
    @Override
    public void onStop() {
        super.onStop();
        Log.d(TAG, "onStop: Fragment is no longer visible");
    }
    
    // 11. View Lifecycle: Cleanup view references (CRITICAL)
    @Override
    public void onDestroyView() {
        super.onDestroyView();
        Log.d(TAG, "onDestroyView: Cleaning up view references");
        sensorDataView = null; // Prevent memory leak
    }
    
    // 12. Instance Lifecycle: Final cleanup of Fragment instance
    @Override
    public void onDestroy() {
        super.onDestroy();
        Log.d(TAG, "onDestroy: Cleaning up sensor listener");
        sensorListener.release();
    }
    
    // 13. Instance Lifecycle: Detach from Activity
    @Override
    public void onDetach() {
        super.onDetach();
        Log.d(TAG, "onDetach: Fragment detached from Activity");
    }
}
```

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct onAttach / onCreate / onDetach / onDestroy (instance lifecycle) | 2 |
| Correct onCreateView / onViewCreated / onDestroyView (view lifecycle) | 2 |
| Correct onResume / onPause (sensor subscription logic) | 1.5 |
| Correct onSaveInstanceState (state preservation) | 0.5 |
| Log statements showing each callback | 0.5 |
| Proper cleanup of sensor listener in onDestroy | 0.5 |

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **❌ Confusing `onCreate()` with `onCreateView()`:** `onCreate()` is for **non-UI** initialization; `onCreateView()` is where you **inflate the layout**. Many students mix these up.
> 
> 2. **❌ Forgetting to call `super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)` etc.:** Every overridden lifecycle method **must call the superclass implementation**, or the Fragment will throw a runtime exception.
> 
> 3. **❌ Using `findViewById()` in `onCreate()`:** This will cause a **NullPointerException** because the view doesn't exist yet. Use it in `onViewCreated()` or later.
> 
> 4. **❌ Not cleaning up in `onDestroyView()`:** This leads to memory leaks — a **favourite KTU question** for 7-mark problems.
> 
> 5. **❌ Assuming `onCreateView()` is called only once:** It can be called **multiple times** if the Fragment is added to the back stack and later restored. This is a common trap question.
> 
> 6. **❌ Mixing Activity and Fragment lifecycle methods:** Activity does NOT have `onCreateView()`, `onDestroyView()`, `onAttach()`, or `onDetach()`. These are Fragment-exclusive.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Lifecycle of Fragments**

### 🎯 Core Concepts
- ✅ A Fragment has **14 lifecycle callbacks**, grouped into **three nested lifecycles**: Instance, View, and Data.
- ✅ `onAttach()` and `onDetach()` mark the **beginning and end** of a Fragment's existence.
- ✅ `onCreateView()` and `onDestroyView()` mark the **beginning and end** of a Fragment's UI hierarchy.
- ✅ The Fragment lifecycle is **bound to** but **not identical to** the host Activity's lifecycle.

### 🔑 Critical Distinctions
- ✅ `onCreate()` ≠ `onCreateView()` — first is for non-UI init, second is for view inflation.
- ✅ `onDestroy()` ≠ `onDestroyView()` — destroy the Fragment instance vs. destroy the view only.
- ✅ View lifecycle can repeat; Instance lifecycle runs once per Fragment.

### 🧠 Must-Remember Callbacks
- ✅ **`onCreateView()`** — only method that **must return a View**.
- ✅ **`onViewCreated()`** — safe place to call `findViewById()`.
- ✅ **`onResume()`** — Fragment is in focus, receives user input.
- ✅ **`onPause()`** — Commit pending changes; pause animations.
- ✅ **`onSaveInstanceState()`** — Called between `onPause()` and `onStop()`.
- ✅ **`onDestroyView()`** — **Critical for memory leak prevention**.

### 📊 Fragment States
- ✅ **RESUMED** → visible + focus + user input
- ✅ **STARTED** → visible + no focus
- ✅ **STOPPED** → not visible, state retained

### 🔄 Configuration Changes
- ✅ Default behavior: Fragment is **destroyed and recreated**.
- ✅ Use **`ViewModel`** to preserve data across configuration changes.
- ✅ `setRetainInstance(true)` is **deprecated** in AndroidX — use ViewModel instead.

### 💡 IoT-Specific Best Practices
- ✅ Subscribe to sensors in `onResume()`.
- ✅ Unsubscribe in `onPause()` to save battery.
- ✅ Release BLE/MQTT resources in `onDestroyView()` or `onDestroy()`.
- ✅ Cache critical data in `onSaveInstanceState()`.

### 🎓 KTU Exam-Specific Tips
- ✅ Always mention **why** a callback is used, not just **when** it is called.
- ✅ For 14-mark questions, include a **diagram** — it earns easy marks.
- ✅ Mention **memory leak prevention** in any answer about `onDestroyView()`.
- ✅ Be explicit about the **difference between Activity and Fragment lifecycle** when asked.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fragment States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fragment States

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Fragment** in Android is a reusable, modular section of an Activity that has its own lifecycle, receives its own input events, and can be added or removed while the host Activity is running. **Fragment States** refer to the distinct, deterministic phases a Fragment transitions through during its existence, governed by the **FragmentManager** and bound to the lifecycle of the host Activity.

The Android documentation formally defines **five core states** in which a Fragment can exist at any given moment:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — The Five Canonical Fragment States**
> 1. **RESUMED** — Fragment is visible, active, and receiving user input.
> 2. **STARTED** — Fragment is visible but does not receive user focus.
> 3. **CREATED** — Fragment instance exists but not yet visible to user.
> 4. **ATTACHED** — Fragment is attached to a host Activity but not yet fully created.
> 5. **DETACHED / DESTROYED** — Fragment is no longer usable; its view is destroyed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Picture Frame" Model

Imagine an **Activity** is a large empty wall in a museum, and each **Fragment** is a self-contained picture frame you can mount on that wall.

- The **picture frame** (Fragment) has its own components: glass, backing, the painting itself, and a label.
- The **wall** (Activity) decides *when* the frame is shown.
- The **museum curator** (FragmentManager) decides which frames are mounted, swapped, or taken down.
- A frame can be **on the wall and lit up** (RESUMED), **on the wall but in dim light** (STARTED), **stored in the back room** (CREATED), **delivered but not yet mounted** (ATTACHED), or **taken down and discarded** (DESTROYED).

This analogy maps perfectly to Android Fragments, where the lifecycle callbacks (onAttach → onCreate → onCreateView → onActivityCreated → onStart → onResume → onPause → onStop → onDestroyView → onDestroy → onDetach) drive the state transitions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams**: Fragment states are *not* the same as Activity states. A Fragment's state is a **sub-state** of the host Activity, but Fragments also have **independent state transitions** triggered by back-stack operations, configuration changes, and explicit transactions.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Fragment minimum SDK** | API Level 11 (Android 3.0) | Fragments introduced in Honeycomb |
| **Back-stack capacity** | **Unlimited** (memory-bounded) | Managed by FragmentManager |
| **onCreateView inflation latency** | Typically **<16ms** (1 frame) | Performance threshold |
| **State save bundle size** | Recommended **<500KB** | Android TransactionTooLargeException threshold |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fragment Lifecycle — State Transition Map

A Fragment moves through states based on **two parallel lifecycles**:
- **Fragment Lifecycle** (independent of UI)
- **Fragment View Lifecycle** (tied to the inflated view hierarchy)

### 2.1.1 Detailed State Definitions

| State | Description | Callback Entry Point | Callback Exit Point |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DETACHED** | Fragment not attached to any Activity | — | `onAttach()` |
| **ATTACHED** | Fragment attached to host Activity | `onAttach()` | `onDetach()` |
| **CREATED** | Fragment instance created; view NOT yet inflated | `onCreate()` | `onCreateView()` |
| **VIEW_CREATED** | View hierarchy inflated via `onCreateView()` | `onCreateView()` | `onDestroyView()` |
| **STARTED** | Fragment visible to user | `onStart()` | `onStop()` |
| **RESUMED** | Fragment active and receiving input | `onResume()` | `onPause()` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point**: The `onActivityCreated()` callback was **deprecated in API 28**. It is replaced by `onViewStateRestored()`. Always mention this in viva questions.

## 2.2 State Transition Rules (KTU Board-Exam Critical)

1. **Forward Transition Invariant**: A Fragment must pass through states in strict order:
$$\text{DETACHED} \rightarrow \text{ATTACHED} \rightarrow \text{CREATED} \rightarrow \text{VIEW\_CREATED} \rightarrow \text{STARTED} \rightarrow \text{RESUMED}$$

2. **Backward Transition Invariant**: Reverse order is also strictly enforced:
$$\text{RESUMED} \rightarrow \text{STARTED} \rightarrow \text{VIEW\_CREATED} \rightarrow \text{CREATED} \rightarrow \text{ATTACHED} \rightarrow \text{DETACHED}$$

3. **State Decoupling Rule**: The Fragment lifecycle and the Fragment View lifecycle are **independent**. The view can be destroyed and recreated (e.g., on configuration change) without destroying the Fragment instance.

4. **Back-Stack Interaction Rule**: When a Fragment is replaced and added to the back-stack, it transitions to `STOPPED` (and its view is destroyed) but its instance is preserved. Popping the back-stack restores it through `STARTED → RESUMED`.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Lifecycle State | Entering Callback | Exiting Callback | UI Visible? | Receives Input? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DETACHED** | None (initial) | `onAttach()` | No | No |
| **ATTACHED** | `onAttach()` | `onCreate()` | No | No |
| **CREATED** | `onCreate()` | `onCreateView()` | No | No |
| **VIEW_CREATED** | `onCreateView()` | `onViewStateRestored()` | No | No |
| **STARTED** | `onStart()` | `onResume()` | Yes | No |
| **RESUMED** | `onResume()` | `onPause()` | Yes | Yes |
| **PAUSED (transient)** | `onPause()` | `onStop()` | Partial | No |
| **STOPPED** | `onStop()` | `onDestroyView()` | No | No |
| **DESTROYED_VIEW** | `onDestroyView()` | `onDestroy()` | No | No |
| **DESTROYED** | `onDestroy()` | `onDetach()` | No | No |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world IoT Utility**: In IoT mobile apps, Fragment states are critical when designing **sensor data dashboards** that must persist across orientation changes. For example, a Fragment displaying live temperature readings from an MQTT broker must save its state in `onSaveInstanceState()` and restore it in `onViewStateRestored()` to avoid losing the connection state.

## 2.4 Why Fragment States Matter — Engineering Rationale

- **Memory Efficiency**: The Android system can destroy a Fragment's view (via `onDestroyView()`) while keeping the Fragment instance alive, freeing up to **~70% of memory** held by the inflated view hierarchy.
- **State Preservation**: Configuration changes (rotation, locale change) trigger view destruction but not Fragment destruction, allowing seamless UI restoration.
- **Back-Stack Navigation**: Fragments on the back-stack are kept in `CREATED` state with their views destroyed, enabling low-memory overhead while preserving user navigation history.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the State Transition Function

Let $S = \{s_1, s_2, ..., s_n\}$ represent the set of all Fragment states. The Fragment lifecycle can be modeled as a **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)** where each state transition is triggered by a specific event.

### 3.1.1 Mathematical Formulation

Let the state transition function be defined as:

$$\delta : S \times E \rightarrow S$$

where $E = \{e_1, e_2, ..., e_m\}$ is the set of lifecycle events.

**Step 1**: Define the state space explicitly.

The Fragment states form an ordered set:
$$S = \{s_{\text{DETACHED}}, s_{\text{ATTACHED}}, s_{\text{CREATED}}, s_{\text{VIEW\_CREATED}}, s_{\text{STARTED}}, s_{\text{RESUMED}}\}$$

**Step 2**: Define the event space.

Each lifecycle callback corresponds to an event:
$$E = \{e_{\text{onAttach}}, e_{\text{onCreate}}, e_{\text{onCreateView}}, e_{\text{onStart}}, e_{\text{onResume}}, e_{\text{onPause}}, e_{\text{onStop}}, e_{\text{onDestroyView}}, e_{\text{onDestroy}}, e_{\text{onDetach}}\}$$

**Step 3**: Express the forward transition rule.

For forward lifecycle progression, the transition is strictly linear:

$$\delta(s_i, e_{\text{next}}) = s_{i+1} \quad \text{for } i = 1 \text{ to } 5$$

**Step 4**: Express the backward transition rule.

For backward progression (e.g., when an Activity is paused):

$$\delta(s_i, e_{\text{prev}}) = s_{i-1} \quad \text{for } i = 2 \text{ to } 6$$

**Step 5**: Express the view lifecycle decoupling.

The view can be destroyed while the Fragment instance persists:

$$\delta(s_{\text{VIEW\_CREATED}}, e_{\text{onDestroyView}}) = s_{\text{CREATED}} \quad \text{(view destroyed, instance preserved)}$$

**Step 6**: Express the back-stack transition.

When a Fragment is replaced and added to the back-stack:

$$\delta(s_{\text{RESUMED}}, e_{\text{replace}}) = s_{\text{CREATED}} \quad \text{(view destroyed, instance on back-stack)}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — State Behavior Under Common Events

| Event | Source State | Destination State | View Destroyed? | Instance Preserved? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Activity created** | DETACHED | CREATED | N/A | Yes |
| **Fragment view inflated** | CREATED | VIEW_CREATED | No (newly created) | Yes |
| **Fragment becomes visible** | VIEW_CREATED | STARTED | No | Yes |
| **Fragment receives focus** | STARTED | RESUMED | No | Yes |
| **User navigates away** | RESUMED | CREATED | Yes | Yes (on back-stack) |
| **Configuration change** | RESUMED | VIEW_CREATED | Yes | Yes |
| **Activity destroyed** | any | DETACHED | Yes | No |
| **Fragment removed (no back-stack)** | any | DETACHED | Yes | No |

### 3.3 Worked Example — IoT Dashboard Fragment

**Problem**: An IoT mobile app has a Fragment displaying real-time sensor data. The user rotates the device. Trace the state transitions.

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: Initial state — Fragment is `RESUMED` and receiving data.
$$S_{\text{initial}} = s_{\text{RESUMED}}$$

**Step 2**: User rotates device → Activity is destroyed and recreated.
$$e_{\text{rotation}} \in E$$

**Step 3**: First, the Fragment receives `onPause()`, then `onStop()`, then `onDestroyView()`.
$$\delta(s_{\text{RESUMED}}, e_{\text{onPause}}) = s_{\text{STARTED}}$$

$$\delta(s_{\text{STARTED}}, e_{\text{onStop}}) = s_{\text{VIEW\_CREATED}}$$

$$\delta(s_{\text{VIEW\_CREATED}}, e_{\text{onDestroyView}}) = s_{\text{CREATED}}$$

**Step 4**: Fragment instance is preserved (not destroyed). The `onSaveInstanceState()` callback saves the data state.
$$S_{\text{preserved}} = s_{\text{CREATED}}, \quad \text{with} \quad \text{state} = \text{savedBundle}$$

**Step 5**: New Activity instance is created. Fragment is re-attached.
$$\delta(s_{\text{CREATED}}, e_{\text{onCreateView}}) = s_{\text{VIEW\_CREATED}}$$

**Step 6**: Fragment view is restored with saved data.
$$\delta(s_{\text{VIEW\_CREATED}}, e_{\text{onViewStateRestored}}) = s_{\text{STARTED}}$$

**Step 7**: Fragment receives focus again.
$$\delta(s_{\text{STARTED}}, e_{\text{onResume}}) = s_{\text{RESUMED}}$$

**Final State**: Fragment is back to `RESUMED` with all sensor data preserved.

$$S_{\text{final}} = s_{\text{RESUMED}}, \quad \text{Data continuity} = 100\%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fragment State Transition Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> sDetached
    sDetached --> sAttached: onAttach()
    sAttached --> sCreated: onCreate()
    sCreated --> sViewCreated: onCreateView()
    sViewCreated --> sStarted: onStart()
    sStarted --> sResumed: onResume()
    sResumed --> sStarted: onPause()
    sStarted --> sViewCreated: onStop()
    sViewCreated --> sCreated: onDestroyView()
    sCreated --> sAttached: onDestroy()
    sAttached --> sDetached: onDetach()
    sViewCreated --> sCreated: ConfigChange / BackStack
    sResumed --> sCreated: Fragment Replace
    sCreated --> sViewCreated: BackStack Pop
```

## 4.2 Fragment View vs Instance Lifecycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fragment Instance Created] --> B[onAttach]
    B --> C[onCreate]
    C --> D{View Needed?}
    D -->|Yes| E[onCreateView]
    D -->|No| F[onDestroy]
    E --> G[onViewStateRestored]
    G --> H[onStart]
    H --> I[onResume]
    I --> J[onPause]
    J --> K[onStop]
    K --> L[onDestroyView]
    L --> M{On Back Stack?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Instance Preserved]
    M -->|No| F
    F --> O[onDetach]
    N --> P[Awaiting Reuse]
    P -.Back Stack Pop.-> E
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — IoT Fragment State Manager

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph HostActivity[Host Activity Layer]
        A1[Activity Lifecycle]
        A2[FragmentManager]
        A3[BackStack Controller]
    end
    subgraph FragmentLayer[Fragment Layer]
        F1[Fragment Instance]
        F2[View Hierarchy]
        F3[State Bundle]
    end
    subgraph IoTDataLayer[IoT Data Layer]
        I1[MQTT Client]
        I2[Sensor Buffer]
        I3[Connection State]
    end
    A1 -->|lifecycle events| F1
    A2 -->|transactions| F1
    A3 -->|push/pop| F1
    F1 -->|inflate| F2
    F1 -->|save/restore| F3
    F2 -->|consume| I1
    F2 -->|display| I2
    F3 -->|persist| I3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide**: The **HostActivity** layer triggers lifecycle events. The **FragmentLayer** manages the instance, view, and state independently. The **IoTDataLayer** represents the application-specific data that must survive state transitions — this is the critical design point for IoT mobile apps where connection state and buffered sensor data must be preserved across configuration changes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the RESUMED state of an Android Fragment. List the callbacks that mark its entry and exit.**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

The **RESUMED state** is the most active state in the Fragment lifecycle. In this state, the Fragment is fully visible to the user, is in the foreground of the host Activity, and is actively receiving user input events such as touch and key events.

- **Entry callback**: `onResume()` — invoked when the Fragment becomes the foreground interactive element.
- **Exit callback**: `onPause()` — invoked when another component (Fragment, Activity, or dialog) takes focus.

**Significance (1 mark)**: The RESUMED state is the only state where the Fragment can interact with the user. Network calls, sensor subscriptions, and animations should ideally be started in `onResume()` and paused in `onPause()` to conserve resources.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between the Fragment lifecycle and the Fragment View lifecycle. Why are they decoupled?**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

| Aspect | Fragment Lifecycle | Fragment View Lifecycle |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Manages the Fragment instance | Manages the inflated view hierarchy |
| **Key callbacks** | `onCreate`, `onDestroy` | `onCreateView`, `onDestroyView` |
| **Triggered by** | Fragment transactions, Activity destruction | Configuration changes, back-stack operations |
| **Persistence** | Survives view destruction | Destroyed and recreated as needed |

**Why decoupled (1 mark)**: The decoupling allows Android to **destroy the view (freeing memory)** during configuration changes or back-stack operations while **preserving the Fragment instance and its state**. This is essential for IoT apps that maintain long-running sensor connections — the connection state survives even when the UI is destroyed and rebuilt.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the complete Fragment lifecycle with a state transition diagram. Describe each state and the corresponding callback methods. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable IoT application example (e.g., a smart home control app), explain how Fragment state management helps in handling configuration changes and back-stack navigation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**1. Introduction (1 mark)**:
A Fragment lifecycle consists of 11 callback methods and 6 distinct states. The Fragment goes through these states in a deterministic order, governed by the host Activity's lifecycle and FragmentManager transactions.

**2. State-by-State Explanation (5 marks)**:

**State 1: DETACHED**
- Fragment is instantiated but not yet attached to any Activity.
- No callbacks have been invoked.
- View does not exist.

**State 2: ATTACHED** — `onAttach(Context)`
- Fragment is associated with a host Activity.
- The `Context` reference becomes available.
- Use this callback to obtain the Activity reference if needed.

**State 3: CREATED** — `onCreate(Bundle)`
- Fragment instance is created.
- Non-UI initialization (e.g., initializing an MQTT client for IoT) should occur here.
- View is **not** yet inflated.

**State 4: VIEW_CREATED** — `onCreateView()`, `onViewStateRestored()`
- The layout is inflated via `onCreateView()`.
- `onViewStateRestored()` restores the saved view state from `onSaveInstanceState()`.
- UI-related initialization (finding views, setting adapters) occurs here.

**State 5: STARTED** — `onStart()`
- Fragment becomes visible to the user.
- Cannot receive input focus yet.

**State 6: RESUMED** — `onResume()`
- Fragment is in the foreground and receives all input events.
- This is the ideal place to start animations, sensor listeners, and network subscriptions.

**Backward Transitions (1 mark)**:
- `onPause()` → STARTED
- `onStop()` → VIEW_CREATED
- `onDestroyView()` → CREATED
- `onDestroy()` → ATTACHED
- `onDetach()` → DETACHED

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**IoT Example: Smart Home Control App** (7 marks):

**Scenario**: A smart home app has three Fragments:
1. **DeviceListFragment** — shows list of IoT devices.
2. **DeviceControlFragment** — shows controls (on/off, temperature).
3. **SensorDataFragment** — displays real-time sensor readings via MQTT.

**Configuration Change Handling (3.5 marks)**:

When the user rotates the device, the Activity is destroyed and recreated. Without proper Fragment state management:
- The MQTT connection would be lost.
- The sensor data buffer would be cleared.
- The user would have to re-authenticate.

**Solution with Fragment States**:
- In `onSaveInstanceState()`, the Fragment saves the MQTT client ID and last received sensor values into the bundle.
- The Fragment **instance is preserved** (Fragment lifecycle is decoupled from View lifecycle).
- After rotation, `onCreateView()` inflates a fresh view, and `onViewStateRestored()` restores the saved data.
- The Fragment re-establishes the MQTT connection in `onStart()` and resumes data streaming in `onResume()`.

**Back-Stack Navigation (3.5 marks)**:

When the user navigates from DeviceList → DeviceControl → SensorData, each Fragment is added to the back-stack.

**State transitions**:
- DeviceListFragment: RESUMED → STARTED → CREATED (view destroyed, instance preserved).
- DeviceControlFragment: DETACHED → ATTACHED → CREATED → VIEW_CREATED → STARTED → RESUMED.
- SensorDataFragment: same as above.

**Memory efficiency**: Only one Fragment has its view inflated at a time. The other two are in `CREATED` state with their views destroyed, saving up to **~70% memory**.

**Back-stack pop**: When user presses back, SensorDataFragment's view is recreated, and it transitions back through `CREATED → VIEW_CREATED → STARTED → RESUMED` — its `onResume()` callback resumes the MQTT subscription seamlessly.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of Fragment back-stack and how it interacts with Fragment states. What happens to a Fragment's state when it is replaced and added to the back-stack? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a detailed note on the importance of `onSaveInstanceState()` and `onViewStateRestored()` callbacks in Fragment state management, especially for IoT applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**1. Fragment Back-Stack Concept (3 marks)**:

The Fragment back-stack is a stack data structure managed by the `FragmentManager`. It records Fragment transactions so that the user can navigate backward (via the back button) through their navigation history, similar to how the Activity back-stack works.

**Key operations**:
- `addToBackStack(String name)` — adds the transaction to the back-stack.
- `popBackStack()` — pops the top transaction and reverses it.
- `popBackStack(String name, int flags)` — pops transactions up to a specific name.

**2. State Interaction (4 marks)**:

When a Fragment is replaced via `FragmentTransaction.replace()` and `addToBackStack()` is called, the following state transitions occur:

**Step 1**: Old Fragment receives `onPause()` → `onStop()` → `onDestroyView()`.
$$\delta(s_{\text{RESUMED}}, e_{\text{replace}}) = s_{\text{CREATED}}$$

**Step 2**: The old Fragment is now in the `CREATED` state — its **view is destroyed** but its **instance is preserved** on the back-stack.

**Step 3**: New Fragment is created and goes through `onAttach → onCreate → onCreateView → onStart → onResume`.

**Step 4**: When the user presses back, the transaction is popped:
- New Fragment is removed: `onPause → onStop → onDestroyView → onDestroy → onDetach`.
- Old Fragment's view is **re-inflated**: `onCreateView → onViewStateRestored → onStart → onResume`.

**Critical insight**: The old Fragment's `onCreate()` is **not** called again (instance preserved), but `onCreateView()` **is** called again (view recreated). This decoupling is what enables efficient memory management.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**1. `onSaveInstanceState(Bundle outState)` (3.5 marks)**:

This callback is invoked **before the Fragment's view is destroyed** (typically before `onDestroyView()`). It receives a `Bundle` parameter that the Fragment can populate with key-value pairs representing UI state.

**KTU Exam Tip**: This callback is called **only when the system needs to save state** (e.g., configuration change, process death). It is **not** called during normal back-stack operations.

**Example for IoT**:
```java
@Override
public void onSaveInstanceState(Bundle outState) {
    super.onSaveInstanceState(outState);
    outState.putString("MQTT_TOPIC", currentTopic);
    outState.putFloat("LAST_TEMP", lastTemperature);
    outState.putBoolean("CONN_STATUS", isConnected);
}
```

**2. `onViewStateRestored(Bundle savedInstanceState)` (3.5 marks)**:

This callback is invoked **after `onCreateView()` and before `onStart()`**. The Fragment should restore any saved state from the bundle.

**Example for IoT**:
```java
@Override
public void onViewStateRestored(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
    super.onViewStateRestored(savedInstanceState);
    if (savedInstanceState != null) {
        currentTopic = savedInstanceState.getString("MQTT_TOPIC");
        lastTemperature = savedInstanceState.getFloat("LAST_TEMP");
        isConnected = savedInstanceState.getBoolean("CONN_STATUS");
        updateUI();
    }
}
```

**Importance for IoT (KTU High-Yield)**:
- **MQTT Connection State**: Preserving the connection status avoids re-authentication on every rotation.
- **Buffered Sensor Data**: The last N sensor readings can be cached in the bundle (within the 500KB limit).
- **User Preferences**: Selected device, filter settings, and time range are preserved.
- **Process Death Recovery**: When the system kills the app to reclaim memory, the saved state allows the Fragment to restore its UI when the user returns.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Confusing `onSaveInstanceState` with `onPause`**: `onSaveInstanceState` is called **between** `onPause` and `onStop`, not during `onPause`. Many students lose **2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting `super.onSaveInstanceState(outState)`**: If the super call is omitted, the saved state of child views (e.g., EditText cursor position) is lost. Common **1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Assuming `onCreate()` is called on back-stack pop**: Wrong — only the view is recreated, not the Fragment instance. This is a **3-mark conceptual error** in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Storing large objects in the bundle**: The bundle has a **~500KB limit** (TransactionTooLargeException). Do not store Bitmaps or large lists directly — use `Parcelable` references or persistent storage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fragment States**

- [ ] **Six states**: DETACHED, ATTACHED, CREATED, VIEW_CREATED, STARTED, RESUMED.
- [ ] **Eleven callbacks**: `onAttach`, `onCreate`, `onCreateView`, `onViewStateRestored`, `onActivityCreated` (deprecated), `onStart`, `onResume`, `onPause`, `onStop`, `onDestroyView`, `onDestroy`, `onDetach`.
- [ ] **Decoupling principle**: Fragment instance lifecycle ≠ Fragment view lifecycle. Views can be destroyed and recreated without destroying the Fragment.
- [ ] **Forward transitions are strictly linear**; reverse transitions follow the inverse order.
- [ ] **Back-stack behavior**: Replaced Fragments go to `CREATED` state with views destroyed; their instance is preserved.
- [ ] **Configuration changes**: Trigger view destruction (`onDestroyView`) but NOT Fragment destruction. State is saved via `onSaveInstanceState` and restored via `onViewStateRestored`.
- [ ] **`onActivityCreated` is deprecated** (API 28+). Use `onViewStateRestored` instead.
- [ ] **Bundle size limit**: ~500KB. Exceeding it throws `TransactionTooLargeException`.
- [ ] **IoT-specific use case**: Preserve MQTT connection state, sensor buffer, and user-selected device in the bundle.
- [ ] **RESUMED is the only state** where the Fragment receives user input events.
- [ ] **`onResume()` and `onPause()`** are the bookend callbacks for the active foreground phase — start/stop sensors, animations, and network subscriptions here.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intent - Using Intents to Launch Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Intent - Using Intents to Launch Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Intent** in the Android operating system is a *messaging object* used to request an action from another app component. In the context of **Mobile App Development for IoT (PECGT415)**, an Intent serves as the fundamental inter-component communication mechanism that enables an Android application to launch Activities, start Services, deliver Broadcasts, and interact with IoT edge devices, sensors, and cloud endpoints.

According to the official Android documentation aligned with the KTU 2024 syllabus:

> An **Intent** is an abstract description of an operation to be performed. It can be used with `startActivity()` to launch an Activity, with `broadcastIntent()` to send it to any interested BroadcastReceiver components, or with `startService(Intent)` or `bindService(Intent, ...)` to communicate with a background Service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Intents are the *backbone* of Activity navigation in Android. For IoT applications, Intents are used to switch between device-control screens, launch camera Activities for QR-based device pairing, invoke Settings panels, and start background Services that maintain MQTT/HTTP connections to IoT brokers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an **Intent** as a *postal envelope* in the real world:

| Real-World Analogy | Android Intent Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A sealed envelope addressed to a specific person | **Explicit Intent** (targets a specific Component by class name) |
| A flyer saying "Anyone who can bake a cake, please contact me" | **Implicit Intent** (declares a general action; the system finds a matching app) |
| Contents inside the envelope (photos, letters) | **Intent Extras** (key-value bundles carrying data) |
| The Indian Postal Service that delivers the envelope | **Android Intent Resolution System / Activity Manager** |
| A return receipt | **Intent Result** (returned via `setResult()` and `onActivityResult()`) |

**Plain English Explanation for First-Time Learners:**

Imagine you are inside a smart-home Android app and you tap a button labeled *"Turn on Living Room Light"*. Behind the scenes, the app constructs an **Intent** (an envelope) saying *"I want to launch the LightControlActivity, and here is the device ID 'LR_01' as extra data"*. The Android system receives this envelope, looks at the address, and opens the right screen. This whole process — creating the message and dispatching it — is what we call **"Using Intents to Launch Activities."**

### 1.3 Three Core Use Cases of Intents (KTU Frequently Tested)

1. **Starting an Activity** → `startActivity(intent)` or `startActivityForResult(intent, requestCode)`
2. **Starting a Service** → `startService(intent)` (used for long-running IoT data polling)
3. **Delivering a Broadcast** → `sendBroadcast(intent)` (used for system-wide IoT alerts)

> [!NOTE]
> For PECGT415 Module 3, the focus is strictly on **Using Intents to Launch Activities**, which corresponds to the first use case above.

### 1.4 Standard Terminology Mandated by KTU 2024

| Term | Symbol / Keyword | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Intent Action | `ACTION_VIEW`, `ACTION_SEND` | The operation to be performed |
| Component Name | `ComponentName(package, class)` | Explicit target address |
| Data URI | `Uri.parse("https://...")` | The data the intent operates on |
| MIME Type | `"text/plain"`, `"image/*"` | Type of data being carried |
| Category | `CATEGORY_LAUNCHER` | Additional information about the action |
| Extras | `intent.putExtra(key, value)` | Key-value data bundle |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an Intent Object

An Intent is built from **six** constituent parts. For exam purposes, you must remember all six:

1. **Component Name** (optional) — The fully qualified class name of the target. Setting this makes the Intent *explicit*.
2. **Action** — A string describing *what* should be done (e.g., `Intent.ACTION_VIEW`).
3. **Data** — A URI and MIME type referring to the data on which the action should be performed.
4. **Category** — Additional descriptive information about the action (e.g., `CATEGORY_BROWSABLE`).
5. **Extras** — A `Bundle` of key-value pairs for additional primitive data.
6. **Flags** — Instructions on how to launch the activity (e.g., `FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK`).

### 2.2 Two Fundamental Types of Intents

#### A. Explicit Intent (Most Common in IoT Apps)

An **Explicit Intent** specifies the *exact* component to start by its fully qualified class name. The Android system delivers the intent directly without consulting the Intent Resolution System.

**Why use it?** In a single IoT app with multiple screens (LoginActivity, DeviceListActivity, ControlActivity), you use explicit intents to navigate *within* your own app.

**Typical Code Pattern (Java):**
```java
Intent i = new Intent(getApplicationContext(), ControlActivity.class);
i.putExtra("DEVICE_ID", "LR_01");
startActivity(i);
```

#### B. Implicit Intent (Cross-App Communication)

An **Implicit Intent** declares a *general action* (e.g., "open a web page") and lets the Android system find any app on the device that can handle it.

**Why use it?** Useful when invoking *system* or *third-party* apps — e.g., opening a URL in Chrome, sharing sensor data via WhatsApp, dialing a phone number on a paired IoT gateway.

**Typical Code Pattern (Java):**
```java
Intent i = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, Uri.parse("https://iot.ktu.edu"));
startActivity(i);
```

### 2.3 Intent Resolution: The "How"

When the system receives an **implicit** intent, it consults the **PackageManager** to query all installed apps' `AndroidManifest.xml` `<intent-filter>` declarations. It builds a list of candidate activities and, if multiple exist, shows a chooser dialog.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rule for KTU Exams:** If no activity matches, calling `startActivity()` throws an `ActivityNotFoundException`. Always wrap implicit intents in a try-catch block.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the core Intent-related constructors, methods, and constants. For the KTU exam, you should be able to write any of these from memory.

| Constructor / Method | Signature / Constant | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Explicit Constructor | `new Intent(Context, Class)` | Create an explicit Intent |
| Implicit Constructor | `new Intent(String action)` | Create an implicit Intent with action |
| Action-Data Constructor | `new Intent(String action, Uri uri)` | Create an implicit Intent with action + data |
| Set Component | `setComponent(ComponentName)` | Convert implicit → explicit |
| Put Extra | `putExtra(String name, X value)` | Attach key-value data |
| Get Extra | `getStringExtra(String name)` | Retrieve String data |
| Start Activity | `startActivity(Intent intent)` | Launch the target activity |
| Start with Result | `startActivityForResult(Intent, int)` | Launch and expect a return value |
| Set Result | `setResult(int, Intent)` | Return data from child to parent |
| Add Flags | `addFlags(int flags)` | Modify launch behavior |
| Standard Actions | `ACTION_VIEW`, `ACTION_SEND`, `ACTION_DIAL` | Common system-defined actions |
| Standard Categories | `CATEGORY_DEFAULT`, `CATEGORY_LAUNCHER`, `CATEGORY_BROWSABLE` | Common categories |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the pipe character `$\vert$` denotes "such that" or alternatives. Notice the use of `\vert` to safely render mathematical/logical separators without breaking Markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in IoT Engineering

In a production-grade **IoT Mobile Application**, intents are used in the following scenarios:

- **Device Pairing:** Launch a `QRScannerActivity` via an explicit Intent, then return the scanned device ID as a result Intent.
- **Cloud Communication:** Open a browser to the device's web configuration page using `ACTION_VIEW` with a URL URI.
- **Sensor Reading Display:** Launch a `GraphActivity` to plot real-time temperature data received via MQTT.
- **System Settings:** Direct the user to enable Bluetooth using `Settings.ACTION_BLUETOOTH_SETTINGS`.
- **Notifications:** Start a `MainActivity` when the user taps a notification about a sensor alert.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through the **complete code construction pipeline** for using Intents to launch activities. Because this is a programming topic, the "derivations" are logical step-by-step code constructions rather than mathematical proofs.

### 3.1 Step-by-Step: Launching an Activity with an Explicit Intent (Java)

**Scenario:** A `LoginActivity` in an IoT app wants to navigate to a `DeviceListActivity` after successful authentication, passing the username.

**Step 1 — Import the required class.**

```java
import android.content.Intent;
```

**Step 2 — Construct the Intent object using the explicit constructor.**

```java
Intent intent = new Intent(LoginActivity.this, DeviceListActivity.class);
```

*Logic:* The first parameter `LoginActivity.this` is the **Context** (required because Intent construction needs access to package info). The second parameter `DeviceListActivity.class` is the **target component** — this makes the Intent *explicit*.

**Step 3 — Attach extra data using putExtra().**

```java
intent.putExtra("USERNAME", "admin");
intent.putExtra("ROLE", "OPERATOR");
```

*Logic:* `putExtra()` stores key-value pairs in the Intent's internal Bundle. Supported primitive types include `String`, `int`, `boolean`, `double`, `long`, `float`, `char`, and their array variants.

**Step 4 — Optionally add launch flags.**

```java
intent.addFlags(Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TOP);
```

*Logic:* `FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TOP` tells the system that if `DeviceListActivity` is already in the back stack, all activities above it should be cleared before delivering the new intent. This prevents stack duplication.

**Step 5 — Launch the activity.**

```java
startActivity(intent);
```

*Logic:* `startActivity()` is the terminal method.
