It hands the Intent to the Android **Activity Manager Service (AMS)**, which then creates a new instance of `DeviceListActivity` (or resumes an existing one depending on launch mode), executes its lifecycle (`onCreate()`, `onStart()`, `onResume()`), and brings it to the foreground.

**Step 6 — Retrieve extras in the receiving activity.**

Inside `DeviceListActivity.java` → `onCreate()`:

```java
String user = getIntent().getStringExtra("USERNAME");
String role = getIntent().getStringExtra("ROLE");
```

*Logic:* `getIntent()` returns the very Intent object that launched this activity. `getStringExtra("USERNAME")` extracts the String value associated with that key. If the key does not exist, it returns `null`.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: Launching an Activity with an Implicit Intent (Java)

**Scenario:** The IoT app wants to open a device's online manual in any installed web browser.

**Step 1 — Build the implicit Intent with action + data.**

```java
Intent intent = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, Uri.parse("https://docs.iotdevice.com/manual"));
```

*Logic:* We provide the **action** `ACTION_VIEW` (meaning "display this data to the user") and the **data URI** pointing to the manual. We do **not** specify a target component — the Android system will query all installed apps.

**Step 2 — Add category information (optional but recommended).**

```java
intent.addCategory(Intent.CATEGORY_BROWSABLE);
```

*Logic:* `CATEGORY_BROWSABLE` indicates that the target activity must be able to be invoked from a browser. Activities that don't declare this category will be excluded from the candidate list.

**Step 3 — Verify and launch with exception handling.**

```java
if (intent.resolveActivity(getPackageManager()) != null) {
    startActivity(intent);
} else {
    Toast.makeText(this, "No browser app installed", Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show();
}
```

*Logic:* `resolveActivity()` queries the PackageManager for the best-matching component. If it returns `null`, no app on the device can handle the intent. KTU exams often test whether students remember this null-check guard.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Returning Data with `startActivityForResult()`

**Scenario:** A `DeviceListActivity` lets the user select a device. It opens a `DevicePickerActivity` and expects the chosen device's ID back.

**Step 1 — Launch from the parent activity with a request code.**

```java
Intent pickerIntent = new Intent(DeviceListActivity.this, DevicePickerActivity.class);
startActivityForResult(pickerIntent, 1001);
```

*Logic:* The integer `1001` is a **request code**. It is used later to identify which child activity returned the result when multiple `startActivityForResult()` calls exist in the same parent.

**Step 2 — In the child activity, set the result before finishing.**

```java
Intent resultIntent = new Intent();
resultIntent.putExtra("SELECTED_DEVICE_ID", "TEMP_SENSOR_07");
setResult(RESULT_OK, resultIntent);
finish();
```

*Logic:* `RESULT_OK` is a predefined constant (`-1`). Alternative results include `RESULT_CANCELED` (`0`) and custom result codes. `finish()` closes the child activity and triggers the parent's `onActivityResult()`.

**Step 3 — Override `onActivityResult()` in the parent to receive the data.**

```java
@Override
protected void onActivityResult(int requestCode, int resultCode, Intent data) {
    super.onActivityResult(requestCode, resultCode, data);
    if (requestCode == 1001 && resultCode == RESULT_OK) {
        String deviceId = data.getStringExtra("SELECTED_DEVICE_ID");
        Log.d("IoTApp", "User selected device: " + deviceId);
    }
}
```

*Logic:* The `requestCode` parameter matches `1001` from Step 1, allowing us to handle multiple child activities independently. `resultCode` confirms whether the user actually made a selection (`RESULT_OK`) or cancelled (`RESULT_CANCELED`).

### 3.4 The Complete Lifecycle of an Intent-Driven Activity Launch

The following mathematical-style flow notation summarizes the entire process:

$$
\text{Source Activity} \xrightarrow{\text{Intent construction}} \text{Intent Object} \xrightarrow{\text{startActivity()}} \text{Activity Manager Service}
$$

$$
\text{Activity Manager Service} \xrightarrow{\text{resolve (if implicit)}} \text{PackageManager Query} \xrightarrow{\text{candidate found}} \text{Target Activity}
$$

$$
\text{Target Activity} \xrightarrow{\text{lifecycle: onCreate() \to onStart() \to onResume()}} \text{Foreground Display}
$$

For result-returning flows:

$$
\text{Source} \xrightarrow{\text{startActivityForResult()}} \text{Target} \xrightarrow{\text{setResult() + finish()}} \text{Source onActivityResult()}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Intent-Driven Activity Launch Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete process of how an Intent travels from the calling Activity to the target Activity in both Explicit and Implicit scenarios.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Source Activity (e.g. LoginActivity)"] --> B["Developer constructs Intent object"]
    B --> C{"Is target component specified?"}

    C -- "Yes, Class name provided" --> D["Build EXPLICIT Intent"]
    C -- "No, only Action + Data provided" --> E["Build IMPLICIT Intent"]

    D --> F["Add Extras (key-value pairs)"]
    E --> F

    F --> G["Add Flags and Categories if needed"]
    G --> H["Invoke startActivity or startActivityForResult"]

    H --> I["Activity Manager Service (AMS)"]
    I --> J{"Intent type?"}

    J -- "Explicit" --> K["Skip resolution, deliver directly"]
    J -- "Implicit" --> L["PackageManager Query against all installed apps"]

    L --> M{"Matching activity found?"}
    M -- "Yes, one match" --> N["Launch Target Activity"]
    M -- "Yes, multiple matches" --> O["Show Chooser Dialog to user"]
    M -- "No match" --> P["Throw ActivityNotFoundException"]

    K --> N
    O --> N

    N --> Q["Target Activity Lifecycle: onCreate to onStart to onResume"]
    Q --> R["Activity displayed in foreground"]
    R --> S{"Is result expected?"}

    S -- "Yes" --> T["User interacts and calls setResult then finish"]
    S -- "No" --> U["Activity remains until user backs out"]

    T --> V["Source onActivityResult callback fired"]
    V --> W["Extract extras from returned Intent"]
```

### 4.2 Intent Component Decomposition Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IntentObj["Intent Object"]

    IntentObj --> Comp["Component Name (optional)"]
    IntentObj --> Action["Action (string)"]
    IntentObj --> Data["Data (Uri + MIME type)"]
    IntentObj --> Cat["Category (list of strings)"]
    IntentObj --> Extras["Extras (Bundle)"]
    IntentObj --> Flags["Flags (int bitmask)"]

    Comp --> CompNote["Makes intent EXPLICIT"]
    Action --> ActNote["E.g. ACTION_VIEW, ACTION_SEND"]
    Data --> DataNote["E.g. https://... or content://..."]
    Cat --> CatNote["E.g. CATEGORY_BROWSABLE"]
    Extras --> ExtrasNote["Key-value payload data"]
    Flags --> FlagNote["E.g. FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK"]
```

### 4.3 Explicit vs Implicit Intent Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Developer needs to launch an Activity"] --> Q1{"Do you know the EXACT target class?"}

    Q1 -- "Yes, target is in MY app" --> EX["Use EXPLICIT Intent"]
    Q1 -- "No, want any app that can handle this" --> IM["Use IMPLICIT Intent"]

    EX --> EXC["new Intent Context TargetClass"]
    EXC --> EXR["startActivity called directly"]
    EXR --> EXDone["Target launched - no chooser dialog"]

    IM --> IMC["new Intent ACTION_URI"]
    IMC --> IMR["System resolves via PackageManager"]
    IMR --> IMD{"Multiple candidates?"}
    IMD -- "One" --> IMRun["Launch that one"]
    IMD -- "Many" --> IMChoose["Show chooser dialog to user"]
    IMD -- "None" --> IMError["ActivityNotFoundException"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Context parameter** in `new Intent(Context, Class)` — results in compilation error. Always use `ActivityName.this` or `getApplicationContext()`.
> 2. **Confusing `startActivity()` with `startActivityForResult()`** — students often forget the request code, making it impossible to distinguish which child activity returned data in `onActivityResult()`.
> 3. **Not overriding `onActivityResult()` correctly** — must call `super.onActivityResult(...)` first, otherwise Android Studio may not even invoke the callback.
> 4. **Mixing up Implicit Intent resolution** — students forget to wrap implicit `startActivity()` calls in a `try-catch` or a `resolveActivity()` null-check, leading to runtime crashes.
> 5. **Wrong terminology** — calling `putExtra()` "getExtra" or confusing `Intent` with `PendingIntent` (a separate class) costs easy marks.

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define an Intent in Android. List any FOUR main components of an Intent object.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **Intent** in Android is a messaging object used to request an action from another app component. It is mainly used to launch activities, start services, or deliver broadcasts.

The four main components of an Intent are:

1. **Component Name** — The fully qualified class name of the target component (e.g., `ComponentName("com.example.iot", "com.example.iot.ControlActivity")`).
2. **Action** — A string constant that describes the operation to be performed (e.g., `Intent.ACTION_VIEW`).
3. **Data** — A URI and MIME type referring to the data on which the action operates (e.g., `Uri.parse("tel:9876543210")`).
4. **Extras** — A `Bundle` of key-value pairs used to pass additional data between components.

*(Alternative valid components: Category, Flags — examiner's discretion.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Differentiate between Explicit Intent and Implicit Intent with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Explicit Intent | Implicit Intent |
|---|---|---|
| Target | Specifies exact component class name | Declares a general action; system decides target |
| Resolution | Direct delivery by AMS | PackageManager-based resolution |
| Use Case | In-app navigation | Cross-app communication |
| Example | `new Intent(this, HomeActivity.class)` | `new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, Uri.parse("https://ktu.edu"))` |

Explicit intents are used when the developer wants to launch a specific known activity (typically within the same app). Implicit intents are used when the developer wants any installed app capable of performing the action to handle the request.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Note:** KTU End Semester Examinations feature a Module-Internal Choice pattern. Two alternative questions are provided; the student answers ONE. Both are given below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the anatomy of an Intent object with a neat diagram. List and briefly describe the role of each of its six constituent parts. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Intent object in Android is composed of six parts. The diagram below (already shown in Section 4.2) illustrates these parts.

The six parts are:

1. **Component Name** — The fully qualified class name of the target. Setting this makes the intent *explicit* and bypasses the resolution process. Example: `intent.setComponent(new ComponentName("com.iot.app", "com.iot.app.ControlActivity"))`.

2. **Action** — A string describing the generic action to be performed. Examples include `Intent.ACTION_VIEW` (display data), `Intent.ACTION_SEND` (share data), `Intent.ACTION_DIAL` (open dialer). Custom actions can also be defined.

3. **Data** — A URI and an associated MIME type that specifies the data on which the action will operate. For example, `Uri.parse("content://contacts/people/1")` for viewing a specific contact.

4. **Category** — A string providing additional categorical information about the action. Common categories include `CATEGORY_DEFAULT`, `CATEGORY_BROWSABLE`, and `CATEGORY_LAUNCHER`. Multiple categories can be added.

5. **Extras** — A `Bundle` of key-value pairs that carry additional primitive or `Parcelable` data between components. Methods: `putExtra(String, X)`, `getStringExtra(String)`, etc.

6. **Flags** — A bitmask of integer flags that instruct the system on how to launch the activity (e.g., whether to create a new task, clear the back stack, etc.). Example: `Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TOP`.

**(b)** Write a Java program snippet for an Android IoT application that uses an **explicit Intent** to navigate from `MainActivity` to `DeviceControlActivity`, passing the device ID `"TEMP_03"` and the operation mode `"READ_ONLY"` as extras. Also show how the receiving activity retrieves these extras. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Sender Activity — `MainActivity.java`:**

```java
package com.example.iot;

import android.content.Intent;
import android.os.Bundle;
import android.view.View;
import android.widget.Button;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);

        Button launchBtn = findViewById(R.id.btnLaunch);
        launchBtn.setOnClickListener(new View.OnClickListener() {
            @Override
            public void onClick(View v) {
                Intent intent = new Intent(MainActivity.this, DeviceControlActivity.class);
                intent.putExtra("DEVICE_ID", "TEMP_03");
                intent.putExtra("OP_MODE", "READ_ONLY");
                startActivity(intent);
            }
        });
    }
}
```

**Explanation (3 marks):**
- `MainActivity.this` provides the `Context` needed for Intent construction.
- `DeviceControlActivity.class` makes the Intent explicit by naming the target.
- Two `putExtra()` calls attach String data.
- `startActivity()` triggers the launch.

**Receiver Activity — `DeviceControlActivity.java`:**

```java
package com.example.iot;

import android.os.Bundle;
import android.util.Log;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class DeviceControlActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_device_control);

        String deviceId = getIntent().getStringExtra("DEVICE_ID");
        String opMode = getIntent().getStringExtra("OP_MODE");

        Log.d("IoTApp", "Controlling device: " + deviceId);
        Log.d("IoTApp", "Operation mode: " + opMode);
    }
}
```

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- `getIntent()` returns the launching Intent.
- `getStringExtra("DEVICE_ID")` retrieves the value attached by the sender.
- If a key does not exist, the method returns `null` (defensive coding recommended).
- The retrieved values are logged for demonstration; in a real app they would be displayed in `TextView` widgets or used to fetch device telemetry.

**Manifest entry** (2 marks worth, often required):
```xml
<activity android:name=".DeviceControlActivity"></activity>
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Alternative]**

**(a)** What is an Implicit Intent? Explain the **Intent Resolution** mechanism with a suitable diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An **Implicit Intent** is a type of Android Intent that does not specify a target component by class name. Instead, it declares an action (and optionally data, category, MIME type) that describes the operation to be performed. The Android system then queries all installed apps to find one or more that can handle the intent.

**Intent Resolution Mechanism:**

1. The developer constructs an implicit intent with an action (e.g., `ACTION_VIEW`) and a data URI.
2. `startActivity(intent)` is called.
3. The Android **Activity Manager Service (AMS)** intercepts the call and consults the **PackageManager**.
4. PackageManager scans the `<intent-filter>` declarations in every installed app's `AndroidManifest.xml`.
5. Each `<intent-filter>` is checked for compatibility against the intent's action, data URI, MIME type, and category.
6. AMS compiles a list of candidate activities:
   - If **exactly one** match → launched directly.
   - If **multiple matches** → a chooser dialog is shown to the user.
   - If **no match** → an `ActivityNotFoundException` is thrown.

**Example Implicit Intent:**
```java
Intent i = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, Uri.parse("https://iot.ktu.edu"));
startActivity(i);
```

This intent will match any installed browser (Chrome, Firefox, etc.) that declares an intent filter for `ACTION_VIEW` with the `https` scheme.

**Sample intent-filter declaration in a target app's manifest:**
```xml
<activity android:name=".WebViewActivity">
    <intent-filter>
        <action android:name="android.intent.action.VIEW" />
        <category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT" />
        <category android:name="android.intent.category.BROWSABLE" />
        <data android:scheme="https" />
    </intent-filter>
</activity>
```

**(b)** Write a complete Java code segment demonstrating how to **send data back** from a child Activity (`DevicePickerActivity`) to its parent (`DeviceListActivity`) using `startActivityForResult()` and `onActivityResult()`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Parent Activity — `DeviceListActivity.java`:**

```java
package com.example.iot;

import android.content.Intent;
import android.os.Bundle;
import android.util.Log;
import android.widget.Toast;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class DeviceListActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    private static final int PICKER_REQUEST = 1001;

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_device_list);

        Intent pickerIntent = new Intent(DeviceListActivity.this, DevicePickerActivity.class);
        startActivityForResult(pickerIntent, PICKER_REQUEST);
    }

    @Override
    protected void onActivityResult(int requestCode, int resultCode, Intent data) {
        super.onActivityResult(requestCode, resultCode, data);

        if (requestCode == PICKER_REQUEST) {
            if (resultCode == RESULT_OK) {
                String selectedDevice = data.getStringExtra("SELECTED_DEVICE_ID");
                Log.d("IoTApp", "Selected: " + selectedDevice);
                Toast.makeText(this, "Selected: " + selectedDevice, Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show();
            } else if (resultCode == RESULT_CANCELED) {
                Toast.makeText(this, "Selection cancelled", Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show();
            }
        }
    }
}
```

**Child Activity — `DevicePickerActivity.java`:**

```java
package com.example.iot;

import android.content.Intent;
import android.os.Bundle;
import android.view.View;
import android.widget.Button;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class DevicePickerActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_device_picker);

        Button confirmBtn = findViewById(R.id.btnConfirm);
        confirmBtn.setOnClickListener(new View.OnClickListener() {
            @Override
            public void onClick(View v) {
                Intent resultIntent = new Intent();
                resultIntent.putExtra("SELECTED_DEVICE_ID", "TEMP_SENSOR_07");
                setResult(RESULT_OK, resultIntent);
                finish();
            }
        });
    }
}
```

**Explanation (Distributed across 7 marks):**
- `startActivityForResult(intent, 1001)` launches the child with a unique request code (2 marks).
- The child builds a result Intent, attaches the selection as an extra, calls `setResult(RESULT_OK, intent)`, and `finish()` (2 marks).
- The parent's `onActivityResult()` is overridden and uses the request code to identify the source (1 mark).
- Defensive checks for both `RESULT_OK` and `RESULT_CANCELED` are shown (1 mark).
- Extras are retrieved via `data.getStringExtra("SELECTED_DEVICE_ID")` (1 mark).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist on the night before your KTU Exam.**

- ✅ An **Intent** is a *messaging object*, not a class instance of the target Activity itself.
- ✅ **Two types**: Explicit (names the target) and Implicit (names only an action).
- ✅ **Core Intent methods**: `putExtra()`, `getStringExtra()`, `setData()`, `setAction()`, `addCategory()`, `setFlags()`, `setComponent()`.
- ✅ **Core launch methods**: `startActivity(Intent)`, `startActivityForResult(Intent, requestCode)`.
- ✅ **Core return methods**: `setResult(resultCode, Intent)`, `finish()`.
- ✅ **Core callback**: `onActivityResult(requestCode, resultCode, Intent data)`.
- ✅ **Always** call `super.onActivityResult(...)` first inside your override.
- ✅ **Always** guard implicit intents with `resolveActivity() != null` or a try-catch.
- ✅ **Six Intent components**: Component Name, Action, Data, Category, Extras, Flags.
- ✅ **Common Actions**: `ACTION_VIEW`, `ACTION_SEND`, `ACTION_DIAL`, `ACTION_GET_CONTENT`.
- ✅ **Common Categories**: `CATEGORY_DEFAULT` (required for explicit startActivityForResult targets), `CATEGORY_LAUNCHER` (entry-point activity), `CATEGORY_BROWSABLE` (web-launchable).
- ✅ **Common Flags**: `FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK`, `FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TOP`, `FLAG_ACTIVITY_SINGLE_TOP`.
- ✅ **Extras must be primitives or Parcelable** — custom objects must implement `Parcelable` or `Serializable`.
- ✅ **`getIntent()` returns the launching Intent** of the current Activity; pair it with `getStringExtra()` etc. to retrieve passed data.
- ✅ For **IoT apps**, intents are the primary mechanism for launching device-control screens, system Bluetooth settings, web configuration URLs, and notification-driven deep links.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Explicitly Starting New Activity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Explicitly Starting New Activity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Android Application Development Framework (a core component of the **Mobile App Development for IoT** curriculum under KTU 2024 Scheme), an **Activity** represents a single, focused screen with which a user can interact. **Explicitly Starting a New Activity** is the process of programmatically launching a *specific*, named Activity using an **Explicit Intent**. An Explicit Intent is an `android.content.Intent` object that explicitly names the target component (typically an Activity class) to be invoked, providing absolute addressability within or across application boundaries.

The fundamental Android API contract for this operation is governed by the `Context.startActivity(Intent)` and `Activity.startActivity(Intent)` methods, which route the request through the **Activity Manager Service (AMS)**, a core system-level process in the Android OS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Explicit Intents are the **only** type of Intent that guarantees a specific component will be invoked, because the target is fully qualified by its `ComponentName` (package + class name). This contrasts with *Implicit Intents*, which rely on action-string resolution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Memo Routing" Model

Imagine you work in a large corporate office (the **Android OS**). You have a document (the **Intent**) that needs to go to a specific colleague (the **Target Activity**). 

- An **Explicit Intent** is like a *sealed internal memo* that has the recipient's full name, employee ID, and desk number written on the envelope. The mailroom (the **Activity Manager Service**) does not need to guess — it walks directly to that desk and hands over the memo.
- A "start" action is the act of dispatching that memo.

In the IoT context (which is this course's focus), starting a new activity might mean: the user taps a "Connect to Sensor" button on the home screen, and your app *explicitly* launches the `SensorDashboardActivity` to display real-time telemetry from a BLE-connected device.

### 1.3 Core Android Components Involved

| Component | Role in Explicit Activity Start |
|---|---|
| **Source Activity** | The currently visible Activity that constructs and dispatches the Intent. |
| **Intent Object** | The message carrier that encapsulates the target ComponentName and optional data Bundle (Extras). |
| **Activity Manager Service (AMS)** | The system binder service that resolves the Intent and triggers Activity lifecycle transitions. |
| **Target Activity** | The destination Activity declared in `AndroidManifest.xml` with a corresponding `<intent-filter>`. |
| **Task & Back Stack** | The LIFO stack managed by AMS that orders Activities for navigation history. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — `ComponentName`:** A unique identifier formed by the concatenation of the **package name** (e.g., `com.example.iotapp`) and the **fully qualified class name** of the target Activity (e.g., `com.example.iotapp.SensorDashboardActivity`). This is what makes an Intent "Explicit."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Four Logical Phases

The process of explicitly starting a new Activity can be decomposed into four sequential, examiner-friendly phases:

1. **Intent Construction Phase** — A Java/Kotlin `Intent` object is instantiated. The target ComponentName is attached, either via the constructor `Intent(Context, Class)` or via `setComponent()` / `setClass()` / `setClassName()` methods.
2. **Data Attachment Phase (Optional)** — Key-value pairs (referred to as **Extras**) are added to the Intent's internal Bundle using `putExtra(key, value)`. This allows passing primitive types, Strings, `Parcelable` objects, `Serializable` objects, etc.
3. **Dispatch Phase** — The Intent is passed to `startActivity(intent)`. This call is non-blocking from the caller's perspective; it enqueues a request with AMS.
4. **Lifecycle Instantiation Phase** — AMS resolves the target, creates a new instance (if not already in the stack), and invokes the target Activity's lifecycle callbacks in order: `onCreate()` → `onStart()` → `onResume()`.

### 2.2 Why Explicit Intents? — The Engineering Rationale

- **Determinism:** Guarantees that *your* code launches *your* activity, eliminating ambiguity in component resolution.
- **Security Boundary Control:** Sensitive internal screens (e.g., an IoT device's "Factory Reset" screen) should never be reachable via Implicit Intents, as that would expose them to any third-party app.
- **Performance:** AMS skips the costly PackageManager query for intent-filter matching, leading to slightly faster dispatch.
- **Compile-Time Safety:** When using `Intent(context, TargetActivity::class.java)`, the class reference is verified at compile time.

### 2.3 The "How" — Anatomy of the Explicit Intent API

The Intent API for explicit starts offers **three canonical construction patterns**, all of which are KTU-favorite exam topics:

**Pattern A — Direct Constructor (Most Common):**
```java
Intent intent = new Intent(CurrentActivity.this, TargetActivity.class);
startActivity(intent);
```

**Pattern B — ComponentName Attachment:**
```java
Intent intent = new Intent();
intent.setComponent(new ComponentName("com.example.iotapp", 
                                      "com.example.iotapp.SensorDashboardActivity"));
startActivity(intent);
```

**Pattern C — setClass() Method:**
```java
Intent intent = new Intent();
intent.setClass(CurrentActivity.this, TargetActivity.class);
startActivity(intent);
```

### 2.4 Passing Data Between Activities — The Extras Contract

Data is passed via the Intent's internal `Bundle`. The receiving Activity retrieves it via `getIntent().getExtras()` or the typed convenience getters like `getStringExtra()`, `getIntExtra()`, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Key Naming Convention:** Always use **package-prefixed** keys for Extras (e.g., `"com.example.iotapp.SENSOR_ID"`) to avoid collisions with system or third-party keys.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Reference Table

| Element | Syntax / Constant | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Intent Constructor (Explicit) | `new Intent(Context, Class<?>)` | Direct component-bound Intent |
| `Intent.setClass()` | `intent.setClass(ctx, cls)` | Sets target class dynamically |
| `Intent.setComponent()` | `intent.setComponent(ComponentName)` | Sets target by package + class strings |
| `Intent.putExtra()` | `intent.putExtra("KEY", value)` | Attaches a key-value pair to the Bundle |
| `startActivity()` | `void startActivity(Intent intent)` | Dispatches the Intent to AMS |
| `startActivityForResult()` *(Deprecated in API 30+)* | Replaced by **Activity Result API** | Starts an Activity expecting a result |
| `getIntent()` | `Intent getIntent()` | Retrieves the launching Intent inside the target |
| `getStringExtra()` | `String getStringExtra(String name)` | Typed getter for String Extras |
| `FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK` | `0x10000000` | Required when starting from a non-Activity Context |
| `FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TOP` | `0x04000000` | Clears all Activities above the target in the stack |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

Since this is an Android development topic (CS / Software Engineering domain), the "derivations" below are exhaustive **code walkthroughs**, **lifecycle transition analyses**, and **intent-state transformation derivations**. Every step is explicitly written out per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE mandate.

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — A Two-Activity IoT App (Home → Sensor Dashboard)

**Step 1: Project Structure Setup**

Create two Activity classes. In a typical Android Studio project under KTU Module 3:

```
app/
├── java/com/example/iotapp/
│   ├── MainActivity.java
│   └── SensorDashboardActivity.java
└── res/
    └── layout/
        ├── activity_main.xml
        └── activity_sensor_dashboard.xml
```

**Step 2: Declare the Target Activity in `AndroidManifest.xml`**

Every Activity in an Android app *must* be declared in the manifest file. The system uses this declaration to register the component with the PackageManager.

```xml
<application
    android:label="IoT Controller"
    ...>
    
    <activity android:name=".MainActivity">
        <intent-filter>
            <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
            <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
        </intent-filter>
    </activity>
    
    <activity android:name=".SensorDashboardActivity"
              android:exported="false" />
              
</application>
```

**Note:** `android:exported="false"` is a critical security best-practice since Android 12 (API 31). It prevents external apps from launching this Activity.

**Step 3: Source Activity — Construct the Explicit Intent**

The `MainActivity` contains a button. Its click handler is the entry point of the explicit start.

```java
Button btnOpenDashboard = findViewById(R.id.btnOpenDashboard);
btnOpenDashboard.setOnClickListener(new View.OnClickListener() {
    @Override
    public void onClick(View v) {
        
        // PHASE 1: Intent Construction
        Intent intent = new Intent(MainActivity.this, 
                                   SensorDashboardActivity.class);
        
        // PHASE 2: Data Attachment (Extras)
        intent.putExtra("SENSOR_ID", "TEMP_SENSOR_01");
        intent.putExtra("REFRESH_INTERVAL_MS", 5000);
        
        // PHASE 3: Dispatch
        startActivity(intent);
    }
});
```

**Step 4: Target Activity — Receive the Intent and Extras**

Inside `SensorDashboardActivity.java`, the launched Intent is retrieved inside `onCreate()`.

```java
@Override
protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
    super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
    setContentView(R.layout.activity_sensor_dashboard);
    
    // Retrieve the launching Intent
    Intent launchingIntent = getIntent();
    
    // Extract Extras with default fallback values
    String sensorId = launchingIntent.getStringExtra("SENSOR_ID");
    int refreshInterval = launchingIntent.getIntExtra("REFRESH_INTERVAL_MS", 1000);
    
    // Update the UI
    TextView txtSensorId = findViewById(R.id.txtSensorId);
    txtSensorId.setText("Monitoring: " + sensorId);
    
    // Start IoT polling logic (out of scope for this derivation)
    startTelemetryPolling(refreshInterval);
}
```

**Step 5: Lifecycle Transition — What AMS Actually Does**

The `startActivity(intent)` call is non-blocking. Behind the scenes, the following sequence occurs in **strict order**:

1. The source Activity's process invokes a **Binder IPC** call to the system_server process hosting the AMS.
2. AMS resolves the `ComponentName` in the Intent against the PackageManager's registered components.
3. AMS checks the current Task stack. Since `SensorDashboardActivity` is not on the stack, a new instance is created.
4. AMS calls `SensorDashboardActivity.onCreate()` → `onStart()` → `onResume()` in the target's process.
5. The source Activity's `onPause()` is invoked (it loses foreground state).
6. The new Activity is rendered; the source becomes paused and may eventually be stopped.

The full lifecycle method order is therefore:

$$
\text{Source.onPause()} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Target.onCreate()} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Target.onStart()} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Target.onResume()} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Source.onStop()}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Explicit Start Scenarios

| Scenario | Source Context | Code Snippet | Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| Start from Activity | Activity | `new Intent(this, Target.class)` | None |
| Start from Fragment | Fragment | `new Intent(getActivity(), Target.class)` | Using `getContext()` may require FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK |
| Start from Service / Application | Non-Activity Context | `new Intent(context, Target.class)` + `FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK` | Forgetting the flag → `AndroidRuntimeException` |
| Start expecting result *(Legacy)* | Activity | `startActivityForResult(intent, REQ_CODE)` | Deprecated; use `registerForActivityResult` |
| Start expecting result *(Modern)* | Activity | `activityResultLauncher.launch(intent)` | Required API for KTU 2024 answers |

### 3.3 The Modern Activity Result API — Step-by-Step

Since `startActivityForResult()` is deprecated from AndroidX Activity 1.2 onwards, the **KTU 2024 Scheme** expects familiarity with the modern `ActivityResultLauncher` pattern.

**Step A: Register the launcher in the source Activity (before `onCreate` finishes):**

```java
private final ActivityResultLauncher<Intent> dashboardLauncher =
    registerForActivityResult(new ActivityResultContracts.StartActivityForResult(),
        result -> {
            if (result.getResultCode() == RESULT_OK) {
                Intent data = result.getData();
                String response = data.getStringExtra("RESPONSE_PAYLOAD");
                // Handle the returned data
            }
        });
```

**Step B: Launch the Intent through the launcher (not `startActivity`):**

```java
Intent intent = new Intent(this, SensorDashboardActivity.class);
intent.putExtra("REQUEST_TYPE", "FETCH_READING");
dashboardLauncher.launch(intent);
```

**Step C: Set a result in the target Activity before finishing:**

```java
Intent resultIntent = new Intent();
resultIntent.putExtra("RESPONSE_PAYLOAD", "23.5°C");
setResult(RESULT_OK, resultIntent);
finish();
```

This three-step flow is the **modern KTU 2024-compliant** pattern.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Explicit Activity Start Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Source Activity onClick Handler]:::src
    nodeB[Phase 1: Construct Explicit Intent]:::intent
    nodeC[Phase 2: Attach Extras to Bundle]:::intent
    nodeD[Phase 3: Call startActivity]:::dispatch
    nodeE[Activity Manager Service AMS]:::system
    nodeF[Phase 4: Resolve ComponentName]:::system
    nodeG{Check Manifest Declaration}:::decision
    nodeH[Target Activity onCreate]:::target
    nodeI[Target Activity onStart]:::target
    nodeJ[Target Activity onResume]:::target
    nodeK[Source Activity onPause]:::src
    nodeL[Source Activity onStop]:::src
    nodeM[Target Activity Displayed on Screen]:::ui

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG -->|Found| nodeH
    nodeG -->|Not Found| nodeErr[Throw ActivityNotFoundException]:::err
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM

    classDef src fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef intent fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dispatch fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef system fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef target fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ui fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef err fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

### 4.2 Back Stack Visualization — LIFO Navigation Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[Stack Bottom: Home Launcher]:::sys
    nodeS2[Layer 1: MainActivity]:::layer1
    nodeS3[Layer 2: SensorDashboardActivity]:::layer2
    nodeS4[Layer 3: DeviceControlActivity]:::layer3
    nodeTop[Stack Top: User Currently Sees]:::top

    nodeS1 --> nodeS2
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4
    nodeS4 --> nodeTop

    classDef sys fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef layer1 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef layer2 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef layer3 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef top fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Every time you explicitly start a new Activity, it gets **pushed onto the top** of the Task Back Stack. Pressing the Back button **pops** the top Activity, invoking `onPause()` → `onStop()` → `onDestroy()` on it, and the user sees the Activity just below it.

### 4.3 Intent Construction Pattern Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Choose Construction Method]:::dec
    nodeA[Pattern A: Direct Constructor]:::p
    nodeB[Pattern B: setComponent with Strings]:::p
    nodeC[Pattern C: setClass with Class Object]:::p
    nodeA1[Use when target is known at compile time]:::note
    nodeB1[Use when target class name is dynamic e.g. plugin]:::note
    nodeC1[Use as equivalent of Pattern A]:::note

    nodeStart --> nodeA
    nodeStart --> nodeB
    nodeStart --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeA1
    nodeB --> nodeB1
    nodeC --> nodeC1

    classDef dec fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef p fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#9370DB,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#F0FFFF,stroke:#5F9EA0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]** [3 Marks]
> **Q:** Define an *Explicit Intent* in Android. How does it differ from an *Implicit Intent*?

**Model Answer:**
An Explicit Intent is an Android `Intent` object that specifies the target component to be invoked by its fully qualified `ComponentName` (package + class). It is used to launch a specific Activity within the same app or a known external app.
**Difference:** An Explicit Intent names the target directly (e.g., `new Intent(this, TargetActivity.class)`), while an Implicit Intent declares a general action (e.g., `Intent.ACTION_VIEW`) and lets the system resolve the target via intent-filter matching. Explicit Intents are deterministic and secure; Implicit Intents are flexible and rely on PackageManager resolution. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]** [3 Marks]
> **Q:** What is the role of the `Activity Manager Service (AMS)` when an Activity is started explicitly?

**Model Answer:**
The Activity Manager Service (AMS) is a system-level binder service that handles all Activity lifecycle transitions. When `startActivity(intent)` is called, AMS:
1. Receives the Intent via Binder IPC.
2. Resolves the `ComponentName` to verify the target is declared in the manifest.
3. Manages the Task Back Stack, pushing the new Activity on top.
4. Triggers the target Activity's lifecycle callbacks (`onCreate()` → `onStart()` → `onResume()`) in the correct order.
5. Sends lifecycle notifications to the source Activity (`onPause()` → `onStop()`).
AMS thus acts as the central orchestrator of all Activity transitions. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a code example how to explicitly start a new Activity in Android and pass data to it using Extras.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of Explicit Start:**
Explicitly starting a new Activity means launching a *specific, named* Activity using an `Intent` whose target is fully identified by its class reference. The system bypasses intent-filter resolution and dispatches the Intent directly to the named component.

**Step 2 — Source Activity Code (e.g., `MainActivity.java`):**

```java
public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {
    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);
        
        Button btnSend = findViewById(R.id.btnSend);
        btnSend.setOnClickListener(v -> {
            // Construct Explicit Intent
            Intent intent = new Intent(MainActivity.this, 
                                       ReceiverActivity.class);
            
            // Attach Extras (data payload)
            intent.putExtra("USERNAME", "KeralaStudent");
            intent.putExtra("MARKS", 95);
            
            // Dispatch
            startActivity(intent);
        });
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Target Activity Code (e.g., `ReceiverActivity.java`):**

```java
public class ReceiverActivity extends AppCompatActivity {
    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_receiver);
        
        Intent in = getIntent();
        String name = in.getStringExtra("USERNAME");
        int marks = in.getIntExtra("MARKS", 0);
        
        TextView txt = findViewById(R.id.txtDisplay);
        txt.setText("Hello " + name + ", Marks: " + marks);
    }
}
```

**Step 4 — Manifest Declaration:**
The `ReceiverActivity` must be declared:

```xml
<activity android:name=".ReceiverActivity" android:exported="false" />
```

**Explanation:** The `new Intent(context, class)` constructor creates an Explicit Intent bound to `ReceiverActivity`. The `putExtra()` calls attach key-value pairs to the internal Bundle. The target retrieves them via `getIntent().getStringExtra("USERNAME")`. (7 marks)

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the Activity lifecycle method invocation order when `Activity_A` explicitly starts `Activity_B`. Why is this order important?

**Model Solution:**

When `Activity_A.startActivity(intent)` is called, the **Activity Manager Service** orchestrates the following lifecycle method calls:

$$
\text{Activity\_A.onPause()} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Activity\_B.onCreate()} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Activity\_B.onStart()} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Activity\_B.onResume()} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Activity\_A.onStop()}
$$

**Why this order matters:**

1. **`onPause()` first on Source:** The source Activity must release foreground resources (e.g., camera, exclusive sensors) *before* the new Activity takes focus, to avoid resource contention.
2. **`onCreate()` of Target:** A new instance is created; UI inflation and `setContentView()` happen here. It is called *only once* per Activity instance.
3. **`onStart()` of Target:** The Activity becomes visible to the user but is not yet interactive.
4. **`onResume()` of Target:** The Activity reaches the "Resumed" state, gains focus, and becomes interactive.
5. **`onStop()` of Source (often):** After the target is fully resumed, the source may be stopped, freeing its UI resources.

**Engineering Significance:** This deterministic ordering is critical in IoT apps. For example, if `Activity_A` holds a BLE GATT connection, `onPause()` is the safe place to *pause* telemetry updates so they don't conflict with the new Activity's data subscriptions. (7 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** [7 Marks] What are the different ways to construct an Explicit Intent in Android? Compare them with code examples.

**Model Solution:**

There are **three primary patterns** for constructing an Explicit Intent:

**Pattern 1 — Direct Constructor:**
```java
Intent i1 = new Intent(CurrentActivity.this, TargetActivity.class);
startActivity(i1);
```
*Best for:* The most common, type-safe case where the target class is known at compile time. Compile errors occur if `TargetActivity` does not exist.

**Pattern 2 — `setClass()` Method:**
```java
Intent i2 = new Intent();
i2.setClass(CurrentActivity.this, TargetActivity.class);
startActivity(i2);
```
*Best for:* Cases where the Intent object is built incrementally (e.g., in a builder or factory method) and the target is decided mid-flow.

**Pattern 3 — `setComponent()` with ComponentName:**
```java
Intent i3 = new Intent();
i3.setComponent(new ComponentName("com.example.app", 
                                    "com.example.app.TargetActivity"));
startActivity(i3);
```
*Best for:* Dynamic targets where the class name is provided as a String at runtime (e.g., plugin systems, deep-link routers). The package and class names must be passed as strings, so this is *not* compile-time safe.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Pattern 1 | Pattern 2 | Pattern 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compile-time safety | **Yes** | **Yes** | No (Strings) |
| Conciseness | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| Runtime flexibility | Low | Medium | **Highest** |
| Use in IoT app | Common screen navigation | Conditional navigation | Plugin / OTA-based feature launches |

All three patterns result in identical runtime behavior — the target is fully resolved by `ComponentName`. (7 marks)

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the modern **Activity Result API** for starting an Activity and retrieving a result back. Why is it preferred over the legacy `startActivityForResult()`?

**Model Solution:**

The **Activity Result API** (introduced in AndroidX Activity 1.2) replaces the deprecated `startActivityForResult()` / `onActivityResult()` pattern. It uses an `ActivityResultLauncher` registered with the host Activity.

**Step 1 — Register the launcher in the calling Activity:**

```java
private final ActivityResultLauncher<Intent> myLauncher =
    registerForActivityResult(
        new ActivityResultContracts.StartActivityForResult(),
        result -> {
            if (result.getResultCode() == RESULT_OK) {
                Intent data = result.getData();
                String reply = data.getStringExtra("REPLY");
                Log.d("RESULT", "Got reply: " + reply);
            }
        }
    );
```

**Step 2 — Launch the Intent through the launcher:**

```java
Intent i = new Intent(this, ReplyActivity.class);
i.putExtra("QUESTION", "What is the temperature?");
myLauncher.launch(i);
```

**Step 3 — In the target `ReplyActivity`, set the result before finishing:**

```java
Intent resultIntent = new Intent();
resultIntent.putExtra("REPLY", "23.5°C");
setResult(RESULT_OK, resultIntent);
finish();
```

**Why the modern API is preferred over `startActivityForResult()`:**

1. **Type safety:** The launcher is generic over the input type (`Intent`) and the result is delivered as a structured `ActivityResult` object, eliminating the need for fragile integer request codes.
2. **Lifecycle safety:** The result callback is delivered only when the Activity is in a valid state (`STARTED`), preventing the crash that occurred in legacy code when the Activity was destroyed before `onActivityResult()` could be called.
3. **Decoupling:** The launcher can be declared as a class member, separated from the click handler, improving readability and testability.
4. **Future-proof:** `startActivityForResult()` was officially deprecated in AndroidX Activity 1.2 and may be removed in future SDK versions. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the Manifest Declaration:** Students often write the Java code to start an Activity but forget to declare it in `AndroidManifest.xml`. This causes a runtime crash with `ActivityNotFoundException` or `ClassNotFoundException`. Always include `<activity android:name=".YourActivity"/>` inside `<application>`.
> 2. **Using `getApplicationContext()` without `FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK`:** When starting an Activity from a non-Activity Context (e.g., a `Service` or `Application`), you *must* add `Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK` or your app will throw `AndroidRuntimeException`.
> 3. **Key collisions in Extras:** Using generic keys like `"name"` or `"id"` can clash with system or third-party Intent keys. Use **package-prefixed** keys (e.g., `"com.example.iotapp.SENSOR_ID"`).
> 4. **Mismatched Lifecycle Diagrams:** Students often write the lifecycle order incorrectly. Remember the canonical rule: **Source onPause → Target onCreate → Target onStart → Target onResume → Source onStop (eventually)**.
> 5. **Confusing Explicit with Implicit Intents:** In exam answers, never describe an Explicit Intent as using `Intent.ACTION_VIEW` or `Intent.ACTION_SEND` — those are *Implicit* Intent actions. An Explicit Intent *must* specify the `ComponentName`.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Explicit Intent** = Intent with a fully specified `ComponentName` (package + class) as the target. It is **deterministic** and does not use intent-filter resolution.
- The three construction patterns are: (1) `new Intent(context, class)`, (2) `intent.setClass()`, (3) `intent.setComponent()`.
- Data is passed via **Extras** (a key-value Bundle) using `putExtra()` and retrieved with typed getters like `getStringExtra("KEY")`.
- The dispatch method is `startActivity(intent)`, which is **non-blocking** and routes the request to the **Activity Manager Service (AMS)**.
- The lifecycle order on explicit start: **Source `onPause()` → Target `onCreate` → `onStart` → `onResume` → Source `onStop`**.
- For receiving a result back, use the **modern `ActivityResultLauncher` API** (AndroidX Activity 1.2+), not the deprecated `startActivityForResult()`.
- **Manifest declaration is mandatory** for every Activity, even if it is only started explicitly.
- **`android:exported`** must be set to `false` for activities that are only launched internally (mandatory for API 31+).
- For non-Activity Contexts (Service/Application), add the flag **`FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK`** to the Intent.
- **Best practice for Extra keys:** Use the format `com.yourcompany.appname.KEY_NAME` to avoid name collisions.
- **Lifecycle pause-before-create rule:** The source Activity's `onPause()` is *always* called before the target's `onCreate()` — this is critical for IoT apps that hold exclusive hardware resources (BLE, camera, sensors).
- **The Back Stack is LIFO:** Pressing the back button pops the topmost Activity, destroying it via `onPause → onStop → onDestroy`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implicit Intents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Implicit Intents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Implicit Intents

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the Android Operating System framework, an **Intent** is a messaging object used to request an action from another app component. Android categorizes intents into two principal types: **Explicit Intents** and **Implicit Intents**.

An **Implicit Intent** is a declarative messaging mechanism in Android where the developer specifies the *desired action* (what to be done) and optionally the *desired data type* (on what kind of data), without naming the specific component (Activity, Service, or BroadcastReceiver) that should execute it. The Android system, through its **Intent Resolution Mechanism** and the **Package Manager**, matches the request against the **Intent Filters** declared in the `AndroidManifest.xml` files of installed applications, and delivers the intent to the most appropriate component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Implicit Intents enable **late runtime binding** of code to the components that will ultimately service the request, forming the foundation of inter-process communication (IPC) in Android and enabling seamless cross-application integration essential for IoT ecosystems (e.g., launching a maps app to display sensor GPS coordinates).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Waiter" Model

Imagine you (the Android app) are sitting at a restaurant table holding a written order. You have **two ways** to give the order to the kitchen:

| Analogy Element | Android Mapping |
|---|---|
| **Explicit Intent** | "Hey, **Chef Rahul** in Kitchen B, make me a Dosa." (You know *exactly* who cooks it — by class name). |
| **Implicit Intent** | "Anyone in the restaurant who can make a Dosa, please bring it to Table 7." (You don't know *who* will serve it — the restaurant manager/host delegates it.) |

In the **Implicit Intent** scenario:
- The **action** = "make a Dosa" (`ACTION_VIEW`, `ACTION_DIAL`, `ACTION_SEND`)
- The **data** = "Dosa recipe parameters" (`Uri` + MIME type)
- The **restaurant manager** = **Android Intent Resolver** (a system service)
- The **kitchen** = Any installed app whose **Intent Filter** says "I can make Dosas" (e.g., Maps, Dialer, Browser, Camera)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Implicit Intents power the **"open with..."** dialog you see in Android when multiple apps can handle a request. They decouple the *requester* from the *provider*, making Android a modular, plug-and-play OS — a critical property for IoT where heterogeneous devices and apps must communicate.

## 1.3 Core Anatomical Components of an Implicit Intent

An Implicit Intent object in `android.content.Intent` is constructed using a small set of fundamental attributes. The three primary attributes that drive implicit resolution are:

1. **Action** — A string constant describing *what* is to be done (e.g., `Intent.ACTION_VIEW`).
2. **Data (URI)** — A `Uri` object pointing to the data on which the action should be performed (e.g., `https://www.ktu.edu.in` or `tel:9447012345`).
3. **Type (MIME)** — A string specifying the data's Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions type (e.g., `text/plain`, `image/*`).

Optional attributes include **Category** (`CATEGORY_DEFAULT` is implicitly added), **Extras** (a `Bundle` of key-value pairs), and **Flags** (control intent delivery behavior).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Standard Metric:** The Android Intent resolution engine operates with **O(N)** lookup complexity over installed packages, where **N** is the number of installed apps with compatible intent filters. For an IoT gateway app, this typically resolves in **< 50ms** on modern devices.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Intent Resolution Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

When `startActivity(implicitIntent)` is invoked, the Android framework executes the following deterministic resolution pipeline:

### Step 1: Construction Phase
The developer instantiates an `Intent` object using the `Intent(String action)` or `Intent(String action, Uri uri)` constructor. The system **implicitly adds** the category `android.intent.category.DEFAULT` to every intent used with `startActivity()`.

### Step 2: Action Validation
The Package Manager scans **all installed applications' `AndroidManifest.xml` files**, looking for `<intent-filter>` blocks whose `<action android:name="..."/>` element matches the intent's action string. The action match is **case-sensitive** and must be **exact** (wildcards are not permitted for actions in standard usage).

### Step 3: Data/Type Triple Verification
For each candidate app, the system performs a triple-test:

$$
\text{Match} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{True} & \text{if (intent.URI is null) AND (filter has no data spec)} \\
\text{True} & \text{if (intent.URI matches filter's scheme/host/path) AND (intent.type matches filter's mimeType)} \\
\text{False} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

The four data attributes checked are: **scheme** (`http`, `tel`, `geo`), **host**, **path**, and **pathPrefix/pathPattern**. The MIME type is matched using pattern rules where `image/*` matches `image/jpeg`, `image/png`, etc.

### Step 4: User Disambiguation (if required)
If **0 apps** match, `ActivityNotFoundException` is thrown. If **2+ apps** match, the system displays a chooser (either the default Android chooser or a custom one built via `Intent.createChooser()`).

### Step 5: Component Launch
The chosen component's `Activity` is instantiated, and `onCreate()` is invoked with `getIntent()` returning the original intent object — granting the target app full access to the extras.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Table — Common Implicit Intent Actions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Focus:** Memorize the action strings, their target data URIs, and the requesting app's role. These appear frequently in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

| # | Action Constant | Action String | Typical Data URI / MIME | Real-World Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `ACTION_VIEW` | `android.intent.action.VIEW` | `https://`, `geo:lat,lng`, `tel:number` | Open URL in browser, show location on map, view contact |
| 2 | `ACTION_DIAL` | `android.intent.action.DIAL` | `tel:9447012345` | Open dialer pre-filled with number (does not call) |
| 3 | `ACTION_CALL` | `android.intent.action.CALL` | `tel:9447012345` | **Directly place** a call (requires `CALL_PHONE` permission) |
| 4 | `ACTION_SEND` | `android.intent.action.SEND` | `text/plain`, `image/*` | Share text/image to WhatsApp, Gmail, etc. |
| 5 | `ACTION_SENDTO` | `android.intent.action.SENDTO` | `mailto:`, `smsto:` | Send email/SMS without generic sharing |
| 6 | `ACTION_WEB_SEARCH` | `android.intent.action.WEB_SEARCH` | query in extras | Perform a web search |
| 7 | `ACTION_GET_CONTENT` | `android.intent.action.GET_CONTENT` | MIME type `"image/*"` | Pick a file (photo, document) from device storage |
| 8 | `ACTION_PICK` | `android.intent.action.PICK` | `content://contacts/people` | Pick from a specific data URI (legacy) |
| 9 | `ACTION_CAMERA` | Custom / `MEDIA_STORE_ACTION_IMAGE_CAPTURE` | (returns image Uri) | Launch camera app and capture photo |

## 2.3 The Three-Attribute Decision Matrix (Critical for KTU)

The following truth table determines when an Intent Filter matches an implicit intent. The system performs the test for **each** of the action, data, and category attributes. A match on **all three** is required.

| Filter has `<action>` matching intent? | Filter has `<data>` matching intent? | Filter's categories ⊇ intent's categories? | Result |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | **MATCH — Activity will be in the candidate list** |
| ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ NO MATCH |
| ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ NO MATCH |
| ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ NO MATCH |
| ✅ Yes (multiple) | ✅ Yes (multiple) | ✅ Yes (multiple) | All combinations checked; each match adds candidate |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in IoT Contexts

In an **IoT Mobile App** (the KTU PECGT415 course focus), implicit intents are the primary mechanism for:

- **Triggering external device control apps** — e.g., an IoT dashboard can fire `ACTION_VIEW` with `geo:` URI to hand off location visualization to Google Maps.
- **Sharing sensor telemetry** — fire `ACTION_SEND` with `text/plain` MIME and a JSON payload in extras to push data to MQTT-broker apps or messaging clients.
- **Calling emergency numbers** when sensor thresholds (gas leak, fire) are breached — `ACTION_DIAL` with `tel:` URI.
- **Launching camera** for QR-code-based device provisioning — `ACTION_IMAGE_CAPTURE`.
- **Cross-app authentication flows** using `ACTION_VIEW` with custom URI schemes registered in OAuth callbacks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Security Note for IoT Apps:** Always wrap `startActivity()` calls in `try { ... } catch (ActivityNotFoundException e) { ... }` because IoT devices may have stripped-down OS images lacking common apps (no dialer, no browser).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Code Analysis & Methodological Derivation

## 3.1 The Canonical Implicit Intent Construction (Java)

Below is the **exhaustive, line-by-line explanation** of the canonical implicit intent workflow. This pattern appears in nearly every Part B (14-mark) KTU question on this topic.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Import the Required Classes

```java
import android.content.Intent;
import android.net.Uri;
import android.os.Bundle;
```

**Explanation:**
- `android.content.Intent` is the core class representing the intent object itself.
- `android.net.Uri` encapsulates the Uniform Resource Identifier (a superset of URLs).
- `android.os.Bundle` is used for the extras key-value payload.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Construct the Intent with Action + Data

```java
Uri webpage = Uri.parse("https://www.ktu.edu.in");
Intent intent = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, webpage);
```

**Line-by-line interpretation:**

1. **`Uri.parse(...)`** converts the raw string `"https://www.ktu.edu.in"` into a parsed `Uri` object. The system extracts the **scheme** (`https`), **host** (`www.ktu.edu.in`), and an empty **path**.
2. **`Intent.ACTION_VIEW`** is a public static final String constant with value `"android.intent.action.VIEW"`. This tells the system: "I want to *view* something."
3. **`new Intent(action, uri)`** is a convenience constructor that internally calls `setAction(action)` and `setData(uri)`.
4. Implicitly, the system adds the **default category** `android.intent.category.DEFAULT`.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Optional: Verify Before Launching

```java
PackageManager packageManager = getPackageManager();
if (intent.resolveActivity(packageManager) != null) {
    startActivity(intent);
} else {
    // Fallback: no browser installed, open settings or show toast
    Toast.makeText(this, "No browser app found", Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show();
}
```

**Reasoning for the guard:** `resolveActivity()` queries the Package Manager and returns the `ComponentName` of the best-matching activity, or `null` if none exists. This is **best practice** and a KTU-favorite defensive programming point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Android (API 30+):** The `resolveActivity` method signature has changed to require a `PackageManager` parameter. For Android 11+ targeting, the recommended approach is `intent.resolveActivity(getPackageManager())`.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Launch the Intent

```java
startActivity(intent);
```

This call is **asynchronous**. The current Activity is paused, and the matched Activity from another app enters the **onCreate → onStart → onResume** lifecycle. If a chooser is needed (multiple matches), the system shows it automatically *unless* wrapped in `Intent.createChooser()`.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Intent Filter Match Formula

The Android Package Manager computes a match score using the following deterministic procedure. Let:

- $A_i$ = the action string of the implicit intent (e.g., `"android.intent.action.VIEW"`)
- $D_i$ = the data URI of the intent (e.g., `https://ktu.edu.in`)
- $T_i$ = the MIME type of the intent (e.g., `null` if not set, or `"text/plain"`)
- $F_a$ = set of `<action>` elements in the candidate app's filter
- $F_d$ = set of `<data>` elements in the candidate app's filter
- $F_t$ = set of `<data android:mimeType="...">` values in the filter
- $F_c$ = set of `<category>` elements in the filter

The match function $M(A_i, D_i, T_i, F)$ is defined as:

$$
M(A_i, D_i, T_i, F) = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } A_i \in F_a \;\land\; \text{DataMatch}(D_i, F_d) \;\land\; \text{TypeMatch}(T_i, F_t) \;\land\; C_i \subseteq F_c \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Where the data match subfunction is:

$$
\text{DataMatch}(D_i, F_d) = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } D_i = \text{null} \;\land\; F_d = \emptyset \\
1 & \text{if } D_i \neq \text{null} \;\land\; \exists\, d \in F_d : \text{scheme}(D_i) = \text{scheme}(d) \;\land\; \ldots \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

And the type match subfunction is:

$$
\text{TypeMatch}(T_i, F_t) = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } T_i = \text{null} \;\land\; F_d = \emptyset \text{ (no data URI either)} \\
1 & \text{if } T_i \neq \text{null} \;\land\; \exists\, t \in F_t : (t = T_i) \;\lor\; (t = T_i \text{ pattern like "image/*" matches "image/jpeg"}) \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** If the exam asks "What is the role of the Package Manager in implicit intent resolution?", the expected answer is: *"The Package Manager scans the `<intent-filter>` elements in the `AndroidManifest.xml` of all installed applications, applies the three-attribute match (action, data, category), and returns a list of candidate components that can handle the request."*

## 3.3 Worked Example — Sending Sensor Data via Email (IoT Scenario)

**Problem Statement:** An IoT air-quality monitoring app detects a PM2.5 level of 350 µg/m³ (hazardous). Construct an implicit intent to email the alert to the city environmental officer.

### Solution Steps:

**Step 1 — Define the recipient and subject:**

```java
String recipient = "environment@city.gov.in";
String subject = Uri.encode("Hazardous Air Quality Alert");
String body = Uri.encode("PM2.5 reading: 350 µg/m³ at Sensor Node #7, Kochi. Immediate action required.");
```

The `Uri.encode()` function escapes special characters like spaces, ensuring URI validity.

**Step 2 — Build the composite URI:**

```java
Uri uri = Uri.parse("mailto:" + recipient 
        + "?subject=" + subject 
        + "&body=" + body);
```

**Step 3 — Construct and launch the intent:**

```java
Intent emailIntent = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_SENDTO);
emailIntent.setData(uri);
// Optional: declare MIME type for chooser filtering
emailIntent.setType("text/plain");
emailIntent.putExtra(Intent.EXTRA_SUBJECT, "Hazardous Air Quality Alert");
emailIntent.putExtra(Intent.EXTRA_TEXT, "PM2.5: 350 µg/m³, Sensor #7, Kochi");

if (emailIntent.resolveActivity(getPackageManager()) != null) {
    startActivity(emailIntent);
}
```

**Why this works:** The action `ACTION_SENDTO` combined with the `mailto:` scheme is a standard pattern recognized by Gmail, Outlook, and Yahoo Mail apps. If no email client is installed (rare on phones, common on IoT gateways), the `resolveActivity` guard prevents a crash.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Implicit Intent Resolution Flow

The following Mermaid block renders a complete block-level functional architecture flow of the implicit intent lifecycle — from construction to component launch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Developer App Activity"] --> B["Construct Intent Object\nIntent.ACTION_VIEW + Uri"]
    B --> C["Optionally add Extras\nBundle of key-value pairs"]
    C --> D["Call startActivity intent"]
    D --> E["Android Activity Manager Service"]
    E --> F["Package Manager Query\nscan all installed apps"]
    F --> G["Retrieve Intent Filter Manifests\nfrom AndroidManifest.xml"]
    G --> H{"Match Test\nAction + Data + Type + Category"}
    H -->|"Zero Matches"| I["Throw ActivityNotFoundException\nor fallback logic"]
    H -->|"Exactly One Match"| J["Launch Target Activity Directly"]
    H -->|"Multiple Matches"| K["Show Chooser Dialog\nIntent.createChooser optional"]
    K --> J
    J --> L["Target App onCreate receives Intent\nvia getIntent method"]
    L --> M["Process Extras and Data\nPerform the requested action"]
    M --> N["Return Result via setResult\nif startActivityForResult used"]
    N --> O["Original App onActivityResult\ncallback invoked"]
    I --> P["Show Toast or open Play Store\nsuggestion to user"]
    style A fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#FFFFFF
    style E fill:#F39C12,stroke:#A04000,color:#FFFFFF
    style F fill:#F39C12,stroke:#A04000,color:#FFFFFF
    style H fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#922B21,color:#FFFFFF
    style J fill:#27AE60,stroke:#145A32,color:#FFFFFF
    style L fill:#27AE60,stroke:#145A32,color:#FFFFFF
    style I fill:#C0392B,stroke:#641E16,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.2 Intent Filter Matching Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Incoming Implicit Intent"] --> Q1{"Action matches\nany intent-filter\naction element?"}
    Q1 -->|No| Reject["REJECTED\nnot added to candidate list"]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{"Data URI scheme\nand host match\nany data element?"}
    Q2 -->|No| Reject
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{"MIME type matches\nany data mimeType\nelement?"}
    Q3 -->|No| Reject
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{"All Intent categories\nare covered by filter\ncategories?"}
    Q4 -->|No| Reject
    Q4 -->|Yes| Accept["ACCEPTED\nadded to candidate list"]
    Accept --> Count{"How many\ncandidates?"}
    Count -->|"Zero"| Crash["ActivityNotFoundException"]
    Count -->|"One"| DirectLaunch["Direct launch\nof matched activity"]
    Count -->|"Multiple"| Chooser["System Chooser Dialog\nor createChooser"]
    style Start fill:#3498DB,stroke:#1B4F72,color:#FFFFFF
    style Accept fill:#27AE60,stroke:#145A32,color:#FFFFFF
    style Reject fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#922B21,color:#FFFFFF
    style DirectLaunch fill:#2ECC71,stroke:#196F3D,color:#FFFFFF
    style Chooser fill:#F1C40F,stroke:#7D6608,color:#000000
    style Crash fill:#C0392B,stroke:#641E16,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.3 IoT Mobile App Use-Case Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph App["IoT Dashboard App PECGT415"]
        Sensor["Sensor Data\nAcquisition Module"]
        Logic["Threshold Logic\nPM2.5 > 300"]
    end
    subgraph External["External Android Apps via Implicit Intents"]
        Maps["Google Maps\ngeo: URI"]
        Email["Gmail App\nmailto: URI"]
        SMS["Messaging App\nsmsto: URI"]
        Camera["Camera App\nACTION_IMAGE_CAPTURE"]
    end
    Sensor --> Logic
    Logic -->|"ACTION_VIEW + geo:"| Maps
    Logic -->|"ACTION_SENDTO + mailto:"| Email
    Logic -->|"ACTION_SENDTO + smsto:"| SMS
    Logic -->|"ACTION_IMAGE_CAPTURE\nfor QR provisioning"| Camera
    style Sensor fill:#5D6D7E,stroke:#212F3D,color:#FFFFFF
    style Logic fill:#E67E22,stroke:#935116,color:#FFFFFF
    style Maps fill:#27AE60,stroke:#145A32,color:#FFFFFF
    style Email fill:#C0392B,stroke:#641E16,color:#FFFFFF
    style SMS fill:#2980B9,stroke:#154360,color:#FFFFFF
    style Camera fill:#8E44AD,stroke:#4A235A,color:#FFFFFF
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Explicit Intents and Implicit Intents in Android. Give one example use-case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Explicit Intent | Implicit Intent |
|---|---|---|
| **Target Component** | Specifies the exact target by class name (e.g., `SecondActivity.class`) | Does **not** specify a target; only declares the action and data |
| **Use Case** | Internal navigation within the same app (e.g., `MainActivity` → `SettingsActivity`) | Cross-app operations (e.g., opening a URL in the browser) |
| **Coupling** | Tightly coupled — both activities must exist in the same project | Loosely coupled — works with any installed app that has a matching intent filter |
| **Example Code** | `Intent i = new Intent(this, SecondActivity.class);` | `Intent i = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, Uri.parse("https://ktu.edu.in"));` |
| **Resolution** | Direct, no resolution needed | Resolved at runtime by Package Manager |

**Example Use-Cases:**
- **Explicit:** Moving from a login screen to a dashboard screen in the same app.
- **Implicit:** Clicking a phone number in a contacts app to open the dialer.

*(Full 3 marks: 1 mark for definition difference, 1 mark for code contrast, 1 mark for valid example.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List and briefly explain any THREE common system action constants used with implicit intents in Android.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per action):**

1. **`Intent.ACTION_VIEW`** — Action string `"android.intent.action.VIEW"`. Used to display data to the user. Example: viewing a webpage in the browser, viewing a location on a map (`geo:lat,lng`), or viewing a contact.

2. **`Intent.ACTION_SEND`** — Action string `"android.intent.action.SEND"`. Used to share data (text, image, file) with another app. The data is provided via `EXTRA_TEXT`, `EXTRA_STREAM` in the extras Bundle, and the MIME type is set via `setType("text/plain")` or `setType("image/jpeg")`.

3. **`Intent.ACTION_DIAL`** — Action string `"android.intent.action.DIAL"`. Opens the dialer with a phone number pre-filled but does **not** place the call. The data URI is `tel:<number>`. This avoids requiring the dangerous `CALL_PHONE` permission.

*(Bonus mention: `ACTION_GET_CONTENT` for file picking, `ACTION_WEB_SEARCH` for search queries.)*

---

## 📌 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### 🔷 **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Implicit Intents in Android with a neat diagram. Describe the role of the **Android Manifest file** and **Intent Filters** in resolving implicit intents. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Android Java code snippet to construct an implicit intent that **opens a Google Map location** for the KTU Main Campus in Thiruvananthapuram (coordinates: 8.5421° N, 76.9030° E) when a button is clicked. Handle the case where no map application is installed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📖 Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):**

An **Implicit Intent** is a declarative request in Android that specifies *what action* should be performed and *on what data*, without naming the specific application component. The Android operating system uses the **Package Manager** to perform runtime resolution.

**Role of the Android Manifest File (`AndroidManifest.xml`):**
- Every Android app must declare its components (Activities, Services, Receivers) in this file.
- Within `<activity>` tags, developers include `<intent-filter>` blocks that advertise the actions, data types, and categories the activity can handle.
- Without an intent filter, an activity is "invisible" to implicit intents and can only be launched via explicit intents.

**Structure of an Intent Filter:**

```xml
<activity android:name=".BrowserActivity">
    <intent-filter>
        <action android:name="android.intent.action.VIEW" />
        <category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT" />
        <category android:name="android.intent.category.BROWSABLE" />
        <data android:scheme="https" />
        <data android:scheme="http" />
    </intent-filter>
</activity>
```

**Resolution Process:**
1. App A constructs an implicit intent with `ACTION_VIEW` and `Uri` `https://ktu.edu.in`.
2. App A calls `startActivity(intent)`.
3. Android's **Activity Manager Service** queries the **Package Manager**.
4. Package Manager enumerates all `<intent-filter>` elements across all installed apps.
5. Apps whose filters contain `<action>` matching `VIEW` AND `<data>` matching `https` scheme AND contain `CATEGORY_DEFAULT` are added to the candidate list.
6. If multiple candidates exist, the system shows a chooser. If one exists, it is launched directly. If none, an `ActivityNotFoundException` is thrown.

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for definition, 2 marks for manifest structure, 2 marks for resolution steps, 1 mark for diagram reference.)*

---

**Part (b) — Code Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Add the button to the layout XML (`activity_main.xml`):

```xml
<Button
    android:id="@+id/mapButton"
    android:layout_width="wrap_content"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:text="Open KTU Campus on Map" />
```

**Step 2:** Implement the click handler in `MainActivity.java`:

```java
import android.content.Intent;
import android.content.pm.PackageManager;
import android.net.Uri;
import android.os.Bundle;
import android.widget.Button;
import android.widget.Toast;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);

        Button mapButton = findViewById(R.id.mapButton);
        mapButton.setOnClickListener(v -> openKtuCampusOnMap());
    }

    private void openKtuCampusOnMap() {
        // KTU Main Campus coordinates: 8.5421, 76.9030
        // The geo: URI scheme is the standard for map intents
        String geoUri = "geo:8.5421,76.9030?q=8.5421,76.9030(APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University)";
        Uri mapUri = Uri.parse(geoUri);

        // Step A: Construct the implicit intent
        Intent mapIntent = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW, mapUri);
        mapIntent.setPackage(null); // Allow any map app to handle it

        // Step B: Verify at least one app can resolve this intent
        PackageManager packageManager = getPackageManager();
        if (mapIntent.resolveActivity(packageManager) != null) {
            startActivity(mapIntent);
        } else {
            // Fallback: open Play Store to install Google Maps
            Toast.makeText(this, "No map application found. Install Google Maps.",
                    Toast.LENGTH_LONG).show();
            
            try {
                startActivity(new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW,
                        Uri.parse("market://details?id=com.google.android.apps.maps")));
            } catch (Exception ex) {
                startActivity(new Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW,
                        Uri.parse("https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.google.android.apps.maps")));
            }
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Lines:**

- **`geo:8.5421,76.9030?q=...`** — The `geo:` scheme is the standard URI scheme for geographic locations. The `?q=` parameter is an optional label shown as a pin name.
