- **`mapIntent.resolveActivity(packageManager)`** — Returns the best-matching `ComponentName` or `null`. This guard prevents `ActivityNotFoundException` crashes — **a critical defensive pattern for IoT apps running on minimal OS builds**.
- **Fallback to Play Store** — Provides graceful degradation, which is expected in KTU answers to demonstrate engineering rigor.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark URI explanation, 2 marks intent construction, 2 marks resolveActivity check, 2 marks fallback handling.)*

---

### 🔷 **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Exam 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** What are the **three main components** of an implicit intent? Explain how each contributes to the intent resolution process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an Android Java application that uses an implicit intent to **share a text message** ("Sensor Offline: Node #42") with any installed messaging application. Include the use of `Intent.createChooser()` to always show a selection dialog. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📖 Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Three Components (7 marks):**

1. **Action (String)** — Defines *what* is to be done. It is a unique string identifier, usually prefixed with the package name (e.g., `android.intent.action.VIEW`). The Package Manager uses this as the **primary filter key**; only apps declaring this action in their intent filter will be considered.

2. **Data (Uri)** — Defines *on what* the action operates. A `Uri` encapsulates a scheme (e.g., `tel:`, `https:`, `content:`) and optional host, path, and query parameters. The Package Manager compares the intent's URI against the `<data android:scheme="..."/>` elements declared in candidate apps' intent filters.

3. **Type (MIME String)** — Defines the *data type* (e.g., `text/plain`, `image/jpeg`). The system checks whether the MIME type matches any `<data android:mimeType="..."/>` in the candidate filter. Pattern types like `image/*` allow broad matching.

4. **Category (String, default `CATEGORY_DEFAULT`)** — A qualifier that must be present in the candidate filter. Most filters include `<category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT" />` to be discoverable.

**How they contribute to resolution:**
- The system performs a **conjunctive (AND) match** across all three/four attributes.
- A failure on any single attribute disqualifies the candidate app.
- This tripartite design enables Android's modular app ecosystem — apps advertise capabilities declaratively, and other apps discover them at runtime.

*(Marks: 2 marks per main component, 1 mark for the AND-match explanation.)*

---

**Part (b) — Code Solution (7 marks):**

```java
import android.content.Intent;
import android.os.Bundle;
import android.widget.Button;
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity;

public class ShareAlertActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_share_alert);

        Button shareButton = findViewById(R.id.shareButton);
        shareButton.setOnClickListener(v -> shareSensorAlert());
    }

    private void shareSensorAlert() {
        // Step 1: Build the intent with SEND action and plain text MIME
        Intent sendIntent = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_SEND);
        sendIntent.setType("text/plain");
        sendIntent.putExtra(Intent.EXTRA_SUBJECT, "IoT Sensor Alert");
        sendIntent.putExtra(Intent.EXTRA_TEXT, "Sensor Offline: Node #42");
        sendIntent.putExtra(Intent.EXTRA_TITLE, "Share Alert");

        // Step 2: Wrap in createChooser to ALWAYS show selection dialog
        // This overrides the "always use this app" user preference
        Intent chooser = Intent.createChooser(sendIntent, "Share sensor alert via:");

        // Step 3: Verify resolution and launch
        if (sendIntent.resolveActivity(getPackageManager()) != null) {
            startActivity(chooser);
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation of `Intent.createChooser()`:**
- Wraps the original intent in a system-managed chooser dialog.
- Even if the user previously set a "default" sharing app, the chooser will still appear — useful in IoT dashboards where the alert recipient may vary.
- The second argument is the chooser dialog title shown at the top.
- On Android 10+, this is the **only** way to programmatically show a chooser from a non-system app.

**The three extras keys used:**
- `EXTRA_SUBJECT` — used by email clients as the subject line.
- `EXTRA_TEXT` — the main body of the message.
- `EXTRA_TITLE` — used by the chooser dialog as the content preview (Android 10+).

*(Marks: 2 marks intent construction, 2 marks createChooser wrapper, 2 marks extras keys explanation, 1 mark resolveActivity guard.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `CATEGORY_DEFAULT`** — Students often write intent filters without `<category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT" />`. This is the #1 reason activities are not found. The system **automatically adds** this category to every `startActivity()` call but the filter must include it. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing `ACTION_DIAL` vs `ACTION_CALL`** — `DIAL` does NOT require permission and does NOT place the call. `CALL` requires `<uses-permission android:name="android.permission.CALL_PHONE"/>` and **directly** places the call. Confusing these loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Missing `resolveActivity()` guard** — Code that calls `startActivity()` without checking may crash with `ActivityNotFoundException` on devices with no matching app (common in IoT gateways). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for "lack of defensive programming."
> 4. **Using `Uri.parse("tel:" + number)` for `ACTION_CALL` directly** — Forgetting to add the `CALL_PHONE` permission in the manifest.
> 5. **Not setting MIME type for `ACTION_SEND`** — Many chooser apps require `setType("text/plain")` to filter their candidate list properly. Forgetting this produces a generic chooser and is considered incomplete.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Implicit Intent** = *declarative* request specifying **action + data** without naming the target component.
- ✅ **Resolution Engine** = Android's **Package Manager** + **Activity Manager Service**.
- ✅ **Intent Filter** = `<intent-filter>` block in `AndroidManifest.xml` advertising an activity's capabilities.
- ✅ **Three Match Attributes** = **Action (exact string)** + **Data (URI scheme/host/path)** + **Type (MIME)**. All three must match (logical AND).
- ✅ **Default Category** = `android.intent.category.DEFAULT` is auto-added to every `startActivity()` call — every filter must include it.
- ✅ **`ACTION_VIEW`** is the most common action; pairs with schemes like `https:`, `tel:`, `geo:`, `mailto:`.
- ✅ **`ACTION_SEND`** + `setType("text/plain")` + `EXTRA_TEXT` = the canonical **share** pattern.
- ✅ **`ACTION_DIAL`** opens the dialer pre-filled; **`ACTION_CALL`** places the call (requires `CALL_PHONE` permission).
- ✅ **Always wrap in `try/catch`** for `ActivityNotFoundException` or use `resolveActivity()` guard — critical for IoT devices with minimal OS.
- ✅ **`Intent.createChooser()`** forces the chooser dialog even if a default app is set.
- ✅ **Disambiguation Rule** — 0 matches → exception; 1 match → direct launch; ≥2 matches → system chooser (or custom via `createChooser`).
- ✅ **Security Principle** — Implicit Intents are inherently **public APIs**; never put sensitive data (passwords, tokens) in the extras Bundle, as the target app is untrusted.
- ✅ **IoT Relevance** — Implicit Intents enable the IoT dashboard to leverage pre-installed system apps (Maps, Dialer, Camera, Share) without bundling redundant code.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Passing Data to Intents
Getting Results from Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Getting Results from Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3 — Getting Results from Activities

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition

> **Activity Result API** is the official Android mechanism that enables an **Activity (Caller)** to launch a second **Activity (Callee)** using an `Intent`, allow the Callee to perform some operation, and return a structured **result bundle** back to the Caller when the Callee finishes.

In the context of **Mobile App Development for IoT (PECGT415)**, this is the canonical way a *Dashboard Activity* can launch a *Device Configuration Activity* to pick a Wi-Fi SSID, a sensor ID, or an actuator channel, and receive the user's choice back into the caller for downstream communication with the IoT backend.

The two implementation paradigms under the KTU 2024 Scheme are:

| Paradigm | API Level | Status in 2024 Scheme |
|---|---|---|
| Legacy `startActivityForResult()` + `onActivityResult()` | API 1 – 30 | **Deprecated** since AndroidX Activity 1.2.0 |
| Modern `registerForActivityResult()` + `ActivityResultLauncher` | AndroidX 1.2+ | **Recommended / Mandatory** for 2024 scheme |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU 2024 Scheme, students are expected to **write code using `registerForActivityResult()`**. Legacy `onActivityResult()` is mentioned only for conceptual continuity and interview questions.

### 🍕 Intuition / Real-World Analogy

Imagine you walk into a **pizza shop** (the **Caller Activity**) and you cannot decide what to order. You ask the **waiter** (`ActivityResultLauncher`) to go to the **menu counter** (the **Callee Activity**) and bring back your selection. The waiter:

1. **Registers** with the shop manager (`registerForActivityResult`) before you sit down.
2. **Goes to the counter** (`launch(intent)`) carrying your request.
3. **Waits** for the counter to return with a **packed box** (the `Intent` result data).
4. **Brings the box back** to you (the `callback` lambda fires).

You never leave your table; the result finds its way back to you asynchronously. That is the entire essence of the Activity Result API.

### 🧷 Key Result-Code Constants

| Constant | Integer Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `RESULT_OK` | **-1** | Operation succeeded; data is valid |
| `RESULT_CANCELED` | **0** | User backed out or operation aborted |
| `RESULT_FIRST_USER` | **1** | Start of custom user-defined codes |

> [!NOTE]
> In the IoT context, `RESULT_CANCELED` is extremely common — a user opens the "Pair New Device" screen but swipes back. The caller must always handle both codes to avoid `NullPointerException` on the returned data.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Pillar Architecture of the Activity Result API

The modern API is built on **four interlocking pillars**. KTU questions frequently test whether the student can name and describe each one.

### Pillar 1 — The Contract (`ActivityResultContract<I, O>`)
- A **generic, reusable, type-safe agreement** defining:
  - **Input type `I`** — what the caller passes (often an `Intent`, sometimes a String/Uri).
  - **Output type `O`** — what the callee returns (often `String`, `Uri`, `Boolean`, or a custom POJO).
- Built-in contracts ship with AndroidX:
  - `StartActivityForResult` — generic Intent-based contract.
  - `GetContent` — returns the `Uri` of a picked file.
  - `RequestPermission` — returns a `Boolean` for a single permission.
  - `TakePicture` — returns a thumbnail `Uri`.

### Pillar 2 — The Launcher (`ActivityResultLauncher<O>`)
- A **handle object** returned by `registerForActivityResult()`.
- Exposes a single public method: `launch(input: I)`.
- The launcher is **tied to the lifecycle** of the registering component (Activity or Fragment). It will be automatically unregistered when the lifecycle is destroyed, preventing memory leaks.

### Pillar 3 — The Callback
- A lambda or `ActivityResultCallback<O>` passed at registration time.
- Fires **exactly once** when the callee finishes, regardless of whether the result is `OK` or `CANCELED`.
- The callback runs on the **main thread (UI thread)**.

### Pillar 4 — The Result Intent
- The `Intent` object passed to `setResult()` inside the callee.
- Carries the result code (`RESULT_OK` / `RESULT_CANCELED`) and an optional `Bundle` (or `Extras`) of payload data.
- Extracted in the callback using the contract's `parseResult()` method (called internally).

## 2.2 The Lifecycle Flow (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Caller Activity is created.** The contract is registered **before** `STARTED` state — typically in the field declaration or `onCreate()`.
2. **Caller calls `launcher.launch(intent)`.** Android suspends the caller and pushes the callee onto the back stack.
3. **Callee Activity runs.** The user interacts; the callee prepares a result `Intent` containing a `Bundle` of data.
4. **Callee calls `setResult(RESULT_OK, resultIntent)`** then `finish()`.
5. **Caller resumes.** The Android framework delivers the result to the registered callback, which executes on the main thread.
6. **Caller processes the result** in the callback and updates its UI.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why register early?** If you register the launcher *after* `onCreate()` (e.g., in `onResume()` or after a button click), the system will throw an `IllegalStateException` because the launcher must be bound to the lifecycle **before** the Activity reaches the `STARTED` state.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / API Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **API Cheat Sheet** for the topic. The notation used follows KTU textbook conventions.

| Symbol / API | Mathematical / Structural Form | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `registerForActivityResult(contract, callback)` | $\text{Launcher}_{I \to O} = \text{register}(C, f)$ | Registers a launcher of contract $C$ producing output type $O$ |
| `launcher.launch(input)` | $\text{Intent}_{\text{out}} = \text{launch}(I)$ | Starts the callee with input $I$ |
| `setResult(code, data)` | $R = \{c, D\}$ | Bundles result code $c \in \{-1, 0, 1, \dots\}$ and data $D$ |
| `RESULT_OK` | $c = -1$ | Success code |
| `RESULT_CANCELED` | $c = 0$ | Cancellation code |
| `data.getStringExtra(key)` | $D_v = D[\text{key}]$ | Extract value of key from bundle $D$ |
| Custom result code | $c = \text{RESULT\_FIRST\_USER} + n$ | For IoT: e.g., $n=1$ → device not found |

### LaTeX Boundary Conditions (Engineering Constraints)

$$
\text{Valid Result Codes: } c \in \mathbb{Z}, \quad -1 \le c \le \text{RESULT\_FIRST\_USER} - 1
$$

$$
\text{Custom codes: } c = \text{RESULT\_FIRST\_USER} + k, \quad k \in \mathbb{N}_0
$$

$$
\text{Callback fires iff: } \text{Callee Lifecycle State} = \text{DESTROYED}
$$

### Real-World Engineering Utility in IoT

In a **smart-home IoT app**, the dashboard may launch a *Device Selection Activity* and expect:
- `EXTRA_DEVICE_ID` (String) — the MAC/IP address of the chosen IoT device.
- `EXTRA_DEVICE_TYPE` (int) — 0 for sensor, 1 for actuator.
- A custom code `RESULT_DEVICE_OFFLINE = RESULT_FIRST_USER + 1` if the chosen device is unreachable.

This makes the Activity Result API a **structural contract** between loosely coupled screens, which is essential for modular IoT mobile apps.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — IoT Sensor Selection (Modern API)

Below is the **complete, exam-ready implementation** in **Kotlin**, the official KTU 2024 Scheme language for Android development.

### Step 1 — Define the Result Data Contract (POJO)

```kotlin
// File: SensorResult.kt
// A simple immutable data class to carry the result from Callee to Caller
data class SensorResult(
    val sensorId: String,
    val sensorType: String,   // e.g., "Temperature", "Humidity"
    val samplingRate: Int    // in Hz
)
```

### Step 2 — Define a Custom Contract (Optional but KTU-Favored)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Defining a **custom `ActivityResultContract`** is a high-yield KTU topic because it demonstrates deep understanding of the API's generic design.

```kotlin
import android.app.Activity
import android.content.Context
import android.content.Intent
import androidx.activity.result.contract.ActivityResultContract

class PickSensorContract : ActivityResultContract<String, SensorResult?>() {

    // Step 2a — Create the intent that will start the Callee
    override fun createIntent(context: Context, input: String): Intent {
        return Intent(context, SensorPickerActivity::class.java).apply {
            putExtra("EXTRA_SENSOR_CATEGORY", input)
        }
    }

    // Step 2b — Parse the result Intent into our typed output
    override fun parseResult(resultCode: Int, intent: Intent?): SensorResult? {
        // Defensive check: handle cancellation explicitly
        if (resultCode != Activity.RESULT_OK) return null
        intent ?: return null

        return SensorResult(
            sensorId      = intent.getStringExtra("EXTRA_SENSOR_ID") ?: return null,
            sensorType    = intent.getStringExtra("EXTRA_SENSOR_TYPE") ?: "Unknown",
            samplingRate  = intent.getIntExtra("EXTRA_SAMPLE_RATE", 1)
        )
    }
}
```

**Derivation of the parsing logic:**

$$
\text{parseResult}(c, I) =
\begin{cases}
\text{SensorResult}(I[\text{id}], I[\text{type}], I[\text{rate}]) & \text{if } c = -1 \text{ and } I \ne \text{null} \\[4pt]
\text{null} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

The function maps the raw `(code, intent)` pair into the strongly-typed output. This is mathematically equivalent to a **partial function** $f: \mathbb{Z} \times \text{Intent} \rightharpoonup O$ defined only on the subset where the callee returned success.

### Step 3 — Register the Launcher in the Caller Activity

```kotlin
// File: DashboardActivity.kt
import androidx.activity.result.ActivityResultLauncher
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity
import android.os.Bundle
import android.widget.Button
import android.widget.TextView
import android.widget.Toast

class DashboardActivity : AppCompatActivity() {

    // Step 3a — Declare the launcher as a class-level field.
    // It MUST be registered before STARTED state.
    private lateinit var sensorLauncher: ActivityResultLauncher<String>

    override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_dashboard)

        val txtResult: TextView = findViewById(R.id.txt_result)
        val btnPick: Button     = findViewById(R.id.btn_pick_sensor)

        // Step 3b — Register the launcher with a callback
        sensorLauncher = registerForActivityResult(PickSensorContract()) { result ->
            // This lambda fires on the MAIN THREAD when callee finishes
            result?.let {
                txtResult.text = """
                    Sensor Selected:
                    ID   : ${it.sensorId}
                    Type : ${it.sensorType}
                    Rate : ${it.samplingRate} Hz
                """.trimIndent()

                // In a real IoT app, you would now call your MQTT/HTTP layer here
                publishToBroker(it)
            } ?: run {
                Toast.makeText(this, "No sensor selected", Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show()
            }
        }

        btnPick.setOnClickListener {
            // Step 3c — Launch with an input (the sensor category)
            sensorLauncher.launch("ENVIRONMENTAL")
        }
    }

    private fun publishToBroker(s: SensorResult) {
        // IoT publish logic — pseudo-code for KTU exam:
        // mqttClient.publish("home/sensors/${s.sensorId}", s.samplingRate.toString())
    }
}
```

### Step 4 — Implement the Callee (SensorPickerActivity)

```kotlin
// File: SensorPickerActivity.kt
import android.app.Activity
import android.content.Intent
import android.os.Bundle
import android.widget.Button
import android.widget.EditText

class SensorPickerActivity : AppCompatActivity() {

    override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_sensor_picker)

        val inputCategory = intent.getStringExtra("EXTRA_SENSOR_CATEGORY")
        val edtId    : EditText = findViewById(R.id.edt_sensor_id)
        val edtType  : EditText = findViewById(R.id.edt_sensor_type)
        val edtRate  : EditText = findViewById(R.id.edt_sample_rate)
        val btnDone  : Button   = findViewById(R.id.btn_done)

        btnDone.setOnClickListener {
            // Step 4a — Build the result Intent
            val resultIntent = Intent().apply {
                putExtra("EXTRA_SENSOR_ID",    edtId.text.toString())
                putExtra("EXTRA_SENSOR_TYPE",  edtType.text.toString())
                putExtra("EXTRA_SAMPLE_RATE",  edtRate.text.toString().toIntOrNull() ?: 1)
            }

            // Step 4b — Set the result code and data
            setResult(Activity.RESULT_OK, resultIntent)

            // Step 4c — Finish to trigger the callback
            finish()
        }
    }

    // Override back press to set RESULT_CANCELED explicitly
    @Suppress("MissingSuperCall")
    override fun onBackPressed() {
        setResult(Activity.RESULT_CANCELED)
        super.onBackPressed()
    }
}
```

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Modern vs Legacy API

| Aspect | Legacy (`startActivityForResult`) | Modern (`registerForActivityResult`) |
|---|---|---|
| Method signature | `startActivityForResult(intent, requestCode)` | `launcher.launch(input)` |
| Result handling | `override fun onActivityResult(req, res, data)` (single giant switch) | Per-launcher typed callback |
| Type safety | ❌ Manual casting | ✅ Generic contract $I \to O$ |
| Lifecycle safety | ❌ Risk of leaks if Activity recreated | ✅ Auto-unregistered with lifecycle |
| Request codes | ❌ Magic integers (error-prone) | ✅ Not required — each launcher is unique |
| KTU 2024 Recommendation | ❌ Deprecated | ✅ **Mandatory** |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step State Transition

$$
\text{Caller}_{CREATED} \xrightarrow{\text{register}} \text{Caller}_{STARTED} \xrightarrow{\text{launch}} \text{Callee}_{CREATED}
$$

$$
\text{Callee}_{STARTED} \xrightarrow{\text{setResult + finish}} \text{Caller}_{RESUMED, \, \text{callback fires}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The `STARTED` → `RESUMED` transition is the **only** moment the callback may fire. If the caller is in the background (process not running), the result is delivered when the process is restored — Android persists the result code and data in the framework.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sequence Diagram — Complete Activity Result Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor User as User
    participant Caller as DashboardActivity
    participant Launcher as ActivityResultLauncher
    participant Framework as Android Framework
    participant Callee as SensorPickerActivity
    participant CB as Result Callback

    User->>Caller: Launch App
    Caller->>Launcher: registerForActivityResult(contract, callback)
    Note over Launcher: Bound to Caller lifecycle
    User->>Caller: Tap "Pick Sensor"
    Caller->>Launcher: launcher.launch("ENVIRONMENTAL")
    Launcher->>Framework: startActivity(intent)
    Framework->>Callee: onCreate / onStart / onResume
    User->>Callee: Fill form, tap "Done"
    Callee->>Callee: setResult(RESULT_OK, resultIntent)
    Callee->>Framework: finish()
    Framework->>Caller: onActivityResult(resultCode, data)
    Framework->>CB: invoke callback
    CB->>Caller: Update UI / publish to MQTT broker
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CallerLayer["CALLER ACTIVITY (DashboardActivity)"]
        A1["UI Button: Pick Sensor"]
        A2["ActivityResultLauncher field"]
        A3["Callback Lambda onResult"]
        A4["UI TextView: Result"]
    end

    subgraph FrameworkLayer["ANDROID FRAMEWORK"]
        F1["Intent Serialization"]
        F2["Back Stack Manager"]
        F3["Result Delivery Service"]
    end

    subgraph CalleeLayer["CALLEE ACTIVITY (SensorPickerActivity)"]
        C1["Read EXTRA_SENSOR_CATEGORY"]
        C2["UI Form Fields"]
        C3["setResult + finish"]
    end

    A1 -->|click| A2
    A2 -->|launch intent| F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 -->|user submits| C3
    C3 -->|setResult OK| F3
    F3 -->|delivers| A3
    A3 --> A4
    A3 -.RESULT_CANCELED.-> A4
```

## 4.3 Class Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class ActivityResultContract~I,O~ {
        <<abstract>>
        +createIntent(context, input I) Intent
        +parseResult(resultCode, intent) O
    }

    class PickSensorContract {
        +createIntent(ctx, category) Intent
        +parseResult(code, intent) SensorResult?
    }

    class ActivityResultLauncher~I~ {
        +launch(input I) void
    }

    class DashboardActivity {
        -sensorLauncher ActivityResultLauncher~String~
        +onCreate() void
    }

    class SensorPickerActivity {
        +onCreate() void
        +onBackPressed() void
    }

    ActivityResultContract <|-- PickSensorContract
    PickSensorContract ..> ActivityResultLauncher : produces
    DashboardActivity --> ActivityResultLauncher : registers
    DashboardActivity ..> SensorPickerActivity : launches via Intent
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between the legacy `startActivityForResult()` approach and the modern `registerForActivityResult()` approach for getting results from an Activity. Highlight two advantages of the modern approach.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `startActivityForResult()` | `registerForActivityResult()` |
|---|---|---|
| Introduced in | API 1 (2008) | AndroidX 1.2 (2019) |
| Result handling | Single `onActivityResult()` switch | Dedicated per-launcher callback |
| Type safety | Manual casting | Generic, type-safe via Contract |
| Lifecycle | Risk of leaks | Auto-unregistered |
| Request codes | Magic integers | Not required |

**Two advantages of the modern approach:**
1. **Type safety** through generic `ActivityResultContract<I, O>` — eliminates manual casting and `ClassCastException` at runtime.
2. **Lifecycle awareness** — the launcher is automatically unregistered when the host Activity is destroyed, preventing memory leaks and stale callbacks.

*(Marks: 1 for table/differentiation, 1 each for two advantages = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Explain the role of `ActivityResultContract` in the modern Activity Result API. List any two built-in contracts provided by AndroidX.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`ActivityResultContract<I, O>` is a **type-safe, reusable agreement** that defines two things:
- The **input type `I`** required to start an activity (often an `Intent`).
- The **output type `O`** produced by the callee (e.g., a `Uri`, `Boolean`, or a custom POJO).

It exposes two abstract methods:
- `createIntent(context, input): Intent`
- `parseResult(resultCode, intent): O`

**Two built-in AndroidX contracts:**
1. `StartActivityForResult` — generic Intent-based contract returning raw `(resultCode, intent)`.
2. `RequestPermission` — returns `Boolean` indicating whether a runtime permission was granted.

*(Marks: 2 for explanation of role, 1 for two built-in contracts = 3 marks)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam Model – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the **modern Activity Result API** in Android. Describe the roles of `ActivityResultContract`, `ActivityResultLauncher`, and the **callback** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the complete Kotlin code for an IoT application where **Activity A (DashboardActivity)** launches **Activity B (DeviceConfigActivity)** to configure a smart bulb. Activity B should return: bulb name (String), brightness level (Integer), and a Boolean indicating if the bulb is on/off. The callback in Activity A should display a toast message on success and a different toast on cancellation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

The modern Activity Result API consists of **three core components** working in tandem:

1. **`ActivityResultContract<I, O>`** — A generic, abstract class parameterized by an **input type `I`** and an **output type `O`**. It encapsulates the rules for creating the `Intent` that starts the callee and for parsing the result `Intent` returned by the callee. Example: `StartActivityForResult()`, `RequestPermission()`.

2. **`ActivityResultLauncher<I>`** — A handle obtained by calling `registerForActivityResult(contract, callback)`. It exposes a single method `launch(input: I)` to start the callee. It is **lifecycle-aware** — Android automatically unregisters it when the host Activity is destroyed.

3. **The Callback** — A lambda or `ActivityResultCallback<O>` executed on the **main thread** when the callee finishes. It receives the parsed output of type `O` and contains the business logic to handle success or cancellation.

**Block Diagram (drawn by student, 2 marks):**

```
+--------------------+        launch(input)        +------------------+
|  DashboardActivity | ----------------------->    | DeviceConfig     |
|  (Caller)          |                            |  Activity        |
|                    | <-----------------------   |  (Callee)        |
|   Launcher<Input>  |     callback(output)       |                  |
+--------------------+                            +------------------+
        |                                                   |
        v                                                   v
   ActivityResultContract<I,O>                  setResult(RESULT_OK, data)
```

*(Marks distribution: 1 for diagram, 2 each for the three components = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Activity A — DashboardActivity.kt**

```kotlin
import android.app.Activity
import android.content.Intent
import android.os.Bundle
import android.widget.Button
import android.widget.Toast
import androidx.activity.result.ActivityResultLauncher
import androidx.activity.result.contract.ActivityResultContract
import androidx.appcompat.app.AppCompatActivity

data class BulbConfig(val name: String, val brightness: Int, val isOn: Boolean)

// (i) Custom Contract [2 marks]
class PickBulbContract : ActivityResultContract<String, BulbConfig?>() {
    override fun createIntent(ctx: android.content.Context, input: String): Intent {
        return Intent(ctx, DeviceConfigActivity::class.java).apply {
            putExtra("EXTRA_ROOM", input)
        }
    }
    override fun parseResult(resultCode: Int, intent: Intent?): BulbConfig? {
        if (resultCode != Activity.RESULT_OK || intent == null) return null
        return BulbConfig(
            name       = intent.getStringExtra("EXTRA_BULB_NAME") ?: return null,
            brightness = intent.getIntExtra("EXTRA_BRIGHTNESS", 0),
            isOn       = intent.getBooleanExtra("EXTRA_IS_ON", false)
        )
    }
}

class DashboardActivity : AppCompatActivity() {
    // (ii) Register the launcher [2 marks]
    private lateinit var bulbLauncher: ActivityResultLauncher<String>

    override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_dashboard)

        bulbLauncher = registerForActivityResult(PickBulbContract()) { result ->
            // (iii) Callback with success / cancel handling [2 marks]
            if (result != null) {
                Toast.makeText(this,
                    "Bulb '${result.name}' configured at ${result.brightness}%",
                    Toast.LENGTH_LONG).show()
            } else {
                Toast.makeText(this, "Configuration cancelled", Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show()
            }
        }

        findViewById<Button>(R.id.btn_configure).setOnClickListener {
            bulbLauncher.launch("Living Room")
        }
    }
}
```

**Activity B — DeviceConfigActivity.kt** *(key snippet — 1 mark)*

```kotlin
btnSave.setOnClickListener {
    val result = Intent().apply {
        putExtra("EXTRA_BULB_NAME", edtName.text.toString())
        putExtra("EXTRA_BRIGHTNESS", seekBar.progress)
        putExtra("EXTRA_IS_ON", switchOn.isChecked)
    }
    setResult(Activity.RESULT_OK, result)
    finish()
}
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam Model – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Intent result codes** in Android. Discuss the significance of `RESULT_OK`, `RESULT_CANCELED`, and custom result codes with an IoT use-case where a Device Pairing Activity returns one of three outcomes: paired successfully, pairing failed, or pairing cancelled. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **legacy Activity result flow** with the **modern AndroidX Activity Result API**. Provide a code snippet showing how the legacy `onActivityResult()` was used and explain why it was deprecated. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

An **Intent result code** is an `Integer` constant passed to `setResult(code, data)` inside the callee Activity. The Android framework bundles this code with the result `Intent` and delivers it to the caller.

**Standard result codes:**

- `RESULT_OK (-1)` — Operation succeeded. The caller should proceed with the returned data.
- `RESULT_CANCELED (0)` — Operation was aborted or user pressed back. The caller should typically abort its own operation.
- `RESULT_FIRST_USER (1)` — The lower bound for custom result codes. Custom codes start from `RESULT_FIRST_USER + 1`.

**IoT use-case — Device Pairing Activity returning three outcomes:**

| Result Code | Value | Meaning | IoT Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| `RESULT_OK` | -1 | Paired successfully | Save MAC to local DB, show success toast |
| `RESULT_PAIRING_FAILED` = `RESULT_FIRST_USER + 1` | 2 | Pairing failed (timeout/auth) | Show retry dialog |
| `RESULT_CANCELED` | 0 | User pressed back | Dismiss pairing screen silently |

**Code snippet inside the Pairing Activity:**

```kotlin
// Success
setResult(Activity.RESULT_OK, resultIntent); finish()

// Failure
val fail = Intent().putExtra("EXTRA_ERROR_MSG", "AUTH_TIMEOUT")
setResult(2, fail)  // RESULT_FIRST_USER + 1
finish()

// Cancellation
setResult(Activity.RESULT_CANCELED); finish()
```

In the **modern API**, the callback receives a typed object. The contract's `parseResult()` can decode custom codes and convert them into a sealed class or enum for type-safe handling.

*(Marks: 2 for standard codes explanation, 2 for IoT use-case table, 2 for code snippet, 1 for modern-API integration = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Legacy Flow (Deprecated):**

```kotlin
// In Caller
companion object {
    const val REQ_PICK_SENSOR = 1001
}

override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
    val intent = Intent(this, SensorPickerActivity::class.java)
    startActivityForResult(intent, REQ_PICK_SENSOR)
}

override fun onActivityResult(requestCode: Int, resultCode: Int, data: Intent?) {
    super.onActivityResult(requestCode, resultCode, data)
    if (requestCode == REQ_PICK_SENSOR) {
        if (resultCode == RESULT_OK) {
            val sensorId = data?.getStringExtra("EXTRA_SENSOR_ID")
            // handle result
        }
    }
}
```

**Why deprecated:**
1. **Manual `requestCode` management** — error-prone; conflicts cause silent bugs.
2. **Single monolithic callback** — `onActivityResult()` must use `if/else` or `switch` over request codes, leading to spaghetti code.
3. **Not lifecycle-safe** — if the Activity is recreated, the `Intent` and request code may be lost.
4. **No type safety** — requires manual casting of extras.

**Modern Flow (AndroidX):**

```kotlin
private val launcher = registerForActivityResult(StartActivityForResult()) { result ->
    if (result.resultCode == RESULT_OK) {
        val id = result.data?.getStringExtra("EXTRA_SENSOR_ID")
    }
}
// Later:
launcher.launch(intent)
```

**Advantages:** Type-safe, lifecycle-aware, no request codes, cleaner code.

*(Marks: 2 for legacy code, 2 for deprecation reasons, 2 for modern code & comparison, 1 for concluding remark = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Registering the launcher in `onClick()`** — Throws `IllegalStateException`. The launcher MUST be registered in the field declaration or inside `onCreate()`. **(Loses 2–3 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting to call `finish()`** after `setResult()` — The callback will NEVER fire. **(Loses 3 marks)**
> 3. **Assuming `parseResult()` handles cancellation** — It does not magically filter; you must check `resultCode` inside `parseResult()`. **(Loses 2 marks)**
> 4. **Using `startActivityForResult()` in 2024 scheme answers** — At most 50% marks awarded, even if code is correct. **(Loses up to 7 marks)**
> 5. **Returning `null` from `parseResult()` for `RESULT_CANCELED`** — This is the **correct** behavior, not an error. Many students incorrectly throw an exception.
> 6. **Confusing `RESULT_OK (-1)` with HTTP 200** — These are unrelated; the value is a magic number for the Android framework.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- ✅ The **Activity Result API** enables a Caller Activity to launch a Callee Activity and receive a structured result back.
- ✅ The **modern API** uses `registerForActivityResult(contract, callback)` and `launcher.launch(input)`.
- ✅ The **legacy API** uses `startActivityForResult()` + `onActivityResult()` — **deprecated**, do not use in 2024 scheme.
- ✅ An `ActivityResultContract<I, O>` is a generic, type-safe bridge defining input and output types.
- ✅ The `ActivityResultLauncher` is **lifecycle-aware** and must be registered **before** the Activity reaches `STARTED`.
- ✅ The callback fires on the **main thread** exactly once when the callee finishes.
- ✅ `setResult(code, intent)` is followed by `finish()` inside the callee.
- ✅ Standard codes: `RESULT_OK = -1`, `RESULT_CANCELED = 0`, custom codes start at `RESULT_FIRST_USER + 1`.
- ✅ Always handle **both** `RESULT_OK` and `RESULT_CANCELED` in `parseResult()` to avoid `NullPointerException`.
- ✅ Built-in contracts include `StartActivityForResult`, `GetContent`, `RequestPermission`, `TakePicture`.
- ✅ For IoT apps, the callback is the ideal place to **publish to MQTT broker, update local DB, or trigger HTTP API** with the returned data.
- ✅ Custom contracts are **strongly recommended** for complex IoT payloads (e.g., `BulbConfig`, `SensorResult` POJOs) — type safety at compile time.
- ✅ Common exam trick: distinguishing between `Activity.RESULT_OK` (constant) and the magic integer `-1`.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for code values:** "OK is negative one, Cancel is zero, Custom starts at one" → `-1, 0, 1+`.
- ✅ Never put the launcher registration inside a `setOnClickListener` — the AndroidX API enforces lifecycle binding at registration time.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Broadcast Receivers - Types of Broadcast Receivers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Broadcast Receivers - Types of Broadcast Receivers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Broadcast Receiver** in the Android Operating System is one of the four fundamental application components (alongside Activities, Services, and Content Providers) that enables an application to *passively listen* for and respond to **system-wide or application-specific broadcast messages** called **Intents**. These broadcasts can originate from the Android Operating System, other applications, or the same application itself.

In the context of **IoT (Internet of Things)** and the KTU 2024 syllabus, Broadcast Receivers act as the **primary reactive trigger mechanism** for IoT applications. They allow mobile applications to respond asynchronously to events such as device sensor state changes (e.g., Bluetooth pairing, Wi-Fi network availability, battery state, location changes, NFC tag detection) without continuously polling the system, thereby preserving battery life and computational resources — a critical concern in IoT deployments.

The fundamental components of a broadcast are:
- **Intent Object**: A message envelope containing the action, data, and category.
- **IntentFilter**: A declarative rule-set that defines which intents a receiver should accept.
- **Action String**: A unique identifier (e.g., `android.intent.action.BOOT_COMPLETED`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Broadcast Receiver **does NOT have a user interface (UI)**. It is a "headless" component that runs in the background for a short duration (typically **~10 seconds**) to process the received intent and trigger the appropriate response, such as starting a Service, displaying a Notification, or updating the UI via a callback.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **newspaper subscription system**:

| Newspaper Concept | Broadcast Receiver Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Newspaper Publisher | Android System / Other App sending the Broadcast Intent |
| Newspaper Headline (Topic) | Action String of the Intent (e.g., `ACTION_AIRPLANE_MODE_CHANGED`) |
| Your Mailing Address | IntentFilter registered by your Receiver |
| The Postman | The Android Framework's Intent Dispatcher |
| You (reading the news) | The `onReceive()` callback method |
| The Mailbox | The BroadcastReceiver Component |

**How it works:** You subscribe to a newspaper (declare an **IntentFilter**). Whenever the publisher prints a relevant headline (system fires a **Broadcast Intent**), the postman (Android system) delivers it to your mailbox (your **BroadcastReceiver**). You then briefly read it in `onReceive()` and decide what action to take (start a service, show a notification).

### 1.3 Core Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Receiver Execution Limit:** **~10 seconds** (Strictly enforced by Android since 3.1 Honeycomb; ANR if exceeded).
- **Sticky Broadcast Retention:** Until explicitly removed via `removeStickyBroadcast()` (Mostly deprecated since API 21/Lollipop).
- **Implicit Broadcast Restrictions (API 26+):** Most implicit system broadcasts **cannot** be registered in the manifest on Android 8.0 (Oreo) and above; runtime/Context registration is mandatory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Broadcast Receivers matter in IoT:** In IoT, devices frequently change state (sensor data arrival, network reachability, MQTT connection loss). Broadcast Receivers allow the IoT app to react to these events without keeping the CPU awake, which is essential for low-power IoT edge devices and mobile gateways.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy of a Broadcast Receiver

A broadcast receiver operates on a **declarative registration** followed by an **event-driven callback** model. The lifecycle consists of the following structured logic steps:

1. **Registration Phase:** The receiver is registered either **statically** (in `AndroidManifest.xml` via `<receiver>` tag with `<intent-filter>`) or **dynamically** (at runtime via `Context.registerReceiver()`).
2. **Broadcast Emission Phase:** A sender calls `sendBroadcast()`, `sendOrderedBroadcast()`, or `sendStickyBroadcast()` with an Intent object.
3. **Intent Filtering Phase:** The Android framework compares the intent's Action, Data, and Category against all registered IntentFilters across the system.
4. **Dispatch Phase:** The framework instantiates the matching BroadcastReceiver class (if not alive) and invokes its `onReceive(Context context, Intent intent)` method on the **main thread (UI thread)**.
5. **Response Phase:** The receiver executes its logic. Since it runs on the main thread, heavy computation should be delegated to a `Service`, `WorkManager`, or `JobScheduler`.
6. **Unregistration Phase:** For dynamic receivers, `unregisterReceiver()` is called when the parent component (Activity/Fragment) is destroyed to prevent memory leaks.

> [!TIP]
> **The 'Why' Behind the 'How':** Why does Android restrict receivers to ~10 seconds? Because they hold a reference to the Activity context (often invisible) and a wake lock implicitly. Long-running tasks would drain battery and cause ANRs, defeating the purpose of an event-driven architecture.

## 2.2 High-Yield Classification Matrix (Cheat Sheet)

The KTU 2024 Scheme focuses heavily on the **classification of Broadcast Receivers**. Below is the comprehensive taxonomy that students must memorize.

### Table 1: Classification by **Registration Mechanism**

| Registration Type | Declaration Location | Lifecycle Scope | IoT Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Static / Manifest-Declared** | `AndroidManifest.xml` | Survives Activity destruction; app-level scope | Listening for `BOOT_COMPLETED` to auto-start IoT sensor services |
| **Dynamic / Context-Registered** | Java/Kotlin code via `registerReceiver()` | Tied to Activity/Fragment lifecycle | Updating a real-time IoT dashboard when Wi-Fi state changes |

### Table 2: Classification by **Broadcast Type**

| Broadcast Type | Delivery Order | Can Receiver Abort? | Modern Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Normal Broadcast** | Unordered, asynchronous to all receivers | No | Generic event notifications |
| **Ordered Broadcast** | Sequential, one at a time, priority-based | Yes (via `abortBroadcast()`) | SMS app intercepting, custom event chains |
| **Sticky Broadcast** | Retained after sending (legacy) | N/A | Deprecated — do not use in new apps |
| **Local Broadcast** | Restricted to the app's own process | No | Secure in-app component communication |

### Table 3: Classification by **Intent Visibility**

| Intent Type | Target Specification | Security Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Implicit Broadcast** | Action-based (e.g., `ACTION_VIEW`) | Any app with matching filter can receive; lower security |
| **Explicit Broadcast** | ComponentName-based (targeted) | Directly delivered to one specific app; higher security |

## 2.3 Detailed Type Analysis for KTU Board Exams

### Type 1: Static (Manifest-Declared) Broadcast Receiver
- **How:** Declared in `AndroidManifest.xml` with the `<receiver>` tag.
- **Key Feature:** Activated even when the app is **not running** (cold start). The system spawns the app process to deliver the broadcast.
- **IoT Application:** Restarting an MQTT client automatically when the IoT gateway device reboots, by listening to `android.intent.action.BOOT_COMPLETED`.

### Type 2: Dynamic (Context-Registered) Broadcast Receiver
- **How:** Registered programmatically using `registerReceiver(receiver, intentFilter)` on a Context (Activity, Service, Application).
- **Key Feature:** Active **only** while the registering component is alive. Must be paired with `unregisterReceiver()` in `onDestroy()` or `onPause()`.
- **IoT Application:** A live IoT monitoring activity that listens to `BluetoothDevice.ACTION_FOUND` to discover nearby BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy) sensors only while the scanning screen is visible.

### Type 3: Normal Broadcast
- Sent via `sendBroadcast(intent)`. Delivered to **all** matching receivers in **unspecified, asynchronous order**. Most efficient. Cannot be aborted.

### Type 4: Ordered Broadcast
- Sent via `sendOrderedBroadcast(intent, receiverPermission)`. Delivered **one at a time** to receivers in descending order of `android:priority` attribute.
- Higher-priority receivers can call `abortBroadcast()` to prevent lower-priority ones from receiving it.
- **IoT Application:** A priority-based alert cascade in an industrial IoT system where the primary controller receives a sensor alert first and decides whether to propagate it to the secondary logging app.

### Type 5: Local Broadcast
- Implemented using `LocalBroadcastManager` (deprecated but conceptually taught) or `androidx.localbroadcastmanager`.
- Broadcasts are **confined to the process** of the application. They cannot leak to other apps, providing inherent security.
- **No IPC overhead**, hence faster.

### Type 6: Sticky Broadcast
- Legacy type that **remained accessible** to future registrants via `registerReceiver()` even after the original broadcast was sent.
- **Deprecated in API 21+** due to security risks. Should not appear in KTU 2024 answers as a recommended approach, only historically.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Smart Home IoT Gateways:** Receivers listen for `CONNECTIVITY_CHANGE` to re-establish MQTT/HTTP connections when the home Wi-Fi flaps.
- **Wearable Health Devices:** `BATTERY_LOW` and `BATTERY_CHANGED` broadcasts trigger low-power mode logic in wearable companion apps.
- **Industrial Automation:** Custom ordered broadcasts propagate emergency shutdown signals through a priority chain of control apps.
- **Geofencing & Location Services:** `LOCATION_CHANGED` and `PROXIMITY_ALERT` broadcasts trigger IoT actions (e.g., unlocking a smart lock when the user approaches).

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Implementation Flow

Since Broadcast Receivers involve **system architecture and event flow logic** rather than closed-form mathematical equations, the "derivation" in this section represents the **exhaustive logical flow of broadcast dispatch**, the **step-by-step evaluation of receiver priority**, and the **derivation of the broadcast decision matrix**.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Broadcast Dispatch Logic

The Android framework's broadcast dispatch follows a deterministic algorithm. Let $B$ be the set of all registered receivers matching intent $I$, and $P(r)$ be the priority of receiver $r \in B$.

### Normal Broadcast Algorithm

For a **Normal Broadcast**, the system delivers the intent to all matching receivers asynchronously:

$$
\forall r \in B : \text{dispatch}(I, r) \text{ in parallel}
$$

There is no ordering guarantee. The set of receivers $B$ is defined as:

$$
B = \{ r \mid \text{match}(I, \text{IntentFilter}(r)) = \text{true} \}
$$

where the match function evaluates **Action**, **Data** (URI + MIME), and **Category** against the filter rules.

### Ordered Broadcast Algorithm

For an **Ordered Broadcast**, dispatch follows a sorted sequence. Let $B_{\text{ordered}}$ be the list of matching receivers sorted by priority in descending order:

$$
B_{\text{ordered}} = \text{sort}(B, \text{descending}, P)
$$

The dispatch sequence is:

$$
\forall r_i \in B_{\text{ordered}} : \text{dispatch}(I, r_i) \text{ sequentially}
$$

The key check inside `onReceive()` is:

$$
\text{if } \text{getResultCode}() == \text{INITIAL\_CODE\_KNOWN} \text{ then continue}
$$

A receiver can call `abortBroadcast()` which sets an internal flag $A$:

$$
A = \text{true} \implies \forall r_j \text{ where } P(r_j) < P(r_i) : \text{skip}(r_j)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Explanation of the Transition:** The first equation says "every receiver $r$ in the set $B$ is dispatched to in parallel" — this means normal broadcasts are fire-and-forget. The second equation derives the sorted list of receivers based on the priority function $P$. The third equation is the sequential loop. The final equation is the **abortion logic** — once a higher-priority receiver aborts, all lower-priority receivers are skipped, which is the core differentiator of ordered broadcasts.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Implementation Flow (Derivation of Receiver Activation)

The following exhaustive trace shows **how** a Broadcast Receiver gets invoked end-to-end. Each step must be understood for KTU long-answer questions.

### Static Receiver Activation (e.g., BOOT_COMPLETED)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \text{Device powers on} \rightarrow \text{Kernel boots Android} \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{PackageManager scans } \text{AndroidManifest.xml} \text{ of all installed apps} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{For each } \langle \text{receiver} \rangle \text{ tag with } \langle \text{intent-filter} \rangle, \text{ register } (r, F) \text{ in system registry } R \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{Android fires Intent with action } \text{BOOT\_COMPLETED} \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{ActivityManagerService queries } R : B = \{ r \mid \text{match}(I_{\text{boot}}, F_r) = \text{true} \} \\
\text{Step 6: } & \text{If } B \neq \emptyset, \text{ start the app's process via } \text{startProcessLocked()} \\
\text{Step 7: } & \text{Instantiate receiver class via } \text{Class.forName}(r.\text{className}).\text{newInstance}() \\
\text{Step 8: } & \text{Invoke } r.\text{onReceive}(\text{context}, I_{\text{boot}}) \text{ on the main thread} \\
\text{Step 9: } & \text{Receiver returns; process may be killed if no other components are active}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Dynamic Receiver Activation (e.g., Bluetooth scan in Activity)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \text{Activity enters } \text{onResume()} \text{ state} \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{Create } I_{bt} = \text{IntentFilter}(\text{BluetoothDevice.ACTION\_FOUND}) \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{Create receiver object: } r = \text{new } \text{MyBtReceiver}() \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{Register: } \text{registerReceiver}(r, I_{bt}) \rightarrow \text{appends to system registry } R \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{System fires } \text{ACTION\_FOUND} \text{ intent when a BLE device advertises} \\
\text{Step 6: } & \text{ActivityManager matches } r \text{ via } I_{bt} \rightarrow \text{calls } r.\text{onReceive}() \\
\text{Step 7: } & \text{Activity moves to } \text{onPause()} \text{ or } \text{onStop()} \\
\text{Step 8: } & \text{Mandatory call: } \text{unregisterReceiver}(r) \rightarrow \text{removes } r \text{ from } R
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall (will be re-emphasized in Section 5):** Skipping Step 8 causes a **memory leak** and `IllegalArgumentException` on subsequent registrations. This is the #1 deduction point in KTU practical exams.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Receiver Type to Choose?"

Students are often asked to *justify* the choice of receiver type. The following decision tree is the analytical "derivation" expected in 14-mark answers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decision}(t) = \begin{cases} \text{Static Manifest} & \text{if } (\text{lifecycle required} = \text{app-wide}) \land (\text{app may be killed}) \\ \text{Dynamic Context} & \text{if } (\text{lifecycle required} = \text{UI-scoped}) \land (\text{activity is foreground}) \\ \text{Ordered} & \text{if } (\text{abort required} = \text{true}) \lor (\text{priority chain required} = \text{true}) \\ \text{Local} & \text{if } (\text{security required} = \text{high}) \land (\text{receiver} \in \text{same process}) \\ \text{Explicit} & \text{if } (\text{target} = \text{single known component}) \\ \text{Implicit} & \text{if } (\text{target} = \text{any matching app}) \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

This analytical matrix directly answers the frequently-asked KTU question: *"Compare Static and Dynamic Broadcast Receivers"* and *"When would you use Ordered vs Normal Broadcasts?"*

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Broadcast Receiver Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sender App / Android System] -->|sendBroadcast Intent| B[ActivityManagerService]
    B --> C{Resolve Matching Receivers}
    C -->|Match Found| D[Instantiate Receiver]
    C -->|No Match| E[Broadcast Discarded]
    D --> F[onReceive on Main Thread]
    F --> G{Receiver Logic}
    G -->|Lightweight Task| H[Show Notification]
    G -->|Heavy Task| I[Delegate to Service or WorkManager]
    G -->|Data Update| J[Update UI via Handler]
    H --> K[Process May Be Killed]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    F --> L{Timer Check}
    L -->|Under 10 sec| M[Receiver Returns]
    L -->|Over 10 sec| N[ANR Application Not Responding]
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Broadcast Receivers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BR[Broadcast Receivers]
    BR --> R1[By Registration]
    BR --> R2[By Delivery Order]
    BR --> R3[By Visibility]
    BR --> R4[By Scope]

    R1 --> S[Static Manifest Declared]
    R1 --> D[Dynamic Context Registered]

    R2 --> NB[Normal Broadcast]
    R2 --> OB[Ordered Broadcast]
    R2 --> SB[Sticky Broadcast Deprecated]

    R3 --> IM[Implicit Broadcast]
    R3 --> EX[Explicit Broadcast]

    R4 --> LO[Local Broadcast Same Process]
    LO --> GM[Global Broadcast Cross Process]
```

## 4.3 Static vs Dynamic Receiver Registration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StaticFlow[Static Registration]
        S1[AndroidManifest.xml] --> S2[receiver tag with intent-filter]
        S2 --> S3[App Cold Start Eligible]
        S3 --> S4[onReceive invoked]
    end

    subgraph DynamicFlow[Dynamic Registration]
        D1[Activity onResume] --> D2[registerReceiver call]
        D2 --> D3[System Registry Updated]
        D3 --> D4[onReceive invoked]
        D4 --> D5[Activity onPause]
        D5 --> D6[unregisterReceiver call]
    end
```

## 4.4 Ordered Broadcast Priority Chain (IoT Alert Cascade)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensor Threshold Breach] --> B[sendOrderedBroadcast]
    B --> C1[Receiver Priority 100: Primary Controller]
    C1 --> Q1{Abort?}
    Q1 -->|No| C2[Receiver Priority 50: Logger App]
    Q1 -->|Yes| STOP[Chain Halted]
    C2 --> Q2{Abort?}
    Q2 -->|No| C3[Receiver Priority 10: User Notification]
    Q2 -->|Yes| STOP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** In the above flow, an IoT temperature sensor breaches a threshold. The Primary Controller (highest priority, $P = 100$) receives the broadcast first. If the situation is critical, it calls `abortBroadcast()`, preventing the Logger and Notification apps from processing it (perhaps to avoid spam during a known shutdown). If not aborted, the chain proceeds in descending priority order.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Broadcast Receiver in Android. List any two system broadcasts relevant to IoT applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Broadcast Receiver is an Android application component that listens for and responds to system-wide or application-specific broadcast messages (Intents). It has no UI and operates in the background for a short duration.

**Two IoT-relevant system broadcasts:**
1. `android.intent.action.BOOT_COMPLETED` — Triggered when the device finishes booting; used to auto-start IoT gateway services.
2. `android.net.wifi.STATE_CHANGE` or `CONNECTIVITY_CHANGE` — Triggered when Wi-Fi state changes; used to re-establish MQTT connections in smart-home apps.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two broadcasts.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Normal and Ordered Broadcasts in Android.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Normal Broadcast | Ordered Broadcast |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Delivery Order | Asynchronous, unordered | Synchronous, priority-based |
| Abort Capability | Cannot be aborted by receivers | Higher-priority receivers can call `abortBroadcast()` |
| API Method | `sendBroadcast()` | `sendOrderedBroadcast()` |
| Use Case | Independent notifications | Priority chains like SMS interception |

*(Award 1 mark each for any 3 valid distinguishing points.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following questions.

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the **lifecycle and working of an Android Broadcast Receiver** with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between **Static (Manifest-declared)** and **Dynamic (Context-registered)** receivers in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the different **types of Broadcast Receivers** based on delivery order and intent visibility. Give a real-world **IoT example** for each type. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Definition (1 Mark):** A Broadcast Receiver is a passive Android component that listens for broadcast Intents via its `onReceive()` callback method, executed on the main thread with a strict **~10 second** execution limit.

**Lifecycle Steps (3 Marks):**
1. **Registration:** Declared in manifest (static) or registered via code (dynamic).
2. **Matching:** The ActivityManagerService matches the broadcast Intent against all registered IntentFilters.
3. **Instantiation:** If the app process is dead, it is spawned; the receiver class is instantiated via reflection.
4. **Execution:** `onReceive(Context, Intent)` is called on the main thread.
5. **Cleanup:** Receiver object is released; process may be killed if no other components are active.

**Static vs Dynamic Comparison (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Static (Manifest) | Dynamic (Context) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Declaration | XML-based | Code-based |
| App Running Required? | No (can start cold) | Yes |
| Lifecycle Scope | Application-wide | Component-tied (Activity) |
| Unregistration Needed? | No | Yes (in `onDestroy`/`onPause`) |
| API 26+ Restrictions | Many implicit broadcasts restricted | Still works |
| IoT Example | Auto-start MQTT on `BOOT_COMPLETED` | Live BLE scan updates in foreground Activity |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**By Delivery Order (3.5 Marks):**
- **Normal Broadcast:** `sendBroadcast()`. Asynchronous, all receivers fired in parallel. *IoT Example:* A weather-station app sending sensor readings to multiple logging and analytics components simultaneously.
- **Ordered Broadcast:** `sendOrderedBroadcast()`. Sequential, priority-based, abortable. *IoT Example:* Industrial IoT emergency shutdown signal where the primary safety controller can abort propagation to prevent redundant alarms.
- **Sticky Broadcast:** Legacy, deprecated in API 21+. *Historical IoT Example:* Battery-level sticky broadcasts once used by old IoT apps.

**By Intent Visibility (3.5 Marks):**
- **Implicit Broadcast:** Specifies only an action (e.g., `android.intent.action.SEND`). Any app with a matching filter receives it. *IoT Example:* Sharing sensor data with any installed smart-home app.
- **Explicit Broadcast:** Specifies a `ComponentName`; delivered to exactly one target. *IoT Example:* A proprietary IoT cloud connector app sending a "sync complete" message directly to its own data-cache service.

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**(a)** With the help of a **flow diagram**, explain how a **Static Broadcast Receiver** is triggered by the Android system when the device receives a `BOOT_COMPLETED` event. Discuss the role of the **IntentFilter** in this process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a **Local Broadcast**? Explain its implementation using `LocalBroadcastManager`. Compare it with **Global Broadcasts** in terms of security, performance, and use cases in IoT applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Flow Diagram Description (3 Marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.3 Static Registration Flow)*

1. Device power-on → Kernel boots Android framework.
2. PackageManager parses all `AndroidManifest.xml` files of installed APKs.
3. For every `<receiver>` with `<intent-filter>` containing `<action android:name="android.intent.action.BOOT_COMPLETED"/>`, the system registers it in the global `ActivityManagerService` registry.
4. Upon boot completion, Android creates an Intent with action `BOOT_COMPLETED` and dispatches it.
5. Matching receivers are instantiated and `onReceive()` is invoked.

**Role of IntentFilter (2 Marks):**
The IntentFilter acts as a **declarative whitelist**. It tells the Android system: *"I am interested in Intents with this specific Action (and optionally Data/Category)."* Without an IntentFilter, the receiver would not be triggered. In the `BOOT_COMPLETED` scenario, the filter must include the `<category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT"/>` and the boot action.

**Code-Side Logic (2 Marks):** The receiver in the IoT app overrides `onReceive()`, extracts the boot action via `intent.getAction()`, and delegates the MQTT client reconnection to a `Service` (since direct heavy work in the receiver is forbidden).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Definition (1 Mark):** A Local Broadcast is a broadcast Intent that is **scoped exclusively to the process** of the sending application. It cannot be intercepted or received by other apps on the device.

**Implementation with LocalBroadcastManager (2 Marks):**
- Obtain the singleton instance: `LocalBroadcastManager lbm = LocalBroadcastManager.getInstance(context);`
- Register: `lbm.registerReceiver(receiver, intentFilter);`
- Send: `lbm.sendBroadcast(intent);`
- Unregister: `lbm.unregisterReceiver(receiver);`

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Aspect | Local Broadcast | Global Broadcast |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Scope | Single app process | All apps system-wide |
| Security | High — cannot leak to other apps | Lower — any matching app can receive |
| Performance | Fast (no IPC marshalling) | Slower (cross-process Binder calls) |
| API | `LocalBroadcastManager` | `Context.sendBroadcast()` |
| IoT Use Case | In-app communication between a BLE-scanning service and the UI Activity | Sending IoT sensor data to a third-party dashboard app |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Points (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Forgetting `unregisterReceiver()` in dynamic receivers:** Examiners will deduct **1 to 2 marks** if you do not mention the mandatory unregistration in `onPause()` / `onDestroy()`.
> 2. **Confusing `BroadcastReceiver` with `Service`:** Receivers are short-lived (~10 sec). They cannot run background tasks directly. Delegating work to a `Service` or `WorkManager` is mandatory — failing to mention this costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Stating Sticky Broadcasts as a current best practice:** They are **deprecated since API 21**. Mentioning them as a recommended type will lose marks.
> 4. **Ignoring API 26+ restrictions:** Since Android 8.0 (Oreo), most implicit system broadcasts **cannot** be registered in the manifest. Examiners expect you to know this.
> 5. **Drawing the diagram but not labeling it:** A flowchart without labels on the edges (Action names, method names) gets only **partial marks** (1/3 instead of 3/3).
> 6. **No real-world/IoT examples:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly rewards **application context**. Always tie your answer to a real IoT scenario (smart home, wearable, industrial sensor) to score the full 14 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A BroadcastReceiver is a headless Android component that responds to system-wide or app-specific Intent broadcasts.
- **Core Callback:** `onReceive(Context, Intent)` runs on the main thread, with a strict **~10-second** execution limit.
- **Two Registration Methods:**
  - **Static (Manifest-declared):** Survives app process death; ideal for `BOOT_COMPLETED` and global system events.
  - **Dynamic (Context-registered):** Tied to the lifecycle of the registering component; must be unregistered to prevent memory leaks.
- **Four Types by Delivery Order:**
  - **Normal:** Async, parallel, non-abortable.
  - **Ordered:** Sync, sequential, priority-based, **abortable via `abortBroadcast()`**.
  - **Sticky:** **Deprecated** since API 21.
  - **Local:** App-process-scoped, secure, fast.
- **Two Types by Intent Visibility:**
  - **Implicit:** Action-based, system-wide delivery.
  - **Explicit:** ComponentName-based, targeted delivery.
- **API 26+ (Oreo) Restriction:** Most implicit system broadcasts must be registered at runtime, not in the manifest.
- **IoT Significance:** Receivers enable event-driven, low-power responses to sensor state changes, network reachability, and Bluetooth discoveries — the cornerstone of efficient mobile IoT apps.
- **Security Best Practice:** Prefer **Local Broadcasts** for in-app communication to prevent data leakage to other apps on the device.
- **Memory Safety Mantra:** *Register in `onResume()`, Unregister in `onPause()`* — for any dynamic receiver.

---

### Final Revision Checklist (Print-and-Carry)
- [ ] I can define a Broadcast Receiver and explain its lifecycle.
- [ ] I can draw and label the dispatch flow diagram from sender to `onReceive()`.
- [ ] I can list at least 5 IoT-relevant system broadcasts.
- [ ] I can differentiate Static vs Dynamic, Normal vs Ordered, Implicit vs Explicit, Local vs Global.
- [ ] I know the 10-second execution limit and the ANR consequence.
- [ ] I remember to unregister dynamic receivers.
- [ ] I can map every receiver type to a real IoT use case.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Broadcast Receivers: Types of Broadcast Receivers**

topics.title: Creation of Broadcast Receivers
Database - Introduction to SQLite Database
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Database - Introduction to SQLite Database # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **SQLite** is an *in-process* library that implements a **self-contained, serverless, zero-configuration, transactional** SQL database engine. Unlike most other SQL databases, SQLite does **not** have a separate server process. It reads and writes directly to ordinary disk files. A complete SQL database with multiple tables, indices, triggers, and views is contained in a **single disk file**. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECGT415 – Mobile App Development for IoT**, SQLite is the **default relational database management system (RDBMS)** integrated with the Android Operating System. It is used to store structured persistent data inside an Android application without requiring a network connection or a remote database server. ### Formal Specifications (Industry Standards) - **File Extension:** `.db` or `.sqlite` - **Library Implementation Language:** **ANSI-C** - **License:** **Public Domain** (free for any use, commercial or private) - **Footprint:** Less than **600 KiB** fully configured - **Compliance:** Fully **ACID-compliant** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) - **SQL Standard:** Implements most of **SQL-92** standard > [!NOTE] > **Why SQLite is the #1 Deployed Database in the World:** It is estimated that there are more than **1 trillion (10¹²)** SQLite databases in active use. This includes every Android device, every iPhone, every copy of Mozilla Firefox, and most IoT edge devices. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Filing Cabinet in Your Pocket" Imagine your Android smartphone is a small office. You need to store information (e.g., sensor readings from an IoT device, user login details, product lists). You have two choices: | Option | Real-World Analogy | Limitation | |---|---|---| | **Remote Database (MySQL, Firebase, MongoDB Cloud)** | A *Post Office* — you send letters to a far-away warehouse, and they send data back. | Needs **Internet/Wi-Fi**, slow, uses mobile data. | | **SQLite (Local Embedded Database)** | A *Personal Filing Cabinet* sitting inside the app itself. | Data is **device-local** (not automatically shared). | > [!TIP] > **For an IoT application reading from a DHT11 sensor every minute**, storing the data in SQLite means: even if the device loses Wi-Fi connectivity, the data is **safely written to the phone's local storage** in a single `.db` file located at `/data/data//databases/`. When the network returns, the data can be synced to the cloud. ## 1.3 Why SQLite is Critical for IoT Mobile Apps > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The reason SQLite is emphasized in **PECGT415 (Mobile App Development for IoT)** is because IoT edge devices and mobile apps are often **resource-constrained, intermittently connected, and need local caching**. SQLite is the *de-facto* solution for these scenarios. The **core benefits** of SQLite for mobile-IoT applications are: - **Zero Configuration:** No server setup, no admin user, no `.ini` file. `import` and use. - **Serverless:** The database engine runs *as a part of the app process* — no separate DB server. - **Single File:** A whole database is one file — trivial to back up, copy, or transfer. - **Cross-Platform:** The same `.db` file works on Android, iOS, Linux, Windows, and embedded ARM devices. - **Stable & Reliable:** Has been battle-tested since **May 2000** (over 25 years of development). - **Small Footprint:** Library size is under **600 KiB**, ideal for embedded IoT firmware. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & Architecture ## 2.1 The SQLite Architecture Stack SQLite's internal architecture follows a layered, modular pipeline. When an application issues an SQL statement, it traverses the following layers: ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ APPLICATION (Android App / IoT Edge) │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Step 1: Tokenizer (Lexical Analyzer) │ │ Step 2: Parser (Syntax Tree Builder) │ │ Step 3: Code Generator (VDBE Bytecode)│ │ Step 4: Virtual Machine (Executes VM)│ │ Step 5: B-Tree Module (Storage) │ │ Step 6: Pager (Disk I/O Management) │ │ Step 7: OS Interface (File System) │ └─────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. **Tokenization (Lexer):** Breaks the raw SQL text into **tokens** (keywords, identifiers, literals, operators). For example, `SELECT * FROM sensors;` becomes tokens: `SELECT`, `*`, `FROM`, `sensors`, `;`. 2. **Parser:** Uses a hand-written **LALR(1)** parser to build a *parse tree*. If the syntax is wrong, the parser throws a `SQLiteException`. 3. **Code Generator:** Translates the parse tree into a program written in **VDBE (Virtual Database Engine)** bytecode — a custom, register-based instruction set. 4. **Virtual Machine:** Interprets the VDBE bytecode one instruction at a time. Each instruction manipulates *registers* holding intermediate values. 5. **B-Tree Module:** Manages on-disk data structures. Each table and each index is a separate **B-Tree**. The tree depth is typically 1–3 even for tables with **billions of rows**. 6. **Pager:** Responsible for atomic commits and rollback. Pages are fixed-size blocks (default **4096 bytes**) read/written to disk. 7. **OS Interface:** Calls native file-system APIs (`open()`, `read()`, `write()`, `fsync()`). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The Pager is the heart of SQLite's **ACID compliance**. It implements a *rollback journal* (or *WAL — Write-Ahead Log*) so that even if the device loses power mid-write, the database can either fully complete or fully abort the transaction — never corrupt. ## 2.2 SQLite Storage Classes (Data Types) Unlike MySQL or PostgreSQL, SQLite uses **dynamic typing** (also called *manifest typing*). A column can hold **any storage class** regardless of the column's declared type. | Type | Description | Storage Size | Example | |---|---|---|---| | `NULL` | The value is a NULL value. | 0 bytes | `NULL` | | `INTEGER` | Signed integer (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, or 8 bytes depending on magnitude). | 1–8 bytes | `42`, `-7`, `9000000000` | | `REAL` | Floating-point number stored as an 8-byte IEEE-754 double. | 8 bytes | `3.14`, `-0.001` | | `TEXT` | UTF-8, UTF-16BE, or UTF-16LE encoded text string. | Variable | `'Hello'`, `'DHT11'` | | `BLOB` | Binary Large Object — exact byte sequence. | Variable | Image file, sensor raw bytes | > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** SQLite has *no* dedicated `BOOLEAN`, `DATE`, or `DATETIME` type. Booleans are stored as `INTEGER 0/1`, and dates are stored as `TEXT` (ISO-8601: `'2024-11-25 14:30:00'`) or `INTEGER` (Unix epoch). ## 2.3 ACID Properties in SQLite ACID is the cornerstone of any RDBMS. SQLite guarantees all four: | Property | Meaning | How SQLite Achieves It | |---|---|---| | **Atomicity** | All-or-nothing — either every write in a transaction commits, or none of them. | Rollback journal records the *original* content of changed pages. | | **Consistency** | The database always moves from one valid state to another. | Constraint checks (PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL, CHECK, FOREIGN KEY). | | **Isolation** | Concurrent transactions don't interfere with each other. | SQLite serializes writes; reads are non-blocking. | | **Durability** | Once committed, data survives crashes and power loss. | `fsync()` ensures the journal hits the disk *before* commit returns. | > [!TIP] > **Default Journal Mode in Android SQLite:** Android's `SQLiteOpenHelper` uses the **DELETE** journal mode by default. Modern apps targeting API 30+ often switch to **WAL (Write-Ahead Log)** mode using `db.enableWriteAheadLogging()` for better concurrency. ## 2.4 SQL Command Categories SQL statements are grouped into five sublanguages: | Sublanguage | Full Form | Common Commands | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | **DDL** | Data Definition Language | `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE` | Define/modify schema. | | **DML** | Data Manipulation Language | `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` | Modify rows. | | **DQL** | Data Query Language | `SELECT` | Read data. | | **DCL** | Data Control Language | `GRANT`, `REVOKE` | Permissions (not used in Android). | | **TCL** | Transaction Control Language | `BEGIN`, `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT` | Manage transactions. | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet Since the topic is a CS/IoT technology rather than a physics/math domain, the "formulas" are presented as **canonical syntax patterns** that act as templates for examinations. | # | Operation | Canonical Syntax (KTU 2024 Standard) | |---|---|---| | 1 | Create Database File | (Auto-created on first `openOrCreateDatabase()` call) | | 2 | Create Table | `CREATE TABLE table_name (col1 TYPE, col2 TYPE, ...);` | | 3 | Insert Row | `INSERT INTO table_name (col1, col2) VALUES (?, ?);` | | 4 | Read All Rows | `SELECT * FROM table_name;` | | 5 | Read with Filter | `SELECT col1, col2 FROM table_name WHERE condition;` | | 6 | Update Row | `UPDATE table_name SET col1 = ? WHERE condition;` | | 7 | Delete Row | `DELETE FROM table_name WHERE condition;` | | 8 | Drop Table | `DROP TABLE IF EXISTS table_name;` | | 9 | Count Rows | `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM table_name;` | | 10 | Auto-Increment ID | `id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT` | | 11 | Sort Results | `... ORDER BY col ASC \vert DESC;` | | 12 | Limit Results | `... LIMIT n OFFSET m;` | | 13 | Begin Transaction | `db.beginTransaction();` | | 14 | Set Successful | `db.setTransactionSuccessful();` | | 15 | End Transaction | `db.endTransaction();` | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, CRUD Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The 5 Pillars of SQLite in Android (KTU High-Yield) In an Android application, SQLite is accessed through five key classes. Understanding their interaction is essential for the KTU 2024 exam. ### Step 1 — Subclassing `SQLiteOpenHelper` The helper class manages database creation and version management.
