It is the **only correct way** to open a writable database in Android. ``` public class SensorDbHelper extends SQLiteOpenHelper { public static final String DB_NAME = "iot_sensors.db"; public static final int DB_VERSION = 1; public SensorDbHelper(Context ctx) { super(ctx, DB_NAME, null, DB_VERSION); } @Override public void onCreate(SQLiteDatabase db) { db.execSQL("CREATE TABLE sensor_reading (" + " id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT," + " device_id TEXT NOT NULL," + " temperature REAL," + " humidity REAL," + " timestamp TEXT DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP);"); } @Override public void onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int oldV, int newV) { db.execSQL("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS sensor_reading;"); onCreate(db); } } ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why `onCreate()` and `onUpgrade()` matter:** Android calls `onCreate()` the **first time** the database is opened. It calls `onUpgrade()` when `DB_VERSION` is incremented in a new app release. This is how you handle schema migrations without crashing on existing user devices. ### Step 2 — The CRUD Operations CRUD = **C**reate, **R**ead, **U**pdate, **D**elete. Every IoT mobile database is built on these four operations. **(a) CREATE — Insert a new IoT sensor reading** ``` public long insertReading(String deviceId, double temp, double hum) { SQLiteDatabase db = this.getWritableDatabase(); ContentValues cv = new ContentValues(); cv.put("device_id", deviceId); cv.put("temperature", temp); cv.put("humidity", hum); long rowId = db.insert("sensor_reading", null, cv); db.close(); return rowId; } ``` **Step-by-step logical breakdown:** 1. `getWritableDatabase()` — opens the database file in **read-write mode**. If disk is full or DB is locked, it throws `SQLiteException`. 2. `ContentValues` — a key-value map (HashMap-like) where keys are **column names** and values are the data to insert. Using `ContentValues` is **always preferred** over string concatenation (which is vulnerable to **SQL injection**). 3. `db.insert(...)` — returns the new row's `id`, or `-1` on failure. 4. `db.close()` — releases the file lock. Forgetting this is a common bug on multi-threaded apps. **(b) READ — Query all readings from a specific device** ``` public Cursor getReadingsForDevice(String deviceId) { SQLiteDatabase db = this.getReadableDatabase(); String[] cols = {"id", "temperature", "humidity", "timestamp"}; String selection = "device_id = ?"; String[] selArgs = { deviceId }; String orderBy = "timestamp DESC"; return db.query("sensor_reading", cols, selection, selArgs, null, null, orderBy); } ``` **Logical explanation of the query API:** - `table` → `"sensor_reading"` - `columns` → only the 4 columns we need (avoids loading `device_id` repeatedly) - `selection` → `device_id = ?` (the `?` is a **placeholder** for safe parameterization) - `selectionArgs` → `{deviceId}` (replaces the `?` in order) - `groupBy`, `having` → `null` (no aggregation in this example) - `orderBy` → newest reading first **(c) UPDATE — Correct a faulty temperature reading** ``` public int updateReading(int id, double correctedTemp) { SQLiteDatabase db = this.getWritableDatabase(); ContentValues cv = new ContentValues(); cv.put("temperature", correctedTemp); int rows = db.update("sensor_reading", cv, "id = ?", new String[]{String.valueOf(id)}); db.close(); return rows; } ``` The return value is the **number of rows affected**. If `0`, the `id` was not found. **(d) DELETE — Remove readings older than 30 days** ``` public int deleteOldReadings() { SQLiteDatabase db = this.getWritableDatabase(); int rows = db.delete("sensor_reading", "timestamp < datetime('now', '-30 days')", null); db.close(); return rows; } ``` `datetime('now', '-30 days')` is an **SQLite built-in date function** that evaluates to a text string. The `<` operator compares it lexicographically (works correctly for ISO-8601 format). ### Step 3 — Working with `Cursor` A `Cursor` is a **pointer to the result set** of a query. It does **not** load all rows into memory — it reads one row at a time as you iterate. ``` Cursor cursor = helper.getReadingsForDevice("DHT11_01"); if (cursor.moveToFirst()) { do { long id = cursor.getLong (cursor.getColumnIndexOrThrow("id")); double temp = cursor.getDouble(cursor.getColumnIndexOrThrow("temperature")); double hum = cursor.getDouble(cursor.getColumnIndexOrThrow("humidity")); String ts = cursor.getString(cursor.getColumnIndexOrThrow("timestamp")); Log.d("IOT_DB", "[" + ts + "] T=" + temp + "°C H=" + hum + "%"); } while (cursor.moveToNext()); } cursor.close(); ``` > [!WARNING] > **Always call `cursor.close()` when finished.** Open cursors leak memory and may keep the database locked, causing "database is locked" errors in production apps. ## 3.2 The `adb shell` Interface for Debugging KTU 2024 labs often require you to verify your database file from a connected device: ``` adb shell run-as com.example.iotapp # Switch to your app's sandbox ls databases/ # Should show iot_sensors.db sqlite3 databases/iot_sensors.db sqlite> .tables sqlite> SELECT * FROM sensor_reading; sqlite> .schema sensor_reading sqlite> .quit ``` ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SQL Joins (Bonus High-Yield Topic) In an IoT app, you may have two tables: `devices` and `readings`. Joining them is a common exam question. | Join Type | Returns | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **INNER JOIN** | Only rows where the join key matches in *both* tables. | "Show readings only for devices that are registered." | | **LEFT JOIN** | All rows from the left table + matching right rows (or `NULL`). | "Show all devices, even those with no readings yet." | | **CROSS JOIN** | Cartesian product (every row × every row). | Rarely used; "find all possible sensor pairs." | | **FULL OUTER JOIN** | Not natively supported in SQLite. | Use `UNION` of LEFT and RIGHT joins. | **Example INNER JOIN query:** ``` SELECT d.device_name, r.temperature, r.timestamp FROM devices AS d INNER JOIN sensor_reading AS r ON d.device_id = r.device_id WHERE r.temperature > 30.0 ORDER BY r.timestamp DESC LIMIT 100; ``` --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SQLite Internal Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Android Application SQL Query] --> B[SQLiteOpenHelper Layer] B --> C[Tokenizer Lexical Analysis] C --> D[Parser Builds Parse Tree] D --> E[Code Generator VDBE Bytecode] E --> F[Virtual Machine Executes] F --> G{B-Tree Module} G --> H[Pager Disk I O Manager] H --> I[OS File System iot_sensors.db] I --> J[Return Result to App] J --> K[Cursor Iterates Rows] ``` ## 4.2 CRUD Operation Flow in Android ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CREATE C1[App calls db.insert] --> C2[ContentValues built] C2 --> C3[SQLiteDatabase writes row] C3 --> C4[Row ID returned to app] end subgraph READ R1[App calls db.query] --> R2[SQLiteDatabase returns Cursor] R2 --> R3[App iterates with moveToNext] R3 --> R4[Extract values getString getDouble] end subgraph UPDATE U1[App calls db.update] --> U2[ContentValues for new values] U2 --> U3[WHERE clause filters rows] U3 --> U4[Affected row count returned] end subgraph DELETE D1[App calls db.delete] --> D2[WHERE clause filters rows] D2 --> D3[Rows removed from B Tree] D3 --> D4[Affected row count returned] end ``` ## 4.3 ACID Transaction Lifecycle ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> BeginTxn: db.beginTransaction BeginTxn --> Writing: INSERT UPDATE DELETE Writing --> Writing: more changes Writing --> Success: db.setTransactionSuccessful Success --> Commit: db.endTransaction Writing --> Rollback: db.endTransaction no success Commit --> Idle Rollback --> Idle Idle --> [*] ``` ## 4.4 IoT Mobile App Database Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MobileApp[Mobile Application Layer] UI[Activity UI Thread] VM[ViewModel] end subgraph DataLayer[Data Access Layer] HLP[SQLiteOpenHelper Subclass] DB[(SQLite Database File
iot_sensors.db)] end subgraph IoTLayer[IoT Sensor Layer] S1[DHT11 Temperature Sensor] S2[MQ135 Air Quality Sensor] S3[HC SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor] end UI --> VM VM --> HLP HLP --> DB S1 --> VM S2 --> VM S3 --> VM ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** Define **SQLite**. List **any four** features that make it suitable for mobile and IoT applications. > **Model Answer:** > **SQLite** is a self-contained, serverless, zero-configuration, transactional SQL database engine implemented as a C-language library. It is the most widely deployed database in the world, used as the default local storage engine on Android. > > **Four features suitable for mobile/IoT:** > 1. **Serverless architecture** — no separate DB process to manage, reducing memory and battery consumption. > 2. **Zero configuration** — no installation or admin user required; ideal for headless IoT devices. > 3. **Small footprint** — library is under **600 KiB**, fitting comfortably on resource-constrained hardware. > 4. **ACID-compliant** — guarantees data integrity even on sudden power loss, critical for IoT edge devices. > 5. *(Bonus)* Cross-platform `.db` file format; single-file portability; public-domain licensing. **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Differentiate between **DML** and **DDL** commands in SQL. Give **two examples** of each. > **Model Answer:** > > | Aspect | DDL (Data Definition Language) | DML (Data Manipulation Language) | > |---|---|---| > | Purpose | Defines or alters the *structure* (schema) of database objects. | Manipulates the *data* (rows) inside existing tables. | > | Auto-commit | Changes are auto-committed; cannot be rolled back in some DBs. | Changes can be wrapped in transactions. | > | Affects | Table schema, indexes, views. | Table rows/records. | > > **DDL examples:** `CREATE TABLE sensor_reading (...);` and `DROP TABLE IF EXISTS sensor_reading;` > > **DML examples:** `INSERT INTO sensor_reading (device_id, temperature) VALUES ('DHT11', 28.5);` and `DELETE FROM sensor_reading WHERE id = 5;` --- ## Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of SQLite**. Describe the role of the **Pager** and **B-Tree modules** in detail. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write an Android Java class that: (i) Creates a SQLite table `iot_devices` with columns: `id` (auto-increment primary key), `device_name` (TEXT, not null), `mac_address` (TEXT, unique), and `registered_on` (TEXT, default current timestamp). **(4 Marks)** (ii) Inserts a new device using `ContentValues`. **(3 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Internal Architecture of SQLite (7 Marks):** The SQLite library is internally organized as a stack of modules, each with a specific responsibility. The diagram below (also shown in Section 4.1) is reproduced for the answer: ``` Application SQL → Tokenizer → Parser → Code Generator (VDBE) → Virtual Machine → B-Tree → Pager → OS Interface → Disk File ``` **Step-by-step role of each module:** 1. **Tokenizer (Lexer):** Performs lexical analysis. Breaks the input SQL string into a stream of tokens. For example, `SELECT id FROM devices;` is tokenized into keywords (`SELECT`, `FROM`), identifier (`id`, `devices`), and punctuation (`;`). 2. **Parser:** Uses a hand-written **LALR(1)** parser to verify grammar and construct a **parse tree** (a hierarchical representation of the SQL statement's structure). Syntax errors are caught here. 3. **Code Generator:** Walks the parse tree and emits **VDBE (Virtual Database Engine) bytecode** — a compact, register-based instruction set. This is conceptually similar to how Java compiles to JVM bytecode. 4. **Virtual Machine:** The VDBE bytecode interpreter. It maintains an array of *registers* (memory cells) and executes instructions one at a time (e.g., `OpenRead`, `Column`, `ResultRow`). 5. **B-Tree Module:** Manages on-disk storage. **Every table is one B-Tree; every index is another B-Tree.** B-Trees are balanced search trees that keep the tree height low (typically depth 1–3) so lookups, inserts, and deletes are O(log n). They also support ordered scans, which makes `ORDER BY` queries efficient. 6. **Pager:** The disk I/O manager. It divides the database file into fixed-size **pages** (default **4096 bytes**). It loads pages into an in-memory cache, marks them dirty on modification, and flushes them to disk. **Crucially**, the Pager also implements the **rollback journal** for atomic transactions. Before changing a page, the *original* version is written to the journal file. If a crash occurs, on restart SQLite simply *replays* the journal in reverse to restore the original state. 7. **OS Interface:** A thin layer abstracting the host operating system (e.g., POSIX `open()`, `read()`, `write()`, `fsync()` on Linux/Android). > **Role of the Pager:** The Pager is the *transactional* heart of SQLite. It guarantees **Atomicity** and **Durability** by writing the rollback journal before modifying the actual database file. It guarantees **Isolation** between writers by serializing write transactions. > **Role of the B-Tree Module:** The B-Tree module provides ordered, efficient access to table rows and index entries. It supports the following operations: `BtreeCreate`, `BtreeInsert`, `BtreeDelete`, `BtreeCursorMove`, `BtreeFirst`, `BtreeNext`. Each B-Tree node is a single database page. **(b) Android Java Code (7 Marks):** ```java import android.content.ContentValues; import android.content.Context; import android.database.sqlite.SQLiteDatabase; import android.database.sqlite.SQLiteOpenHelper; public class DeviceDbHelper extends SQLiteOpenHelper { private static final String DB_NAME = "iot_devices.db"; private static final int DB_VERSION = 1; public static final String TABLE_DEVICES = "iot_devices"; public static final String COL_ID = "id"; public static final String COL_DEVICE_NAME = "device_name"; public static final String COL_MAC_ADDRESS = "mac_address"; public static final String COL_REGISTERED = "registered_on"; public DeviceDbHelper(Context context) { super(context, DB_NAME, null, DB_VERSION); } @Override public void onCreate(SQLiteDatabase db) { String createSql = "CREATE TABLE " + TABLE_DEVICES + " (" + COL_ID + " INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, " + COL_DEVICE_NAME + " TEXT NOT NULL, " + COL_MAC_ADDRESS + " TEXT UNIQUE, " + COL_REGISTERED + " TEXT DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP" + ");"; db.execSQL(createSql); } @Override public void onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int oldV, int newV) { db.execSQL("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS " + TABLE_DEVICES + ";"); onCreate(db); } // (b)(ii) Insert a new device public long insertDevice(String name, String mac) { SQLiteDatabase db = this.getWritableDatabase(); ContentValues cv = new ContentValues(); cv.put(COL_DEVICE_NAME, name); cv.put(COL_MAC_ADDRESS, mac); long newId = db.insert(TABLE_DEVICES, null, cv); db.close(); return newId; } } ``` **Explanation of key parts (for 3 marks on part ii):** - `getWritableDatabase()` opens the database in read-write mode. - `ContentValues` is a `Map` used to bind column names to values safely — preventing SQL injection. - `db.insert(...)` returns the new row's primary key, or `-1` if the insert failed (e.g., a duplicate `mac_address` violates the `UNIQUE` constraint). - `db.close()` releases the file lock. --- ### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper] **(a)** Explain the **ACID properties** of SQLite. How does SQLite ensure **Atomicity** and **Durability** even when the device loses power mid-transaction? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Consider the following two tables in an Android SQLite database for an IoT app: ``` Table: devices id (INTEGER PK) | name (TEXT) | location (TEXT) Table: readings id (INTEGER PK) | device_id (INTEGER FK) | temperature (REAL) | timestamp (TEXT) ``` Write SQL queries to: (i) Insert a new device "DHT11_Lab1" located in "Block-A Room 204". **(2 Marks)** (ii) Display the **device name**, **location**, and **latest temperature** reading for **every** device, even if a device has no readings yet. **(5 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) ACID Properties (7 Marks):** | Property | Meaning | |---|---| | **Atomicity** | A transaction is an all-or-nothing unit of work. Either *every* SQL statement inside the transaction is applied, or *none* of them are. There is no partial state. | | **Consistency** | The database always transitions from one *valid* state to another. All defined rules (constraints, triggers, cascades) must be satisfied. | | **Isolation** | The intermediate state of one transaction is *invisible* to other concurrent transactions. | | **Durability** | Once a transaction is committed, its effects survive subsequent crashes, power loss, or OS errors. | **How SQLite ensures Atomicity and Durability on power loss:** SQLite uses a **rollback journal** (or, alternatively, **Write-Ahead Logging**) to guarantee these properties. The default flow is: 1. **Begin transaction:** `db.beginTransaction()` is called. The Pager enters "journal mode." 2. **First write attempt:** When the Pager needs to modify page **P** of the database file, it **first** writes the *original* contents of page P into the **rollback journal file** (e.g., `iot_devices.db-journal`). 3. **fsync journal:** The Pager calls `fsync()` on the journal file, forcing the OS to flush it to physical storage. This is the critical step that *guarantees durability of the original page*. 4. **Modify page P:** The Pager modifies the in-memory copy of page P and marks it dirty. 5. **Write page P to main DB:** The modified page is written into the main `.db` file. If a **power failure** occurs at any point before this step, on next startup SQLite reads the journal and copies the original page back — **undoing** the partial change (atomicity preserved). 6. **Commit — delete journal:** After the modified pages are themselves `fsync()`'d to disk, the journal file is *deleted*. The deletion is itself `fsync()`'d to ensure the commit is permanent. > **In WAL mode** (Write-Ahead Log), the mechanism is reversed: changes are appended to a separate `*.db-wal` file *first*, and the main DB file is checkpointed later. WAL provides better concurrency (readers don't block writers) and is often faster on flash storage. **(b) SQL Queries (7 Marks):** **(i) Insert a new device (2 Marks):** ```sql INSERT INTO devices (name, location) VALUES ('DHT11_Lab1', 'Block-A Room 204'); ``` **(ii) Latest temperature per device (5 Marks):** We need a `LEFT JOIN` because some devices may have **no** readings yet, and we want to include them. The "latest reading" is a classic `GROUP BY` problem with a correlated subquery: ```sql SELECT d.name, d.location, r.temperature AS latest_temperature FROM devices d LEFT JOIN readings r ON r.device_id = d.id AND r.timestamp = ( SELECT MAX(r2.timestamp) FROM readings r2 WHERE r2.device_id = d.id ); ``` **Step-by-step explanation:** - `FROM devices d` — start with all devices (the *left* side of the LEFT JOIN). - `LEFT JOIN readings r` — keep every device even if the join finds no match. - `ON r.device_id = d.id` — the foreign-key link. - `AND r.timestamp = (subquery)` — match **only** the row with the maximum timestamp for that device. - The correlated subquery `SELECT MAX(r2.timestamp) FROM readings r2 WHERE r2.device_id = d.id` runs **once per device** and returns the most recent timestamp. The outer `LEFT JOIN` then matches the row that has that timestamp. - `d.name`, `d.location`, `r.temperature` are projected. - Devices with no readings will have `r.temperature = NULL` in the output — this is the correct behavior for `LEFT JOIN`. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Using `WHERE` instead of `AND` inside the JOIN:** A `WHERE` clause filters rows *after* the join, which would convert the `LEFT JOIN` into an *inner* join and **drop devices with no readings**. Always filter the *right* table inside the `ON` clause. > 2. **Forgetting `GROUP BY`:** Some students write `SELECT MAX(timestamp) FROM readings GROUP BY device_id` and stop there, forgetting to fetch the *corresponding temperature*. The correlated subquery pattern above is the correct way. > 3. **Forgetting `db.close()`:** In Java code, leaving the database open causes the next `db.insert` to throw `IllegalStateException: database not open` or `SQLiteDatabaseLockedException`. > 4. **Mixing up `getReadableDatabase()` and `getWritableDatabase()`:** For read-only queries, `getReadableDatabase()` is preferred — it doesn't fight for the write lock and is faster. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):** - **SQLite =** Self-contained, serverless, zero-config, single-file, ACID-compliant SQL DB. **C library**, under 600 KiB, public domain. - **Default DB in Android.** Stored at `/data/data//databases/.db`. - **5 Storage Classes:** `NULL`, `INTEGER`, `REAL`, `TEXT`, `BLOB` (no dedicated `BOOLEAN` or `DATE` type). - **Use `SQLiteOpenHelper`** to manage DB lifecycle (`onCreate`, `onUpgrade`). - **Use `ContentValues` + `db.insert/update/delete`** for DML — *never* concatenate SQL strings (SQL injection risk). - **Use `db.query(...)` or `db.rawQuery(...)`** for SELECT; result is a `Cursor`. - **Always `close()`** the database and the cursor when done. - **ACID** is enforced by the **Pager** module (rollback journal) or **WAL** mode. - **B-Tree module** handles all on-disk storage — tables and indexes are both B-Trees. - **Five SQL sublanguage categories:** DDL, DML, DQL, DCL, TCL. - **Common SQL clauses to memorize:** `SELECT`, `FROM`, `WHERE`, `ORDER BY`, `GROUP BY`, `HAVING`, `LIMIT`, `JOIN` (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, CROSS). - **Aggregate functions:** `COUNT(*)`, `SUM()`, `AVG()`, `MIN()`, `MAX()`. - **Built-in date function:** `datetime('now')`, `datetime('now', '-30 days')`, `date(timestamp)`, `strftime('%Y-%m-%d', timestamp)`. - **`AUTOINCREMENT`** keyword ensures monotonically increasing IDs (slightly slower than default `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`, which uses the rowid). - **For IoT apps:** Use SQLite as a **local cache** for sensor data; sync to the cloud when network is available. - **KTU 2024 Module 4 emphasis:** CRUD operations, schema design, `SQLiteOpenHelper`, `Cursor`, and `ContentValues` are the most-asked topics. - **Always parameterize queries** with `?` placeholders to prevent SQL injection — this is a favourite sub-question in Part A.

topics.title: Creating and Opening a Database
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Creating and Opening a Database

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Mobile App Development for IoT**, **Creating and Opening a Database** refers to the procedural and programmatic establishment of a persistent, structured storage repository within the local file system of a mobile device (Android, iOS) or embedded IoT edge node, which is then bound to a handle through which the application can perform Data Definition Language (DDL) and Data Manipulation Language (DML) operations.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for Module 4, this is executed primarily through the **SQLite** relational database engine — a serverless, self-contained, transactional SQL database engine that is embedded directly into the mobile application process. The two canonical entry points taught in the KTU curriculum are:

1. The high-level **SQLiteOpenHelper** contract class (legacy / explicit control).
2. The high-level **Room Persistence Library** abstraction over SQLite (modern / Jetpack).

A database, once created, is physically stored as a single cross-platform file (typically with `.db` or `.sqlite` extension) in the application's private internal storage directory:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Android, the canonical path is  
> `/data/data/<your.package.name>/databases/<database_name>.db`  
> This directory is private to the application and is purged when the app is uninstalled.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are moving into a **new office** (your app):

- **Creating a Database** is like **commissioning a new filing cabinet**. You decide how many drawers (tables) it will have, what labels go on each folder (columns), and what type of file fits where (data types). If the cabinet doesn't exist, you buy a new one and stamp the rules on the front.
- **Opening a Database** is like **unlocking the cabinet at the start of the workday**. You don't need a separate server (no remote receptionist), and you don't need a network cable. The cabinet is *right there* in your office, and you just turn the key.
- **Closing a Database** is locking the cabinet at the end of the day — except SQLite is smart enough that it usually manages this for you.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SQLite for IoT Mobile Apps?**
> - **Zero-configuration** — no server process to manage.
> - **Serverless** — runs in the same process as the app, critical for low-power IoT edge devices.
> - **Cross-platform** — same `.db` file works on Android, iOS, embedded Linux.
> - **Single-file storage** — trivial to sync, back up, or ship inside an APK/AAB.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Maximum Database Size:** Up to **140 TB** (theoretically), practically constrained by device storage.
- **Maximum Number of Tables:** **62** tables per attached database (older limit) — modern SQLite has lifted this; current limit is around **2³² − 1** rows per table.
- **Default Page Size:** **4096 bytes**.
- **SQLite Version bundled in Android 14+:** **SQLite 3.42+**.
- **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) Mode:** Default journal mode in modern Android — allows concurrent readers + one writer.
- **ACID Compliance:** Fully **ACID-compliant** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Approaches Taught in KTU Module 4

### Approach 1: The Android `SQLiteOpenHelper` Class (Foundational)

This is the **canonical KTU-board-exam method**. It is an abstract class in the `android.database.sqlite` package designed to manage database creation and version management.

#### Logical Step-by-Step Breakdown

1. **Subclass `SQLiteOpenHelper`** — You create your own class (e.g., `DBHelper`) and extend `SQLiteOpenHelper`.
2. **Override the Constructor** — Pass `Context`, `Database Name`, optional `CursorFactory`, and `Database Version` (an integer).
3. **Override `onCreate(SQLiteDatabase db)`** — Invoked by the Android framework **automatically and only once** when the database file does not yet exist. You place your `CREATE TABLE` SQL statements here.
4. **Override `onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int oldVersion, int newVersion)`** — Invoked when the existing database version is **lower** than the version passed in the constructor. Used for schema migrations.
5. **Call `getWritableDatabase()` or `getReadableDatabase()`** — These methods are the *actual triggers* that open (or create, if absent) the database and return a `SQLiteDatabase` handle.

### Approach 2: The `openOrCreateDatabase()` Convenience Method

For trivial apps, Android provides a one-liner inside any `Activity` or `Context`:

```java
SQLiteDatabase db = openOrCreateDatabase("MyDB.db", MODE_PRIVATE, null);
```

This is the most direct KTU-board-friendly way to demonstrate the "create + open" lifecycle.

### Approach 3: Room Persistence Library (Modern Jetpack)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although Room is mentioned in advanced KTU electives, Module 4's core focus is on raw `SQLiteOpenHelper` and `SQLiteDatabase`. Room is built *on top of* SQLite and uses `@Database` annotation with `Room.databaseBuilder()` to create + open databases.

## 2.2 The `SQLiteOpenHelper` Constructor Signature

$$
\text{SQLiteOpenHelper}(\text{Context context},\, \text{String name},\, \text{SQLiteDatabase.CursorFactory factory},\, \text{int version})
$$

| Parameter | Type | Description | KTU-Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| `context` | `Context` | Usually `this` from the calling Activity | Always pass the Application context to avoid memory leaks |
| `name` | `String` | Filename of the DB (e.g., `"StudentDB.db"`) | If `null`, an in-memory database is created (not persisted) |
| `factory` | `CursorFactory` | Custom cursor; usually `null` | KTU exam answer: pass `null` unless specifically asked |
| `version` | `int` | Schema version starting from `1` | Increment this on schema change to trigger `onUpgrade()` |

## 2.3 Lifecycle Hooks: When Does Android Call What?

| Method | When Invoked | Frequency | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `onCreate(SQLiteDatabase db)` | First-time access; `.db` file does not exist | **Exactly once** per app install | Create tables, indices, seed data |
| `onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int old, int new)` | `version` argument is higher than stored version | As needed per version bump | Schema migration, data preservation |
| `onDowngrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int old, int new)` | Stored version is *higher* than supplied | Rare | Rollback logic |

## 2.4 The Open-Trigger Methods

| Method | Returns | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `getReadableDatabase()` | `SQLiteDatabase` | Read operations; falls back to read-only if disk full |
| `getWritableDatabase()` | `SQLiteDatabase` | Read + Write operations; throws if disk full |

> Both methods internally check whether the `.db` file exists. If **not**, they call `onCreate()`. If yes, they compare versions and call `onUpgrade()` if needed.

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| Element | Symbol / Form | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Database File Path | `/data/data/<pkg>/databases/<name>.db` | Private to the app |
| Constructor Version Start | $v_0 = 1$ | First release of schema |
| Upgrade Trigger Condition | $v_{new} > v_{stored}$ | Integer comparison |
| Default Journal Mode (Modern) | $\text{WAL}$ | Write-Ahead Log |
| Maximum Concurrent Readers (WAL) | $\infty$ (bounded by memory) | Writers are exclusive |
| ACID Property Set | $\lbrace A,\, C,\, I,\, D \rbrace$ | Fully supported |
| `onCreate` Invocation Count | $1$ per fresh install | Per (package, db name) tuple |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in IoT Engineering

- **Edge Caching of Sensor Telemetry:** IoT gateways running Android Things / embedded Linux store sensor bursts in SQLite before uploading to the cloud during connectivity windows.
- **Offline-First Apps:** Field-service, agricultural monitoring, and asset-tracking apps where network is intermittent.
- **Local Configuration Storage:** Storing device IDs, calibration constants, MQTT broker addresses.
- **Data Aggregation:** Buffering thousands of rows per minute from BLE-paired sensors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Code: Creating + Opening a Database using `SQLiteOpenHelper`

Below is the **canonical KTU 14-mark model answer** skeleton. Every line is annotated.

```java
// STEP 1: Imports
import android.content.Context;
import android.database.sqlite.SQLiteDatabase;
import android.database.sqlite.SQLiteOpenHelper;
import android.util.Log;

// STEP 2: Create the Helper class by extending SQLiteOpenHelper
public class DBHelper extends SQLiteOpenHelper {

    // STEP 3: Define a TAG for logging (best practice)
    private static final String TAG = "DBHelper";

    // STEP 4: Define the database name and version as constants
    private static final String DATABASE_NAME = "IoTSensorDB.db";
    private static final int DATABASE_VERSION = 1;

    // STEP 5: Define the CREATE TABLE statement as a constant
    private static final String SQL_CREATE_SENSOR_TABLE =
            "CREATE TABLE " + "sensor_data " + "(" +
                    "id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, " +
                    "device_id TEXT NOT NULL, " +
                    "temperature REAL, " +
                    "humidity REAL, " +
                    "timestamp INTEGER NOT NULL)";

    // STEP 6: Constructor — forwards to superclass
    public DBHelper(Context context) {
        super(context, DATABASE_NAME, null, DATABASE_VERSION);
        Log.d(TAG, "DBHelper constructor called");
    }

    // STEP 7: onCreate — invoked automatically on FIRST creation
    @Override
    public void onCreate(SQLiteDatabase db) {
        Log.d(TAG, "onCreate: creating sensor_data table");
        db.execSQL(SQL_CREATE_SENSOR_TABLE);
        // Seed optional initial data here if required
    }

    // STEP 8: onUpgrade — invoked on schema version bump
    @Override
    public void onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int oldVersion, int newVersion) {
        Log.d(TAG, "onUpgrade from " + oldVersion + " to " + newVersion);
        db.execSQL("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS sensor_data");
        onCreate(db);
    }
}
```

### How to **Open** the Database from an Activity

```java
public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    private DBHelper dbHelper;
    private SQLiteDatabase database;

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);

        // STEP A: Instantiate the helper (no DB file is opened yet)
        dbHelper = new DBHelper(this);

        // STEP B: Trigger the open (or create) — THIS is the lifecycle pivot
        database = dbHelper.getWritableDatabase();

        // STEP C: Now you can run DML/DDL
        // database.insert(...);
        // database.rawQuery("SELECT * FROM sensor_data", null);
    }

    @Override
    protected void onDestroy() {
        // STEP D: Always close to release file handle
        if (database != null && database.isOpen()) {
            database.close();
        }
        dbHelper.close();
        super.onDestroy();
    }
}
```

## 3.2 The Lightweight Alternative: `openOrCreateDatabase()`

For a one-liner, KTU boards often ask: *"Write a single line of code to create and open a database named `UserDB` in private mode."*

```java
SQLiteDatabase db = openOrCreateDatabase("UserDB", MODE_PRIVATE, null);
```

### Exhaustive Analytical Matrix

| # | API Call | Underlying OS Action | Disk Effect | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `new DBHelper(context)` | None | None | Helper object (not yet opened) |
| 2 | `helper.getWritableDatabase()` | Checks `/databases/<name>.db` | If absent → creates; if present → opens | `SQLiteDatabase` |
| 3 | Inside `getWritableDatabase()`: file absent | Calls `onCreate(db)` | Writes tables, journal file | — |
| 4 | Inside `getWritableDatabase()`: file present, version older | Calls `onUpgrade(db, old, new)` | Executes migration SQL | — |
| 5 | `db.insert(...)` | Writes row | Mutates `.db` file | `long` (row ID) |
| 6 | `db.close()` | Flushes WAL, closes file handle | Releases lock | `void` |

## 3.3 Lifecycle Derivation: When is `onCreate` Actually Called?

Let $S$ be the set of files in the `databases` directory of the app. Let $v_{stored}$ be the version encoded in the file header of `<name>.db`.

$$
\text{Action} =
\begin{cases}
\text{create new file} \;\to\; \text{call onCreate}() & \text{if } \langle\text{name}\rangle.\text{db} \notin S \\[6pt]
\text{compare } v_{param} \text{ vs } v_{stored} & \text{otherwise} \\[6pt]
\text{call onUpgrade}(v_{stored}, v_{param}) & \text{if } v_{param} > v_{stored} \\[6pt]
\text{open existing file} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

This branch logic is what `SQLiteOpenHelper.getWritableDatabase()` performs internally. KTU examiners love this as a **3-mark short answer**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Database Creation & Opening Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[App Starts: Activity onCreate] --> B[Instantiate DBHelper]
    B --> C[Call getWritableDatabase]
    C --> D{Database file exists?}
    D -- No --> E[Framework creates empty .db file]
    E --> F[Invoke onCreate callback]
    F --> G[Execute CREATE TABLE statements]
    G --> H[Return SQLiteDatabase handle]
    D -- Yes --> I{Version newer than stored?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Invoke onUpgrade callback]
    J --> K[Run migration SQL]
    K --> H
    I -- No --> L[Open existing database]
    L --> H
    H --> M[App runs DML: INSERT SELECT UPDATE DELETE]
    M --> N[Activity onDestroy]
    N --> O[Call db.close and dbHelper.close]
    O --> P[File handle released]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Class Diagram: SQLiteOpenHelper Hierarchy

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class SQLiteOpenHelper {
        <<abstract>>
        +SQLiteOpenHelper(Context, String, CursorFactory, int)
        +onCreate(SQLiteDatabase)*
        +onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase, int, int)*
        +getWritableDatabase() SQLiteDatabase
        +getReadableDatabase() SQLiteDatabase
        +close()
    }
    class DBHelper {
        -String DATABASE_NAME
        -int DATABASE_VERSION
        -String SQL_CREATE_TABLE
        +onCreate(SQLiteDatabase)
        +onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase, int, int)
    }
    class SQLiteDatabase {
        +execSQL(String)
        +insert(String, String, ContentValues) long
        +query(...) Cursor
        +rawQuery(String, String[]) Cursor
        +close()
    }
    class Context {
        +openOrCreateDatabase(String, int, CursorFactory) SQLiteDatabase
    }
    SQLiteOpenHelper <|-- DBHelper
    DBHelper ..> SQLiteDatabase : creates / opens
    Context ..> SQLiteDatabase : openOrCreateDatabase
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Android Storage Stack for SQLite

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    A["Java/Kotlin Application Code"] B["SQLiteOpenHelper Wrapper"] C["SQLiteDatabase API"]
    D["JNI Bridge libsqlite.so"] E["Linux Kernel VFS Layer"] F["Block Device Flash Storage"]
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, Module 4]**
**Define `SQLiteOpenHelper`. List its two mandatory callback methods to be overridden.**

**Model Answer:**
`SQLiteOpenHelper` is an abstract class in the `android.database.sqlite` package that simplifies database creation and version management in Android applications. It encapsulates the logic for opening a database, creating it if it does not exist, and upgrading it when the schema version changes.

The two mandatory callback methods to override are:

1. **`onCreate(SQLiteDatabase db)`** — Called by the framework automatically the first time the database is accessed. It is used to execute `CREATE TABLE` SQL statements.
2. **`onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int oldVersion, int newVersion)`** — Called when the database version supplied in the constructor is higher than the version stored in the existing database file. It handles schema migration.

*(2 marks for definition, 1 mark for listing the two methods with one-line purpose each.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, Module 4]**
**Differentiate between `getReadableDatabase()` and `getWritableDatabase()` methods of `SQLiteOpenHelper`.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `getReadableDatabase()` | `getWritableDatabase()` |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Use | Read-only operations | Read + Write operations |
| Behaviour when disk is full | Falls back to a read-only handle | Throws `SQLiteException` |
| Returns | `SQLiteDatabase` (may be read-only) | `SQLiteDatabase` (always writable) |
| Triggers `onCreate`? | Yes, if file is missing | Yes, if file is missing |

Both methods internally perform the same "create-if-missing, upgrade-if-needed" lifecycle; they differ only in the access privileges granted on the returned handle. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Sub-parts A1 and A2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Module 4]**

#### (a) Explain the architecture of SQLite as used in Android. How is a database file physically stored? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

SQLite is a **serverless, self-contained, transactional SQL database engine** that is embedded inside the application process. Unlike client-server DBMS (MySQL, PostgreSQL), there is no separate daemon — the SQLite library is linked directly into the Android app via the `libsqlite.so` native library.

**Architectural Layers (from top to bottom):**

1. **Application Layer** — Java/Kotlin code uses `SQLiteOpenHelper` and `SQLiteDatabase` APIs.
2. **Java API Layer** — `android.database.sqlite` package provides the OO wrappers.
3. **JNI Bridge** — Translates Java calls to native C functions.
4. **SQLite Core Engine** — Written in C; performs parsing, planning, execution.
5. **OS File System** — Standard Linux ext4/f2fs on the device.
6. **Block Device** — eMMC/UFS flash storage.

**Physical Storage Location:**

For an app with package `com.example.iotapp`, the database file is stored at:

$$
\text{/data/data/com.example.iotapp/databases/IoTSensorDB.db}
$$

This directory is:
- **Private** to the application (sandboxed by Linux UID per app).
- **Purged** automatically when the user uninstalls the app.
- **Not accessible** by other apps without explicit `ContentProvider` exposure.
- Backed up to Google Drive if the developer enables `android:allowBackup="true"`.

The `.db` file contains one or more **pages** (default 4096 bytes each) that store tables, indices, and B-tree structures. A companion `-journal` or `-wal` file is also present for crash safety.

*(7 marks — 3 for architecture, 2 for storage path, 2 for properties.)*

---

#### (b) Write a complete Android Java program using `SQLiteOpenHelper` to create a database named `EmployeeDB` with one table `employees` having columns `id (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT)`, `name (TEXT)`, `salary (REAL)`, and `dept (TEXT)`. Demonstrate opening the database and inserting one row. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

```java
// File: EmployeeDBHelper.java
import android.content.ContentValues;
import android.content.Context;
import android.database.sqlite.SQLiteDatabase;
import android.database.sqlite.SQLiteOpenHelper;

public class EmployeeDBHelper extends SQLiteOpenHelper {

    private static final String DB_NAME = "EmployeeDB";
    private static final int DB_VERSION = 1;
    private static final String TABLE = "employees";

    public EmployeeDBHelper(Context context) {
        super(context, DB_NAME, null, DB_VERSION);
    }

    @Override
    public void onCreate(SQLiteDatabase db) {
        String createSQL = "CREATE TABLE " + TABLE + " ("
                + "id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, "
                + "name TEXT, "
                + "salary REAL, "
                + "dept TEXT)";
        db.execSQL(createSQL);
    }

    @Override
    public void onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int oldV, int newV) {
        db.execSQL("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS " + TABLE);
        onCreate(db);
    }

    // Helper method to insert
    public long insertEmployee(String name, double salary, String dept) {
        SQLiteDatabase db = this.getWritableDatabase(); // opens or creates
        ContentValues cv = new ContentValues();
        cv.put("name", name);
        cv.put("salary", salary);
        cv.put("dept", dept);
        long rowId = db.insert(TABLE, null, cv);
        return rowId;
    }
}
```

```java
// File: MainActivity.java (relevant snippet)
EmployeeDBHelper helper = new EmployeeDBHelper(this);
long insertedId = helper.insertEmployee("Arun", 55000.0, "IoT");
Log.d("DB", "Inserted row id = " + insertedId);
```

**Explanation of flow:**

1. `new EmployeeDBHelper(this)` — Just constructs the helper; no file I/O yet.
2. `helper.insertEmployee(...)` calls `getWritableDatabase()` — This is the actual create-or-open trigger.
3. Since the file does not exist, Android creates it and invokes `onCreate()`, which executes our `CREATE TABLE` SQL.
4. `db.insert(...)` writes the row to the `employees` table and returns the auto-incremented `rowid`.
5. The `db` handle is held by the helper; closing is recommended in `onDestroy()`.

*(7 marks — 2 for helper class, 2 for onCreate, 2 for insert logic, 1 for explanation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Sub-parts B1 and B2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style, Module 4]**

#### (a) Compare and contrast the three methods of creating/opening a SQLite database in Android: `openOrCreateDatabase()`, `SQLiteOpenHelper`, and Room Persistence Library. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `openOrCreateDatabase()` | `SQLiteOpenHelper` | Room (Jetpack) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Package | `Context` (built-in) | `android.database.sqlite` | `androidx.room` |
| Boilerplate Code | Minimal (1 line) | Moderate (full subclass) | High (Entity + DAO + DB class) |
| Versioning Support | None (manual) | Built-in via `onUpgrade` | Built-in via `Migration` classes |
| Compile-time SQL Verification | No | No | **Yes** (validates @Query) |
| Threading | Manual handling | Manual handling | Built-in coroutine support |
| Best For | Quick demos, trivial apps | Mid-complexity apps with migrations | Production-grade, complex apps |
| KTU Board Likelihood | **High** | **High** | Low (elective only) |

**Verdict:** For KTU Module 4 examinations, `SQLiteOpenHelper` is the **most-tested** approach, with `openOrCreateDatabase()` as the simpler 3-mark alternative.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for table comparison, 2 marks for trade-offs, 2 marks for KTU-context conclusion.)*

---

#### (b) With a neat flow diagram, explain the lifecycle of `SQLiteOpenHelper.getWritableDatabase()`. What happens if the database file already exists with a higher version than the version passed in the constructor? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Lifecycle Flow:**

```
getWritableDatabase() called
        │
        ▼
Is <name>.db present in /databases folder?
        │
   ┌────┴────┐
   No        Yes
   │          │
   ▼          ▼
Create    Read stored version
file      from file header
   │          │
   ▼          ▼
Call      Is new version > stored version?
onCreate       │
   │      ┌────┴────┐
   ▼      No        Yes
Return   │          │
SQLite   ▼          ▼
Database Open     Call onUpgrade
handle    file       │
   │       │         ▼
   │       ▼      Apply migration
   │     Return   SQL, then
   │     handle   onCreate if needed
   │       │         │
   └───────┴─────────┘
            ▼
       Return writable
       SQLiteDatabase handle
```

**Higher stored version case:**
If the existing database file has a **higher** version number than the version passed in the helper's constructor, the framework invokes the **`onDowngrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int oldVersion, int newVersion)`** method (default implementation throws `SQLiteException`). The developer should override this to handle rollbacks, e.g., by re-creating the schema or reverting the schema to the older version.

Alternatively, in development, the developer may simply **uninstall the app** to clear the databases directory and start fresh, or programmatically call `context.deleteDatabase(name)` before re-opening.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for explanation, 2 marks for onDowngrade handling.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing `onCreate` of the Helper with `onCreate` of the Activity.** They are entirely different! The helper's `onCreate(SQLiteDatabase db)` is for *table creation*, not UI inflation.
> 2. **Forgetting to call `getWritableDatabase()` / `getReadableDatabase()`.** Just instantiating the helper does **not** open the database — the file is not created until one of these methods is invoked. KTU examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Passing `name = null` in the constructor.** This creates an **in-memory database** that is destroyed when the helper is closed. Always pass a real filename for persistence.
> 4. **Modifying the `DATABASE_VERSION` constant inconsistently.** A version bump from 1 → 3 without writing an `onUpgrade` for version 2 will throw a runtime exception.
> 5. **Not closing the database.** Memory and file-handle leaks. Always close in `onDestroy()` or use `try-with-resources`-style patterns.
> 6. **Writing SQL keywords in lowercase.** While SQLite is case-insensitive, KTU boards prefer uppercase SQL keywords (`CREATE TABLE`, `INSERT INTO`) for clarity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Database creation is lazy:** Instantiating `SQLiteOpenHelper` does **not** create the file. The `.db` file is created only when `getWritableDatabase()` or `getReadableDatabase()` is first called and the file is missing.
- **`onCreate` runs exactly once per fresh install** (or after `onUpgrade` drops and recreates tables).
- **`onUpgrade` runs when** $v_{new} > v_{stored}$; always perform a migration, not a blind DROP, in production code.
- **Default physical path:** `/data/data/<package>/databases/<name>.db` — sandboxed, private, auto-removed on uninstall.
- **`openOrCreateDatabase()` is the one-liner shortcut** for trivial cases.
- **Always close** the `SQLiteDatabase` in `Activity.onDestroy()` to release the file lock.
- **Use `ContentValues`** for `insert()` and `update()` — never concatenate raw user input into SQL (SQL-injection risk).
- **ACID-compliant** — safe for transactional IoT data writes.
- **WAL journal mode** is default on modern Android — enables concurrent readers + 1 writer.
- **Room is a modern wrapper** — uses `@Database`, `@Entity`, `@Dao` annotations, but Module 4 focuses on raw SQLite APIs.
- **Cursor factory** is almost always `null` unless implementing a custom cursor class.
- **Version starts at 1**, not 0 — convention.
- **No external server needed** — perfect for offline-first IoT edge scenarios.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Tables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Creating Tables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Mobile Application Development for IoT (PECGT415)**, the term **"Creating Tables"** refers to the systematic process of defining, structuring, and instantiating persistent data storage structures (relational tables or NoSQL document/collection structures) within a mobile application environment to store, retrieve, update, and delete (CRUD) data generated or consumed by IoT devices, sensors, actuators, and gateways.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this topic falls under Module 4, which covers the data persistence and backend integration layer of mobile IoT applications. The two dominant paradigms are:

1. **Local Persistence:** SQLite database tables, Room Persistence Library (Android Jetpack).
2. **Cloud Persistence:** Firebase Realtime Database (JSON tree nodes) and Cloud Firestore (collections of documents).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must understand both *local* table creation (SQLite/Room) and *cloud-based* structured data creation (Firebase Firestore collections and Realtime Database nodes) since IoT mobile apps almost always require both offline buffering and cloud synchronization.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Smart Warehouse Ledger**
>
> Imagine you are running a smart warehouse where hundreds of IoT temperature sensors stream readings every second. You cannot afford to lose data when the internet goes down, and you also need the same data visible to managers in another city.
>
> - A **Table** is like a **structured register (ledger book)** with a fixed number of columns: *Timestamp*, *Sensor ID*, *Temperature*, *Humidity*. Each new reading is a new row in that register.
> - The **mobile device's local SQLite table** is the *on-site accountant's personal notebook* — fast, offline, always available.
> - The **Firebase cloud collection** is the *head office's central database* — accessible globally, synchronized in real time.
> - **"Creating a Table"** therefore means *designing the columns, choosing the data types, setting a primary key, and finally executing the CREATE command* so the notebook/ledger is ready to receive entries.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Table** | A logical structure of rows (records) and columns (fields) holding related data. |
| **Schema** | The formal blueprint describing table name, columns, data types, and constraints. |
| **Primary Key** | A column (or set of columns) that uniquely identifies each row. |
| **Auto-Increment** | An attribute that automatically generates a sequential numeric value for new rows. |
| **CRUD** | **C**reate, **R**ead, **U**pdate, **D**elete — the four fundamental data operations. |
| **DAO** | Data Access Object — a design pattern used in Android's Room library to abstract database access. |
| **Entity** | In Room, an Entity is a Kotlin/Java class annotated with `@Entity` that maps to a database table. |
| **Collection** | In Firestore, a Collection is a container for Documents (analogous to a table). |
| **Document** | In Firestore, a Document is a set of key-value pairs (analogous to a row). |
| **JSON Tree** | The hierarchical node-based data model used by Firebase Realtime Database. |
| **Offline Cache** | Local persistence layer enabling the app to function without network connectivity. |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Defaults to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Constants to Memorize for KTU Board Exams**
> - **SQLite maximum database size:** **140 TB** (terabytes) per database file.
> - **SQLite maximum columns per table:** **2000** (default limit, can be raised to 32766 at compile time).
> - **SQLite maximum rows per table:** **2^31** (approximately **2.147 billion** rows).
> - **Firebase Firestore document size limit:** **1 MiB** (1,048,576 bytes).
> - **Firebase Realtime Database single node data limit:** **16 MB** at the path level (older limits were smaller).
> - **Android default SQLite version shipped with API:** **SQLite 3.x** (version evolves per Android API level).
> - **Room library compile-time validation:** Database schema is checked at compile time, eliminating runtime SQL errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of "Creating Tables" in IoT Mobile Apps

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4**, table creation in IoT mobile apps is taught across three complementary layers. A premium understanding requires mastering all three.

### Pillar A — Local SQLite Table Creation (Raw SQL Approach)

The traditional Android approach uses the `SQLiteOpenHelper` class to override `onCreate()` and `onUpgrade()` lifecycle methods. The actual table is created using a **Data Definition Language (DDL)** statement.

**Generic SQL DDL Template for IoT Sensor Data:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE sensor_readings (
    id            INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    sensor_id     TEXT    NOT NULL,
    timestamp     INTEGER NOT NULL,
    temperature   REAL,
    humidity      REAL,
    latitude      REAL,
    longitude     REAL,
    is_synced     INTEGER DEFAULT 0
);
```

**Explanation of each element:**

- `CREATE TABLE <name>` — initiates table creation with a unique identifier.
- `id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT` — creates a **monotonically increasing unique identifier** for each row; `AUTOINCREMENT` guarantees monotonicity (without it, SQLite may reuse deleted IDs).
- `sensor_id TEXT NOT NULL` — string field that cannot be null; identifies the physical IoT sensor (e.g., `"DHT22-NODE-04"`).
- `timestamp INTEGER NOT NULL` — stores time as **Unix epoch milliseconds** (a long integer), which is the universally recommended IoT time format.
- `temperature REAL` — floating-point value from the sensor (Celsius).
- `humidity REAL` — floating-point value (percentage).
- `latitude REAL` / `longitude REAL` — optional GPS coordinates.
- `is_synced INTEGER DEFAULT 0` — a **flag column** indicating whether the row has been uploaded to the cloud; `0 = pending`, `1 = synced`. This is the *core* of offline-first IoT apps.

### Pillar B — Room Persistence Library (Modern Android Approach)

Room is the **Android Jetpack abstraction** over SQLite, introduced to eliminate boilerplate and enforce compile-time SQL validation.

**Three annotated components are required:**

1. **Entity (the Kotlin data class mapped to a table):**

```kotlin
@Entity(tableName = "iot_devices")
data class IoTDevice(
    @PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true) val id: Int = 0,
    @ColumnInfo(name = "device_uid") val deviceUid: String,
    @ColumnInfo(name = "device_type") val deviceType: String,
    @ColumnInfo(name = "last_seen")  val lastSeen: Long,
    @ColumnInfo(name = "battery_level") val batteryLevel: Float
)
```

2. **DAO (Data Access Object with table-operation methods):**

```kotlin
@Dao
interface IoTDeviceDao {
    @Insert(onConflict = OnConflictStrategy.REPLACE)
    suspend fun insert(device: IoTDevice): Long

    @Query("SELECT * FROM iot_devices ORDER BY last_seen DESC")
    fun getAllDevices(): LiveData<List<IoTDevice>>

    @Query("SELECT * FROM iot_devices WHERE device_type = :type")
    suspend fun getDevicesByType(type: String): List<IoTDevice>
}
```

3. **Database (the holder class):**

```kotlin
@Database(entities = [IoTDevice::class], version = 1, exportSchema = true)
abstract class IoTAppDatabase : RoomDatabase() {
    abstract fun iotDeviceDao(): IoTDeviceDao
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Room over raw SQLite for KTU Answers?**
> Room provides: (1) compile-time SQL verification, (2) automatic mapping between Kotlin objects and SQL rows, (3) built-in support for Kotlin coroutines and LiveData/Flow, and (4) automatic migration generation. Examiners awarding full marks in 14-mark questions look for these justifications.

### Pillar C — Firebase Cloud Table Creation

Cloud-based tables in Firebase come in two flavors.

**C-1: Firebase Realtime Database (RTDB)** — a giant JSON tree.

```json
{
  "sensors": {
    "sensor_01": {
      "temperature": 24.5,
      "humidity": 60.2,
      "timestamp": 1700000000000
    },
    "sensor_02": {
      "temperature": 22.1,
      "humidity": 58.7,
      "timestamp": 1700000060000
    }
  }
}
```

**C-2: Cloud Firestore** — collections of documents, with structured queries.

```javascript
// Collection: "sensor_readings"
// Document example:
{
  "sensorId": "DHT22-NODE-04",
  "timestamp": 1700000000000,
  "temperature": 24.5,
  "humidity": 60.2,
  "location": new GeoPoint(10.0261, 76.3125)
}
```

## 2.2 Data Type Mapping Cheat Sheet (SQLite ↔ Kotlin ↔ Firestore)

This is one of the **highest-yield KTU exam tables** — examiners love asking type-mapping questions.

| SQLite Type | Kotlin/Java Type | Firestore Type | Typical IoT Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `INTEGER` | `Long` / `Int` | `Number` (long) | Timestamps (epoch ms), counters, IDs |
| `REAL` | `Float` / `Double` | `Number` (double) | Temperature, humidity, voltage |
| `TEXT` | `String` | `String` | Sensor UID, device name, status |
| `BLOB` | `ByteArray` | `Bytes` | Raw binary payloads, firmware |
| `BOOLEAN` (stored as INTEGER 0/1) | `Boolean` | `Boolean` | Flags (is_synced, is_active) |
| (not native) | Custom `Date` | `Timestamp` | Human-readable time |
| (not native) | Custom class | `GeoPoint` | GPS latitude/longitude |
| (not native) | `List<String>` | `Array` | Arrays of tags or sensor IDs |

## 2.3 The IoT "Create Table" Operational Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why and How of each step in a real IoT mobile app:**

1. **Identify the data domain.** Decide *what* the IoT system measures (temperature, GPS, motion, energy).
2. **Design the schema.** Define column names, types, constraints, primary keys, and indexes.
3. **Choose the persistence layer.** Local-only, cloud-only, or hybrid (offline-first).
4. **Write the DDL / Annotate the Entity.** Encode the schema either as SQL or as Kotlin annotations.
5. **Initialize the database.** Instantiate the `RoomDatabase` or `FirebaseFirestore` singleton.
6. **Handle schema migration.** As IoT requirements evolve, add `Migration` objects to Room or use Firestore's versioned schema.
7. **Test the table.** Insert sample rows, query, and validate constraints.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Syntax Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Create Table (SQL) | `CREATE TABLE name (col TYPE CONSTRAINTS, ...);` | Raw SQLite |
| Primary Key (SQL) | `column TYPE PRIMARY KEY [AUTOINCREMENT]` | Unique row ID |
| Not Null (SQL) | `column TYPE NOT NULL` | Mandatory field |
| Default Value (SQL) | `column TYPE DEFAULT <value>` | Pre-fill value (e.g., `is_synced=0`) |
| Index (SQL) | `CREATE INDEX idx_name ON table(column);` | Speed up queries |
| Room Entity | `@Entity(tableName = "...")` | Map class to table |
| Room Primary Key | `@PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true)` | Auto-increment ID |
| Room Column | `@ColumnInfo(name = "...")` | Rename column |
| Room DAO Insert | `@Insert(onConflict = OnConflictStrategy.REPLACE)` | Insert row |
| Room DAO Query | `@Query("SELECT ... FROM ...")` | Read/filter |
| Firestore Collection Ref | `FirebaseFirestore.getInstance().collection("name")` | Reference a "table" |
| Firestore Add Document | `collectionRef.add(data)` | Insert a new "row" |
| RTDB Push Node | `databaseReference.push().setValue(data)` | Insert with auto-generated key |
| Epoch Time (formula) | `epochMs = System.currentTimeMillis()` | Convert date to integer |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** Local table stores soil moisture; cloud Firestore collection aggregates across farms.
- **Health Wearables:** RTDB tree mirrors heart-rate stream; SQLite caches last 24 hours for offline review.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Room database with timestamped telemetry, batch-synced to cloud when Wi-Fi returns.
- **Smart Homes:** Firestore document per device, updated on state change; Room stores last 100 events for quick UI rendering.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Designing an IoT Sensor Table Schema from Requirements

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** An IoT-enabled cold-chain logistics company needs a mobile app that records temperature from refrigerated trucks every 30 seconds. Trucks operate in zones with poor connectivity. Requirements:
> 1. Store readings locally even when offline.
> 2. Sync to cloud when connectivity returns.
> 3. Track truck ID, GPS, and battery level.
> 4. Each reading must be uniquely identifiable for audit purposes.

**Step 1 — Identify the data domain (the 'What').**

The mobile app must capture:
- Truck identifier (string, e.g., `"TRK-KL-07-2241"`)
- Temperature reading (float, Celsius, range −40 to +30)
- GPS coordinates (two floats)
- Battery level of the IoT sensor (float, percentage)
- Reading timestamp (long, epoch ms)
- Sync status (boolean flag)

**Step 2 — Choose data types per KTU standard mapping.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{truck\_id}     &\rightarrow \text{TEXT} \\
\text{temperature}   &\rightarrow \text{REAL} \\
\text{latitude}      &\rightarrow \text{REAL} \\
\text{longitude}     &\rightarrow \text{REAL} \\
\text{battery\_pct}  &\rightarrow \text{REAL} \\
\text{epoch\_ms}     &\rightarrow \text{INTEGER} \\
\text{is\_synced}    &\rightarrow \text{INTEGER (0/1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the primary key.**

A natural primary key (truck + timestamp) is risky because two readings from the same truck could theoretically share a millisecond. Hence, an **auto-increment surrogate key** is introduced:

$$
\text{id} = \text{INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply constraints.**

- `truck_id NOT NULL` — every reading must belong to a truck.
- `temperature NOT NULL` — temperature is the core metric.
- `epoch_ms NOT NULL` — time is mandatory for audit.
- `is_synced DEFAULT 0` — new rows start as unsynced.

**Step 5 — Add performance indexes.**

Since the sync process will query by `is_synced` to find pending rows, an index dramatically speeds it up:

```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_sync_status ON cold_chain_readings(is_synced);
```

**Step 6 — Final DDL statement (the 'synthesized schema'):**

```sql
CREATE TABLE cold_chain_readings (
    id           INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    truck_id     TEXT    NOT NULL,
    temperature  REAL    NOT NULL,
    latitude     REAL,
    longitude    REAL,
    battery_pct  REAL,
    epoch_ms     INTEGER NOT NULL,
    is_synced    INTEGER DEFAULT 0
);

CREATE INDEX idx_sync_status ON cold_chain_readings(is_synced);
```

**Step 7 — Room equivalent (Kotlin):**

```kotlin
@Entity(
    tableName = "cold_chain_readings",
    indices = [Index(value = ["is_synced"], name = "idx_sync_status")]
)
data class ColdChainReading(
    @PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true) val id: Long = 0,
    @ColumnInfo(name = "truck_id")     val truckId: String,
    @ColumnInfo(name = "temperature")  val temperature: Double,
    @ColumnInfo(name = "latitude")     val latitude: Double?,
    @ColumnInfo(name = "longitude")    val longitude: Double?,
    @ColumnInfo(name = "battery_pct")  val batteryPct: Float?,
    @ColumnInfo(name = "epoch_ms")     val epochMs: Long,
    @ColumnInfo(name = "is_synced")    val isSynced: Boolean = false
)
```

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Converting a Firestore "Pseudo-Table" Design into a Cloud Schema

**Step 1 — Decide between RTDB and Firestore.**

- Choose **Firestore** for complex querying, structured documents, and better offline persistence on mobile.
- Choose **RTDB** for ultra-low-latency streams (e.g., live joystick control).

**Step 2 — Define the collection (= "table") and document (= "row") shape.**

Let the collection be `cold_chain_readings` and each document contain a single reading.

**Step 3 — Encode the data using Firestore types:**

```kotlin
val reading = hashMapOf(
    "truck_id"    to "TRK-KL-07-2241",
    "temperature" to 4.5,
    "latitude"    to 10.0261,
    "longitude"   to 76.3125,
    "battery_pct" to 87.0,
    "epoch_ms"    to System.currentTimeMillis(),
    "is_synced"   to false
)

FirebaseFirestore.getInstance()
    .collection("cold_chain_readings")
    .add(reading)
```

**Step 4 — Derive the equivalent SQL DDL for cross-reference.**

Firestore is schemaless, but you may export documents to BigQuery; the inferred SQL DDL would mirror the Room schema above.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Local vs Cloud Table Creation

| Evaluation Criterion | SQLite / Room (Local) | Firebase Firestore (Cloud) | RTDB (Cloud JSON) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Offline Support | **Yes** (native) | **Yes** (built-in cache) | **Yes** (built-in) |
| Schema Enforcement | **Strict** (typed columns) | **Schemaless** (flexible) | **Schemaless** (flexible) |
| Query Power | **Full SQL** (joins, subqueries) | **Limited** (composite indexes needed) | **Very Limited** (shallow queries) |
| Latency | **Sub-millisecond** | Tens of ms | **Sub-10 ms** |
| Cost | **Free** (device storage) | Pay per read/write | Pay per GB transferred |
| Best For IoT Use | **On-device buffer & audit log** | **Multi-user dashboards & analytics** | **Real-time control signals** |
| Migration Handling | Manual `Migration` objects | Automatic, versioned docs | Manual JSON restructuring |
| Max Scale | ~2.1 B rows per table | Millions of documents | Hundreds of MB per node |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Creating a Table in Android (Complete Lifecycle)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this 7-step lifecycle for any 14-mark "Explain table creation" KTU question.**

1. **Subclass `SQLiteOpenHelper` OR define a Room `@Database`.**
2. **Override `onCreate(SQLiteDatabase db)`** — Room does this automatically via `@Entity`.
3. **Execute `db.execSQL("CREATE TABLE ...")`** inside `onCreate`.
4. **Override `onUpgrade(...)`** to handle schema evolution (Room uses `Migration` objects).
5. **Provide `getWritableDatabase()` / `getReadableDatabase()`** accessors.
6. **Wrap operations in a `ContentValues` / DAO method.**
7. **Close the database** in `onDestroy()` of the Activity or via a singleton.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level "Create Table" Flow in an IoT Mobile App

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IoT Sensor Hardware] --> B[BLE / MQTT / HTTP Gateway]
    B --> C[Mobile App Ingestion Layer]
    C --> D{Connectivity Check}
    D -- Online --> E[Direct Cloud Write]
    D -- Offline --> F[Local Table Insert]
    F --> G[SQLite via Room]
    E --> H[Firestore Collection]
    G --> I[Sync Service Worker]
    I -- Wi-Fi Restored --> J[Batch Upload to Cloud]
    H --> K[Dashboard / Analytics UI]
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Room Database Architecture for IoT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MobileApp[Mobile Application]
        UI[Activity / Compose UI]
        VM[ViewModel]
        Repo[Repository]
    end
    subgraph RoomLayer[Room Persistence Layer]
        DAO[IoTDeviceDao]
        DB[IoTAppDatabase]
        ENT[IoTDevice Entity]
    end
    subgraph CloudLayer[Firebase Layer]
        FS[Firestore Collection]
    end
    UI --> VM
    VM --> Repo
    Repo --> DAO
    DAO --> DB
    DB --- ENT
    Repo -. async sync .-> FS
```

## 4.3 Schema Design Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to Persist IoT Data] --> Q1{Offline Required?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Complex Queries?}
    Q1 -- No --> Cloud[Use Firestore Directly]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Room[Use Room with Local SQL Table]
    Q2 -- No --> RTDB[Use RTDB with JSON Tree]
    Room --> Hybrid[Add Sync Service to Cloud]
    RTDB --> Hybrid
    Hybrid --> Done[Unified IoT Data Pipeline]
```

## 4.4 Table-Creation Lifecycle (Android `SQLiteOpenHelper`)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Mobile App
    participant Helper as SQLiteOpenHelper
    participant DB as SQLite Database
    participant Code as Developer Code

    App->>Helper: new MyDbHelper(context)
    Helper->>DB: getWritableDatabase()
    DB-->>Helper: open
    alt First Install
        Helper->>DB: onCreate(SQLiteDatabase)
        DB->>DB: execSQL(CREATE TABLE ...)
    else App Updated
        Helper->>DB: onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase, oldV, newV)
        DB->>DB: ALTER TABLE / CREATE TABLE migration
    end
    Helper-->>App: ready
    App->>Code: insert(query, params)
    Code->>DB: insert / query
    DB-->>Code: cursor / rowId
    App->>Helper: close()
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: Define a database table in the context of mobile IoT applications. List any four SQL data types supported by Android SQLite.**

**Model Answer:**

A **database table** in a mobile IoT application is a structured, two-dimensional data store consisting of rows (records/tuples) and columns (fields/attributes) that persistently holds IoT telemetry such as sensor readings, device metadata, and sync status. Each table has a defined schema specifying column names, data types, and constraints.

Four SQL data types supported by Android SQLite are:
1. **INTEGER** — used for primary keys, timestamps, boolean flags (0/1).
2. **REAL** — floating-point values such as temperature, humidity, GPS coordinates.
3. **TEXT** — strings such as sensor UID, device name, status messages.
4. **BLOB** — binary large objects such as raw audio, images, or firmware.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always mention that SQLite is *typeless by default* (manifest typing) but the declared type is used for type affinity. This demonstrates depth and earns full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: Differentiate between Firebase Realtime Database and Cloud Firestore with respect to data model and querying.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Firebase Realtime Database (RTDB) | Cloud Firestore |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Model** | One large **JSON tree**; nodes can be nested arbitrarily | Hierarchical **collections of documents** (similar to tables/rows) |
| **Querying** | Shallow queries; limited filtering; no joins | Compound queries with `whereEqualTo`, `orderBy`, `limit`; composite indexes |
| **Offline Support** | Built-in disk persistence | Built-in multi-region offline persistence |
| **Scalability** | Single-region sharding | Auto-scaling multi-region |
| **Best Use in IoT** | Low-latency control signals (e.g., real-time actuator commands) | Structured telemetry storage and analytics dashboards |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q: (a)** Design and explain the schema of an SQLite table named `iot_sensor_data` for a mobile app that records temperature, humidity, and GPS coordinates from an IoT weather station. Justify the choice of primary key and include appropriate constraints and indexes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of the Android Room Persistence Library. Show how an Entity, DAO, and Database are wired together to create a table and perform CRUD operations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Identify the data entities and constraints:**

The mobile IoT app must store continuous weather telemetry. Each reading has:
- A unique identifier
- Timestamp
- Temperature (°C)
- Humidity (%)
- GPS coordinates
- Sync status flag

**Step 2 — Write the SQL DDL statement:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE iot_sensor_data (
    id           INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    station_id   TEXT    NOT NULL,
    temperature  REAL    NOT NULL,
    humidity     REAL    NOT NULL,
    latitude     REAL,
    longitude    REAL,
    epoch_ms     INTEGER NOT NULL,
    is_synced    INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    CHECK (humidity  >= 0 AND humidity  <= 100),
    CHECK (temperature >= -50 AND temperature <= 70)
);
```

**Step 3 — Justify the primary key choice:**

A composite primary key of `(station_id, epoch_ms)` was considered, but two readings from the same station could in theory share a millisecond during high-frequency sampling. Hence, a **surrogate auto-increment integer primary key** is used, providing guaranteed uniqueness and minimal storage overhead (8 bytes per row).

**Step 4 — Add an index for performance:**

```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_station_time ON iot_sensor_data(station_id, epoch_ms DESC);
```

**Step 5 — Justify constraints:**

- `NOT NULL` on `station_id`, `temperature`, `humidity`, `epoch_ms` ensures no incomplete readings.
- `CHECK` constraints enforce physical sensor limits (humidity 0–100%, temperature −50 to +70 °C).
- `is_synced DEFAULT 0` ensures new readings are marked unsynced by default, enabling the offline-first sync model.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Architecture of Room Persistence Library:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UI[Activity / Fragment / Compose Screen] --> VM[ViewModel]
    VM --> Repo[Repository]
    Repo --> DAO[IoTDeviceDao - Annotated Interface]
    DAO --> DB[IoTAppDatabase - RoomDatabase subclass]
    DB --> SQL[(SQLite Database File)]
    DB --> Ent1[IoTDevice Entity]
    DB --> Ent2[SensorReading Entity]
```

**Explanation of each layer:**

1. **Entity:** A Kotlin data class annotated with `@Entity`. Room reads the annotations at compile time and generates the table schema. Example:
   ```kotlin
   @Entity(tableName = "iot_devices")
   data class IoTDevice(
       @PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true) val id: Int = 0,
       val deviceUid: String,
       val lastSeen: Long
   )
   ```
2. **DAO (Data Access Object):** An interface annotated with `@Dao` whose methods are annotated with `@Insert`, `@Query`, `@Update`, `@Delete`. Room generates the implementation at compile time.
3. **Database:** An abstract class extending `RoomDatabase`, annotated with `@Database(entities = [...], version = N)`. Acts as the singleton entry point.
4. **Repository / ViewModel / UI:** Higher architectural layers that consume the DAO via coroutines, LiveData, or Flow.

**CRUD operation flow:**

- **Create:** `dao.insert(entity)` → `@Insert` annotation → SQL `INSERT` → returns row ID.
- **Read:** `dao.getAll(): LiveData<List<X>>` → `@Query("SELECT * FROM ...")` → cursor → mapped to objects.
- **Update:** `dao.update(entity)` → `@Update` annotation → SQL `UPDATE`.
- **Delete:** `dao.delete(entity)` → `@Delete` annotation → SQL `DELETE`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Do not forget to declare the DAO inside the `RoomDatabase` subclass via an `abstract fun` returning the DAO. Losing this loses 2 marks.
> 2. Do not instantiate the `RoomDatabase` multiple times — it must be a **singleton** (use a `companion object` with double-checked locking or a Dagger/Hilt module).
> 3. Always increment the `version` number and provide `Migration` objects when the schema changes, or supply `fallbackToDestructiveMigration()` only in development builds.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Internal Choice)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q: (a)** Explain the steps to create a table in Firebase Cloud Firestore from a mobile app. Include a code snippet and describe the data model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast local SQLite (Room) table creation with cloud Firestore collection creation in IoT mobile apps, highlighting at least six points. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Add Firebase to the Android project.**

Include the Google Services plugin in `build.gradle (project)` and the `firebase-bom` and `firebase-firestore-ktx` dependencies in `build.gradle (app)`:

```gradle
implementation platform('com.google.firebase:firebase-bom:32.7.0')
implementation 'com.google.firebase:firebase-firestore-ktx'
```

**Step 2 — Initialize Firestore.**

```kotlin
val db = FirebaseFirestore.getInstance()
```

**Step 3 — Define a collection reference (the "table" name).**

```kotlin
val sensorReadings = db.collection("sensor_readings")
```

**Step 4 — Build a Document (the "row").**

```kotlin
val reading = hashMapOf(
    "sensor_id"   to "DHT22-NODE-04",
    "temperature" to 24.5,
    "humidity"    to 60.2,
    "epoch_ms"    to System.currentTimeMillis(),
    "is_synced"   to true
)
```

**Step 5 — Add the document to the collection.**

```kotlin
sensorReadings
    .add(reading)
    .addOnSuccessListener { docRef ->
        Log.d("Firestore", "Document added with ID: ${docRef.id}")
    }
    .addOnFailureListener { e ->
        Log.w("Firestore", "Error adding document", e)
    }
```

**Data Model Description:**

The Firestore data model is hierarchical: a **collection** (`sensor_readings`) contains multiple **documents**, each identified by a unique auto-generated ID. Every document holds a set of key-value pairs that can be of types: `String`, `Number`, `Boolean`, `Array`, `Map`, `Timestamp`, `GeoPoint`, or `Null`. Unlike SQL tables, documents can be heterogeneous (no enforced schema) unless Firestore Security Rules are added.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparison Table (minimum six points):**

| # | Local SQLite / Room | Cloud Firestore |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Storage location:** Device's internal storage (file-based) | **Storage location:** Google Cloud Platform, multi-region replicated |
| 2 | **Schema:** Strict, declared at compile time via `@Entity` | **Schema:** Schemaless by default; enforced via Security Rules |
| 3 | **Query language:** Full SQL with joins, subqueries, aggregations | **Query language:** NoSQL with `where`, `orderBy`, `limit`; joins not supported |
| 4 | **Offline support:** Native and automatic | **Offline support:** Built-in cache, transparent to the app |
| 5 | **Latency:** Sub-millisecond (local) | **Latency:** Tens of milliseconds (network round-trip) |
