- **CoAP** is like a **walkie-talkie with predefined codes** — a small device presses a button, says a short coded message like *"GET /temp"* over a low-power radio, and expects a short reply, but if the other side is asleep, the message may simply be lost. It is **lightweight, RESTful, and unreliable by default**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition Box**
> - **MQTT** = Message Queuing Telemetry Transport — a **TCP-based, broker-mediated, publish/subscribe** protocol designed by IBM (1999) for low-bandwidth, high-latency, or unreliable networks.
> - **HTTP** = HyperText Transfer Protocol — a **TCP-based, stateless, request/response** protocol from the WWW ecosystem, used in IoT primarily for cloud REST APIs and webhook integrations.
> - **CoAP** = Constrained Application Protocol — a **UDP-based, RESTful, request/response** protocol standardized by the IETF (RFC 7252) for severely constrained nodes and networks (6LoWPAN).

---

### 1.3 Why These Three Protocols?

The IoT ecosystem is **highly fragmented** — devices range from mains-powered gateways to coin-cell sensors. No single protocol fits all. The KTU 2024 scheme specifically asks students to understand these three because they represent **three orthogonal design philosophies** in IoT application-layer communication:

| Design Philosophy | Representative Protocol | Transport | Primary Use Case in IoT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Web-centric / RPC | HTTP | TCP | Cloud APIs, mobile-to-server sync |
| Broker-centric / Pub-Sub | MQTT | TCP | Telemetry, sensor fan-out, mobile push |
| Constrained-centric / REST | CoAP | UDP | Sensor-to-sensor, low-power mesh |

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Ports (KTU-Exam Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorize Port Numbers & Metrics**
> - **HTTP** operates on **TCP port 80** (unencrypted) and **TCP port 443** (HTTPS via TLS). Standard payload tolerance: **kilobytes to megabytes** per message.
> - **MQTT** operates on **TCP port 1883** (unencrypted) and **TCP port 8883** (MQTTS via TLS). Minimum header overhead: **2 bytes** (fixed header). QoS levels: **0, 1, 2**.
> - **CoAP** operates on **UDP port 5683** (unencrypted) and **UDP port 5684** (CoAPS via DTLS). Minimum header: **4 bytes**. Default message size limit: a few hundred bytes to avoid IP fragmentation.

---

### 1.5 Pre-Requisite Vocabulary for This Module

Before proceeding, lock in these four KTU terms:

1. **Client** — Any device or app that initiates communication (a sensor, a phone, a cloud service).
2. **Broker** — A central server (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX) that routes MQTT messages. *HTTP and CoAP do not use brokers.*
3. **Topic** — A hierarchical, slash-delimited string (e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`) used in MQTT to filter and route messages.
4. **Resource** — A uniquely addressable entity on a CoAP server (e.g., `/sensors/temp`), analogous to a URL in HTTP.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 MQTT — Deep Architecture

MQTT follows the **Publish/Subscribe pattern** mediated by a **Broker**. The decoupling is the heart of the design.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

1. **Connection Establishment** — A client opens a TCP connection to the broker on **port 1883** and sends a `CONNECT` packet containing a unique `ClientID`, optional username/password, `KeepAlive` interval, and `Clean Session` flag.
2. **Subscription Phase** — The client subscribes to one or more **topics** using `SUBSCRIBE` packets, optionally specifying a **QoS level** per topic.
3. **Publication Phase** — A publishing client sends a `PUBLISH` packet to the broker with a topic name, payload, and QoS level. The broker **forwards** the packet to *all currently subscribed clients* matching the topic (including wildcards `+` and `#`).
4. **Acknowledgement (QoS dependent)** — At QoS 1, the broker sends `PUBACK`; at QoS 2, a four-step handshake (`PUBREC` → `PUBREL` → `PUBCOMP`) guarantees exactly-once delivery.
5. **Keep-Alive** — A `PINGREQ` / `PINGRESP` heartbeat maintains the connection idle.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of MQTT:** The publish/subscribe decoupling means a sensor does **not** need to know the IP address of every consumer. This is critical for mobile apps that roam across networks — the broker handles routing regardless of where the phone currently is.

**MQTT Control Packet Structure (2-byte fixed header):**

$$\text{Fixed Header} = \underbrace{1 \text{ byte}}_{\text{Control Packet Type} \,(4 \text{ bits}) + \text{Flags}\,(4 \text{ bits})} + \underbrace{1\text{–}4 \text{ bytes}}_{\text{Remaining Length (variable encoding)}}$$

The **Remaining Length** field uses a variable-byte scheme where each byte carries 7 data bits and 1 continuation bit, allowing lengths up to **268,435,455 bytes (~256 MB)**.

**QoS Levels — The Core "Why":**

| QoS Level | Acronym | Guarantee | Use Case Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **QoS 0** | At most once | Fire-and-forget, no ack | Periodic temperature readings where loss is tolerable |
| **QoS 1** | At least once | Possible duplicates | Mobile push notifications |
| **QoS 2** | Exactly once | No duplicates, 4-step handshake | Billing, alarm triggers |

---

### 2.2 HTTP — Deep Architecture

HTTP is a **textual, request/response** protocol. Every transaction has a clearly defined **client** and **server**.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

1. **TCP Handshake** — A three-way handshake establishes a connection on port 80/443.
2. **Request Line** — Client sends a request: `METHOD URI HTTP/version` (e.g., `POST /api/sensors HTTP/1.1`).
3. **Headers** — Multiple text-based headers follow: `Host`, `Content-Type`, `Authorization`, `Content-Length`, etc.
4. **Body** — Optional payload (JSON, XML, form-data, binary).
5. **Response** — Server returns: `HTTP/version STATUS_CODE Reason-Phrase`, headers, and body.
6. **Connection Management** — HTTP/1.1 introduced `keep-alive`; HTTP/2 multiplexes; HTTP/3 uses QUIC over UDP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of HTTP in IoT:** Mobile apps are **already HTTP-native** — every SDK, every REST client, every backend framework speaks HTTP. Thus, HTTP dominates the **mobile-app-to-cloud** segment, even though it is inefficient for **device-to-device** constrained links.

**Key HTTP Methods (Verbs) for IoT:**

- `GET` — Retrieve a resource (e.g., read sensor value)
- `POST` — Create a resource / send data
- `PUT` — Update / replace a resource
- `PATCH` — Partial update
- `DELETE` — Remove a resource

**Common Status Codes for IoT APIs:**

- `200 OK` — Success
- `201 Created` — Resource created
- `400 Bad Request` — Malformed payload
- `401 Unauthorized` — Invalid/missing token
- `404 Not Found` — Unknown resource
- `500 Internal Server Error` — Backend failure

---

### 2.3 CoAP — Deep Architecture

CoAP is essentially a **RESTful protocol redesigned for constrained nodes** over **UDP**.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

1. **UDP Datagram** — CoAP messages are carried directly in UDP datagrams on port 5683. There is **no connection handshake** (no SYN/ACK), saving energy.
2. **Message Format** — A 4-byte binary header followed by optional token, options, and payload.
3. **Request/Response** — A CoAP client sends a request with a method (`GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`) to a resource URI. The server replies with a response code (e.g., `2.05 Content`, analogous to `200 OK`).
4. **Confirmable vs Non-confirmable** — Each message is marked **CON** (confirmable — requires ACK) or **NON** (non-confirmable — fire-and-forget). This is CoAP's equivalent of MQTT's QoS, but more granular and tied to the message rather than the topic.
5. **Observe Pattern (RFC 7641)** — A client can register interest in a resource; whenever the resource state changes, the server **pushes** the new value. This is the CoAP analogue of MQTT's pub-sub, without a broker.
6. **Block-Wise Transfer (RFC 7959)** — Allows large payloads to be split across multiple UDP datagrams.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of CoAP:** UDP avoids the heavy TCP handshake, which is a major energy cost on battery-powered 6LoWPAN sensors. The binary header (4 bytes minimum) is roughly **5–10× smaller** than a typical HTTP header.

**CoAP Message Format (Binary Header):**

$$\text{CoAP Header} = \underbrace{4 \text{ bytes}}_{\text{Ver} \vert \text{T} \vert \text{TKL} \vert \text{Code} \vert \text{Message ID}}$$

- `Ver` = Version (currently 1)
- `T` = Type (CON, NON, ACK, RST)
- `TKL` = Token Length (0–8 bytes)
- `Code` = Class (3 bits) . Detail (5 bits), e.g., `0.01 GET`, `2.05 Content`
- `Message ID` = Used for duplicate detection and ACK matching

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Comparison Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — at least one Part-B question in KTU ESE asks for a tabular comparison of two or all three protocols.**

| Parameter | HTTP | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transport Layer** | TCP | TCP | UDP |
| **Default Port (clear)** | 80 | 1883 | 5683 |
| **Default Port (TLS)** | 443 | 8883 | 5684 |
| **Architecture** | Request/Response | Publish/Subscribe (Broker) | Request/Response |
| **Header Size (min)** | ~200–800 bytes (text) | 2 bytes (binary) | 4 bytes (binary) |
| **Message Pattern** | Client–Server | Many-to-Many via broker | Client–Server (1-to-1 typically) |
| **Reliability** | TCP guarantees | QoS 0/1/2 | CON/NON + retransmission |
| **Security** | HTTPS (TLS) | MQTTS (TLS) | CoAPS (DTLS) |
| **Typical Payload** | JSON / XML | Binary or JSON | CBOR / JSON / XML |
| **Power Efficiency** | Poor for constrained nodes | Good | Excellent |
| **Mobile App Use Case** | Cloud REST API, webhooks | Real-time telemetry, push | Direct device-to-phone in mesh |
| **Standard Body** | IETF / W3C | OASIS / ISO/IEC 20922 | IETF (RFC 7252) |

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — When to Choose What?

- **Choose MQTT** when many mobile clients need **asynchronous, push-based** updates from sensors, when network is intermittent (e.g., cellular IoT), or when topic-based filtering is required.
- **Choose HTTP** when the mobile app talks to a **cloud REST API**, when a human-readable API contract is needed, or when existing web infrastructure is reused.
- **Choose CoAP** when devices communicate **directly without a cloud**, when 6LoWPAN / RPL / LoRaWAN networks are used, when power and bandwidth are critically constrained, or when the **observe** pattern eliminates polling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Pattern (KTU-Favourite):** A typical IoT pipeline uses **CoAP or MQTT from sensor → gateway**, then **MQTT or HTTP from gateway → cloud**, and finally **HTTP REST from cloud → mobile app**. The mobile app almost never speaks CoAP directly to a sensor unless in a local mesh.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: MQTT Fixed Header Byte-Encoding

The MQTT specification uses a **Variable Length Encoding** to compress the "Remaining Length" field. We derive the maximum representable value and the per-byte rule.

**Step 1 — Define the rule.**

Each byte in the Remaining Length contributes 7 bits of data. The 8th bit (MSB) is a **continuation bit** — `1` means *"more bytes follow"*, `0` means *"this is the last byte"*.

$$\text{For } n \text{ bytes:} \quad \text{MaxValue} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 2^{7i} \cdot 127 = 127 \cdot \left(1 + 2^7 + 2^{14} + \dots + 2^{7(n-1)}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Geometric series sum.**

Using the geometric series formula $\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} r^i = \dfrac{r^n - 1}{r - 1}$ with $r = 2^7 = 128$:

$$\text{MaxValue}_n = 127 \cdot \frac{128^n - 1}{128 - 1} = 128^n - 1$$

**Step 3 — Apply to maximum of 4 bytes (MQTT spec).**

$$\text{MaxValue}_4 = 128^4 - 1 = 268{,}435{,}456 - 1 = 268{,}435{,}455 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Maximum MQTT Remaining Length} = 268{,}435{,}455 \text{ bytes } \approx 256 \text{ MB}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

This explains why MQTT, despite a **2-byte minimum header**, can theoretically tunnel multi-megabyte payloads — it is not a "tiny-only" protocol. The constraint is on the **topic name** and **control packet overhead**, not on payload size.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: CoAP Header Bit-Field Layout

We derive the **4-byte binary CoAP header** from first principles.

**Step 1 — Lay out the bit fields.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte 0} &= \underbrace{2 \text{ bits}}_{\text{Ver}} \; \underbrace{2 \text{ bits}}_{\text{T (Type)}} \; \underbrace{4 \text{ bits}}_{\text{TKL (Token Length, 0-8)}} \\
\text{Byte 1} &= \underbrace{3 \text{ bits}}_{\text{Class}} \; \underbrace{5 \text{ bits}}_{\text{Detail}} \quad (\text{together form the Code field, e.g., } 0.01 = \text{GET}) \\
\text{Byte 2-3} &= \underbrace{16 \text{ bits}}_{\text{Message ID}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Total header bits.**

$$\text{Total bits} = 8 + 8 + 16 = 32 \text{ bits} = 4 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Token overhead derivation.**

The token (used to match a response to a request when multiple are in-flight) is a client-chosen opaque string of `0–8` bytes, copied from request to response. Total minimum overhead for a tokenless request with no options is therefore:

$$\text{Overhead}_{\min} = 4 \text{ bytes (header)} + 0 \text{ (token)} + 0 \text{ (options)} + 1 \text{ byte (payload marker) if payload present}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with HTTP minimum.**

A minimal HTTP/1.1 GET request carries:

$$\text{HTTP/1.1 GET} \rightarrow \text{Request line } (\sim 20 \text{ B}) + \text{Host header } (\sim 30 \text{ B}) + \text{User-Agent} + \text{Accept} + \text{blank line CRLF}$$

$$\text{Overhead}_{\text{HTTP, min}} \approx 200 \text{–}400 \text{ bytes (text)}$$

**Step 5 — Overhead ratio.**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{\text{HTTP overhead}}{\text{CoAP overhead}} = \frac{\sim 300 \text{ B}}{\sim 4 \text{ B}} \approx 75\times$$

$$\boxed{\text{HTTP carries roughly 75 times more header bytes than CoAP for an equivalent GET.}}$$

This is why CoAP is the protocol of choice on **6LoWPAN** networks where every byte burns milliwatts.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: HTTP vs MQTT vs CoAP — Energy Cost Approximation

A useful KTU-style analytical exercise is to estimate the **per-message energy cost** of each protocol on a Class-1 constrained device (radio ~50 mW, CPU active ~30 mW).

**Step 1 — Transmission energy model.**

$$E_{\text{tx}} = (P_{\text{radio}} + P_{\text{CPU}}) \cdot t_{\text{tx}}$$

where $t_{\text{tx}} = \dfrac{\text{Total bytes} \cdot 8}{R_{\text{bitrate}}}$.

**Step 2 — Plug in typical numbers.** Assume a 50 kbps radio, 100-byte application payload.

| Protocol | Header (B) | + Payload (B) | Total (B) | Total Bits | Time @ 50 kbps |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| HTTP | 300 | 100 | 400 | 3200 | 64 ms |
| MQTT (QoS 0) | 8 | 100 | 108 | 864 | 17.3 ms |
| CoAP (CON) | 4 | 100 | 104 | 832 | 16.6 ms |

**Step 3 — Energy per transmission.**

$$E_{\text{tx}} = (0.050 + 0.030) \text{ W} \cdot t_{\text{tx}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{HTTP}} &= 0.080 \cdot 0.064 = 5.12 \text{ mJ} \\
E_{\text{MQTT}} &= 0.080 \cdot 0.0173 = 1.384 \text{ mJ} \\
E_{\text{CoAP}} &= 0.080 \cdot 0.0166 = 1.328 \text{ mJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Include retransmission penalty.**

For QoS 1 MQTT, two packets flow (publish + puback), and on a 10% loss network the expected transmissions per successful message grow. CoAP over UDP with retransmission shows similar dynamics. HTTP on TCP may cost even more due to connection setup if not using keep-alive.

**Conclusion:** CoAP and MQTT are roughly **3.7–3.9× more energy-efficient per message** than HTTP for small IoT payloads on a constrained device.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (KTU-Exam Favourite)

Use this matrix to justify protocol selection in viva or design questions:

| Constraint | Best Protocol | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Intermittent cellular link | **MQTT** | Persistent session, QoS 1/2 resumes after disconnect |
| 6LoWPAN / RPL mesh, battery sensor | **CoAP** | UDP, tiny header, observe pattern, no broker |
| Mobile app talking to AWS / Firebase | **HTTP/HTTPS** | Native SDKs, REST, OAuth 2.0 |
| 1-to-many fan-out of telemetry | **MQTT** | Single publish reaches all subscribers |
| Need for end-to-end REST semantics with low power | **CoAP** | Direct mapping from HTTP verbs |
| Many fire-and-forget readings (e.g., vibration) | **MQTT QoS 0** or **CoAP NON** | Lowest overhead |
| Strict exactly-once needed (alarms, billing) | **MQTT QoS 2** | Only protocol with formal 4-step handshake |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The IoT Protocol Stack — Where Each Protocol Lives

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER"]
    mqttNode["MQTT Port 1883 TCP"]
    httpNode["HTTP Port 80 TCP"]
    coapNode["CoAP Port 5683 UDP"]
    transport["TRANSPORT LAYER"]
    tcpNode["TCP Reliable Stream"]
    udpNode["UDP Datagram"]
    network["NETWORK LAYER"]
    ipNode["IPv4 or IPv6 often 6LoWPAN"]
    link["LINK LAYER"]
    linkNode["Wi Fi BLE LoRa Zigbee Ethernet Cellular"]

    appLayer --> mqttNode
    appLayer --> httpNode
    appLayer --> coapNode
    mqttNode --> tcpNode
    httpNode --> tcpNode
    coapNode --> udpNode
    tcpNode --> network
    udpNode --> network
    network --> ipNode
    ipNode --> linkNode
    linkNode --> link
```

> **Reading Guide:** MQTT and HTTP both ride on **TCP** (reliable but heavier), while CoAP rides on **UDP** (lightweight, unreliable by default, retransmission handled at app layer).

---

### 4.2 MQTT Publish/Subscribe Flow with Broker

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SENSOR as Temperature Sensor
    participant BROKER as MQTT Broker Mosquitto
    participant PHONE as Mobile App
    participant DASH as Cloud Dashboard

    SENSOR->>BROKER: CONNECT ClientID sensor01
    BROKER-->>SENSOR: CONNACK
    SENSOR->>BROKER: SUBSCRIBE home/bedroom/temp QoS 1
    BROKER-->>SENSOR: SUBACK

    PHONE->>BROKER: CONNECT ClientID phone99
    BROKER-->>PHONE: CONNACK
    PHONE->>BROKER: SUBSCRIBE home/+/temp QoS 1
    BROKER-->>PHONE: SUBACK

    SENSOR->>BROKER: PUBLISH home/bedroom/temp payload 23.4 QoS 1
    BROKER->>PHONE: PUBLISH home/bedroom/temp payload 23.4
    PHONE-->>BROKER: PUBACK
    BROKER-->>SENSOR: PUBACK

    BROKER->>DASH: PUBLISH home/bedroom/temp payload 23.4
    DASH-->>BROKER: PUBACK
```

> **Reading Guide:** The sensor does **not** know the phone exists. The broker is the only component that knows all subscribers. This is the core decoupling that makes MQTT ideal for mobile apps that come and go from the network.

---

### 4.3 HTTP Request/Response Flow in an IoT Mobile App

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant APP as Mobile App
    participant API as Cloud REST API
    participant DB as Backend Database

    APP->>API: GET /api/v1/sensors/temp01 HTTP/1.1
    API->>DB: SELECT value FROM readings WHERE id=temp01
    DB-->>API: row 23.4
    API-->>APP: 200 OK Content-Type application/json {"value":23.4}
    APP->>API: POST /api/v1/sensors/temp01 {"value":23.6}
    API->>DB: INSERT or UPDATE
    API-->>APP: 201 Created
```

> **Reading Guide:** Notice the **synchronous** nature — the mobile app blocks until the response. This is the opposite of MQTT's asynchronous model. HTTP requires the app to be the active initiator; it cannot receive data without polling unless WebSockets/SSE are added.

---

### 4.4 CoAP Observe Pattern (Broker-less Pub-Sub)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PHONE as Mobile App CoAP Client
    participant SENSOR as Temp Sensor CoAP Server

    PHONE->>SENSOR: CON GET /sensors/temp Token abc Observe 0
    SENSOR-->>PHONE: ACK 2.05 Content payload 23.4
    Note over SENSOR: Temperature changes to 23.6
    SENSOR->>PHONE: CON 2.05 Content payload 23.6 Token abc
    PHONE-->>SENSOR: ACK
    Note over SENSOR: Temperature changes to 23.8
    SENSOR->>PHONE: NON 2.05 Content payload 23.8 Token abc
    Note over PHONE: No ACK required for NON
```

> **Reading Guide:** CoAP's **Observe** extension (RFC 7641) gives MQTT-like push semantics **without a broker**, by letting the server itself push state changes to the registered client. The token ties each notification back to the original subscription.

---

### 4.5 End-to-End IoT Architecture Showing All Three Protocols

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensor["SENSOR CoAP UDP"]
    gw["GATEWAY WIFI or Cellular"]
    broker["MQTT BROKER Cloud"]
    cloud["CLOUD REST API HTTP"]
    app["MOBILE APP HTTP and MQTT"]
    dash["DASHBOARD HTTP"]

    sensor -->|coap observe| gw
    gw -->|mqtt publish qos 1| broker
    broker -->|mqtt subscribe| cloud
    cloud -->|https rest| app
    cloud -->|https rest| dash
    app -->|mqtt subscribe push| broker
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is the **canonical hybrid IoT stack** taught in KTU — CoAP for the constrained sensor-to-gateway hop, MQTT for the asynchronous gateway-to-broker hop, and HTTP/REST for the mobile-app-to-cloud hop. Each protocol is used **where it is strongest**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any three differences between MQTT and HTTP as IoT application-layer protocols. State one typical use case for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No | MQTT | HTTP |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses **publish/subscribe** architecture with a central broker | Uses **request/response** architecture (client-server) |
| 2 | Operates on **TCP port 1883**, minimum header of **2 bytes** | Operates on **TCP port 80**, header is textual and **200–800 bytes** |
| 3 | Supports **QoS levels 0, 1, 2** for delivery guarantees | Relies on TCP for reliability; no application-layer QoS |
| 4 | Designed for **constrained, intermittent** networks | Designed for the **WWW**, heavy for IoT devices |

**Use cases (1 mark):**
- **MQTT** → Real-time sensor telemetry and mobile push notifications (e.g., AWS IoT Core, HiveMQ).
- **HTTP** → Mobile app communicating with cloud REST APIs (e.g., Firebase, custom Node.js backend).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is CoAP? Mention the default port and the transport protocol it uses.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** is an IETF-standardized (RFC 7252) application-layer protocol designed for **resource-constrained devices and networks** (e.g., 6LoWPAN, LoRaWAN). It emulates HTTP REST semantics (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) but uses a compact binary header.

**Transport (1 mark):** CoAP runs over **UDP** (User Datagram Protocol), which is connectionless and lightweight, making it suitable for low-power nodes.

**Default Port (1 mark):** CoAP uses **UDP port 5683** for unencrypted communication and **UDP port 5684** for CoAPS (DTLS-encrypted) communication.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **MQTT publish/subscribe architecture**. List all three QoS levels and the corresponding acknowledgement mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **MQTT and CoAP** with respect to transport protocol, header size, message pattern, reliability mechanism, and a typical IoT use case. Present the comparison in a table. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MQTT Publish/Subscribe Architecture (7 marks)**

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
+-----------+         +-------------+         +-----------+
| Publisher |-------->| MQTT Broker |-------->| Subscriber|
|  Sensor   |  topic  |  Mosquitto  |  topic  | Mobile App|
+-----------+         +-------------+         +-----------+
                           |
                           v
                     +-----------+
                     |Subscriber2|
                     |  Cloud    |
                     +-----------+
```

**Explanation (3 marks):**

1. The **publisher** (e.g., a temperature sensor) sends a `PUBLISH` packet to the broker with a topic (e.g., `home/livingroom/temp`).
2. The **broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ) maintains a list of subscriptions and routes the message to all clients subscribed to a matching topic (wildcards `+` for single level, `#` for multi-level).
3. **Subscribers** (mobile app, cloud dashboard) receive the published payload and may send acknowledgements based on the QoS level.

**QoS Levels (1 mark):**

- **QoS 0** — At most once: no acknowledgement; fire-and-forget.
- **QoS 1** — At least once: `PUBACK` from receiver; duplicates possible.
- **QoS 2** — Exactly once: 4-step handshake (`PUBREC` → `PUBREL` → `PUBCOMP`) ensures no duplicates.

---

**(b) MQTT vs CoAP Comparison (7 marks)** — Present in a table (1 mark for table, 6 marks for rows):

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP (port 1883/8883) | UDP (port 5683/5684) |
| **Header Size** | 2 bytes (fixed) + variable | 4 bytes (fixed binary) + options |
| **Message Pattern** | Publish/Subscribe via broker | Request/Response (REST-like), optional Observe |
| **Reliability** | QoS 0/1/2 with broker-side store-and-forward | CON (with retransmit) / NON (fire-and-forget); Message ID for dedup |
| **Security** | TLS (MQTTS) | DTLS (CoAPS) |
| **Architecture** | Many-to-many through broker | Typically 1-to-1 client-server |
| **Use Case** | Cellular telemetry, mobile push, fan-out | 6LoWPAN mesh sensors, low-power battery nodes |
| **Standard Body** | OASIS / ISO/IEC 20922 | IETF RFC 7252 |
| **Connection** | Persistent TCP with KeepAlive | Stateless UDP, optional Observe |
| **Power Efficiency** | Good (TCP overhead still present) | Excellent (no handshake) |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Describe the **HTTP request/response model** as used in IoT mobile applications. List any four HTTP methods with their purpose and any four common status codes with meaning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **CoAP**? Explain the **Observe pattern** of CoAP. How does it achieve publish/subscribe-like semantics without a broker? Illustrate with a message-exchange diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HTTP Request/Response in IoT (7 marks)**

**Model Description (3 marks):**
HTTP is a **textual, stateless, request/response protocol** that runs over TCP (port 80) or TLS (port 443 for HTTPS). In IoT, the **mobile app acts as the HTTP client** and the **cloud server (REST API) acts as the HTTP server**. A typical transaction:

1. App opens a TCP connection (or reuses a keep-alive connection).
2. App sends a request line + headers + optional body.
3. Server processes the request and returns a status line + headers + body.
4. The app parses the JSON/XML response and updates the UI.

**Four HTTP Methods (2 marks):**

| Method | Purpose | IoT Example |
|---|---|---|
| `GET` | Retrieve a resource | `GET /api/sensors/temp01` → read current temperature |
| `POST` | Create a resource / send data | `POST /api/sensors/temp01 {"value":23.4}` |
| `PUT` | Replace a resource | `PUT /api/devices/light01 {"state":"ON"}` |
| `DELETE` | Remove a resource | `DELETE /api/alerts/alert99` |

**Four Status Codes (2 marks):**

| Code | Meaning | IoT Context |
|---|---|---|
| `200 OK` | Success | Sensor reading retrieved |
| `201 Created` | Resource created | New device registered |
| `401 Unauthorized` | Invalid/missing auth token | Expired OAuth token |
| `404 Not Found` | Resource missing | Unknown sensor ID queried |
| `500 Internal Server Error` | Backend crash | Database down |

---

**(b) CoAP and the Observe Pattern (7 marks)**

**CoAP Definition (2 marks):**
**CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)**, defined in **RFC 7252**, is an application-layer protocol for constrained devices. It runs over **UDP port 5683**, uses a **4-byte binary header**, and emulates HTTP methods (`GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`) with response codes like `2.05 Content`. It is designed for **6LoWPAN, LoRaWAN, and other low-power lossy networks**.

**Observe Pattern (3 marks):**
The **Observe** extension (RFC 7641) allows a CoAP client to register interest in a resource once, and the server then **pushes** new values whenever the state changes — achieving MQTT-like push semantics **without a broker**. The client sends a `GET` request with the `Observe: 0` option. The server responds with the current value (`2.05 Content`). On every state change, the server sends a new notification message (CON or NON) with the same token. The client can cancel by sending `GET` with `Observe: 1` (deregister).

**Message-Exchange Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Client                                  Server
  |  ------ CON GET /sensors/temp ---->  |   (Observe: 0)
  |  <----- ACK 2.05 Content (23.4) ---- |
  |                                       |   (state changes to 23.6)
  |  <----- CON 2.05 Content (23.6) ---- |
  |  ------ ACK ---------------------->   |
  |                                       |   (state changes to 23.8)
  |  <----- NON 2.05 Content (23.8) ---- |   (no ack needed)
```

**Why no broker is needed:** The CoAP server itself maintains the registration list and pushes notifications directly. This eliminates one network hop and one point of failure compared to MQTT, at the cost of the server tracking all observers.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU ESE**
>
> 1. **Confusing MQTT QoS with HTTP status codes.** QoS 0/1/2 is a **delivery guarantee**, not a status. HTTP `200`/`404` is a **response code**, not a reliability level.
> 2. **Writing the wrong port.** HTTP is **80**, MQTT is **1883**, CoAP is **5683** (all cleartext). Mixing them up costs 1 mark immediately.
> 3. **Forgetting that CoAP is UDP, not TCP.** This is the most common error in KTU papers. CoAP can run over TCP (RFC 8323) but the default and exam-expected answer is **UDP**.
> 4. **Not drawing a diagram in long-answer questions.** Part-B 14-mark questions **require a diagram**. A textual-only answer loses 2–3 marks even if the content is correct.
> 5. **Claiming MQTT is "small enough for any IoT device".** MQTT still uses TCP — it is *not* suitable for severely constrained Class-0 devices. CoAP is the correct choice there.
> 6. **Saying "HTTP is secure" without specifying HTTPS/TLS.** Marks are awarded for the *correct* security term, not the generic word "secure".
> 7. **Omitting the broker in the MQTT architecture diagram.** The broker is the heart of MQTT — every diagram must show it.
> 8. **Comparing only features, not use cases.** KTU examiners reward "**justify with a use case**" — always end a comparison with one real-world example per protocol.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (pin this before the exam)**
>
> - **HTTP** = Request/Response, **TCP/80**, textual headers (~300 B), REST verbs `GET/POST/PUT/DELETE`, status `200/201/400/401/404/500`, secure variant = **HTTPS (443/TLS)**, primary IoT role = **mobile-app-to-cloud**.
> - **MQTT** = Publish/Subscribe via **Broker**, **TCP/1883**, 2-byte binary header, **QoS 0 (at most once)**, **QoS 1 (at least once, PUBACK)**, **QoS 2 (exactly once, 4-step handshake)**, topics use `+/` wildcards, secure = **MQTTS (8883/TLS)**, primary IoT role = **asynchronous telemetry fan-out**.
> - **CoAP** = RESTful, **UDP/5683**, 4-byte binary header, methods `GET/POST/PUT/DELETE` with codes like `2.05 Content`, **Observe (RFC 7641)** for push semantics without broker, secure = **CoAPS (5684/DTLS)**, primary IoT role = **constrained sensor mesh**.
> - **Broker** is a concept **only** in MQTT (and AMQP). HTTP and CoAP are direct client-server.
> - **Header size ranking (smallest to largest):** CoAP (4 B) < MQTT (2 B fixed + variable) < HTTP (~300 B). For exam: quote all three numbers.
> - **Energy per message ranking (most efficient first):** CoAP ≈ MQTT QoS 0 < MQTT QoS 1 < HTTP.
> - **Reliability mechanisms:** HTTP → TCP retransmission; MQTT → QoS levels; CoAP → CON messages with exponential-backoff retransmit + Message ID deduplication.
> - **Hybrid stack to remember:** Sensor (CoAP) → Gateway (MQTT) → Cloud (HTTP/REST) → Mobile App.
> - **Memorize the four acronyms:** QoS (MQTT), DTLS (CoAP), TLS (HTTP/MQTT), REST (HTTP/CoAP style).
> - **Standards bodies:** MQTT → OASIS / ISO/IEC 20922; CoAP → IETF RFC 7252; HTTP → IETF RFC 9110.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing MQTT in Mobile Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Implementing MQTT in Mobile Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Implementing MQTT in Mobile Applications

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is an OASIS-standardized, lightweight **publish/subscribe** messaging protocol designed for constrained devices and unreliable networks. It operates over TCP/IP (port **1883** for plain, **8883** for TLS) and is the de-facto standard for IoT telemetry transport.

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
MQTT is an **asynchronous, event-driven, broker-mediated pub/sub protocol** built on top of the TCP transport layer. It is engineered for low-bandwidth, high-latency, or unreliable networks where a small code footprint is required on the client. The protocol follows the client–broker architecture, where clients do not communicate with each other directly; all messages are routed through a central **MQTT Broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX, AWS IoT Core).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Newspaper Stand 🍞📰
Think of MQTT like a **newspaper delivery system**:
- **You (the Mobile App)** don't walk to the printer to get today's paper. You simply **subscribe** to a topic, say *"Sports Daily"*.
- The **Broker** is the **newspaper vendor** standing in the middle of the town. The vendor collects newspapers from publishers and delivers them to every subscriber who asked for that title.
- **Publishers (IoT Sensors)** drop their news (temperature, GPS, switch status) onto the vendor's desk tagged with a topic name.
- If the vendor's shop is closed (broker down), you can still call back later — MQTT supports **persistent sessions** and **retained messages** so you never miss the latest headline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** MQTT is **NOT** HTTP. HTTP is request/response (client pulls data). MQTT is **push-based** — the broker pushes new data to the mobile app the moment it is published, which is critical for real-time IoT dashboards.

### Core Components in a Mobile App Context

| Component | Role in Mobile App | Standard / Library |
|---|---|---|
| **MQTT Client** | Embedded library inside the Android/iOS app | Eclipse Paho, HiveMQ MQTT Client, MqttAndroidClient |
| **Broker** | Remote server that routes messages | Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX, Cloud IoT Core |
| **Topic** | Hierarchical UTF-8 string used for routing | e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature` |
| **Message (Payload)** | The actual sensor data (JSON, binary) | Up to **256 MB** (practical: < 1 KB) |
| **QoS Level** | Delivery guarantee level | **0**, **1**, or **2** |
| **Will Message** | Last-resort notification when client disconnects ungracefully | Set at connect time |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus specifically asks for **implementation details** — students must be ready to write about library selection, connection callbacks, publish/subscribe methods, thread management, and **handling reconnection** in mobile apps.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The MQTT Pub/Sub Model — Operational Breakdown

The pub/sub model has three actors and one routing fabric:

1. **Publisher** (e.g., a DHT22 sensor via ESP32)
2. **Subscriber** (e.g., your Android phone running the IoT app)
3. **Broker** (e.g., Mosquitto running on a Raspberry Pi or cloud VM)

The publisher and subscriber are **decoupled in space, time, and synchronization**:
- **Space-decoupled:** Publisher doesn't need to know the IP of the subscriber.
- **Time-decoupled:** Publisher can publish even if the subscriber is offline (with QoS 1/2 + persistent session).
- **Synchronization-decoupled:** Both sides can publish/receive without blocking each other.

## 2.2 MQTT Message Lifecycle in a Mobile App

### Step-by-Step Operational Flow

1. **App Initialization**
   - Instantiate the MQTT client with `brokerURI`, `clientId`, `MqttAndroidClient.Ack` callback.
   - Configure `MqttConnectOptions` → set `cleanSession`, `keepAliveInterval`, `userName`, `password`, `willMessage`.
2. **Connection Establishment (CONNECT/CONNACK handshake)**
   - App calls `client.connect(options, context, callback)`.
   - Broker responds with CONNACK containing a **return code** (`0` = Connection Accepted, `1`-`5` = various failures).
3. **Subscription (SUBSCRIBE/SUBACK)**
   - App calls `client.subscribe(topic, qos, listener)` for every topic of interest.
   - Broker confirms with SUBACK, including the granted QoS (may be lower than requested).
4. **Publish (PUBLISH/PUBACK/PUBREC/PUBREL/PUBACK)**
   - To send data: `client.publish(topic, message, qos, retained)`.
   - The `MqttMessage` object wraps the payload byte array.
5. **Message Arrival Callback**
   - Override `messageArrived(String topic, MqttMessage message)` in `IMqttMessageListener`.
   - The Android app must marshal the callback onto the **UI thread** (e.g., using `runOnUiThread` or `LiveData`).
6. **Disconnection & Reconnection**
   - On `disconnect()` or unexpected network loss, the Paho library auto-reconnects **only if** `setAutomaticReconnect(true)` is set.
   - On reconnect, **persistent session** (`cleanSession = false`) restores subscriptions.

## 2.3 MQTT QoS Levels — The Three Delivery Guarantees

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **QoS is the single most examinable concept in this module.** Memorize the handshake diagram.

| QoS Level | Name | Handshake | Use Case in Mobile App | Overhead |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **QoS 0** | At most once | PUBLISH only | Fire-and-forget telemetry (e.g., GPS pings every 5s) | Lowest |
| **QoS 1** | At least once | PUBLISH → PUBACK | Sensor readings where duplicate is acceptable (e.g., temperature) | Medium |
| **QoS 2** | Exactly once | PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCMP | Critical control commands (e.g., unlock door, fire alarm) | Highest |

## 2.4 MQTT Control Packet Structure (Foundations for Packet Analysis)

Every MQTT control packet has a **2-byte fixed header**:

$$\text{Fixed Header} = \underbrace{\text{Byte 1: Type + Flags}}_{\text{4-bit type, 4-bit flags}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{Byte 2+: Remaining Length}}_{\text{1-4 bytes, variable encoding}}$$

The **Remaining Length** uses a variable-byte scheme where each byte contributes 7 bits of value and 1 continuation bit, supporting packet sizes from 1 byte to **268,435,455 bytes (~256 MB)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for the KTU exam:** A common 2-mark question asks: *"What is the maximum MQTT packet size?"* — the answer is **256 MB**, derived from the variable encoding of the Remaining Length field.

## 2.5 Android-Specific Implementation Concerns

### Threading & the UI Thread Trap ⚠️
- MQTT callbacks (`messageArrived`, `deliveryComplete`) fire on **background threads** spawned by the Paho client.
- You **cannot** directly update `TextView`, `ProgressBar`, or `RecyclerView` from these callbacks.
- Solution: post to the main looper using `new Handler(Looper.getMainLooper()).post(() -> { ... })`.

### Battery & Network Optimization
- Set `keepAliveInterval` carefully: too low → battery drain; too high → slow disconnect detection. Default: **60 seconds**.
- Use **MQTT over WebSockets** (port 9001) only when crossing HTTP proxies; otherwise prefer native TCP (1883/8883).
- Implement **exponential back-off** reconnection: 1s → 2s → 4s → 8s → cap at 30s.

### Security in Mobile MQTT
- Always use **TLS/SSL** (`ssl://broker:8883`) in production.
- For device-to-cloud auth, use **X.509 client certificates** or **username/password with JWT tokens** that expire.
- Store credentials in Android **Keystore** or iOS **Keychain**, never in `SharedPreferences` in plain text.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Value / Rule | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Default MQTT port (plain) | **1883** | TCP, unencrypted |
| Default MQTT port (TLS) | **8883** | TCP, encrypted |
| Default MQTT port (WebSockets) | **9001** | For proxy-friendly setups |
| Max packet size | **256 MB** | Variable-byte Remaining Length |
| QoS 0 handshake | 1 packet | At most once |
| QoS 1 handshake | 2 packets (PUBACK) | At least once |
| QoS 2 handshake | 4 packets | Exactly once |
| `cleanSession` default | `true` (no persistence) | Persistent = `false` |
| `keepAliveInterval` | **60 s** (default) | Sent in CONNECT |
| `willMessage` | Set before CONNECT | Last-will-and-testament |
| Max topic length | **65535 bytes** | UTF-8 encoded |
| Topic wildcards | `+` (single level), `#` (multi-level) | Used only in SUBSCRIBE |
| `retained` flag | Broker stores last message | New subscribers get latest immediately |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Protocol Walk-through

> [!NOTE]
> Since this module is implementation-focused, the "derivations" below are **analytical walkthroughs** of MQTT control packet exchanges, QoS handshakes, and Android client lifecycle. Every transition is explained line-by-line, exactly as the protocol spec mandates.

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — A Complete CONNECT/CONNACK Exchange

Let us trace the **first handshake** a mobile app performs when joining a broker.

**Step 1 — Mobile App Side: Construct MqttConnectOptions**

$$\text{options.setCleanSession(false)} \quad \text{(persistent session enabled)}$$

$$\text{options.setKeepAliveInterval(60)} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

$$\text{options.setUserName("mobile_user_42")} \quad \text{(auth identity)}$$

$$\text{options.setPassword("s3cret".toCharArray())} \quad \text{(auth secret)}$$

The library then serializes these into the **MQTT CONNECT packet**, whose variable header contains:
- **Protocol Name:** `"MQTT"` (length-prefixed UTF-8, value: `0x00 0x04 0x4D 0x51 0x54 0x54`)
- **Protocol Level:** `0x04` (denoting MQTT v3.1.1) or `0x05` (MQTT v5.0)
- **Connect Flags:** 1 byte containing bits for `User Name`, `Password`, `Will Retain`, `Will QoS`, `Will Flag`, `Clean Session`

**Step 2 — TCP Socket Open & CONNECT Transmission**

The Paho client opens a TCP socket to `tcp://broker.hivemq.com:1883` and writes the CONNECT packet bytes.

**Step 3 — Broker Side Parses CONNECT**

The broker reads the Fixed Header, decodes the Remaining Length, and validates the variable header. The **Username and Password** are authenticated against the broker's ACL (Access Control List).

**Step 4 — Broker Emits CONNACK**

The broker constructs a 4-byte CONNACK:
- Byte 1: `0x20` (type = 2, CONNACK)
- Byte 2: `0x02` (Remaining Length = 2)
- Byte 3: **Session Present** flag (1 bit) + reserved (7 bits)
- Byte 4: **Return Code** (`0x00` = Accepted, `0x01`–`0x05` = various rejections)

$$\text{Return Code} = \begin{cases} 0x00 & \text{Connection Accepted} \\ 0x01 & \text{Refused: unacceptable protocol version} \\ 0x02 & \text{Refused: identifier rejected} \\ 0x03 & \text{Refused: server unavailable} \\ 0x04 & \text{Refused: bad username or password} \\ 0x05 & \text{Refused: not authorized} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Mobile App Receives CONNACK**

The Paho library invokes `IMqttActionListener.onSuccess(IMqttToken asyncActionToken)` on the callback thread. The Android app typically posts a Toast or updates a `LiveData<Boolean>` flag to indicate the connection is live.

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough — QoS 2 Exactly-Once Handshake

The QoS 2 flow is the **most examined** part of the MQTT spec. Let us trace the four-packet dance for a message `M` published by a mobile app.

$$\text{Publisher (Mobile App)} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{Broker} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{Subscriber (Other Device)}$$

| # | Direction | Packet | Packet Identifier (PID) | State at Publisher | State at Broker |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | App → Broker | PUBLISH (`M`, QoS 2, DUP=0, PID=0x1234) | 0x1234 | Waiting for PUBREC | `M` stored as "unconfirmed QoS 2" |
| 2 | Broker → App | PUBREC (PID=0x1234) | 0x1234 | Discards `M`, waits for PUBREL | `M` moved to "pending release" state |
| 3 | App → Broker | PUBREL (PID=0x1234) | 0x1234 | Waits for PUBCMP | Delivers `M` to subscriber, waits for PUBCMP |
| 4 | Broker → App | PUBCMP (PID=0x1234) | 0x1234 | Transaction complete | Discards PID 0x1234 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The PUBLISH in step 1 carries the actual payload `M`. The subsequent PUBREC, PUBREL, and PUBCMP packets are **acknowledgments only** (2-byte variable header, no payload). This is why QoS 2 has the **highest network overhead** but **guarantees zero duplicates**.

## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough — Persistent Session & Reconnection

When a mobile app loses its network (user enters an elevator, switches Wi-Fi to 4G), the broker notices the absence of the PINGREQ heartbeat within `1.5 × keepAliveInterval` seconds. The session then follows this state machine:

$$\text{Active Session} \xrightarrow{\text{PINGREQ timeout}} \text{Expired Session} \xrightarrow{\text{Will Message fired (if set)}} \text{Subscribers notified}$$

If the app reconnects with `cleanSession = false` and the same `clientId`, the broker restores:
- All existing subscriptions.
- All unacknowledged QoS 1 and QoS 2 messages.
- The session state.

$$\text{Reconnection Flow} = \text{TCP reconnect} \rightarrow \text{CONNECT (same clientId, clean=false)} \rightarrow \text{CONNACK (Session Present = 1)} \rightarrow \text{Broker replays queued messages}$$

## 3.4 Android Lifecycle — `MqttAndroidClient` State Transitions

The Paho `MqttAndroidClient` exposes these key states observable via `IMqttActionListener`:

$$\text{None} \rightarrow \text{Connecting} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Connected} \\ \text{Disconnected (manual)} \\ \text{Disconnected (error)} \end{cases}$$

The mobile app developer must implement handlers for **all four terminal states**. The KTU 2024 marking scheme explicitly allocates marks for discussing **error handling** in the `onFailure` callback — particularly for distinguishing `MqttException.REASON_CODE_CLIENT_NOT_CONNECTED` from network timeouts.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below use Mermaid syntax. Node IDs are alphanumeric, labels are plain uppercase text, and reserved keywords are avoided as node names.

## 4.1 MQTT Pub/Sub Architecture in a Mobile App Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorA[IoT Sensor A\nESP32 Temperature] -->|PUBLISH home/livingroom/temp| broker((MQTT Broker\nMosquitto / HiveMQ))
    sensorB[IoT Sensor B\nESP32 Humidity] -->|PUBLISH home/livingroom/humidity| broker
    cloudAct[Cloud Actuator\nAWS Lambda] -->|PUBLISH home/livingroom/fan/cmd| broker
    broker -->|QoS 1 message| mobileApp[Mobile App\nAndroid Paho Client]
    broker -->|Retained last value| mobileApp
    broker -->|Push notification on Will| dashboard[Web Dashboard]
    mobileApp -->|PUBLISH home/livingroom/fan/cmd QoS 2| broker
```

## 4.2 QoS 2 Four-Way Handshake Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Mobile App Publisher
    participant Broker as MQTT Broker
    participant Sub as Subscriber Device

    App->>Broker: PUBLISH (QoS 2, PID 1234, payload M)
    Note over Broker: Stores M in unconfirmed state
    Broker->>App: PUBREC (PID 1234)
    Note over App: Original M discarded
    App->>Broker: PUBREL (PID 1234)
    Note over Broker: Promotes M to pending release
    Broker->>Sub: Forwards M to subscriber
    Broker->>App: PUBCMP (PID 1234)
    Note over App: Transaction complete
```

## 4.3 Mobile App MQTT Connection State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> None
    None --> Connecting: client.connect()
    Connecting --> Connected: CONNACK return code 0
    Connecting --> Failed: CONNACK return code 1 to 5
    Failed --> Connecting: automaticReconnect true
    Connected --> Disconnected: client.disconnect()
    Connected --> Lost: keepAlive timeout
    Lost --> Connecting: automaticReconnect true
    Disconnected --> Connecting: client.connect()
    Failed --> Disconnected: manual abort
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Android MQTT App

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UI_LAYER[UI LAYER Android Activity]
        activityA[MainActivity]
        viewModelA[MQTT ViewModel]
    end

    subgraph LOGIC_LAYER[LOGIC LAYER Kotlin Java]
        mqttManagerA[MQTT Manager Singleton]
        callbackA[IMqttActionListener Impl]
        listenerA[IMqttMessageListener Impl]
    end

    subgraph NETWORK_LAYER[NETWORK LAYER Paho Library]
        clientA[MqttAndroidClient]
        optionsA[MqttConnectOptions]
    end

    subgraph BROKER_REMOTE[REMOTE BROKER]
        brokerNode[MQTT Broker Mosquitto]
    end

    activityA --> viewModelA
    viewModelA --> mqttManagerA
    mqttManagerA --> callbackA
    mqttManagerA --> listenerA
    mqttManagerA --> clientA
    mqttManagerA --> optionsA
    clientA -->|TCP TLS 8883| brokerNode
    brokerNode -->|PUSH messages| clientA
    clientA -->|callback| listenerA
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to draw a "mobile MQTT architecture" in the exam, use the **4-layer separation** (UI / Logic / Network / Remote) as shown above. It instantly earns the full 7 marks for a "Design / Architecture" sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between MQTT and HTTP as communication protocols for IoT applications. List any four differences.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per difference + 1 for conclusion):**

| Aspect | MQTT | HTTP |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Publish/Subscribe (broker-mediated) | Request/Response (client-server) |
| **Direction** | Bi-directional push | Client-initiated pull |
| **Overhead** | 2-byte minimum header | Hundreds of bytes of headers |
| **Power use** | Low (suitable for battery devices) | High (keep-alive connections) |
| **State** | Stateful (persistent sessions) | Stateless |
| **Real-time** | Native push, instant delivery | Requires polling or WebSockets |

MQTT is preferred for IoT because it minimizes bandwidth, supports unreliable networks, and is push-native, whereas HTTP wastes battery on polling.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the three Quality of Service (QoS) levels in MQTT with one use case each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **QoS 0 — At most once:** Single PUBLISH packet, no acknowledgment. Suitable for non-critical telemetry like periodic GPS pings where a missed reading is acceptable. *(1 mark)*
- **QoS 1 — At least once:** PUBLISH + PUBACK handshake; duplicates possible. Suitable for sensor readings like temperature where one extra reading is harmless. *(1 mark)*
- **QoS 2 — Exactly once:** Four-packet handshake (PUBLISH/PUBREC/PUBREL/PUBCMP), guarantees single delivery. Suitable for critical commands like "unlock door" or "fire alarm trigger". *(1 mark)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain the MQTT publish/subscribe architecture in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the role of the **broker**, **publisher**, **subscriber**, and **topic**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **MQTT CONNECT/CONNACK handshake** between a mobile app and a broker. Include the fields of the CONNECT packet and the meaning of the CONNACK return codes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture Overview (2 marks):**
The MQTT architecture is a **broker-mediated publish/subscribe** model with three logical roles:
1. **Publisher** — produces messages (e.g., an ESP32 sensor publishing temperature).
2. **Subscriber** — receives messages it has registered interest in (e.g., a mobile app dashboard).
3. **Broker** — the central server that accepts messages from publishers, filters by topic, and distributes to all interested subscribers (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ).

The publisher and subscriber never know each other's IP address; they communicate **only** through the broker. This is called **space-decoupling**.

**Topic Hierarchy (2 marks):**
Topics are **forward-slash separated UTF-8 strings** that form a hierarchy. Example:
```
home/livingroom/temperature
home/livingroom/humidity
home/bedroom/temperature
home/kitchen/fan/cmd
```
A subscriber to `home/+/temperature` would receive both `home/livingroom/temperature` and `home/bedroom/temperature` due to the `+` (single-level) wildcard.

**QoS (2 marks):**
Each publish carries one of three QoS levels (0, 1, or 2) defined in section 2.3 above.

**Retained Messages (1 mark):**
A publisher can set the **retained flag = true** on a message. The broker stores this as the "last known good value" for the topic. Any new subscriber immediately receives this retained message upon subscription, eliminating cold-start issues in mobile dashboards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram to draw (1 mark extra):** Refer to Section 4.1 — three publishers, one broker, two subscribers, with topic labels.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Connect Options Construction (2 marks):**
The Android app builds an `MqttConnectOptions` object:
```java
MqttConnectOptions options = new MqttConnectOptions();
options.setCleanSession(true);
options.setKeepAliveInterval(60);
options.setUserName("iot_device_42");
options.setPassword("secret123".toCharArray());
```

**Step 2 — CONNECT Packet Fields (3 marks):**
The library serializes these into the MQTT CONNECT packet:

| Field | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Protocol Name | `MQTT` | Identifies protocol |
| Protocol Level | `0x04` (v3.1.1) or `0x05` (v5.0) | Version negotiation |
| Connect Flags | 1 byte | Username, Password, Will Retain, Will QoS, Will Flag, Clean Session bits |
| Keep Alive | 60 seconds | Heartbeat interval |
| Client Identifier | Unique string | Session identity |
| Will Topic | `device42/status` | If set, broker publishes this on ungraceful disconnect |
| Will Message | `{"status":"offline"}` | Payload of will |
| Username / Password | Auth credentials | ACL check by broker |

**Step 3 — CONNACK Return Codes (2 marks):**
- `0` → Connection Accepted ✓
- `1` → Refused: unacceptable protocol version
- `2` → Refused: identifier rejected
- `3` → Refused: server unavailable
- `4` → Refused: bad username or password
- `5` → Refused: not authorized

The Android callback `onSuccess` fires for code `0`; `onFailure` fires for any other code, with the exception available via `asyncActionToken.getException()`.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the **QoS 2 exactly-once delivery** mechanism in MQTT with a sequence diagram and discuss when a mobile app should use it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe how an **Android mobile app** implements MQTT using the **Eclipse Paho** library. Cover connection, subscription, publish, message arrival callback, and reconnection handling. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Why QoS 2 Exists (1 mark):**
QoS 1 guarantees delivery but allows **duplicates** — the broker may redeliver a PUBLISH if the PUBACK is lost. For commands like "fire alarm trigger" or "unlock door", duplicates could cause catastrophic actions. QoS 2 eliminates this risk.

**The Four-Packet Handshake (4 marks — 1 mark per packet):**

| Step | Packet | Direction | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PUBLISH (QoS 2, PID) | App → Broker | Initial send with payload M |
| 2 | PUBREC (PID) | Broker → App | Broker confirms receipt, takes ownership of M |
| 3 | PUBREL (PID) | App → Broker | App confirms it will not resend M |
| 4 | PUBCMP (PID) | Broker → App | Broker confirms M is fully delivered, releases PID |

> The **Packet Identifier (PID)** is a unique 16-bit value per transaction, used to correlate the four packets.

**When Mobile Apps Should Use QoS 2 (2 marks):**
- Sending a control command to an actuator (e.g., turn on a fan, open a smart lock).
- Triggering a one-time alert that must not be repeated.
- Logging critical events that must be stored exactly once in the database.
- **Avoid QoS 2 for high-frequency telemetry** (e.g., GPS at 1Hz) — the 4-packet overhead quadruples bandwidth use and increases latency.

**Sequence Diagram (refer Section 4.2)** — earn 1 mark extra for an accurate diagram.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Add Paho Dependency (1 mark):**
In `build.gradle (Module)`:
```gradle
implementation 'org.eclipse.paho:org.eclipse.paho.android.service:1.1.1'
```

**Step 2 — Initialize the Client (1 mark):**
```java
String broker = "ssl://broker.hivemq.com:8883";
String clientId = "AndroidClient_" + System.currentTimeMillis();
MqttAndroidClient client = new MqttAndroidClient(this, broker, clientId);
```

**Step 3 — Connect with Options (1 mark):**
```java
MqttConnectOptions options = new MqttConnectOptions();
options.setCleanSession(false);
options.setKeepAliveInterval(60);
options.setAutomaticReconnect(true);
client.connect(options, null, new IMqttActionListener() {
    @Override public void onSuccess(IMqttToken token) { /* connected */ }
    @Override public void onFailure(IMqttToken token, Throwable ex) { /* handle */ }
});
```

**Step 4 — Subscribe to a Topic (1 mark):**
```java
client.subscribe("home/sensors/temperature", 1, new IMqttMessageListener() {
    @Override public void messageArrived(String topic, MqttMessage msg) {
        // Marshal to UI thread
        new Handler(Looper.getMainLooper()).post(() -> {
            textView.setText(new String(msg.getPayload()));
        });
    }
});
```

**Step 5 — Publish a Command (1 mark):**
```java
MqttMessage msg = new MqttMessage("ON".getBytes());
msg.setQos(2);
msg.setRetained(false);
client.publish("home/livingroom/fan/cmd", msg);
```

**Step 6 — Handle Reconnection (1 mark):**
Set `options.setAutomaticReconnect(true)`. Paho will internally retry with exponential back-off. To restore subscriptions after reconnect, the `connectSuccess` callback should re-subscribe, or use **persistent sessions** (`cleanSession = false`) to let the broker replay queued messages.

**Step 7 — Clean Disconnect (1 mark):**
```java
@Override protected void onDestroy() {
    try { client.disconnect(); } catch (MqttException e) { e.printStackTrace(); }
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse QoS 1 with QoS 2** — students often write PUBACK for QoS 2. Remember: QoS 2 uses PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCMP (three acknowledgments, not one).
> - **Do NOT write Python or C code** in your Android answer — KTU expects Java/Kotlin syntax for mobile app questions.
> - **Do NOT forget the UI thread rule** — MQTT callbacks fire on background threads; direct `TextView` updates will throw `CalledFromWrongThreadException`. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if this is omitted.
> - **Do NOT state "MQTT uses UDP"** — MQTT is TCP-based. (MQTT-SN is the UDP variant, but the mobile syllabus is MQTT-over-TCP.)

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **MQTT** = lightweight **pub/sub** protocol on **TCP** ports **1883** (plain) / **8883** (TLS).
- ✅ Architecture = **Publisher + Subscriber + Broker** (decoupled).
- ✅ Topics = hierarchical UTF-8 strings; wildcards `+` (one level) and `#` (multi-level).
- ✅ **QoS 0** = fire-and-forget; **QoS 1** = at-least-once with PUBACK; **QoS 2** = exactly-once with PUBREC/PUBREL/PUBCMP.
- ✅ **CONNACK return codes** 0–5 must be memorized.
- ✅ Max packet size = **256 MB**; max topic length = **65535 bytes**.
- ✅ Android library = **Eclipse Paho** (`MqttAndroidClient` + `MqttConnectOptions`).
- ✅ **Always** marshal MQTT callbacks to the **UI thread** using `Handler(Looper.getMainLooper())`.
- ✅ **Always** use **TLS** and store credentials in **Android Keystore**.
- ✅ **Reconnection**: set `automaticReconnect = true` and use **persistent sessions** (`cleanSession = false`).
- ✅ **Retained messages**: broker stores the last value — new subscribers get it instantly.
- ✅ **Last Will and Testament**: fires on ungraceful disconnect; set before `connect()`.
- ✅ Exam focus areas: **QoS handshake diagrams, CONNACK codes, Android lifecycle, threading pitfalls**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Transmission Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECGT415 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Transmission Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Mobile App Development for IoT**, **Data Transmission Techniques** refer to the systematic methods, protocols, physical media, and encoding schemes used to transfer sensor data, control commands, and application payloads between heterogeneous IoT endpoints (sensors, actuators, microcontrollers, edge gateways) and a mobile application / cloud backend.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framing, these techniques are broadly classified along three orthogonal axes:

1. **Physical / Link Layer Medium** — Wired (UART, SPI, I2C, Ethernet, USB) vs Wireless (Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, LoRa, NB-IoT, Cellular).
2. **Network Reach** — Personal Area Network (PAN), Local Area Network (LAN), Wide Area Network (WAN), Low-Power Wide Area Network (LPWAN).
3. **Application Layer Messaging Pattern** — Request/Response (HTTP, CoAP), Publish/Subscribe (MQTT, AMQP), Streaming (WebSockets).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Mobile apps in IoT rarely "talk" directly to raw sensors. They typically interact with a **gateway/cloud broker** using a structured **application-layer protocol**. Therefore, students must master the **protocol stack (PHY → MAC → Network → Transport → App)** to design efficient data paths.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal System" of IoT

Imagine an **IoT data packet as a letter** you want to send from a village (sensor) to a friend in a city (mobile app):

- The **letter paper** = the *payload* (temperature value, GPS coordinate).
- The **envelope format (addressing)** = the *protocol* (MQTT, HTTP, CoAP).
- The **transport vehicle** = the *physical medium* (Wi-Fi radio, cellular signal, copper wire).
- The **postal rules** = the *OSI/TCP-IP layer rules* governing how the letter is sealed, stamped, and routed.

Choosing **Wi-Fi** is like using a *fast courier* in a city (high bandwidth, short range). Choosing **LoRa** is like using a *bullock cart on a long dirt road* (very low bandwidth, extremely long range). Choosing **MQTT** is like choosing a *newspaper subscription model* — the mobile app subscribes to topics, and the publisher pushes only relevant data.

> [!NOTE]
> A good IoT engineer chooses the **transport (vehicle)** AND the **protocol (letter format)** together. An inappropriate pairing (e.g., sending heavy HTTP video over LoRa) is one of the most common design failures evaluated in KTU examinations.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Data Transmission

The following metrics are **bold-marked constants/units** that KTU examiners expect candidates to recall without prompting:

- **Bandwidth** — measured in **bits per second (bps)**, commonly **kbps** or **Mbps**.
- **Latency** — measured in **milliseconds (ms)**; critical for real-time control loops.
- **Range** — measured in **meters (m)** or **kilometers (km)**.
- **Power Consumption** — measured in **milliwatts (mW)** or **microamps (µA)** during transmission.
- **Duty Cycle** — a dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) representing the fraction of time a radio is active; regulated by **ETSI EN 300 220** in Europe.
- **Throughput** — useful data delivered per unit time, distinct from *bandwidth* (raw channel capacity).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Data Transmission Techniques

Data transmission in IoT is best understood as a layered decision. Below is the KTU-aligned taxonomy.

### A. Wired Transmission Techniques (Short-Distance, High-Reliability)

| Technique | Full Form | Typical Use in IoT | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **UART** | Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter | Debug console, GPS modules, GSM modems | Up to 1 Mbps |
| **SPI** | Serial Peripheral Interface | SD cards, OLED displays, fast sensors | 10 – 50 Mbps |
| **I2C** | Inter-Integrated Circuit | Low-speed sensors (BMP280, MPU6050) | 100 kbps – 3.4 Mbps |
| **Ethernet** | IEEE 802.3 | Industrial gateways, Raspberry Pi | 10/100/1000 Mbps |
| **USB** | Universal Serial Bus | Firmware flashing, tethered debug | 480 Mbps (USB 2.0) |

### B. Wireless Transmission Techniques (Short-Range)

| Technique | Standard | Range | Band | Data Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi** | IEEE 802.11 b/g/n/ac | ~50 m | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 11 – 1200 Mbps |
| **BLE** | Bluetooth Low Energy 5.x | ~100 m | 2.4 GHz | 2 Mbps |
| **Classic Bluetooth** | IEEE 802.15.1 | ~10 m | 2.4 GHz | 1 – 3 Mbps |
| **ZigBee** | IEEE 802.15.4 | ~100 m | 2.4 GHz | 250 kbps |
| **Thread** | IEEE 802.15.4 + IPv6 | ~100 m | 2.4 GHz | 250 kbps |
| **NFC** | ISO/IEC 14443 | <10 cm | 13.56 MHz | 106 – 424 kbps |

### C. Wireless Transmission Techniques (Long-Range / LPWAN)

| Technique | Modulation | Range | Data Rate | Power |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LoRa** | CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum) | 2 – 10 km | 0.3 – 50 kbps | Very Low |
| **NB-IoT** | LTE-based | <35 km | 26 kbps DL / 62 kbps UL | Low |
| **LTE-M (CAT-M1)** | LTE-based | <300 km cell edge | 1 Mbps | Low |
| **SigFox** | UNB (Ultra-Narrow Band) | 30 – 50 km | 100 – 600 bps | Ultra Low |
| **Cellular 4G/5G** | OFDMA | Wide | 100 Mbps – 10 Gbps | High |

### D. Application-Layer Messaging Protocols

| Protocol | Pattern | Transport | Header Size | Suitability for IoT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HTTP/HTTPS** | Request/Response | TCP | Large (~200 B min) | Rich apps, non-critical |
| **MQTT** | Publish/Subscribe | TCP | 2-byte minimum | **Gold standard for IoT** |
| **CoAP** | Request/Response | UDP | 4-byte | Constrained networks |
| **AMQP** | Pub/Sub + Queues | TCP | Heavy | Industrial, banking-grade |
| **WebSockets** | Full-duplex | TCP | Moderate | Real-time dashboards |
| **DDS** | Pub/Sub | UDP/TCP | Lightweight | Autonomous vehicles, robots |

## 2.2 Operational Theory — The "Why and How" Behind IoT Data Flow

1. **Why multiple protocols exist:** No single protocol can simultaneously offer *high bandwidth*, *long range*, *low power*, AND *low cost*. Engineers trade-off the **Pareto frontier** of these four metrics.
2. **Why MQTT dominates mobile-IoT:** A mobile app maintains a *persistent TCP connection* to a broker (e.g., Mosquitto, AWS IoT Core, HiveMQ). The app subscribes to topics like `home/livingroom/temperature`. The sensor publishes only when state changes, drastically saving battery.
3. **How CoAP works on UDP:** CoAP mimics HTTP semantics (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) but uses a compact 4-byte binary header, ideal for **8-bit microcontrollers** like Arduino.
4. **How LoRa achieves long range:** It spreads the signal over a wide bandwidth using **chirps**, trading data rate for sensitivity (can decode signals below the noise floor, down to **−137 dBm**).
5. **How BLE advertising works:** A peripheral broadcasts small **Advertising Packets (31 bytes legacy / 255 bytes extended)** on 3 fixed channels; a central scanner wakes periodically to listen, achieving months of battery life on a coin cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** When asked *"Why is MQTT preferred over HTTP for battery-powered IoT devices?"*, the expected answer is: **MQTT uses a persistent TCP connection with a 2-byte fixed header and supports QoS, retain flags, and last-will messages, whereas HTTP requires a fresh TCP handshake per request (high overhead) and a verbose plaintext header.**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity** | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | C in bps, B in Hz, S/N dimensionless |
| 2 | **Link Budget (Free Space Path Loss)** | $FSPL(dB) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | d in km, f in MHz |
| 3 | **Received Signal Strength** | $RSSI = Tx_{power} - PL_{total}$ | dBm |
| 4 | **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** | $SNR_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | dB |
| 5 | **Data Rate over Time** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Payload bits}}{\text{Transmission time (s)}}$ | bps |
| 6 | **Airtime (LoRa example)** | $T_{air} = T_{preamble} + T_{payload}$ | seconds |
| 7 | **Energy per Bit** | $E_b = \dfrac{P_{tx}}{R}$ | Joules/bit |
| 8 | **UART Frame Size** | $N_{bits} = 1_{start} + N_{data} + P_{parity} + N_{stop}$ | bits |
| 9 | **I2C Address Space** | 7-bit = 128 devices, 10-bit = 1024 devices | nodes |
| 10 | **MQTT QoS Levels** | 0 = at-most-once, 1 = at-least-once, 2 = exactly-once | integers |
| 11 | **CoAP Message Format** | 4-byte header = Ver(2) \vert T(2) \vert TKL(4) \vert Code(8) \vert MsgID(16) | bits |
| 12 | **Duty Cycle Limit (EU868)** | $\text{Max} = 1\%$ (sub-band dependent) | ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Shannon-Hartley theorem and Free-Space Path Loss equation are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 numerical questions. Memorize the constants: **32.44** (FSPL at km/MHz) and **−174 dBm/Hz** (thermal noise floor).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** LoRa soil-moisture sensors → LoRaWAN gateway → MQTT broker → Mobile app dashboard.
- **Wearable Health:** BLE heart-rate sensor → Smartphone (acts as gateway) → HTTPS REST API → Cloud → Doctor's mobile app.
- **Smart Home (Matter/Thread):** Thread mesh + Wi-Fi backhaul → Mobile app via cloud-to-cloud API.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Modbus over RS-485 wired + MQTT bridge → Time-series database (InfluxDB) → Grafana mobile view.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity

The Shannon-Hartley theorem defines the **maximum achievable error-free data rate** over a band-limited channel corrupted by additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN).

**Step 1 — Statement of the theorem:**

A communication channel of bandwidth $B$ (Hz) and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ has a theoretical maximum capacity $C$ given by:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\; \text{bits/second}
$$

**Step 2 — Physical intuition derivation:**

- An ideal channel with infinite $S/N$ would have infinite capacity, but real channels are bandwidth-limited to $B$.
- The ratio $S/N$ captures the *distinguishability* between symbols. If $S/N$ is high, we can pack more bits per Hz.
- The $\log_2$ arises from information theory: transmitting one bit requires the receiver to distinguish between 2 equiprobable voltage levels; the capacity scales logarithmically with the number of distinguishable levels.

**Step 3 — Worked numerical example (KTU style):**

*Given:* A Wi-Fi channel has bandwidth $B = 20$ MHz and $S/N = 63$ (linear scale). Find the channel capacity.

*Solution:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\
  &= 20 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 63) \\
  &= 20 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(64) \\
  &= 20 \times 10^6 \cdot 6 \\
  &= 120 \times 10^6 \;\; \text{bps} \\
  &= 120 \;\; \text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Result:* The theoretical maximum throughput on this 20 MHz channel is **120 Mbps** (this is exactly the peak rate of IEEE 802.11n with a single spatial stream, validating the theorem).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

**Step 1 — Physical setup:**

Consider an isotropic radiator transmitting power $P_t$ uniformly in all directions. At distance $d$, the power is distributed over the surface of a sphere of area $4\pi d^2$. The received power per unit area is:

$$
\text{Power Density} = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \;\; \text{W/m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by effective aperture of receiving antenna:**

The effective aperture $A_e$ of an antenna is related to wavelength by $A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4\pi}$.

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{\lambda^2}{4\pi} = P_t \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Express in decibels and substitute $c = f\lambda$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_r}{P_t} &= \left(\frac{c}{4\pi d f}\right)^2 \\
10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_r}{P_t}\right) &= 20\log_{10}(c) - 20\log_{10}(4\pi) - 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) \\
FSPL(dB) &= 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44
\end{aligned}
$$

where $d$ is in **km** and $f$ is in **MHz**.

**Step 4 — Worked example:**

*Given:* A LoRa node operates at $f = 868$ MHz and is $d = 5$ km from the gateway. Compute FSPL.

$$
\begin{aligned}
FSPL &= 20\log_{10}(5) + 20\log_{10}(868) + 32.44 \\
     &= 20 \cdot 0.6990 + 20 \cdot 2.9385 + 32.44 \\
     &= 13.98 + 58.77 + 32.44 \\
     &= 105.19 \;\; \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Result:* The signal attenuates by **~105 dB** over 5 km at 868 MHz, consistent with LoRa's typical link budget of **150+ dB** which includes transmit power, antenna gain, and receiver sensitivity.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — UART Frame Bit Budget

**Step 1 — Components of a UART frame:**

A standard UART frame comprises: 1 start bit, $N$ data bits (typically 8), an optional parity bit ($P = 0$ or 1), and $S$ stop bits (typically 1 or 2).

**Step 2 — Total frame size:**

$$
N_{frame} = 1 + N_{data} + P + S \;\; \text{bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective throughput:**

If the baud rate is $B$ symbols/second and one symbol carries one bit (no modulation), then:

$$
R_{effective} = \frac{N_{data} \cdot B}{N_{frame}} \;\; \text{bps}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked example (typical 9600 8N1):**

For 8N1 (8 data, No parity, 1 stop) at 9600 baud:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{frame} &= 1 + 8 + 0 + 1 = 10 \;\; \text{bits} \\
R_{effective} &= \frac{8 \cdot 9600}{10} = 7680 \;\; \text{bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Result:* Only **80%** of the nominal 9600 baud is actual payload. KTU students must remember this 20% overhead when estimating link capacity.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Technique

| Criterion | Wi-Fi | BLE | ZigBee | LoRa | NB-IoT | Cellular 4G/5G |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Data Rate** | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Power Efficiency** | ★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★ |
| **Mobility Support** | ★★ | ★★ | ★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Cost (module)** | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★ |
| **Mobile App Integration** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Best for Mobile App?** | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓✓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Verdict:** For *direct mobile app ↔ device communication*, **BLE** is the strongest candidate (Android `BluetoothGatt`, iOS `CoreBluetooth`). For *device ↔ cloud ↔ mobile app*, **Wi-Fi + MQTT** or **NB-IoT + MQTT** dominate.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as IoT Sensor
    participant Broker as MQTT Broker
    participant App as Mobile App

    App->>Broker: SUBSCRIBE topic home/livingroom/temperature
    Broker-->>App: SUBACK acknowledged
    loop Periodic Updates
        Sensor->>Broker: PUBLISH topic home/livingroom/temperature payload 24.5

    end
    Note over App,Sensor: Mobile app sends control command
    App->>Broker: PUBLISH topic home/livingroom/fan payload ON
    Broker-->>Sensor: Deliver control command
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]  
**Differentiate between MQTT and HTTP as application-layer protocols for IoT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | MQTT | HTTP |
|---|---|---|
| **Pattern** | Publish/Subscribe | Request/Response |
| **Transport** | TCP | TCP |
| **Header Size** | 2-byte fixed minimum | Variable, verbose (often 200+ bytes) |
| **Connection** | Persistent | Per-request handshake |
| **Power Use** | Low (fewer packets) | High (headers + handshakes) |
| **Best For** | Sensor telemetry, control | Web pages, file transfer |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** MQTT is preferred in battery-constrained IoT scenarios due to its compact binary header and persistent connection, whereas HTTP is suited for browser-rich applications.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]  
**What is LoRa? Explain the role of Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) modulation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** LoRa (Long Range) is a proprietary spread-spectrum modulation technique derived from Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS), developed by Semtech, used in LPWAN applications.

**CSS Mechanism (2 marks):**  
- The carrier frequency linearly sweeps (chirps) across a defined bandwidth $B$ over a symbol period $T_s$.  
- Each symbol is encoded by the starting frequency offset of the chirp.  
- Spreading the signal energy over a wide bandwidth grants **processing gain** $\approx 10\log_{10}(B/R)$, allowing reception below the noise floor (sensitivity as low as **−137 dBm**).  
- This trade-off sacrifices data rate for extreme range and robustness.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **MQTT protocol architecture**, clearly labeling the roles of Publisher, Subscriber, Broker, Topic, and QoS levels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Wi-Fi channel of bandwidth **20 MHz** has an SNR of **15 dB**. Compute the Shannon-Hartley channel capacity. If the application layer payload is **1200 bytes** per packet, calculate the **minimum time** required to transmit one packet assuming maximum channel efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — MQTT Architecture (7 marks):**

MQTT follows a **Publish/Subscribe pattern** mediated by a central **Broker**.

- **Publisher (1 mark):** The IoT sensor/device that sends (publishes) messages on a specific *topic*. Example: a DHT22 sensor publishes `home/room1/temp` with value `24.5`.
- **Subscriber (1 mark):** The mobile app or backend service that registers interest in one or more topics using `SUBSCRIBE` packets.
- **Broker (1 mark):** The central server (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ, AWS IoT Core) that receives all published messages and forwards them to matching subscribers. It also handles authentication, retained messages, and Last Will & Testament (LWT).
- **Topic (1 mark):** A hierarchical UTF-8 string (slash-separated) acting as a routing key, e.g., `home/floor2/bedroom/humidity`. Wildcards `+` (single level) and `#` (multi-level) are allowed in subscriptions.
- **QoS Levels (2 marks):**
  - **QoS 0** — At-most-once (fire-and-forget; no ACK).
  - **QoS 1** — At-least-once (PUBACK ensures delivery; duplicates possible).
  - **QoS 2** — Exactly-once (4-step handshake: PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOM).
- **Control Packet Types (1 mark):** CONNECT, CONNACK, PUBLISH, PUBACK, SUBSCRIBE, SUBACK, UNSUBSCRIBE, DISCONNECT, plus optional AUTH and PINGREQ/PINGRESP for keep-alive.

**Diagram (1 mark — already covered in Section 4.2):**
