| Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\max}$ | ECG: $f_s = 250$ Hz | Vital sign fidelity |
| LoRa Range vs. SF | $R = \text{SF} \cdot \dfrac{BW}{2^{\text{SF}}}$ | SF7–SF12 | Long-range, low-power |
| Energy Saved | $E_{\text{saved}} = \dfrac{\sum(P_i \Delta t_i)}{\sum P_i t_{i,\text{manual}}} \times 100\%$ | 20–40 % typical | Home automation thesis |
| Haversine Distance | $d = 2R \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2(\tfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}) + \cos\phi_1\cos\phi_2\sin^2(\tfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2})}\right)$ | $R = 6371$ km | GPS route optimization |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These five project types collectively demonstrate that **IoT is not a single technology** — it is the **convergence** of embedded systems, wireless communication, cloud computing, and human-centered UI/UX. In India:
- The **Ministry of Electronics & IT (MeitY)** funds Smart City IoT pilots.
- The **Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM)** mandates interoperable health records — exactly what Project 1 delivers.
- **Kerala's K-FON** free Wi-Fi makes Projects 3 & 4 commercially deployable today.
- **Kudumbashree** women's collectives are direct beneficiaries of Project 2 (Smart Farming for terrace gardens).
- **Smart City Kochi & Trivandrum** run active pilots resembling Project 5.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The IoT Project Selection Matrix (How a KTU Group Should Choose)

Choosing the right project from these five is itself a **decision-making derivation**. The KTU 2024 assessment expects you to justify your choice using an analytical matrix. We derive it step by step.

### Step 1: Define the Evaluation Criteria

Let us assign a weight $w_k$ to each criterion (sum to 1):

$$w_1 = 0.30 \quad (\text{Novelty}), \quad w_2 = 0.25 \quad (\text{Feasibility}),$$
$$w_3 = 0.20 \quad (\text{Cost}), \quad w_4 = 0.15 \quad (\text{Social Impact}), \quad w_5 = 0.10 \quad (\text{Publications})$$

### Step 2: Score Each Project (out of 10) on Each Criterion

We define a score vector $\vec{S}_i = (S_{i,1}, S_{i,2}, S_{i,3}, S_{i,4}, S_{i,5})$ for project $i$.

| Project | Novelty | Feasibility | Cost | Impact | Publications |
|---------|---------|-------------|------|--------|--------------|
| P1 – Patient Health | 8 | 7 | 6 | 9 | 8 |
| P2 – Smart Farming | 7 | 8 | 7 | 8 | 7 |
| P3 – Home Automation | 6 | 9 | 9 | 7 | 6 |
| P4 – College Alert | 7 | 9 | 9 | 7 | 5 |
| P5 – Garbage Mgmt. | 9 | 7 | 6 | 9 | 8 |

### Step 3: Compute the Weighted Total Score

The total weighted score for project $i$ is:

$$T_i = \sum_{k=1}^{5} w_k \cdot S_{i,k}$$

Computing for **Project 1 (Patient Health Monitoring)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= (0.30 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 8) \\
&= 2.40 + 1.75 + 1.20 + 1.35 + 0.80 \\
&= \mathbf{7.50}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing for **Project 2 (Smart Farming)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= (0.30 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 7) \\
&= 2.10 + 2.00 + 1.40 + 1.20 + 0.70 \\
&= \mathbf{7.40}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing for **Project 3 (Home Automation)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_3 &= (0.30 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 6) \\
&= 1.80 + 2.25 + 1.80 + 1.05 + 0.60 \\
&= \mathbf{7.50}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing for **Project 4 (College Alert)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_4 &= (0.30 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 5) \\
&= 2.10 + 2.25 + 1.80 + 1.05 + 0.50 \\
&= \mathbf{7.70}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing for **Project 5 (Garbage Management)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_5 &= (0.30 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 8) \\
&= 2.70 + 1.75 + 1.20 + 1.35 + 0.80 \\
&= \mathbf{7.80}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Final Ranking

$$\boxed{\;T_5 = 7.80 \;>\; T_4 = 7.70 \;>\; T_1 = T_3 = 7.50 \;>\; T_2 = 7.40\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Under this weighting scheme, **Project 5 (Garbage Management)** ranks highest due to its novelty and social impact. However, **Project 4 (College Alert)** wins on cost-feasibility for student groups. **KTU Takeaway:** Always show this kind of *quantitative justification* in your synopsis — examiners love it.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Energy-Saved Equation (Project 3)

Let's prove the energy-saving formula step by step for a typical B.Tech home automation scenario.

Suppose a household has $n = 3$ appliances — a fan (75 W), a tube-light (40 W), and an AC (1500 W). The *manual* and *automated* daily ON-times are:

| Appliance | Power $P_i$ (W) | Manual $t_{i,m}$ (h) | Auto $t_{i,a}$ (h) |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Fan | 75 | 12 | 8 |
| Tube-light | 40 | 6 | 4 |
| AC | 1500 | 8 | 6 |

Daily energy consumption in each case:

$$E_{\text{manual}} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} P_i \cdot t_{i,m}$$
$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{manual}} &= (75 \times 12) + (40 \times 6) + (1500 \times 8) \\
&= 900 + 240 + 12000 \\
&= 13{,}140 \;\text{Wh} = 13.14\;\text{kWh}
\end{aligned}$$

$$E_{\text{auto}} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} P_i \cdot t_{i,a}$$
$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{auto}} &= (75 \times 8) + (40 \times 4) + (1500 \times 6) \\
&= 600 + 160 + 9000 \\
&= 9{,}760 \;\text{Wh} = 9.76\;\text{kWh}
\end{aligned}$$

Energy saved per day:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= E_{\text{manual}} - E_{\text{auto}} \\
&= 13{,}140 - 9{,}760 \\
&= 3{,}380\;\text{Wh} = 3.38\;\text{kWh}
\end{aligned}$$

Percentage energy saved (using the universal formula):

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{saved}} &= \frac{\Delta E}{E_{\text{manual}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{3{,}380}{13{,}140} \times 100\% \\
&\approx \mathbf{25.72\%}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> This derivation is a **guaranteed 7-mark KTU question** — examiners love to give a real scenario and ask you to compute energy savings. Practice plugging in different values.

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Haversine-Based Route (Project 5)

Suppose a garbage truck starts at the depot (Kakkanad, Kochi) at coordinates $(\phi_1, \lambda_1) = (10.0154°\text{N}, 76.3419°\text{E})$ and must visit 2 full bins at:
- Bin A: $(10.0250°\text{N}, 76.3500°\text{E})$
- Bin B: $(10.0080°\text{N}, 76.3300°\text{E})$

We need to compute distances using Haversine.

**Step 1: Convert degrees to radians** (multiply by $\pi/180$).

$$\phi_1 = 10.0154 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.17480\;\text{rad}$$
$$\lambda_1 = 76.3419 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.33240\;\text{rad}$$
$$\phi_A = 10.0250 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.17498\;\text{rad}$$
$$\lambda_A = 76.3500 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.33259\;\text{rad}$$
$$\phi_B = 10.0080 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.17469\;\text{rad}$$
$$\lambda_B = 76.3300 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.33224\;\text{rad}$$

**Step 2: Compute deltas.**

$$\Delta\phi_A = \phi_A - \phi_1 = 0.17498 - 0.17480 = 0.00018\;\text{rad}$$
$$\Delta\lambda_A = \lambda_A - \lambda_1 = 1.33259 - 1.33240 = 0.00019\;\text{rad}$$

**Step 3: Apply Haversine formula (Depot → Bin A).**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{Depot-A}} &= 2R \cdot \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\phi_A}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1\cos\phi_A \cdot \sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\lambda_A}{2}\right)}\right) \\
&\approx 2 \times 6371 \times \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{(0.00009)^2 + 0.9851 \times 0.9851 \times (0.000095)^2}\right) \\
&\approx 2 \times 6371 \times \arcsin(0.000132) \\
&\approx 2 \times 6371 \times 0.000132 \\
&\approx \mathbf{1.68\;\text{km}}
\end{aligned}$$

By similar calculation, $d_{\text{A-B}} \approx 2.40\;\text{km}$ and $d_{\text{Depot-B}} \approx 1.32\;\text{km}$.

**Step 4: Compare two routes:**
- Route 1 (Depot → A → B): $1.68 + 2.40 = 4.08\;\text{km}$
- Route 2 (Depot → B → A): $1.32 + 2.40 = 3.72\;\text{km}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Route 2 (Depot → Bin B → Bin A) is **shorter by 0.36 km**. In a real KTU demo, you would plot this in your Android app using **Google Maps Directions API**, and the **nearest-neighbor heuristic** would automatically recommend Route 2. This is the kind of analytical reasoning that earns 14/14 marks.

## 3.4 Project Effort Estimation (COCOMO-Inspired)

For your synopsis, you can estimate development effort using a simple linear model:

$$E_{\text{months}} = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^{b}$$

Where **KLOC** = thousands of lines of code, $a = 2.5$, $b = 1.05$ (organic-mode COCOMO for a small B.Tech team).

For an Android app of ~5,000 lines (5 KLOC):

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= 2.5 \times (5)^{1.05} \\
&\approx 2.5 \times 5.50 \\
&\approx \mathbf{13.75\;\text{person-months}}
\end{aligned}$$

Divided among 4 students for a 4-month semester: $\approx 3.4$ months/student — feasible. This **quantitative justification** of feasibility is a hallmark of a KTU distinction-grade synopsis.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
> List **any six sensors** that can be used in an Android-App-based IoT project for **Patient Health Monitoring** and state the physiological parameter each one measures.

**Model Answer (6 points):**

| # | Sensor | Physiological Parameter |
|---|--------|------------------------|
| 1 | MAX30102 | Heart rate (BPM) and SpO₂ (%) |
| 2 | AD8232 | Single-lead ECG waveform |
| 3 | DS18B20 | Body temperature (°C) |
| 4 | MPU6050 | Body posture / fall (accelerometer + gyroscope) |
| 5 | LM35 | Auxiliary temperature (analog) |
| 6 | Galvanic Skin Response (GSR) | Sweat / stress level |

These sensors are wired to an ESP32 MCU, which transmits the readings over **MQTT/Wi-Fi** to a **Firebase** backend, and the Android app subscribes to the realtime stream and displays the values.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
> What is **MQTT**? Why is it preferred over plain **HTTP** for IoT projects like Smart Farming or Garbage Management?

**Model Answer (3 points):**

> **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a **lightweight, publish-subscribe messaging protocol** designed for low-bandwidth, high-latency, or unreliable networks, standardized as **ISO/IEC 20922**.

It is preferred over HTTP for these projects because:
1. **Low overhead** — fixed 2-byte header vs. HTTP's hundreds of bytes, ideal for soil-moisture or bin-level telemetry.
2. **Push-based delivery** — pub/sub model avoids repeated polling, saving battery on solar-powered dustbins.
3. **QoS levels (0, 1, 2)** — guarantees message delivery for critical alerts (e.g., LPG leak, full dustbin overflow).
4. **Works on 3G/4G/LoRa** — robust in remote farms where HTTP would time out.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks, Module 4]**

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **(a)** Describe the **architecture and working of an Android App for Smart Farming** with a neat block diagram. List at least four sensors and two actuators used. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Explain how **adaptive irrigation logic** is implemented in the Android app using soil-moisture sensor data. Derive the linear-interpolation formula for pump ON-time and compute the ON-time for a dry reading of 800, current of 500, and wet reading of 200 (12-bit ADC, $T_{\max} = 60$ seconds). **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture and Working (7 Marks):**

A Smart Farming IoT system has **four layers**:

1. **Perception Layer:** Soil-moisture capacitive sensor, DHT22 (air T & RH), BH1750 (light intensity), NPK sensor (nutrients), rain-drop sensor — all connected to **ESP32** MCU.
2. **Network Layer:** **MQTT over Wi-Fi** (IEEE 802.11) with **LoRa 433 MHz** for far-away fields. Publish topic: `farm/node1/moisture`.
3. **Processing Layer:** **Firebase / ThingSpeak** time-series database with MATLAB Analytics for trend prediction.
4. **Application Layer:** **Android app** with four tabs:
   - *Live Field* — real-time gauges
   - *Crop Calendar* — sowing/harvest reminders
   - *Pump Control* — manual + auto toggle
   - *Weather* — OpenWeatherMap REST integration

**Two actuators:** 12V DC water pump (via relay) and 5V solenoid valve (drip lines).

**Working:** Sensors → ESP32 → MQTT broker → Firebase → Android app → user decision → MQTT command → relay → pump.

**(b) Adaptive Irrigation — Derivation (7 Marks):**

The Android app must decide **for how long** to switch the pump ON. We use **linear interpolation** between the dry and wet sensor ADC values.

$$T_{\text{pump}} = T_{\max} \cdot \frac{M_{\text{dry}} - M_{\text{current}}}{M_{\text{dry}} - M_{\text{wet}}}$$

Plugging in $M_{\text{dry}} = 800$, $M_{\text{current}} = 500$, $M_{\text{wet}} = 200$, $T_{\max} = 60$ s:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{pump}} &= 60 \cdot \frac{800 - 500}{800 - 200} \\
&= 60 \cdot \frac{300}{600} \\
&= 60 \cdot 0.5 \\
&= \mathbf{30\;\text{seconds}}
\end{aligned}$$

The pump will run for **30 s**, watering just enough to bring the moisture back toward the wet value — saving water and preventing over-irrigation. This value is sent via MQTT topic `farm/node1/pump` with payload `{"duration": 30}`. The ESP32 actuates the relay for the specified duration and publishes an acknowledgement.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> **(a)** With a neat architecture diagram, explain the working of an **Android App for Patient Health Monitoring**. Discuss the role of **FCM push notifications** in alerting the doctor during emergencies. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** For a Garbage Management IoT system, three dustbins are located at:
> - Bin A: (10.025, 76.350)
> - Bin B: (10.030, 76.345)
> - Bin C: (10.020, 76.355)
>
> The depot is at (10.015, 76.341). Use the **Haversine formula** to compute the shortest route and the total distance the garbage truck must travel. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Patient Health Monitoring — Architecture & FCM (7 Marks):**

The system has the following modules:

1. **Wearable hardware:** ESP32 + MAX30102 (HR, SpO₂) + AD8232 (ECG) + DS18B20 (temperature). Data is collected at 1 Hz.
2. **Cloud:** **Firebase Realtime Database** stores vitals in a JSON tree `/patients/{uid}/vitals/{timestamp}`.
3. **Threshold rule (Cloud Function):**

$$V_{\text{alert}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } V < V_{\min} \text{ or } V > V_{\max} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

4. **FCM Push Notification:** When the Cloud Function detects $V_{\text{alert}} = 1$, it calls `admin.messaging().send()` with topic `doctor_<id>` and an emergency payload. The **doctor's Android app** receives a high-priority push even when the app is in the background (because FCM uses a persistent TCP connection to Google servers).
5. **Android App UI:** Three fragments — *Live Vitals* (current), *History* (line chart via MPAndroidChart), and *Doctor Chat* (prescription exchange).

**Why FCM?** It is **free, battery-efficient, reliable, and supports topic-based multicasting** — sending one alert to all on-duty doctors in a hospital simultaneously.

**(b) Garbage Management — Haversine Shortest Route (7 Marks):**

We must compute the four possible depot → 3-bin → depot route distances and pick the shortest.

**Convert all coordinates to radians** (multiply by $\pi/180$):

| Point | $\phi$ (rad) | $\lambda$ (rad) |
|-------|--------------|-----------------|
| Depot | 0.17480 | 1.33240 |
| Bin A | 0.17498 | 1.33259 |
| Bin B | 0.17513 | 1.33249 |
| Bin C | 0.17489 | 1.33254 |

**Apply Haversine** with $R = 6371$ km:

$$d = 2R \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)}\right)$$

Computing each leg (after applying the formula, intermediate values abbreviated):

$$d_{\text{Depot-A}} \approx 1.68\;\text{km}, \quad d_{\text{Depot-B}} \approx 1.84\;\text{km}, \quad d_{\text{Depot-C}} \approx 0.96\;\text{km}$$
$$d_{A-B} \approx 0.74\;\text{km}, \quad d_{A-C} \approx 0.74\;\text{km}, \quad d_{B-C} \approx 1.18\;\text{km}$$

**Compute total distance for each of the 6 possible permutations (depot fixed, return-to-depot):**

| Route | Total Distance (km) |
|-------|---------------------|
| D → A → B → C → D | $1.68 + 0.74 + 1.18 + 0.96 = 4.56$ |
| D → A → C → B → D | $1.68 + 0.74 + 1.18 + 1.84 = 5.44$ |
| D → B → A → C → D | $1.84 + 0.74 + 0.74 + 0.96 = 4.28$ |
| D → B → C → A → D | $1.84 + 1.18 + 0.74 + 1.68 = 5.44$ |
| D → C → A → B → D | $0.96 + 0.74 + 0.74 + 1.84 = 4.28$ |
| D → C → B → A → D | $0.96 + 1.18 + 0.74 + 1.68 = 4.56$ |

**Shortest route:** D → B → A → C → D (or equivalently D → C → A → B → D), each **≈ 4.28 km**. The Android driver app uses the **Google Maps Directions API** with `optimize:true` to render this route on a live map.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> - **Common Mistake #1 (–2 marks):** Using **Euclidean distance** $d = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$ instead of the correct **Haversine** formula for GPS coordinates. The Earth is curved — examiners *will* deduct for this.
> - **Common Mistake #2 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to **convert degrees to radians** before applying $\sin$ and $\cos$. Always multiply by $\pi/180$ first.
> - **Common Mistake #3 (–2 marks):** Considering only one route permutation instead of all six. Use the nearest-neighbor heuristic as a quick way to find a *good* route, but mention that the **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** exact solution uses **branch-and-bound** or **Held–Karp DP** for completeness.
> - **Common Mistake #4 (–1 mark):** Using `R = 6378 km` (equatorial) vs `R = 6357 km` (polar). Mention "average Earth radius ≈ 6371 km" and you'll be safe.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These 12 Bullets Before the Exam:**

- ✅ All five recommended projects follow the **4-layer IoT architecture**: Perception → Network → Processing → Application.
- ✅ **MQTT** is the **default protocol** for these projects — topic-based pub/sub, port **1883** (TCP) / **8883** (TLS), QoS 0/1/2.
- ✅ **Firebase** is the most common cloud backend — uses **Realtime DB** for low-latency streaming and **Firestore** for rich queries (used in College Alert).
- ✅ **ESP32** is the workhorse MCU — supports **Wi-Fi (2.4 GHz) + BLE 4.2 + dual-core 240 MHz** for under ₹500.
- ✅ **Android front-end** uses **Java/Kotlin** in **Android Studio**, with libraries like **MPAndroidChart** (graphs), **Google Maps SDK** (location), and **Firebase SDK** (auth + DB + FCM).
- ✅ **Threshold-based alerts** follow $V \notin [V_{\min}, V_{\max}] \Rightarrow \text{Trigger Push Notification via FCM}$.
- ✅ **Energy-saving formula** for Home Automation: $E_{\text{saved}} = \dfrac{\sum P_i (t_{m,i} - t_{a,i})}{\sum P_i t_{m,i}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Haversine distance** is mandatory for GPS route optimization — never use Euclidean distance on lat/lon pairs.
- ✅ **Linear interpolation** is the simplest control law for adaptive irrigation: $T = T_{\max} \cdot \dfrac{M_{\text{dry}} - M_{\text{current}}}{M_{\text{dry}} - M_{\text{wet}}}$.
- ✅ **Weighted score matrix** is the right way to justify project selection in a synopsis — show $T_i = \sum w_k S_{i,k}$ with $\sum w_k = 1$.
- ✅ **COCOMO effort estimate** for a 5-KLOC Android app ≈ 13.75 person-months, feasible for a 4-member B.Tech team in one semester.
- ✅ Always end your answer with **"social impact + future scope + publication opportunity"** — these three closing lines alone can earn you 2–3 extra marks from a sympathetic KTU evaluator.
